$$ \text{ID}_{\text{new}} = \log_2 \left( \frac{2 \times 200}{40} \right) = \log_2(10) $$

$$ \text{ID}_{\text{new}} = \log_{10}(10) / \log_{10}(2) = 1 / 0.3010 $$

$$ \text{ID}_{\text{new}} \approx 3.322 \text{ bits} $$

$$ \text{MT}_{\text{new}} = 50 + 100 \times 3.322 = 50 + 332.2 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{MT}_{\text{new}} \approx 382.2 \text{ ms}} $$

### Engineering Interpretation

| Parameter | Original (W=20mm) | Enlarged (W=40mm) | Improvement |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| ID (bits) | 4.322 | 3.322 | **−23.1%** |
| MT (ms) | 482.2 | 382.2 | **−20.7%** |
| IP (bits/s) | 8.96 | 8.69 | Slight reduction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** Doubling the button width reduces movement time by **~100 ms** — a critical safety margin in time-stressed driving scenarios. This is why modern car infotainment systems use *oversized* physical buttons for primary controls and *touch targets* of at least 12 mm × 12 mm (Apple HIG) to maintain usable performance.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Hick's Law Menu Design

### Problem Statement

A handheld device menu has **N = 7 options**. Empirical testing gave $a = 200$ ms and $b = 120$ ms/bit. Calculate:

1. The **Choice Reaction Time**.
2. The **% increase in RT** if menu items are increased to 15.
3. The new RT if the menu is redesigned with sub-grouping into 3 sub-menus of 5 items each (averaged).

### Solution

**Step 1: Calculate RT for N = 7**

$$ \text{RT} = a + b \cdot \log_2(N+1) = 200 + 120 \cdot \log_2(8) $$

$$ \text{RT} = 200 + 120 \times 3 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{RT} = 560 \text{ ms}} $$

**Step 2: Calculate RT for N = 15**

$$ \text{RT}_{15} = 200 + 120 \cdot \log_2(16) = 200 + 120 \times 4 = 680 \text{ ms} $$

$$ \text{\% increase} = \frac{680 - 560}{560} \times 100\% $$

$$ \boxed{\text{\% increase} \approx 21.4\%} $$

**Step 3: Calculate RT with hierarchical sub-menus**

In the hierarchical design, the user first chooses *one of 3* sub-menus, then *one of 5* items:

$$ \text{RT}_{\text{total}} = \text{RT}_{\text{level 1}} + \text{RT}_{\text{level 2}} $$

$$ \text{RT}_{\text{level 1}} = 200 + 120 \cdot \log_2(4) = 200 + 240 = 440 \text{ ms} $$

$$ \text{RT}_{\text{level 2}} = 200 + 120 \cdot \log_2(6) = 200 + 120 \times 2.585 = 200 + 310.2 = 510.2 \text{ ms} $$

$$ \text{RT}_{\text{total}} = 440 + 510.2 = 950.2 \text{ ms} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-Intuitive Result:** Although hierarchical sub-menus *appear* more user-friendly, Hick's Law reveals the **total reaction time is 950.2 ms** — *significantly worse* than the flat menu's 560 ms! This is why modern smartphones prefer *flat* navigation with search/predictions rather than deep hierarchies.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Characteristic | Hick's Law | Fitts' Law |
|----------------|------------|------------|
| **Domain** | Cognitive / Decision | Motor / Movement |
| **Independent Variable** | Number of choices ($N$) | Distance / Target width ($A/W$) |
| **Dependent Variable** | Reaction Time | Movement Time |
| **Mathematical Form** | Logarithmic | Logarithmic |
| **Engineering Use** | Menu/interface design | Control/button sizing |
| **Information Metric** | Bits | Bits |
| **Typical Range (humans)** | 100–150 ms/bit | 80–150 ms/bit |
| **Limitation** | Assumes equal probability | Assumes discrete targets |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Human Response Time Architecture

The following functional block diagram illustrates the sequential cognitive-motor processing stages of the human operator and how each stage contributes to the overall temporal characteristic of a man-machine system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stimulus Event] --> B[Sensory Receptor]
    B --> C[Neural Transduction]
    C --> D[Perceptual Processing]
    D --> E[Short Term Memory]
    E --> F[Decision Selection]
    F --> G[Response Programming]
    G --> H[Motor Command]
    H --> I[Muscular Activation]
    I --> J[Physical Response]
    J --> K[Feedback Loop]

    S1[Sensory Latency 50-200 ms] -.-> B
    S2[Perception 100-150 ms] -.-> D
    S3[Reaction Time 150-500 ms] -.-> F
    S4[Movement Time 100-1000 ms] -.-> I
    S5[Total Response Time TRT] -.-> J
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Fitts' Law Application in Control Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Operator Reach Envelope] --> B[Measure Amplitude A]
    B --> C[Determine Target Width W]
    C --> D[Calculate Index of Difficulty ID]
    D --> E{ID Acceptable?}
    E -- No --> F[Redesign Geometry]
    F --> B
    E -- Yes --> G[Predict Movement Time MT]
    G --> H[Compare with Task Time Budget]
    H --> I{Budget Met?}
    I -- No --> J[Increase Target Width W]
    J --> C
    I -- Yes --> K[Finalize Control Layout]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Hick's Law Choice Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Stimulus Presented] --> Det[Detection 50 ms]
    Det --> Enc[Encoding 80 ms]
    Enc --> Choice{N Choices}
    Choice --> C1[Identify Option 1]
    Choice --> C2[Identify Option 2]
    Choice --> C3[Identify Option N]
    C1 --> Sel[Response Selection]
    C2 --> Sel
    C3 --> Sel
    Sel --> Exec[Execution]
    Exec --> End[Response Initiated]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Schematic — Speed vs. Accuracy Tradeoff

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HIGH_SPEED
        A1[High Movement Speed] --> A2[High Error Rate]
        A2 --> A3[Many Misses/Mistakes]
    end
    subgraph LOW_SPEED
        B1[Low Movement Speed] --> B2[Low Error Rate]
        B2 --> B3[High Precision]
    end
    subgraph OPTIMUM
        C1[Balanced Speed] --> C2[Acceptable Error Rate]
        C2 --> C3[Ergonomic Sweet Spot]
    end
```

## 4.5 Vigilance Decay Curve — Temporal Behavior over Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[T equals 0 minutes] --> P1[Detection 95 percent]
    P1 --> T30[T equals 30 minutes]
    T30 --> P2[Detection 80 percent]
    P2 --> T60[T equals 60 minutes]
    T60 --> P3[Detection 65 percent]
    P3 --> T120[T equals 120 minutes]
    T120 --> P4[Detection 50 percent]
    P4 --> Note[Vigilance Decay Per Exponential Model]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Reading of the Vigilance Diagram:** The detection probability of a rarely-occurring critical signal in a monitoring task can drop by **30–50%** within the first 30 minutes of watch-keeping. This is why air traffic controllers, nuclear plant operators, and aircraft pilots have mandatory shift rotation schedules to combat vigilance decay.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Reaction Time. Differentiate between Simple Reaction Time and Choice Reaction Time. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Reaction Time (RT)** is the time interval between the presentation of an unexpected stimulus and the initiation of a voluntary response by the operator. It is a measure of central nervous system processing speed and excludes the time for physical movement.

| Feature | Simple Reaction Time (SRT) | Choice Reaction Time (CRT) |
|---------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| Number of stimuli | One | Multiple (≥ 2) |
| Number of responses | One pre-specified | Multiple, mapped to stimuli |
| Cognitive load | Minimal — pre-decided | Higher — must identify + select |
| Typical duration | 180–250 ms | 300–500 ms |
| Brain regions | Primary motor cortex | Prefrontal cortex + motor cortex |
| Example | Pressing a brake when a single light appears | Selecting the correct switch from a panel of 8 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students confuse "Discrimination Reaction Time" (DRT) with "Choice Reaction Time" (CRT). DRT requires the operator to first *identify* which stimulus is relevant (e.g., red light only) and *inhibit* responses to distractors. DRT is therefore **longer** than CRT for the same number of alternatives.

---

### Question 2: State and explain Fitts' Law. Mention its significance in the design of controls. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Fitts' Law (1954)** states that the time required to move rapidly from a starting position to a target area is a logarithmic function of the ratio between the distance to the target and the width of the target.

$$ \text{MT} = a + b \cdot \log_2 \left( \frac{2A}{W} \right) $$

where $A$ = amplitude (distance), $W$ = target width, and $a, b$ are empirical constants.

**Significance in Control Design:**

1. **Button Sizing:** Larger targets reduce MT. Designers ensure critical buttons (e.g., emergency stop) are at least 25–40 mm.
2. **Control Layout:** Frequently-used controls should be placed close to the operator's resting hand position (minimizing $A$).
3. **Touchscreen UI:** Apple's Human Interface Guidelines specify minimum 44 pt × 44 pt touch targets — derived from Fitts' Law.
4. **Safety-Critical Systems:** Pilots' controls are oversized to ensure rapid, accurate actuation under stress.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[Internal Choice Option 1]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the various components of human response time. Explain how the Total Response Time is mathematically modeled and identify the typical duration ranges for each component. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A forklift operator must reach a control lever located **350 mm** away from the resting hand position. The lever has a **grip width of 25 mm**. Laboratory Fitts' Law testing has established $a = 60$ ms and $b = 110$ ms/bit. Determine:
- (i) The Index of Difficulty in bits.
- (ii) The Movement Time in ms.
- (iii) The Index of Performance in bits/s.
- (iv) The new MT if the lever is redesigned with a 50 mm grip.
- (v) Engineering recommendations. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Answer — Question A

**Part (a) — Components of Human Response Time**

The Total Response Time (TRT) of a human operator comprises four sequential components:

**Component 1: Sensory Latency (50–200 ms)**
- Time from stimulus onset to neural signal reaching the cerebral cortex
- Depends on stimulus modality: Auditory (~150 ms) < Visual (~180 ms) < Tactile (~155 ms)

**Component 2: Perception & Encoding (100–150 ms)**
- Identification of stimulus features (color, size, location)
- Comparison with stored mental templates (pattern recognition)

**Component 3: Decision / Reaction Time (150–500 ms)**
- Selection of appropriate response from alternatives
- Governed by **Hick's Law**: $\text{RT} = a + b \log_2(N+1)$
- Increases with number of choices, task complexity, uncertainty

**Component 4: Movement Time (100–1000 ms)**
- Physical execution of the motor response
- Governed by **Fitts' Law**: $\text{MT} = a + b \log_2(2A/W)$
- Increases with distance and required precision

**Mathematical Model:**

$$ \text{TRT} = t_{\text{sensory}} + t_{\text{perception}} + \text{RT} + \text{MT} $$

Or more compactly, where RT includes the cognitive phases:

$$ \boxed{\text{TRT} = t_{\text{latency}} + \text{RT}_{\text{cognitive}} + \text{MT}} $$

Typical industrial values: **TRT ≈ 0.5 to 2.0 seconds** for skilled operators on standard tasks.

---

**Part (b) — Fitts' Law Numerical Problem**

**Given:**
- $A = 350$ mm
- $W_1 = 25$ mm
- $a = 60$ ms, $b = 110$ ms/bit

**(i) Index of Difficulty (ID):**

$$ \text{ID} = \log_2 \left( \frac{2A}{W} \right) = \log_2 \left( \frac{2 \times 350}{25} \right) = \log_2 \left( \frac{700}{25} \right) = \log_2(28) $$

$$ \text{ID} = \frac{\log_{10}(28)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{1.4472}{0.3010} \approx 4.808 \text{ bits} $$

**(ii) Movement Time (MT):**

$$ \text{MT}_1 = a + b \cdot \text{ID} = 60 + 110 \times 4.808 $$

$$ \text{MT}_1 = 60 + 528.9 \approx 588.9 \text{ ms} $$

**(iii) Index of Performance (IP):**

$$ \text{IP} = \frac{\text{ID}}{\text{MT}_1} = \frac{4.808}{0.5889} \approx 8.16 \text{ bits/s} $$

**(iv) New MT with $W_2 = 50$ mm:**

$$ \text{ID}_2 = \log_2 \left( \frac{2 \times 350}{50} \right) = \log_2(14) = \frac{\log_{10}(14)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{1.1461}{0.3010} \approx 3.807 \text{ bits} $$

$$ \text{MT}_2 = 60 + 110 \times 3.807 = 60 + 418.8 \approx 478.8 \text{ ms} $$

**Improvement:**

$$ \Delta\text{MT} = 588.9 - 478.8 = 110.1 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(an 18.7\% reduction)} $$

**(v) Engineering Recommendations:**

1. The redesigned wider grip (50 mm) reduces MT by over 100 ms, which is operationally significant in time-critical material handling.
2. Alternatively, reducing amplitude $A$ (placing the lever closer to the resting position) would yield even greater gains — recommended in conjunction.
3. For high-throughput forklift operations, Fitts' Law performance indices of 8+ bits/s indicate a well-trained operator; indices below 4 bits/s suggest the need for retraining.
4. Apply anti-slip texture on the lever to maintain effective width $W$ in oily/dusty environments.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[Internal Choice Option 2]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain Hick's Law in detail. Derive the relationship between Choice Reaction Time and the number of stimulus-response alternatives. Discuss its application in menu design. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, describe the Speed-Accuracy Tradeoff in human performance. Explain how this tradeoff influences the design of inspection tasks in a manufacturing environment. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Answer — Question B

**Part (a) — Hick's Law Derivation and Application**

**Statement:** Hick's Law (1952) states that the Choice Reaction Time (CRT) increases linearly with the **information content** of the stimulus set, which is mathematically equivalent to $\log_2(N+1)$ where $N$ is the number of equally probable alternatives.

**Derivation:**

By Shannon's information theory, the information conveyed by selecting one stimulus from $N$ equiprobable alternatives is:

$$ H = \log_2(N) \quad [\text{bits}] $$

Hick hypothesized that the human decision process is an **information-limited channel**, and therefore:

$$ \text{CRT} = a + b \cdot H = a + b \cdot \log_2(N) $$

For the case where one *additional* "no-stimulus" or "null" option exists, the modified form is:

$$ \boxed{\text{CRT} = a + b \cdot \log_2(N+1)} $$

where:
- $a$ = baseline reaction time (intercept)
- $b$ = time per bit of information processed (~100–150 ms/bit)
- $N$ = number of stimulus-response alternatives

**Empirical Constants** (Card, Moran & Newell, 1983 — GOMS Model):
- $a \approx 100$ ms
- $b \approx 150$ ms/bit

**Application in Menu Design:**

- **Flat menus** with 7±2 items (Miller's limit) minimize Hick's Law RT.
- **Predictive menus** (e.g., smartphone autocomplete) effectively reduce $N$ by presenting only the most likely options first, reducing $\log_2(N)$ to a small value.
- **Voice assistants** rely on Hick's Law: opening with "How can I help?" converts a 100+ alternative menu into a free-form search, dramatically reducing decision time.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often quote Hick's Law as $\text{RT} = k \log_2(N)$ without the $+1$ term. The $+1$ accounts for the *implicit* "do nothing" option in choice tasks. Losing this term costs **1 mark** in Part B numerical questions.

---

**Part (b) — Speed-Accuracy Tradeoff (SAT)**

**Concept:** The Speed-Accuracy Tradeoff (SAT) is a fundamental behavioral phenomenon where an operator's emphasis on either *speed* or *accuracy* in a task comes at the cost of the other. It is formalized by the **cross-over effect** (Woodworth, 1899).

**Diagram (Description for Answer Sheet):**

Draw a graph with:
- **X-axis:** Time allowed per task (sec) — decreasing left to right
- **Y-axis:** % Errors committed
- The curve is **monotonically decreasing** and **S-shaped (sigmoidal)**, with a sharp "cross-over" inflection point at a critical time $T_c$.

**Critical Time $T_c$:** Below $T_c$, the operator shifts from "accuracy priority" to "speed priority" strategy, causing the error rate to climb rapidly.

**Mathematical Model:**

$$ P(\text{Error}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{k(T - T_c)}} $$

where $T$ is the available time, $T_c$ is the cross-over time, and $k$ is the steepness parameter.

**Influence on Inspection Task Design:**

1. **Line Speed Setting:** Industrial conveyor speeds must be calibrated such that $T_{\text{available}} > T_c$ for the inspection task, ensuring operators remain in the "accuracy regime."
2. **Jidoka Philosophy (Toyota Production System):* Production stops when defects are detected — eliminates the speed-accuracy conflict by separating inspection from production.
3. **Automation Decision Rule:** Tasks with cycle times below human $T_c$ should be automated (machine vision, robotic inspection).
4. **Lighting & Ergonomics:** Improving visibility effectively increases the operator's "perceptual time budget," shifting $T_c$ leftward on the curve and allowing higher line speeds at acceptable accuracy.

**Typical Industrial Values:**
- Visual inspection: $T_c \approx 2$–5 seconds per item
- Quality-control go/no-go: $T_c \approx 1$–2 seconds
- Force-sensitive assembly: $T_c \approx 3$–8 seconds

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Part (b), students frequently write only about the *qualitative* trade-off without mentioning the **cross-over effect** or the **sigmoidal shape** of the SAT curve. This omission typically results in a **2–3 mark deduction**. Always include: (1) the curve shape, (2) the $T_c$ concept, and (3) the strategic shift in operator mode.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Temporal Characteristics**

### Core Concepts (Must Know)
- [ ] **Total Response Time (TRT)** = Sensory Latency + RT + MT
- [ ] **Simple RT ≈ 180–250 ms**; **Choice RT ≈ 300–500 ms**
- [ ] **Auditory RT < Visual RT** (modality hierarchy)
- [ ] **Fitts' Law** = $\text{MT} = a + b \log_2(2A/W)$
- [ ] **Hick's Law** = $\text{RT} = a + b \log_2(N+1)$
- [ ] **Index of Difficulty (ID)** is measured in **bits**
- [ ] **Index of Performance (IP)** = $1/b$ ≈ 4–12 bits/s for humans
- [ ] **Speed-Accuracy Tradeoff** has a sigmoidal curve with cross-over time $T_c$

### Key Numbers to Memorize
- [ ] Auditory RT: ~150 ms
- [ ] Visual RT: ~180 ms
- [ ] Fitts' constant $b$: ~100 ms/bit
- [ ] Hick's constant $b$: ~120 ms/bit
- [ ] Fitts' IP range: 4–12 bits/s
- [ ] Miller's magic number: $7 \pm 2$
- [ ] Vigilance decay: 30–50% detection loss in 30 min

### Design Implications (Application Layer)
- [ ] Larger buttons → shorter MT (Fitts' Law)
- [ ] Fewer menu items → shorter RT (Hick's Law)
- [ ] Avoid deep menu hierarchies — Hick's Law RT compounds additively
- [ ] Use auditory signals for time-critical warnings (faster modality)
- [ ] Provide pre-cues / warnings to reduce choice uncertainty
- [ ] Set conveyor speeds above $T_c$ only when inspection is automated
- [ ] Combat vigilance decay with scheduled breaks and job rotation
- [ ] Apply Yerkes-Dodson: optimal arousal minimizes RT

### Common Exam Pitfalls
- [ ] Confusing SRT vs. CRT vs. DRT
- [ ] Forgetting the "+1" in Hick's Law
- [ ] Using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\log_2$ in Fitts'/Hick's Laws
- [ ] Not stating the Fitts' Law variant (original / Welford / MacKenzie)
- [ ] Confusing IP (bits/s) with $b$ (ms/bit) — they are reciprocals
- [ ] Forgetting the units when reporting ID (bits) and MT (ms)

### One-Line Mnemonics
- [ ] **"FAR"** — Fitts = **A**mplitude and **R**atio ($2A/W$)
- [ ] **"HIC"** — Hick = **I**nformation in **C**hoices ($\log_2 N$)
- [ ] **"SAM"** — Speed-Accuracy = **S**haped (sigmoidal) curve with **A**ccuracy vs **M**is-speed trade-off

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Luminance and Color Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Luminance and Color Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Luminance — The Formal Definition

**Luminance (L)** is the photometric measure of the luminous intensity per unit area of light travelling in a specified direction. In the **SI system**, it is expressed in **candela per square metre (cd/m²)**, which carries the special name **"nit"**.

Mathematically, the formal definition is:

$$L = \frac{dI}{dA_{\perp}}$$

where $I$ is the luminous intensity (in candela) emitted/reflected from an infinitesimal area element $dA$, and $A_{\perp}$ is the *projected* area of that surface element perpendicular to the specified viewing direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Luminance is the physical correlate of perceived brightness.** The human visual system does not measure light flux (lumens) or illuminance (lux) directly — it responds to luminance. Every display screen rating, every visibility regulation, and every ergonomic lighting code is fundamentally anchored to luminance.

## 1.2 Colour — The Formal Definition

**Colour** is the perceptual result of light of varying spectral power distributions interacting with the three cone types in the human retina. It is described by three perceptual attributes:

| Attribute | Definition | Engineering Analogue |
|-----------|------------|----------------------|
| **Hue** | The dominant wavelength (or mixture) — red, green, blue, etc. | The *type* of light |
| **Saturation (Chroma)** | Purity / colourfulness of the stimulus | The *intensity* of colour |
| **Lightness (Value)** | Perceived reflectance from a surface | The *brightness* of the surface |

> [!NOTE]
> In the **CIE 1931 system**, colour is quantified by the **tristimulus values X, Y, Z**, where **Y is defined to be exactly the luminance** of the stimulus. This is the most important link between photometry and colorimetry you will encounter in KTU examinations.

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — "Luminance and Colour in the Real World"

Imagine you are standing in a **dark room with a single candle** (luminance source). Now imagine you **paint the wall** behind the candle red. The candle's light hits the wall, and the wall radiates light back into your eyes. The amount of light coming from each square metre of the wall towards your eye is the **luminance**. The "redness" you perceive is the **colour characteristic** of that wall.

- The **candle** represents a *primary* (emissive) source of luminance.
- The **painted wall** represents a *secondary* (reflective) source — it borrows luminance from the candle and filters it spectrally.
- The **darkness of the room** is the *luminance contrast* (the difference between object and background).

If you are a **dashboard designer** in an aircraft cockpit, the markings on the glass must have the right luminance against the sky, and the right hue (red = danger, green = normal). Luminance tells the pilot *"I can see it"*; colour tells the pilot *"I know what it means"*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (KTU Examiner's Favourite):** Visual performance depends on **luminance contrast (C)** far more than on raw luminance level. A low luminance with high contrast is often more legible than a high luminance with poor contrast.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Human Factors Engineering

Luminance and colour are the two foundational **visual ergonomics variables**. They govern:

- **Legibility of displays** (cockpits, control rooms, dashboards)
- **Workplace safety** (signage, hazard marking, emergency lighting)
- **Visual fatigue and comfort** (VDT screen design, factory floor lighting)
- **Accident prevention** (visibility of pedestrians, machinery, road signs)

Every KTU question in this module reduces to: *"How does the human eye detect light and colour, and how must we engineer the environment to make the eye perform optimally and safely?"*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Luminance — Operational Mechanics

Luminance is governed by three physical inputs and one perceptual output:

1. **Luminous intensity (I)** — the directional flux emitted by a source (cd).
2. **Projected area (A⊥)** — the area as seen from the viewing direction.
3. **Viewing direction (θ)** — the angle between the surface normal and the line of sight.
4. **Perceived brightness (B)** — the subjective correlate of luminance in the brain.

### Step 2.1.1 — Lambert's Cosine Law for Luminance

For a perfectly *diffuse* (Lambertian) surface, the luminance is **independent of the viewing direction**. This is a unique property — although the intensity drops with cos θ and the projected area also drops with cos θ, the ratio remains constant.

$$L_{\text{Lambertian}} = \frac{\rho \cdot E}{\pi}$$

where $\rho$ is the reflectance (0 to 1) and $E$ is the illuminance (lux) on the surface.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why a sheet of white paper looks **equally bright** from any angle. KTU students must remember: the *cos θ cancels itself out* in a perfect diffuser.

### Step 2.1.2 — Directional (Specular) Luminance

For a mirror-like (specular) surface, luminance is highly directional. The reflected intensity follows the angle of reflection. Glossy instruments, instrument glass, and windscreens produce this kind of luminance — a frequent cause of *disability glare*.

### Step 2.1.3 — The Luminance Coefficient (q)

In real engineering practice, reflectance is often replaced by the **luminance coefficient**:

$$q = \frac{L}{E}$$

with units $\text{cd}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{lx}^{-1}$. For a Lambertian surface, $q = \rho / \pi$.

## 2.2 Luminance Contrast — The Critical Quantity

Contrast is the difference in luminance between an object and its background, normalised to the background. **Two formal definitions** are used in KTU:

**Michaelson contrast** (preferred for periodic patterns like gratings):

$$C_M = \frac{L_{\max} - L_{\min}}{L_{\max} + L_{\min}}$$

**Weber contrast** (preferred for small object on uniform background):

$$C_W = \frac{\vert L_{\text{object}} - L_{\text{background}} \vert}{L_{\text{background}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** Use $C_M$ for *textures and gratings*; use $C_W$ for *legibility of a single object* (e.g., a sign on a wall). The KTU 2024 examiner will explicitly mark this.

## 2.3 Colour — Three Theories You Must Know

### Step 2.3.1 — Trichromatic Theory (Young-Helmholtz, 1802)

The human retina contains **three types of cone photoreceptors**, each with a different peak spectral sensitivity:

| Cone Type | Peak Wavelength | Common Name | Colour Sensation |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|------------------|
| S (Short) | ~420 nm | Blue cones | Blue |
| M (Medium) | ~534 nm | Green cones | Green |
| L (Long) | ~564 nm | Red cones | Red |

Any perceived colour is the *sum* of the responses of these three cone types. This is the **physical basis of all colour reproduction** (TVs, monitors, print).

### Step 2.3.2 — Opponent-Process Theory (Hering, 1872)

Neural signals from the cones are *coded* into **opponent channels** before reaching the brain:

- **Red ↔ Green** channel
- **Blue ↔ Yellow** channel
- **Black ↔ White** (luminance) channel

This explains why we can perceive **reddish-green** as impossible, but **yellowish-red** (orange) is possible. KTU loves this question.

### Step 2.3.3 — CIE 1931 System (The Engineering Standard)

The **Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage (CIE)** defined a *standard observer* with three colour matching functions: $\bar{x}(\lambda), \bar{y}(\lambda), \bar{z}(\lambda)$.

For a light source with spectral power distribution $S(\lambda)$:

$$X = k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda) \bar{x}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$

$$Y = k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda) \bar{y}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$

$$Z = k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda) \bar{z}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Y tristimulus value is, by CIE convention, the luminance of the stimulus.** $\bar{y}(\lambda)$ is normalised to the photopic luminous efficiency function $V(\lambda)$. This is the bridge between photometry and colorimetry.

The chromaticity coordinates are then:

$$x = \frac{X}{X + Y + Z}, \quad y = \frac{Y}{X + Y + Z}$$

These two coordinates locate any colour on the famous **CIE chromaticity diagram** (the horseshoe shape).

## 2.4 Colour Temperature and CRI

**Correlated Colour Temperature (CCT)** — describes the colour appearance of a *white* light source by comparing it to a black-body radiator. A 2856 K incandescent lamp appears "warm" (yellowish); a 6500 K daylight appears "cool" (bluish-white).

**Colour Rendering Index (CRI or Ra)** — measures how faithfully a light source renders the colours of objects, compared to a reference source of the same CCT. Ra = 100 is perfect rendering. Industrial workplaces should have **Ra ≥ 80**; colour-critical tasks (textile inspection, surgery) need **Ra ≥ 90**.

## 2.5 Munsell Colour System (Engineering Reference)

The **Munsell system** organises colours by three perceptually uniform scales:

- **Hue (H)** — 10 basic hues (R, YR, Y, GY, G, BG, B, PB, P, RP)
- **Value (V)** — lightness on 0 (black) to 10 (white)
- **Chroma (C)** — saturation, starting at 0 (grey) and increasing outward

The relation between Munsell Value (V) and CIE luminance factor (Y, in %) is:

$$V = 10 \sqrt{Y}$$

This is a **high-yield KTU formula** — it allows conversion between the *psychophysical* Munsell system and the *physical* CIE system.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit / Range |
|---|---------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | $L = dI / dA_{\perp}$ | Fundamental definition of luminance | cd/m² (nit) |
| 2 | $L = \rho E / \pi$ | Lambertian luminance from illuminance | cd/m² |
| 3 | $q = L / E$ | Luminance coefficient | cd·m⁻²·lx⁻¹ |
| 4 | $C_W = \vert \Delta L \vert / L_b$ | Weber contrast | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_M = (L_{\max} - L_{\min})/(L_{\max} + L_{\min})$ | Michaelson contrast | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\Delta I / I = k$ | Weber's law — Just Noticeable Difference | k ≈ 0.01 to 0.08 |
| 7 | $K(\lambda) = 683 \cdot V(\lambda)$ | Spectral luminous efficacy | lm/W |
| 8 | $X = k \int S(\lambda) \bar{x}(\lambda) d\lambda$ | CIE X tristimulus | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Y = k \int S(\lambda) \bar{y}(\lambda) d\lambda$ | CIE Y (= luminance) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Z = k \int S(\lambda) \bar{z}(\lambda) d\lambda$ | CIE Z tristimulus | dimensionless |
| 11 | $x = X/(X+Y+Z)$, $y = Y/(X+Y+Z)$ | CIE chromaticity | dimensionless |
| 12 | $V = 10\sqrt{Y}$ | Munsell Value from CIE Y | Y in % |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Luminance Application | Colour Application |
|--------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **Aviation** | Runway light intensity, cockpit display luminance | Standardised signal colours (red = danger) |
| **Automotive** | Headlamp beam luminance, dashboard contrast | Brake-light red, indicator amber |
| **Industrial Safety** | Minimum luminance for escape routes | Red for fire equipment, yellow for caution |
| **Healthcare** | Operating-theatre luminance (3000–10 000 nit displays) | High CRI light (Ra > 95) for tissue discrimination |
| **UI/UX Design** | WCAG 4.5:1 contrast ratio for text legibility | Colour-blind safe palettes (deuteranopia, protanopia) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Lambertian Luminance

**Starting premise:** A flat diffuse surface receives illuminance $E$ (lx). The luminous flux incident on an area $A$ is:

$$\Phi_{\text{inc}} = E \cdot A$$

The reflected flux is reduced by the reflectance $\rho$:

$$\Phi_{\text{ref}} = \rho \cdot E \cdot A$$

For a perfect Lambertian emitter, the **luminous intensity** in any direction $\theta$ from the surface normal is:

$$I(\theta) = I_0 \cos \theta$$

where $I_0$ is the intensity in the normal direction.

The total emitted flux is obtained by integrating over the hemisphere:

$$\Phi_{\text{emitted}} = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} I_0 \cos \theta \cdot \sin \theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{\text{emitted}} &= I_0 \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\phi \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \cos \theta \sin \theta \, d\theta \\
&= I_0 (2\pi) \cdot \left[ \frac{\sin^2 \theta}{2} \right]_{0}^{\pi/2} \\
&= I_0 (2\pi) \cdot \frac{1}{2} \\
&= \pi I_0
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$I_0 = \frac{\Phi_{\text{ref}}}{\pi} = \frac{\rho E A}{\pi}$$

Now, the **luminance** is intensity divided by the *projected* area in the viewing direction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{I(\theta)}{A \cos \theta} = \frac{I_0 \cos \theta}{A \cos \theta} = \frac{I_0}{A} \\
L &= \frac{\rho E}{\pi}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key insight:** The $\cos \theta$ cancels exactly. The luminance of a perfect diffuser is *direction-independent*. This is the *defining optical property* of a Lambertian surface.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Michelson Contrast from a Sinusoidal Grating

**Starting premise:** Consider a target with luminance varying sinusoidally:

$$L(x) = L_0 + L_m \sin\left(\frac{2\pi x}{\lambda}\right)$$

The maximum luminance is $L_{\max} = L_0 + L_m$ and minimum is $L_{\min} = L_0 - L_m$.

Substituting into the definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_M &= \frac{L_{\max} - L_{\min}}{L_{\max} + L_{\min}} \\
&= \frac{(L_0 + L_m) - (L_0 - L_m)}{(L_0 + L_m) + (L_0 - L_m)} \\
&= \frac{2 L_m}{2 L_0} = \frac{L_m}{L_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key insight:** For a sinusoidal grating, the Michelson contrast reduces to the **modulation ratio** $L_m / L_0$. This is the *modulation* used in the display industry to specify the strength of a visual pattern.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Luminance vs. Illuminance vs. Luminous Intensity

This is a classic KTU differentiation table. Memorise it.

| Property | Symbol | SI Unit | Definition | Depends on? |
|----------|--------|---------|------------|-------------|
| **Luminous Flux** | $\Phi$ | lumen (lm) | Total light emitted per second | Source power, spectral content |
| **Luminous Intensity** | $I$ | candela (cd) | Flux per unit solid angle | Direction of emission |
| **Illuminance** | $E$ | lux (lx) = lm/m² | Flux incident on a surface | Distance from source, angle of incidence |
| **Luminance** | $L$ | cd/m² (nit) | Intensity per unit projected area | Reflectance, viewing angle, illuminance |
| **Reflectance** | $\rho$ | dimensionless | Ratio of reflected to incident flux | Material property, wavelength |

**Derivation of the inverse-square law for illuminance** (frequently asked):

A point source of intensity $I$ emits uniformly over a sphere of radius $r$. The surface area of the sphere is $4\pi r^2$. The flux per unit area (illuminance) at distance $r$ is:

$$E = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi r^2} = \frac{I}{r^2}$$

For a tilted surface, the projected area reduces by $\cos \theta$, giving:

$$E = \frac{I \cos \theta}{r^2}$$

This is the **cosine law of illuminance**, which directly determines the illuminance (and hence, through Lambertian reflection, the luminance) at any point in a workspace.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Contrast Calculation for a Display

> A digital instrument display has a background luminance of **50 cd/m²** and a text luminance of **200 cd/m²**. Calculate the Weber and Michelson contrast. Is this contrast adequate for legibility?

**Solution using Weber contrast:**

$$C_W = \frac{\vert L_{\text{text}} - L_{\text{bg}} \vert}{L_{\text{bg}}} = \frac{200 - 50}{50} = 3.0$$

This means the text is 4× brighter than the background. In percentage terms, $C_W = 300\%$.

**Solution using Michelson contrast:**

$$C_M = \frac{L_{\max} - L_{\min}}{L_{\max} + L_{\min}} = \frac{200 - 50}{200 + 50} = \frac{150}{250} = 0.60$$

**Adequacy check:**
- Weber contrast $C_W = 3.0$ is well above the legibility threshold of 0.5 (or 50%) recommended by ergonomic standards.
- Michelson contrast $C_M = 0.60$ is above the 0.5 threshold for comfortable reading.

> **Engineering verdict:** The display has excellent contrast and will be highly legible. KTU students should always validate their numerical answer against a published ergonomic threshold.

## 3.5 Worked Example — Tristimulus and Chromaticity Coordinates

> A light source has the following CIE tristimulus values: $X = 42.5$, $Y = 40.0$, $Z = 17.5$. Find (a) the luminance, (b) the chromaticity coordinates, (c) the dominant wavelength if $x = 0.3$, $y = 0.4$ lies on a line from the white point $(0.33, 0.33)$ to the spectrum locus at $(0.45, 0.55)$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Luminance:** By CIE convention, $Y$ *is* the luminance (proportional). Assuming the values are normalised so that $Y$ is in cd/m² for a 1-lux stimulus, $L = 40.0$ cd/m² (relative).

**(b) Chromaticity coordinates:**

$$x = \frac{X}{X + Y + Z} = \frac{42.5}{42.5 + 40.0 + 17.5} = \frac{42.5}{100.0} = 0.425$$

$$y = \frac{Y}{X + Y + Z} = \frac{40.0}{100.0} = 0.400$$

$$z = 1 - x - y = 1 - 0.425 - 0.400 = 0.175$$

**(c) Dominant wavelength** is found by extrapolating the straight line from the white point $(0.33, 0.33)$ through the test colour $(0.425, 0.400)$ to the spectrum locus. The intersection at $(0.45, 0.55)$ corresponds to a wavelength of approximately **500 nm** (cyan-green).

> This problem typically appears as a 14-mark question in KTU. Show *every* step of the integration by parts and the chromaticity calculation.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common Luminance Levels in Engineering

| Environment | Typical Luminance Range | Engineering Standard |
|-------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Office paper (white) under 500 lx | 150–180 cd/m² | ISO 9241-303 |
| LCD computer display | 100–350 cd/m² | TCO Certified |
| Mobile phone screen (sunlight) | 500–1000 cd/m² | IEC 61966 |
| Outdoor sign (daylight) | 1000–3000 cd/m² | ISO 3864 (safety signs) |
| Car headlamp (low beam) | 10 000–30 000 cd/m² | ECE R112 |
| Sun (at noon, disc) | ~1 600 000 000 cd/m² | Solar constant reference |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Visual Perception Pathway from Luminance to Brain

This block diagram traces how a luminance stimulus is converted into a colour/brightness perception in the human brain — the most frequently drawn schematic in KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Light Source\nSpectral Power S lambda] --> B[Surface Interaction\nReflectance rho]
    B --> C[Luminance L\ncd per m squared]
    C --> D[Retinal Image\nOptics and Pupil]
    D --> E[Photoreceptors\nRods and Cones]
    E --> F1[S Cones 420 nm\nBlue channel]
    E --> F2[M Cones 534 nm\nGreen channel]
    E --> F3[L Cones 564 nm\nRed channel]
    F1 --> G[Opponent Processing\nRetinal Ganglion Cells]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H1[Red versus Green]
    G --> H2[Blue versus Yellow]
    G --> H3[Black versus White Luminance]
    H1 --> I[Visual Cortex V1]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Perception\nHue Saturation Brightness]
```

**Block reading guide:**
- **Light source** emits flux with spectral distribution $S(\lambda)$.
- **Surface interaction** (reflection, transmission, absorption) modifies the spectrum. The *reflected luminance* $L$ leaves the surface.
- **Retinal image** formation is governed by optics and pupil aperture.
- **Photoreceptors** transduce light into neural signals. Rods handle scotopic (low-light) vision; cones handle photopic (colour) vision.
- **Opponent processing** re-codes the three cone signals into three opponent channels.
- **Visual cortex** reconstructs the unified percept: hue, saturation, brightness.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — The Photometric to Colorimetric Bridge

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Spectral Power Distribution\nS of lambda] --> B[Photometric Weighting\nV lambda function]
    A --> C[Colorimetric Weighting\nx bar y bar z bar functions]
    B --> D[Luminance Y\ncd per m squared]
    C --> E[Tristimulus X Y Z\ndimensionless]
    D --> F[Perceived Brightness\nsubjective]
    E --> G[Chromaticity x y\nhorseshoe diagram]
    E --> H[Munsell Value V\n10 sqrt Y formula]
    F --> I[Engineering Output\nVisibility and Safety]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

**Reading guide:** The diagram shows that the *same* spectral power distribution $S(\lambda)$ feeds two parallel pipelines: a photometric one (producing luminance $Y$) and a colorimetric one (producing tristimulus $X, Y, Z$). These pipelines converge into engineering decisions about visibility, legibility, and safety.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Contrast and Legibility Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Background Luminance Lb] --> C[Visual Cortex\nPerceived Contrast]
    B[Object Luminance Lo] --> C
    C --> D{Contrast above\nthreshold C greater than 0.5}
    D -- Yes --> E[Legible]
    D -- No --> F[Invisible or Causes Eye Strain]
    E --> G[Engineering Decision\nAccept Design]
    F --> H[Redesign Lighting\nor Colour Coding]
```

**Reading guide:** This is the decision-loop used in *display engineering* and *signage design*. The contrast threshold (typically $C_W \geq 0.5$ or $C_M \geq 0.5$) determines whether the visual element is legible. If not, the engineer must adjust luminance (e.g., increase backlight) or change colour coding (e.g., switch to a more saturated hue).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]

**Q1.** Define *luminance*. State its SI unit and explain, with one example, why it is the most important photometric quantity in display engineering.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Luminance is the luminous intensity per unit projected area of a surface, in a specified direction. Its SI unit is candela per square metre (cd/m²), also called a *nit*. The formal definition is $L = dI/dA_{\perp}$.

*Why it matters in display engineering:* Luminance is the physical correlate of perceived brightness. The human visual system's response is governed by the luminance reaching the retina, not by the raw luminous flux. Therefore, a display's *rated luminance* (typically 200–1000 cd/m² for modern screens) directly determines its visibility under ambient lighting. A high-luminance display is required for outdoor sunlight viewing, while a moderate-luminance display suffices for office work.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for unit, 1 mark for engineering relevance.)*

---

> [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]

**Q2.** Differentiate between the **trichromatic theory** and the **opponent-process theory** of colour vision. Give one engineering implication of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Theory | Core Idea | Engineering Implication |
|--------|-----------|------------------------|
| **Trichromatic** | Three types of cones (S, M, L) encode colour via their differential responses. | Justifies the *RGB additive* system used in all digital displays, cameras, and sensors. |
| **Opponent-Process** | Neural signals are re-coded into Red↔Green and Blue↔Yellow opponent channels. | Explains *colour blindness* (e.g., deuteranopia = red-green deficiency) and motivates *colour-blind safe palettes* in UI/UX design. |

*(Award 1 mark for each theory's core idea, 1 mark for each engineering implication.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the expression for the luminance of a perfectly diffuse (Lambertian) surface, illuminated by an illuminance $E$. State the assumptions clearly. (7 marks)

**(b)** A control-room display has a screen luminance of 250 cd/m² and is viewed against a wall of luminance 60 cd/m². Calculate (i) Weber contrast and (ii) Michaelson contrast. Comment on the legibility of the display. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The surface is *perfectly diffuse* (Lambertian) — it reflects light equally in all directions.
2. The reflectance $\rho$ is constant across the visible spectrum (grey surface).
3. The illuminance $E$ is uniform across the surface.

**Derivation:**

The luminous flux incident on a small area $A$ is:

$$\Phi_{\text{inc}} = E \cdot A$$

The reflected flux is reduced by reflectance:

$$\Phi_{\text{ref}} = \rho \cdot E \cdot A$$

For a Lambertian emitter, intensity in direction $\theta$ is $I(\theta) = I_0 \cos \theta$. Integrating over the hemisphere to find the total emitted flux:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{\text{emitted}} &= \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} I_0 \cos \theta \cdot \sin \theta \, d\theta \, d\phi \\
&= I_0 \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \pi I_0
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating $\Phi_{\text{emitted}} = \Phi_{\text{ref}}$:

$$\pi I_0 = \rho E A \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_0 = \frac{\rho E A}{\pi}$$

The luminance is intensity per unit projected area:

$$L = \frac{I(\theta)}{A \cos \theta} = \frac{I_0 \cos \theta}{A \cos \theta} = \frac{I_0}{A} = \frac{\rho E}{\pi}$$

*Result:* $L = \rho E / \pi$ — **direction-independent** for a Lambertian surface.

*(Award 2 marks for assumptions, 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for final expression and direction-independence statement.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Given: $L_{\text{screen}} = 250$ cd/m², $L_{\text{wall}} = 60$ cd/m².

**(i) Weber contrast:**

$$C_W = \frac{\vert L_{\text{screen}} - L_{\text{wall}} \vert}{L_{\text{wall}}} = \frac{250 - 60}{60} = \frac{190}{60} = 3.167$$

**(ii) Michaelson contrast:**

$$C_M = \frac{L_{\max} - L_{\min}}{L_{\max} + L_{\min}} = \frac{250 - 60}{250 + 60} = \frac{190}{310} = 0.613$$

**Legibility comment:** A Weber contrast of **3.17 (317%)** is well above the recommended threshold of 0.5. A Michelson contrast of 0.61 is also above the 0.5 threshold. The display is *highly legible* and should be comfortable to view for extended periods.

*(Award 2 marks for Weber calculation, 2 marks for Michaelson calculation, 3 marks for engineering comment with threshold comparison.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **CIE 1931 colour system** with the help of tristimulus values $X, Y, Z$ and chromaticity coordinates $x, y$. Why is the $Y$ value taken to represent luminance? (7 marks)

**(b)** A workshop uses a fluorescent lamp with CCT = 4000 K. The work-surface illuminance is 500 lux. The work-piece has a reflectance of 0.65. Calculate (i) the luminance of the work-piece assuming Lambertian behaviour, and (ii) the recommended minimum luminance for inspection work. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The CIE 1931 system was established by the *Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage* as a standard method of specifying any colour perceivable by the *standard observer*. The system uses three *colour matching functions*: $\bar{x}(\lambda)$, $\bar{y}(\lambda)$, $\bar{z}(\lambda)$, derived from experiments on human trichromatic matching.

**Tristimulus values** are computed by integrating the product of the spectral power distribution of the source $S(\lambda)$ with each matching function:

$$X = k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda) \bar{x}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$

$$Y = k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda) \bar{y}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$

$$Z = k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda) \bar{z}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$

**Why $Y$ represents luminance:** The CIE deliberately chose $\bar{y}(\lambda)$ to be *identical* to the photopic luminous efficiency function $V(\lambda)$. By the photometric definition, luminance is flux weighted by $V(\lambda)$. Therefore, the $Y$ tristimulus value *is* the luminance (up to a normalising constant $k$). This is the elegant bridge between photometry and colorimetry.

**Chromaticity coordinates** (dimensionless, separating colour from brightness):

$$x = \frac{X}{X+Y+Z}, \quad y = \frac{Y}{X+Y+Z}, \quad z = \frac{Z}{X+Y+Z}$$

with the constraint $x + y + z = 1$. The two coordinates $(x, y)$ locate the colour on the *CIE chromaticity diagram*, a horseshoe-shaped plot of all visible colours.

*(Award 3 marks for tristimulus explanation, 2 marks for $Y$ as luminance, 2 marks for chromaticity coordinates.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Given: $E = 500$ lx, $\rho = 0.65$, CCT = 4000 K.

**(i) Luminance of work-piece** (Lambertian):

$$L = \frac{\rho E}{\pi} = \frac{0.65 \times 500}{3.14159} = \frac{325}{3.14159} = 103.45 \text{ cd/m}^2$$

**(ii) Recommended minimum luminance for inspection work:** Industrial standards (e.g., ISO 8995 / CIE S 008) recommend **at least 500 lx of illuminance** for inspection work. The corresponding luminance of a typical work-piece ($\rho = 0.5$) would be:

$$L_{\min} = \frac{0.5 \times 500}{\pi} = 79.6 \text{ cd/m}^2$$

Our calculated luminance of **103.45 cd/m²** exceeds this minimum, so the work-piece is sufficiently bright. However, for *fine* inspection (e.g., electronics assembly), the standard recommends 1000 lx, which would push the required luminance to ~160 cd/m² — the current setup would be marginal.

*(Award 2 marks for calculation, 2 marks for $L$ value, 3 marks for engineering recommendation with comparison.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing luminance with illuminance.** Luminance is in cd/m² (a property of the *emitting/reflecting* surface). Illuminance is in lux (a property of the *receiving* surface). Mixing up units will cost you 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Using the wrong contrast formula.** Weber is for *object on background*. Michaelson is for *periodic patterns*. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting that $Y$ in CIE = luminance.** This is the single most-likely "trap" in any CIE-based question.
> 4. **Not validating against ergonomic thresholds.** A numerical contrast value without a "is this adequate?" comment loses the engineering-application marks.
> 5. **Treating colour as wavelength only.** Colour is a *percept*, not a wavelength. Always mention the three perceptual attributes: hue, saturation, lightness.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Luminance $L$** is luminous intensity per unit projected area; unit is **cd/m²** (nit). Formula: $L = dI/dA_{\perp}$.
- [ ] **Lambertian luminance** is direction-independent: $L = \rho E / \pi$.
- [ ] **Luminance coefficient** $q = L/E$ — used in road lighting design.
- [ ] **Weber contrast** $C_W = \vert L_o - L_b \vert / L_b$ — for object on background.
- [ ] **Michaelson contrast** $C_M = (L_{\max} - L_{\min})/(L_{\max} + L_{\min})$ — for gratings.
- [ ] **Contrast threshold** for legibility ≈ 0.5 (both Weber and Michaelson).
- [ ] **Weber's law:** $\Delta I / I = k$, with $k$ depending on stimulus (e.g., 0.01 for light, 0.08 for weight).
- [ ] **Spectral luminous efficacy** $K(\lambda) = 683 \cdot V(\lambda)$, with peak 683 lm/W at 555 nm.
- [ ] **Three cone types:** S (420 nm), M (534 nm), L (564 nm) → Trichromatic theory.
- [ ] **Opponent channels:** Red↔Green, Blue↔Yellow, Black↔White.
- [ ] **CIE 1931** uses $\bar{x}, \bar{y}, \bar{z}$; **$Y$ tristimulus = luminance**.
- [ ] **Chromaticity coordinates** $x, y$ with $x + y + z = 1$ on horseshoe diagram.
- [ ] **Munsell value** $V = 10\sqrt{Y}$ (with $Y$ in %).
- [ ] **CCT** = correlated colour temperature; **CRI/Ra** = colour rendering index (Ra ≥ 80 industrial, ≥ 90 critical).
- [ ] **Legibility rule of thumb:** contrast first, then luminance level, then colour coding.
- [ ] **Engineering applications** to remember: cockpit displays, road signs, VDT screens, surgical lighting, colour-blind safe UI.

> **Mnemonic for contrast formulas:** "**W**eber = **W**all (single object on a background); **M**ichaelson = **M**odulation (sinusoidal pattern)."

> **Mnemonic for cones:** "**S**hort = **S**ky (blue); **M**edium = **M**eadow (green); **L**ong = **L**ava (red)."

> **Mnemonic for CIE bridge:** "**Y** in CIE = **Y**our luminance. It's not a coincidence — it's a convention."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Display Formatting - Size and Spacing of Displayed Characters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Display Formatting - Size and Spacing of Displayed Characters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Display Formatting** refers to the systematic and human-engineered arrangement of alphanumeric characters, symbols, and graphical elements on a visual display surface (such as a CRT, LCD, LED, HUD, instrument panel, or printed interface) to optimize **legibility**, **readability**, and **comprehension speed** for the human observer.

The **size** of a displayed character is the geometric dimension of the character (height, width, stroke width), typically measured in millimeters (mm) or as a **visual angle** (arcminutes) subtended at the eye. **Spacing** refers to the controlled horizontal and vertical distances maintained between characters, words, lines, and groups of information to reduce visual crowding and search time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Display formatting is a sub-topic under *Visual Displays* in Human Factors Engineering. It is directly tied to the human visual system's resolving power, accommodation, and saccadic eye movements.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you are driving on a highway at 100 km/h. A road sign displaying a sharp curve warning. If the letters on that sign are too small, too tightly packed, or too far away — even though the words are correct — you **miss the message** and possibly have an accident. The sign designer (an applied human-factors engineer) must ensure the letters are **large enough** to be read at high speed, **spaced wide enough** to be distinguished from each other, and positioned so your eyes can sweep across them effortlessly.

Now translate this to a **cockpit Multi-Function Display (MFD)**, a **patient monitor in an ICU**, or a **CNC machine operator panel**. The same principle applies: characters that are the *right size* and *correctly spaced* allow the operator to extract critical information within fractions of a second — often under stress, low light, or vibration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept Snapshot:** Good display formatting is *invisible* to the user. Bad display formatting is *immediately* noticed and can cause **errors, fatigue, and accidents**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Visual Acuity Threshold (Snellen 1.0):** A normal eye resolves detail at a **visual angle of 1 arcminute (1′)** under optimal conditions.
- **Recommended Minimum Character Visual Angle:** **16 to 22 arcminutes** (approximately 12× the acuity limit) for comfortable, error-free reading.
- **Standard Viewing Distance Reference:** **71 cm (28 inches)** — the canonical "office viewing distance" used in most ergonomic standards.
- **Stroke Width Ratio (SW : CH):** **1 : 6 to 1 : 8** of character height.
- **Character Aspect Ratio (Width : Height):** **0.6 : 1 to 0.9 : 1** for alphanumeric digits.
- **Line Spacing:** Typically **1.0× to 1.5×** the character height between baselines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Theoretical Principles

The science of character sizing rests on three pillars rooted in **visual perception physiology**:

1. **Visual Acuity Limit** — The human eye, under good lighting, can resolve features as small as **1 arcminute**. For practical, error-free reading, designers use a safety factor of **10×–20×** the acuity limit.

2. **Reading Speed & Comprehension** — Reading speed (words per minute) increases with character size up to a point, then plateaus. Too-large characters actually **reduce the number of characters visible per fixation**, slowing down scanning.

3. **Visual Crowding Effect (Bouma's Law)** — When characters are too close horizontally, the brain cannot separate them, and identification errors increase. Hence, **minimum inter-character spacing** must be enforced.

### 2.2 The Geometry of Visual Angle

When a character of height **h** is viewed from a distance **D**, it subtends a **visual angle θ** at the eye node. For small angles (which is the case in normal display viewing):

$$
\theta \approx \tan(\theta) = \frac{h}{D} \quad \text{(radians)}
$$

Converting to **arcminutes** (the unit used in display standards):

$$
\theta_{\text{arcmin}} = \frac{h}{D} \times \frac{180}{\pi} \times 60 = 3438 \times \frac{h}{D}
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Standard / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|----------------------|---------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Visual angle (radians) | $\theta = h / D$ | radians (rad) | Small-angle approximation |
| 2 | Visual angle (arcminutes) | $\theta' = 3438 \cdot (h / D)$ | arcminutes (′) | Display sizing standard |
| 3 | Visual angle (degrees) | $\theta^{\circ} = (180/\pi) \cdot (h / D)$ | degrees (°) | Quick estimation |
| 4 | Minimum character height | $h_{\min} = D \cdot \tan(16') \approx 0.00465 \cdot D$ | mm (if D in mm) | Console/dashboard design |
| 5 | Recommended character height | $h_{\text{rec}} = D \cdot \tan(22') \approx 0.0064 \cdot D$ | mm (if D in mm) | Comfortable reading |
| 6 | Critical Print Size (CPS) | $CPS = 0.005 \cdot D$ | mm | Minimum legible size |
| 7 | Stroke width | $SW = h / 6$ to $h / 8$ | mm | Legibility threshold |
| 8 | Inter-character spacing | $ICS \geq 1 \cdot SW$ | mm | Crowding prevention |
| 9 | Inter-word spacing | $IWS \geq 1 \cdot CH$ | mm | Word boundary clarity |
| 10 | Line spacing (leading) | $LS = 1.0\cdot CH \text{ to } 1.5\cdot CH$ | mm | Vertical separation |
| 11 | Character width : height | $W : H = 0.6 : 1 \text{ to } 0.9 : 1$ | dimensionless | Aspect ratio guideline |
| 12 | Number of characters per line | $N \approx \text{Field Width} / (W + ICS)$ | count | Layout planning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for Tables:** All absolute-value notations use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation Cockpit Displays (HUDs, PFDs):** Pilots read flight data at 60–80 cm under vibration; character heights are computed using the 22-arcminute rule.
- **Medical Patient Monitors:** ICU nurses must read vitals from 1–2 m; character size influences alarm-response time.
- **Automotive Instrument Clusters:** Speedometer digits must be readable at arm's length during day/night driving.
- **CNC / Industrial Control Panels:** Operators stand 0.5–1 m away under factory lighting; optimum size minimizes error rate.
- **Smartphone & Web UX:** Designers translate visual-angle rules into pixel dimensions for typical 30–40 cm viewing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Character Height from a Given Viewing Distance

**Problem Statement:** A control-room operator views a VDU screen from a distance of **80 cm**. Determine the **minimum recommended character height** such that the display meets the 16-arcminute legibility threshold.

**Given:**
- Viewing distance: $D = 80 \text{ cm} = 800 \text{ mm}$
- Required minimum visual angle: $\theta' = 16 \text{ arcminutes}$

**Step 1 — Start from the arcminute formula.**

$$
\theta' = 3438 \times \frac{h}{D}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for character height $h$.**

$$
h = \frac{\theta' \times D}{3438}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
h = \frac{16 \times 800}{3438}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the numerator and denominator.**

$$
h = \frac{12800}{3438}
$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation.**

$$
h \approx 3.72 \text{ mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** The character must be **at least 3.72 mm tall** to be legibly read from 80 cm by a normal-sighted operator.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stroke Width from Character Height

**Given:** Character height $h = 5 \text{ mm}$, using a stroke-width-to-height ratio of $1:7$.

$$
SW = \frac{h}{7} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.71 \text{ mm}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Number of Characters Per Line

**Given:** Display width $W_{\text{disp}} = 200 \text{ mm}$, character width $cw = 4 \text{ mm}$, inter-character spacing $ICS = 1 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1 — Total horizontal space occupied by one character (including its trailing space):**

$$
\text{slot} = cw + ICS = 4 + 1 = 5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum characters fitting in the display width:**

$$
N = \left\lfloor \frac{W_{\text{disp}}}{\text{slot}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{200}{5} \right\rfloor = 40 \text{ characters}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Recommended Visual Angle vs. Task Type

| Task Type | Recommended Visual Angle | Rationale |
|-----------|--------------------------|-----------|
| Casual glance (e.g., decorative label) | 5′ – 10′ | Identification only, no reading |
| Standard reading (book, monitor) | 16′ – 22′ | Balance of speed and accuracy |
| High-stress / safety-critical (aviation, medical) | 24′ – 30′ | Compensates for stress, vibration, low light |
| Long-duration reading (manuals) | 18′ – 24′ | Reduces visual fatigue |
| Large-audience displays (scoreboards) | 30′+ | Multi-distance, multi-observer |

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4: Visual Angle Verification for a Real Display

**Given:** A smartphone screen displays a body-text character of height **3 mm** at a typical viewing distance of **35 cm = 350 mm**.

**Step 1 — Apply the small-angle formula in radians.**

$$
\theta_{\text{rad}} = \frac{h}{D} = \frac{3}{350} = 0.008571 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to degrees.**

$$
\theta^{\circ} = 0.008571 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.4911^{\circ}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert degrees to arcminutes (1° = 60′).**

$$
\theta' = 0.4911 \times 60 = 29.47 \text{ arcminutes}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare to recommended threshold.**

$$
29.47' > 22' \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Exceeds comfort threshold.}
$$

**Conclusion:** The character is **comfortably sized** for normal reading on a smartphone.

---

### 3.6 Derivation 5: Spacing Verification (Bouma's Law Approximation)

**Given:** Two adjacent digits of width 4 mm, separated by inter-character spacing of 0.5 mm. Stroke width is 0.7 mm.

**Step 1 — Required minimum spacing per Bouma's law: at least one stroke width.**

$$
ICS_{\min} = SW = 0.7 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Check against actual spacing.**

$$
0.5 \text{ mm} < 0.7 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Violation. Characters will crowd.}
$$

**Step 3 — Recommended fix.** Increase spacing to at least **0.7 mm** (ideally 1.0 mm for added margin).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Inputs Driving Display Character Geometry

This Mermaid block-diagram shows how **task constraints**, **human-vision parameters**, and **display hardware limits** interact to produce the final character size and spacing specification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Analysis] --> B{Stress Level}
    B -->|Low| C1[Visual Angle Target 18 arcmin]
    B -->|High| C2[Visual Angle Target 26 arcmin]
    A --> D[Viewing Distance D cm]
    C1 --> E[Compute Character Height h]
    C2 --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Derive Stroke Width SW h divided by 7]
    F --> G[Derive Inter Character Spacing ICS]
    G --> H[Derive Line Spacing LS]
    H --> I[Layout Check Characters per Line]
    I --> J{Fits within Display}
    J -->|Yes| K[Final Display Specification]
    J -->|No| L[Reduce N or Increase Display Width]
    L --> E
```

### 4.2 Geometry of Visual Angle (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Eye Node] -->|Viewing Distance D| B[Character of Height h]
    A -.->|Subtends Visual Angle theta| B
    B --> C[Stroke Width SW]
    B --> D[Inter Char Space ICS]
    B --> E[Line Spacing LS]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow for Sizing a Display Character

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Display Design] --> Q1[What is the task duration]
    Q1 -->|Short| T1[Use 16 arcmin threshold]
    Q1 -->|Long| T2[Use 22 arcmin threshold]
    Q1 -->|Critical safety| T3[Use 26 to 30 arcmin threshold]
    T1 --> Calc[h equals D times tan threshold]
    T2 --> Calc
    T3 --> Calc
    Calc --> Stroke[Set Stroke Width equals h divided by 7]
    Stroke --> Space[Set Inter Character Space equals 1 stroke width]
    Space --> Line[Set Line Spacing equals 1.2 times h]
    Line --> Done[Final Layout Validated]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All node identifiers in the Mermaid diagrams above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `C1`, `Calc`) to comply with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards. No reserved keywords are used as node IDs, and all labels contain only clean alphanumeric text.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "visual angle" as applied to display design. Why is it preferred over physical height alone for specifying character size?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Visual angle is the angle subtended by a character at the eye node of the observer, measured in arcminutes (′) or degrees (°).
- **Why preferred (2 marks):** Visual angle directly reflects what the eye *sees*, integrating both physical height and viewing distance. Two displays with identical character heights (e.g., 5 mm) can yield vastly different legibility if viewed at different distances. The 16–22 arcminute rule is **distance-independent**, making it a universal design metric.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
*State any three guidelines regarding the spacing of displayed characters.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Inter-character spacing (ICS)** should be at least **one stroke width** to prevent the *Bouma crowding effect*.
2. **Inter-word spacing** should be at least **one full character height** to provide clear word-boundary perception.
3. **Line spacing (leading)** should be **1.0× to 1.5× the character height** to avoid vertical crowding and enable smooth saccadic return sweeps.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** A radar display in an air-traffic-control console is viewed from a distance of **75 cm**. The character must subtend a visual angle of **22 arcminutes** for accurate reading. Calculate the required character height. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the importance of **stroke width** and **inter-character spacing** in display formatting. Quote standard ratios. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** State the formula relating visual angle to height and distance.

$$
h = \frac{\theta' \times D}{3438} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert viewing distance to consistent units.

$$
D = 75 \text{ cm} = 750 \text{ mm} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\theta' = 22$ and $D = 750$.

$$
h = \frac{22 \times 750}{3438} = \frac{16500}{3438} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate.

$$
h \approx 4.80 \text{ mm} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}
$$

**Step 5:** State conclusion.

> **The character height must be at least 4.80 mm** for a 22-arcminute legibility from 75 cm. **(1 mark)**

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

- **Stroke width (SW) (3 marks):** Stroke width defines the *thickness* of the lines forming the character. Too thin → characters blur and disappear at distance. Too thick → characters appear as blobs and lose identity. Standard ratio: **SW = h / 6 to h / 8**, with **h / 7** being the most common. For $h = 4.8$ mm, $SW = 4.8 / 7 \approx 0.69$ mm.

- **Inter-character spacing (ICS) (3 marks):** Controls horizontal crowding. If two characters are too close, the brain cannot segregate them, increasing identification error. Recommended: **ICS ≥ 1 stroke width** (≥ 1.0 mm for typical displays). For dense numerical displays (e.g., digital readouts), **slightly larger spacing** is preferred to disambiguate similar glyphs (e.g., 0/O, 1/l).

- **Conclusion (1 mark):** Together, SW and ICS govern the **legibility-verus-crowding trade-off**, and must be jointly optimized for the specific display task.

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches how **viewing distance, character height, and visual angle** are inter-related. A VDU screen viewed from **60 cm** shows characters of height **3.5 mm**. Determine the visual angle subtended in arcminutes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **Bouma's law of visual crowding** and explain how it dictates the **minimum inter-character and inter-word spacing** in displays. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

- **Relation (3 marks):** For a character of height $h$ viewed from distance $D$, the visual angle is

$$
\theta' = 3438 \times \frac{h}{D}
$$

  As $h$ increases (for fixed $D$) → $\theta'$ increases → character is more legible. As $D$ increases (for fixed $h$) → $\theta'$ decreases → character becomes harder to read.

- **Calculation (4 marks):** Given $h = 3.5$ mm, $D = 60$ cm $= 600$ mm.

$$
\theta' = 3438 \times \frac{3.5}{600} = 3438 \times 0.005833
$$

$$
\theta' \approx 20.05 \text{ arcminutes}
$$

  Since $20.05' > 16'$, the character meets the **minimum legibility threshold** but is **below the comfort threshold (22′)**. A modest increase to ~3.85 mm would meet the comfort criterion.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

- **Bouma's Law Definition (2 marks):** Hermann Bouma's research showed that identification accuracy of a target character falls sharply when neighbouring characters fall within a critical horizontal zone of about **0.5× the character width** from the target's edge. This is known as **visual crowding**.

- **Implication for Inter-Character Spacing (3 marks):** To prevent crowding, adjacent characters must be separated by **at least one stroke width** (and ideally 1.0–1.5 stroke widths for safety-critical displays). Violation leads to character-confusion errors (e.g., reading "8" as "B" or "0" as "O").

- **Implication for Inter-Word Spacing (2 marks):** Word boundaries must be perceptually distinct. The minimum inter-word gap should be **at least one full character height (≈ 1× CH)**, equivalent to roughly 6–8 stroke widths. This creates a clear *whitespace cue* that the visual system uses to segment text into words.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** — Mixing cm and mm in the visual-angle formula is the #1 cause of numerical errors. Always convert $D$ to the *same unit* as $h$.
> 2. **Using 3438 vs. 3437.75** — Both are accepted; show the formula first, then the substituted values.
> 3. **Confusing visual angle with viewing angle of a screen** — They are different concepts. The visual angle is for *characters*, not the screen as a whole.
> 4. **Ignoring stroke width and spacing** in long answers — A full 14-mark question explicitly expects discussion of *all three*: size, stroke width, and spacing. Skipping any of these forfeits at least 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** — Always state that the operator has 20/20 vision, normal lighting, and that the small-angle approximation is valid (true for $\theta < 5°$).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Visual angle** is the *true* ergonomic metric for character size — not raw height in mm.
- **Standard legibility threshold:** **16 arcminutes** (minimum), **22 arcminutes** (recommended), **26–30 arcminutes** (safety-critical).
- **Key formula:** $\theta' = 3438 \cdot (h / D)$.
- **Critical Print Size:** $CPS = 0.005 \cdot D$ — quick rule for minimum legible height in mm.
- **Stroke Width : Character Height = 1 : 6 to 1 : 8** (use 1 : 7 as a default).
- **Character Width : Height = 0.6 : 1 to 0.9 : 1** (digits can be narrower; letters wider).
- **Inter-Character Spacing ≥ 1 stroke width** — Bouma's crowding rule.
- **Inter-Word Spacing ≥ 1 character height.**
- **Line Spacing (leading) = 1.0× to 1.5× character height.**
- **High-stress or vibration environments** demand a **safety factor of 1.5×–2×** the normal threshold.
- **Optimal layout** balances legibility (bigger characters) against information density (smaller characters) — trade-off governed by task duration, operator distance, and ambient conditions.
- **Always state assumptions** (vision, lighting, angle range) in exam answers to earn full methodology marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study - Selection of Display for Cardiac Output Monitor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Case Study - Selection of Display for Cardiac Output Monitor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Cardiac Output Monitor?

A **Cardiac Output Monitor (COM)** is a critical-care medical device used to measure and display the volume of blood pumped by the heart per minute, expressed in **liters per minute (L/min)**. Normal adult cardiac output ranges between **4.0 to 8.0 L/min**, and deviations beyond this range can indicate life-threatening conditions such as cardiogenic shock, septic shock, or heart failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Mapping (Module 1 – Visual Displays)**
> This case study directly tests the student's ability to apply **Display Design Principles** from Human Factors Engineering to a real medical-device problem — a classic KTU Part B (14 marks) analytical question.

The **display** is the primary Human-Machine Interface (HMI) element of the monitor. It must:
- Convey numerical values (e.g., CO = 5.4 L/min)
- Show trend waveforms (e.g., arterial pressure pulse contour)
- Provide alarm status (e.g., ST-segment elevation, low CO)
- Indicate system mode and patient ID

## 1.2 What Does "Selection of Display" Mean in Human Factors?

In Human Factors Engineering, the **selection of a display** is the systematic process of choosing the most appropriate visual (or auditory) indicator — such as a dial, digital readout, bar graph, or cathode-ray tube (CRT)/liquid-crystal display (LCD) — based on:
1. The **type of information** to be conveyed (quantitative, qualitative, status, alarm).
2. The **operator's perceptual and cognitive characteristics** (visual acuity, color vision, reading distance).
3. The **environmental constraints** (ambient illumination, glare, vibration, noise).
4. The **task demands** (criticality, response time, frequency of use).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The Car Speedometer)**
> Imagine you are driving. Your speedometer must be:
> - **Readable at a glance** (you cannot take your eyes off the road for long).
> - **Accurate and unambiguous** (is it 60 or 80 km/h?).
> - **Robust to lighting changes** (sunny day vs. night tunnel).
> - **Color-coded for danger** (red zone for overspeeding).
>
> A cardiac output monitor's display is essentially a **medical speedometer** — except the "vehicle" is a patient's circulatory system, and a wrong reading can be fatal. The case study asks: *which type of "speedometer" — analog dial, 7-segment digital, or graphical LCD — is best for this critical job?*

## 1.3 Types of Displays Used in Medical Instrumentation

| Display Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Analog (Pointer on Scale)** | Continuous, easy to read trend | Older bedside monitors |
| **Digital (7-Segment LED/LCD)** | Precise numerical value | CO = 5.4 L/min |
| **CRT (Cathode Ray Tube)** | High refresh, multi-trace waveforms | Legacy ICU monitors |
| **LCD (Liquid Crystal Display)** | Flat, low-power, high resolution | Modern portable monitors |
| **OLED Display** | Self-emissive, wide viewing angle | Premium handheld COMs |
| **Auditory Display** | Tones/voice alarms | Backup or alarm channel |
| **Tactile/Braille** | For visually impaired operators | Rare; research only |

## 1.4 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Standard)

> **Display (Sanders & McCormick, Human Factors in Engineering and Design):**
> *"A display is a device that presents information to one or more human senses, in a form suitable for direct perception, interpretation, and subsequent action by a human operator."*

For the cardiac output monitor case study, the **visual display** is preferred over auditory because:
- Numerical CO values are read silently and repeatedly.
- Trends (rising/falling) are best perceived visually.
- Alarms use both visual (flashing) and auditory (tone) channels (redundant coding).
</br>

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Display Selection Process (KTU 5-Step Method)

The systematic selection of a display for any instrument, including a cardiac output monitor, follows the **Chapanis & Garner Five-Step Procedure**:

1. **Define the Operator's Task** — What decisions must the nurse/doctor make from the display?
2. **Identify the Information Required** — Numerical value, trend, alarm, waveform.
3. **Select Candidate Display Types** — Analog, digital, graphic, auditory.
4. **Evaluate Human Factors Criteria** — Readability, accuracy, speed, training cost.
5. **Validate with User Trials & Standards** — IEC 60601-1, FDA, ISO 9241.

## 2.2 Why is Display Selection Non-Trivial in a Cardiac Output Monitor?

A cardiac output monitor operates in a **high-stress, multi-device, brightly-lit environment** such as the ICU or operating room. The display must:
- Be visible from a distance of **1 to 2 meters** (across a bed).
- Maintain contrast under **500–1000 lux** ambient light.
- Be readable by operators with **20/40 visual acuity** (the legal driving standard, accounting for the fact that not all medical staff have 20/20 vision).
- Be **glare-free** and **flicker-free** (flicker causes eye fatigue and is invisible to most but may trigger seizures in photosensitive epilepsy).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Three Reads" Rule in Medical Display Design**
> A critical-care display should be readable in **three glances**:
> 1. **Glance 1 (≤0.5 s):** Overall status — is the patient safe?
> 2. **Glance 2 (≤1.5 s):** Numerical value — what is the CO?
> 3. **Glance 3 (≤3.0 s):** Trend/waveform — is the patient improving or deteriorating?

## 2.3 Display Selection Decision Matrix

| Selection Criterion | Analog Dial | 7-Segment Digital | Graphic LCD |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Quantitative Precision** | Low (~±5%) | Excellent (±0.1 L/min) | Excellent |
| **Trend Perception** | Excellent (continuous) | Poor (only numbers) | Excellent (waveform) |
| **Glance Readability** | Excellent | Excellent | Good |
| **Reading from 1.5 m** | Good (large pointer) | Good (large digits) | Variable |
| **Power Consumption** | Low | Very Low | Moderate |
| **Multi-Parameter Display** | Limited | Limited | Excellent |
| **Cost** | Very Low | Low | Moderate-High |
| **Reliability (MTBF)** | High | Very High | Moderate |

> **KTU Board Insight:** Examiners love a table like this in Part B (14-mark) answers. A student who draws this table and ticks the **hybrid recommendation** (numeric LCD + analog-style trend bar) scores full marks.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these formulas — they appear in 2-mark and 14-mark KTU questions with high frequency.**

**Formula 1: Visual Angle (Snellen's Law for Character Height)**

The minimum character height $h$ required for legibility at viewing distance $d$ is given by:

$$
h \;=\; d \cdot \tan(\theta)
