KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** **Q1.** Define the term **"90% accommodation range"** in the context of ergonomic design. Express it in terms of the population mean μ and standard deviation σ. State its numerical value when σ = 60 mm. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The 90% accommodation range is the dimensional span required to accommodate 90% of the intended user population — specifically from the **5th percentile (P5)** to the **95th percentile (P95)**. In terms of μ and σ: $$ R_{90} = P95 - P5 = (\mu + 1.645\sigma) - (\mu - 1.645\sigma) = 3.29 \cdot \sigma $$ For σ = 60 mm: $$ R_{90} = 3.29 \cdot 60 = 197.4\ mm \approx 197\ mm $$ > **Mark Allocation:** Definition (1 mark), Formula (1 mark), Numerical value (1 mark). --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** **Q2.** Distinguish between **"static anthropometry"** and **"dynamic anthropometry"**. Give one engineering example where each is applied. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Static Anthropometry | Dynamic Anthropometry | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Body dimensions measured with the subject in fixed, stationary postures | Body dimensions measured during movement or functional activity | | **Nature** | Time-invariant, geometric | Time-varying, kinematic | | **Example** | Doorway height designed for P95 stature | Leg room under a desk designed for knee-and-thigh clearance during leg movement | Static example: **Cockpit ceiling height** based on P95 sitting height (stationary). Dynamic example: **Car footwell depth** based on P95 leg extension during clutch operation (moving). > **Mark Allocation:** Definition of each (1 mark each), Example (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern, ESE Module 2 Choice Question]** ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **Q A.** A standing workstation is to be designed for an industrial assembly line. The **elbow height (standing)** of the worker population follows a normal distribution with **mean μ = 1050 mm** and **standard deviation σ = 50 mm**. **(a)** [7 marks] Determine: (i) The counter height to fit the **5th percentile** worker (with 25 mm clearance for clothing). (ii) The counter height to fit the **95th percentile** worker (with 25 mm clearance for clothing). (iii) The **required adjustability range** to accommodate 90% of the population. **(b)** [7 marks] If the workstation is to be **fixed (non-adjustable)**, determine the **single optimal counter height** that minimizes the maximum discomfort for the population. Justify the choice of percentile with a one-sentence engineering rationale. Also, calculate the **maximum dimensional error** any worker will experience, and comment on whether this error is acceptable for a 4-hour assembly shift. --- **MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A** **Part (a)(i):** 5th percentile counter height $$ H_5 = \mu - 1.645 \cdot \sigma = 1050 - 1.645 \cdot 50 $$ $$ H_5 = 1050 - 82.25 = 967.75\ mm $$ Adding 25 mm clearance for clothing: $$ H_{5,\text{design}} = 967.75 + 25 = 992.75\ mm \approx 993\ mm $$ **Part (a)(ii):** 95th percentile counter height $$ H_{95} = \mu + 1.645 \cdot \sigma = 1050 + 1.645 \cdot 50 $$ $$ H_{95} = 1050 + 82.25 = 1132.25\ mm $$ Adding 25 mm clearance for clothing: $$ H_{95,\text{design}} = 1132.25 + 25 = 1157.25\ mm \approx 1157\ mm $$ **Part (a)(iii):** Adjustability range $$ \Delta H = 3.29 \cdot \sigma = 3.29 \cdot 50 = 164.5\ mm $$ Therefore, the counter must be **adjustable over 165 mm** (from 993 mm to 1157 mm) to serve 90% of the population. --- **Part (b):** Single optimal fixed counter height For a non-adjustable design, the optimal fixed height is the **P50 (median)** value, because for a normal distribution the median equals the mean, and the mean minimizes the **sum of squared deviations** from the optimum (least-squares principle). $$ H_{fixed} = \mu = 1050\ mm $$ Adding 25 mm clothing clearance: $$ H_{fixed,\text{design}} = 1050 + 25 = 1075\ mm $$ **Maximum dimensional error:** The maximum error occurs at the 5th and 95th percentiles: $$ E_{max} = 1.645 \cdot \sigma + 25 = 82.25 + 25 = 107.25\ mm $$ Wait — re-evaluating. The clothing clearance is added uniformly, so it does not contribute to the error. The true error is: $$ E_{max} = 1.645 \cdot \sigma = 82.25\ mm \approx 82\ mm $$ **Comment on acceptability for 4-hour shift:** According to ergonomic standards (e.g., **NIOSH**, **ANSI Z365**), a postural deviation exceeding **25–30 mm** at the elbow is associated with measurable musculoskeletal strain for shifts exceeding **2 hours**. An 82 mm error would cause significant shoulder and lower-back discomfort over a 4-hour shift. **Therefore, the fixed-height design is NOT acceptable** for sustained assembly work; an adjustable workstation is required. > **Mark Allocation for Q A:** (a)(i) 2 marks, (a)(ii) 2 marks, (a)(iii) 3 marks; (b) height 2 marks, error 2 marks, comment 3 marks. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q B.** A machine enclosure is to be designed for a seated operator. The following anthropometric data are given for the target population (values in mm, all normal distributions): | Dimension | Mean (μ) | Std Dev (σ) | |---|---|---| | Sitting height (erect) | 900 | 35 | | Shoulder breadth | 460 | 25 | | Thigh clearance (above seat) | 145 | 12 | | Eye height (above seat) | 780 | 30 | **(a)** [7 marks] Calculate the **minimum internal height** of the enclosure to fit the 95th percentile operator wearing a 40 mm thick hard hat, with an additional 50 mm safety margin. Also, calculate the **minimum internal width** of the enclosure for shoulder passage with a 30 mm lateral clearance. **(b)** [7 marks] An emergency stop button is to be mounted on the side wall of the enclosure. Determine the **maximum mounting height** of this button so that the 5th percentile operator (seated) can reach it with a comfortable (normal) reach. Use the empirical formula: **normal reach (seated) ≈ 0.42 × stature**, and assume the 5th percentile stature is **1580 mm**. Comment on whether the button should also be visible from the seated position (use eye-height data). --- **MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B** **Part (a):** Minimum internal height of enclosure The controlling dimension is **sitting height (erect)** at P95. $$ SH_{95} = 900 + 1.645 \cdot 35 = 900 + 57.575 = 957.575\ mm $$ Adding hard-hat thickness (40 mm) and safety margin (50 mm): $$ H_{min} = 957.575 + 40 + 50 = 1047.575\ mm \approx 1048\ mm $$ **Minimum internal width of enclosure:** The controlling dimension is **shoulder breadth** at P95. $$ SB_{95} = 460 + 1.645 \cdot 25 = 460 + 41.125 = 501.125\ mm $$ Adding 30 mm lateral clearance on each side (60 mm total): $$ W_{min} = 501.125 + 60 = 561.125\ mm \approx 561\ mm $$ > [!NOTE] > In practice, **a 30 mm lateral clearance per side** means 30 mm × 2 = 60 mm added to the body dimension. Some textbooks specify total clearance as a single 30 mm value — students should clarify in their answer which convention is being used. The **more conservative (and KTU-preferred) convention is 30 mm per side**. --- **Part (b):** Maximum mounting height of emergency stop button Normal reach (5th percentile, seated): $$ R_{normal,5} = 0.42 \cdot H_{stature,5} = 0.42 \cdot 1580 $$ $$ R_{normal,5} = 663.6\ mm \approx 664\ mm $$ The button can be mounted at a height above the seat pan equal to the **eye height (P5) + normal reach**, so the operator can both see and reach it. **Eye height (5th percentile) above seat:** $$ EH_5 = 780 - 1.645 \cdot 30 = 780 - 49.35 = 730.65\ mm \approx 731\ mm $$ **Maximum mounting height (above seat pan) for reachability:** $$ H_{button,max} = EH_5 + R_{normal,5} = 731 + 664 = 1395\ mm $$ Alternatively, if we interpret "above seat" loosely as the reach envelope's upper bound: $$ H_{button,max} = R_{normal,5} = 664\ mm $$ (above the shoulder pivot of the 5th percentile seated operator) **Visibility comment:** The 5th percentile seated operator's eye line is at **731 mm above the seat**. The enclosure ceiling is at **1048 mm**, giving a vertical viewing window of **~317 mm**. The button must be placed within the **normal visual cone** (approximately **30° below horizontal eye line**), which corresponds to a button placement **no more than ~400 mm below eye level** for comfortable viewing without head tilt. Therefore, the button should be mounted at a height between **~331 mm and ~1395 mm above the seat pan**, and within the operator's normal visual cone (i.e., **at or slightly below eye level**). A recommended mounting height is **700–800 mm above the seat pan** — within reach of the 5th percentile operator and within the normal line of sight. > **Mark Allocation for Q B:** (a) height 4 marks, width 3 marks; (b) reach 3 marks, visibility 2 marks, recommendation 2 marks. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistakes — Where Marks Are Lost:** > > 1. **Forgetting to add clothing/PPE clearance** (25–50 mm). A clearance design that is dimensionally perfect on paper but ignores shoes, helmets, or jackets is **worth zero in a KTU exam**. Always state: *"A 25 mm clearance is added for clothing/shoe allowance."* > > 2. **Confusing clearance percentile with reach percentile.** Students frequently write P95 for a reach dimension. **Rule of thumb:** *If the body part must FIT, use P95. If the body part must REACH, use P5.* > > 3. **Using P50 for adjustable designs.** P50 is acceptable only for **fixed** designs with documented justification. Adjustable designs **must** span P5 to P95. > > 4. **Forgetting units.** A formula like "1050 − 1.645 × 50 = 967.75" without the **mm** unit loses 0.5 mark per occurrence. > > 5. **Skipping the Z-score justification.** KTU examiners award marks for stating *why* Z = 1.645 (it is the 90% confidence Z-value, not just a number). > > 6. **Omitting the normal-distribution assumption.** The percentile formulas are valid only when the underlying data is approximately normal. For skewed data (e.g., strength), the **median** is preferred over the **mean** — state this explicitly. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Space & Physical Constraints** - **Definition:** Space and physical constraints are dimensional rules (clearance, reach, volume, posture) derived from anthropometric data to ensure human-system fit. - **Five Pillars:** Clearance, Reach, Posture, Adjustability, Safety. - **Core Percentile Rules:** - 5th Percentile: $P5 = \mu - 1.645 \cdot \sigma$ — used for **reaches** and **lower-bound dimensions**. - 95th Percentile: $P95 = \mu + 1.645 \cdot \sigma$ — used for **clearances** and **upper-bound dimensions**. - 50th Percentile: $P50 = \mu$ — used for **fixed non-adjustable** dimensions. - **The 90% Accommodation Range:** $R_{90} = P95 - P5 = 3.29 \cdot \sigma$ — defines the **minimum adjustability** for a 90% inclusive design. - **Clearance Additives (always add to the bare dimension):** - Static posture: **+25 mm** - Dynamic posture: **+50 to 75 mm** - With PPE: **+50 mm** - **Standard Reach Formulas (seated, 5th percentile):** - Functional reach: $\approx 0.40$ to $0.42$ × stature. - Maximum reach: $\approx 0.60$ × stature. - Vertical reach above floor: shoulder height + arm length. - **Safety Spacing Rules (high-yield for KTU):** - Two-hand control separation: $\geq 550$ mm. - Reach-over guard distance: $\geq 850$ mm (ISO 13857). - Light curtain finger resolution: $14$ mm. - **Selection Logic — Clearance vs Reach:** - **Clearance** (must contain the body) → P95. - **Reach** (body must access) → P5. - **Critical safety** → P1 to P99 (99% accommodation). - **Fixed public infrastructure** → P50 with documented exclusion of tails. - **Always state in your KTU answer:** 1. The normal distribution assumption. 2. The Z-score used and its justification (e.g., 1.645 for 90% CI). 3. The clothing/PPE clearance added (with a specific value). 4. The units (mm) in every numerical result. 5. A one-sentence rationale for the chosen percentile. - **Reference Standards (cite where relevant):** - **ISO 7250** — Basic human anthropometric measurements. - **ISO 13857** — Safety distances to prevent reach to hazards. - **EN 547 / EN 61496** — Safety of machinery (distances, ESPE). - **ANSI/HFES 100** — Computer workstation standards. - **MIL-STD-1472** — Military human factors design criteria. > **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — General and Specific Design Guidelines for Space and Physical Constraints.**

topics.title: Lighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Lighting # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Lighting** in the context of Human Factors Engineering is the controlled application of visible radiant energy (wavelength range **380 nm to 780 nm**) to a work environment in order to optimise **visual performance, visual comfort, safety, productivity, and human well-being**. It is treated as a primary *environmental ergonomic variable* that directly influences the physiological and psychological state of the operator. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Examination Ready):** > "Lighting is the science of providing adequate quantity and quality of illumination on a task surface so that the human visual system can perform with maximum efficiency, minimum fatigue, acceptable accuracy, and without compromising occupational health and safety." The human eye perceives light through **photopic vision** (cone cells, well-lit conditions) and **scotopic vision** (rod cells, dim conditions). Lighting design must satisfy both modes depending on task luminance levels. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spotlight on a Stage" Intuition Imagine a singer performing on a dim stage. If the spotlight (lighting) is **too dim**, the audience cannot read the singer's expressions and loses interest (poor visual performance → errors, fatigue). If the spotlight is **too bright**, the singer is blinded and loses confidence (glare, discomfort). If the light shines **sideways**, the singer's face is in shadow (poor contrast → eye strain). Lighting design is the **engineer's job of being that perfect spotlight operator** — giving the *right amount* of light, from the *right direction*, of the *right colour*, on the *right task*, while keeping the audience (worker's eyes) completely comfortable. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Memorise for KTU Boards):** > - **Luminous Efficacy of Monochromatic Radiation at 555 nm = 683 lm/W** (this is the maximum theoretical conversion of electrical power to visible light) > - Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s > - Photopic peak sensitivity: $\lambda = 555$ nm (green-yellow region) > - Scotopic peak sensitivity: $\lambda = 507$ nm (blue-green region) > - **1 foot-candle (fc) = 10.76 lux (lx)** (common conversion asked in KTU) ## 1.3 The Five Photometric Quantities (Foundation Block) | Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Meaning | |----------|--------|------|---------| | Luminous Flux | $\Phi$ | **lumen (lm)** | Total light energy emitted by a source per second, weighted by eye sensitivity | | Luminous Intensity | $I$ | **candela (cd)** | Luminous flux per unit solid angle in a given direction | | Illuminance | $E$ | **lux (lx) = lm/m²** | Luminous flux incident per unit area on a surface | | Luminance | $L$ | **cd/m² (nit)** | Light emitted/reflected per unit apparent area (perceived brightness) | | Luminous Efficacy | $\eta$ | **lm/W** | Light output per unit electrical power input | > [!TIP] > **Quick Memory Hook (Mnemonic):** *"**F**lux **I**lluminates **E**verything **L**ightly"* > $\Phi \rightarrow I \rightarrow E \rightarrow L$ > > Going down the chain: each new quantity = previous one **divided by a geometric quantity** (solid angle, distance², area). ## 1.4 Visual Performance Curve — The Ergonomic Foundation The relationship between **illuminance** (lux) and **visual performance** is **not linear** but follows an S-shaped (sigmoidal) curve: - At very low illuminance (< 50 lx): performance is poor, error rate is high - The "**inflection zone**" (50 – 500 lx) is where performance rises steeply — this is where most KTU design problems live - Beyond ~1000 lx: diminishing returns — adding more light gives marginal improvement but wastes energy - **KTU standard for precision tasks: 750 – 1500 lx** (depends on task category per IS 3646 / IES standards) > [!WARNING] > **Common Board Mistake:** Students often write "more light = better visibility" linearly. The KTU examiner will deduct marks because lighting design is governed by the **law of diminishing returns** — *optimal* is not *maximum*. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Photometric Quantities — Detailed Engineering Definitions ### 2.1.1 Luminous Flux ($\Phi$) - Definition: The *rate of flow* of light energy, evaluated according to the eye's wavelength sensitivity (the **photopic luminosity function** $V(\lambda)$). - Mathematical form: $$\Phi = 683 \int_{380}^{780} V(\lambda) \cdot P(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$ - Where $P(\lambda)$ is the spectral radiant power (W/nm) and $V(\lambda)$ is the dimensionless luminosity function (peaks at 1.0 at 555 nm). - **Why it matters:** Lamp manufacturers rate bulbs in lumens, not watts. A 100 W incandescent gives ~1500 lm; an LED gives the same 1500 lm at only ~15 W. ### 2.1.2 Luminous Intensity ($I$) - Definition: Luminous flux emitted per unit **solid angle** in a specified direction. - Solid angle $\omega$ is measured in **steradians (sr)**, where a full sphere = $4\pi$ sr. $$I = \frac{d\Phi}{d\omega} \quad \text{[unit: cd = lm/sr]}$$ - **Practical use:** Used to characterise the directional output of spotlights, LEDs, and reflectors. A point source radiating uniformly in all directions: $I = \Phi / (4\pi)$. ### 2.1.3 Illuminance ($E$) - Definition: Luminous flux **incident on** a surface per unit area. $$E = \frac{d\Phi}{dA} \quad \text{[unit: lx = lm/m}^2\text{]}$$ - Illuminance is what an **illuminance meter (lux meter)** actually measures — placing a sensor on the work surface. - This is the *primary* quantity used in all KTU lighting calculations. ### 2.1.4 Luminance ($L$) — The "Perceived Brightness" - Definition: Luminous intensity per unit **projected** (apparent) area of a surface in a given direction. $$L = \frac{I}{A \cos \theta} \quad \text{[unit: cd/m}^2\text{]}$$ - **Why $\cos\theta$?** A surface tilted away from the viewer appears *larger* in apparent area but emits the *same* light, so brightness per unit area decreases. - **Critical KTU point:** Luminance is what the eye actually perceives. Two surfaces can have identical illuminance but vastly different luminance (e.g., black paper vs white paper under the same lamp). ### 2.1.5 Luminous Efficacy ($\eta$) - Definition: Ratio of luminous flux emitted to electrical power consumed. $$\eta = \frac{\Phi}{P_{\text{electric}}} \quad \text{[unit: lm/W]}$$ - Incandescent bulb: 10–15 lm/W - Fluorescent tube: 50–80 lm/W - LED (modern): 100–200 lm/W - Theoretical max at 555 nm: **683 lm/W** ## 2.2 The Two Laws of Illumination (Most Repeated KTU Topic) ### Law 1 — Inverse Square Law > "The illuminance at a point on a surface is **inversely proportional to the square of the distance** from a point source, provided the surface is held normal to the direction of incidence." $$E = \frac{I}{d^2}$$ ### Law 2 — Cosine Law (Lambert's Law of Incidence) > "The illuminance on a surface is **proportional to the cosine of the angle** between the direction of incident light and the surface normal." $$E = \frac{I \cos \theta}{d^2}$$ This combines into the **fundamental law of illumination** for a point source: $$E = \frac{I}{d^2} \cos \theta$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** > - **Inverse Square Law** governs the *spacing* of lamps in a room. Doubling the mounting height reduces illuminance to 1/4 — a critical design decision. > - **Cosine Law** governs *glare* and the *angle of incidence* from windows — direct sunlight at noon (small $\theta$, large $\cos\theta$) gives high illuminance but harsh glare. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use | |---|---------|---------|-------------| | 1 | $I = \Phi / \omega$ | Intensity from flux & solid angle | Point source radiation | | 2 | $E = \Phi / A$ | Illuminance from total flux | Average illuminance in a room | | 3 | $E = I / d^2$ | Inverse square law | Point source, normal incidence | | 4 | $E = (I \cos \theta) / d^2$ | Cosine law + inverse square | Point source, oblique incidence | | 5 | $L = I / (A \cos \theta)$ | Luminance definition | Brightness calculations | | 6 | $E = E_n \cos \theta$ | Illuminance on horizontal vs vertical | Sunlight on wall vs floor | | 7 | $N = (E \cdot A) / (\Phi \cdot \eta \cdot UF \cdot MF)$ | Lumen method (number of lamps) | Indoor design | | 8 | $E_v / E_h = \tan \alpha$ | Vertical/horizontal illuminance ratio | Daylight design | | 9 | $UF = \Phi_{\text{utilised on work plane}}}{\Phi_{\text{lamp total}}}$ | Utilisation factor (0.3–0.8) | Indoor lumen method | | 10 | $MF = 1 / (LLF) = 1/(\text{light loss factor})$ | Maintenance factor (typically 0.7–0.8) | Account for lamp ageing | | 11 | $\text{Contrast} = (L_b - L_o)/L_b$ | Weber's contrast ratio | Visibility analysis | | 12 | $E_{avg} = N \cdot \Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF / A$ | Avg. illuminance in a room | Verification after lamp selection | > [!TIP] > **Boundary Conditions to Remember:** > - Point source assumption: valid when source dimension $< d/10$ > - Cosine law valid: surface is a *diffuse* (Lambertian) reflector > - $E$ and $I$ relate through a *direction*; $L$ and $E$ relate through *surface reflectance* $\rho$: $E = \pi L / \rho$ for a diffuse surface ## 2.4 Lighting Design Process (KTU Standard Steps) The KTU syllabus expects the student to know the **systematic design methodology**: 1. **Identify tasks** and their illuminance requirements (per IS 3646 / IES table) 2. **Determine room dimensions** (L × W × H) and work-plane height 3. **Select luminaire type** and lamp (incandescent / fluorescent / LED) 4. **Choose mounting height** and spacing 5. **Apply Lumen Method** to calculate number of lamps: $$N = \frac{E \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF}$$ 6. **Apply Point-by-Point Method** to verify illuminance at critical points 7. **Check glare, shadows, colour rendering, and uniformity** 8. **Iterate** until design satisfies all ergonomic and energy criteria # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Inverse Square Law (Geometric Proof) **Setup:** Consider an isotropic point source $S$ at the centre of two concentric spherical surfaces of radii $d_1$ and $d_2$. The source emits total luminous flux $\Phi$ uniformly in all directions ($4\pi$ sr). **Step 1 — Luminous intensity of source:** The intensity is the same in every direction: $$I = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi}$$ **Step 2 — Flux intercepted by first sphere of area $4\pi d_1^2$:** By conservation, all flux $\Phi$ falls on the sphere: $$\Phi_1 = \Phi = I \cdot 4\pi d_1^2 \cdot (\text{unit factor})$$ Wait — let us be rigorous. The flux crossing any closed sphere centred on the source equals the total flux emitted (Gauss's law analogue for light): $$\Phi = I \cdot \omega_{\text{full sphere}} = I \cdot 4\pi$$ **Step 3 — Illuminance on a surface of radius $d$ held normal to the rays:** The flux through a small patch of area $dA$ at distance $d$ (perpendicular incidence, so $\theta = 0$, $\cos\theta = 1$): $$E = \frac{\Phi}{A} = \frac{I \cdot 4\pi}{4\pi d^2} = \frac{I}{d^2}$$ **Step 4 — Verify scaling:** $$E \propto \frac{1}{d^2}$$ If $d_2 = 2 d_1$, then $E_2 = E_1 / 4$. Doubling distance → quarter the illuminance. **This is the KTU-board favourite sanity check.** ## 3.2 Derivation of the Cosine Law (Oblique Incidence) **Setup:** A point source $S$ at height $h$ above a horizontal work surface. Light strikes a point $P$ on the surface at an angle $\theta$ to the *normal* of that surface. **Step 1 — Geometry:** The slant distance from $S$ to $P$ is $d$, where: $$d = \frac{h}{\cos \theta}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Inverse Square Law along the ray:** $$E_{\text{along ray}} = \frac{I}{d^2} = \frac{I \cos^2 \theta}{h^2}$$ **Step 3 — Account for surface tilt (the cosine effect):** A horizontal patch of area $dA_h$ projects to a smaller area $dA_n = dA_h \cos\theta$ when viewed along the ray. The same flux spreads over a *larger* projected area on the horizontal surface, so illuminance on the horizontal surface is *reduced* by $\cos\theta$: $$E_h = \frac{I \cos^2 \theta}{h^2} = \frac{I \cos \theta}{d^2}$$ This is the **combined Inverse Square + Cosine Law** result, which is the *single most-tested formula* in KTU lighting modules. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Difference Engineers Must Note:** > - $E_n$ (normal/ray-direction illuminance) $= I/d^2$ > - $E_h$ (horizontal surface illuminance) $= (I \cos^2 \theta)/h^2 = (I \cos \theta)/d^2$ > - $E_v$ (vertical surface illuminance) $= (I \sin \theta \cos \theta)/h^2 = (I/h^2) \cdot \sin\theta\cos\theta$ > - These three are **different** — confusion here is the most common KTU error. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Lumen Method (Number of Lamps Formula) **Engineering goal:** Given a room of area $A$ requiring average illuminance $E$, find the number of lamps $N$ of flux $\Phi$ each. **Step 1 — Total flux required on the work plane:** $$\Phi_{\text{required}} = E \cdot A$$ **Step 2 — Account for Utilisation Factor (UF):** Only a fraction $UF$ of the lamp flux reaches the work plane (rest is lost to walls, ceiling, fixtures). $UF$ depends on room index, luminaire type, and reflectance. Typical range: **0.3 to 0.8**. $$\Phi_{\text{lamp total}} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{required}}}{UF} = \frac{E \cdot A}{UF}$$ **Step 3 — Account for Maintenance Factor (MF):** As the lamp ages and dust accumulates, output drops. $MF < 1$ (typically 0.7–0.8). Inverse of *light loss factor*. $$\Phi_{\text{lamp installed}} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{lamp total}}}{MF} = \frac{E \cdot A}{UF \cdot MF}$$ **Step 4 — Number of lamps:** Each lamp provides $\Phi$ lumens, so: $$N = \frac{E \cdot A}{UF \cdot MF \cdot \Phi}$$ **Final Lumen Method Equation (KTU examination-ready):** $$\boxed{N = \frac{E \cdot A}{UF \cdot MF \cdot \Phi_{\text{lamp}}}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Most Common KTU Board Error:** Forgetting to include $MF$ or $UF$, leading to a lamp count that is **too small**, producing an under-lit installation. Always include both factors with explicit reasoning. ## 3.4 Worked Example (Full Lumen Method Calculation) > **Problem:** Design a lighting installation for a workshop of dimensions 20 m × 10 m × 5 m (L × W × H). Work-plane is 1 m above floor. Required illuminance: 200 lux. Lamps: 1500-lumen fluorescent tubes, $UF = 0.5$, $MF = 0.8$. **Step 1 — Room area:** $$A = L \times W = 20 \times 10 = 200 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Number of lamps required:** $$N = \frac{E \cdot A}{UF \cdot MF \cdot \Phi} = \frac{200 \times 200}{0.5 \times 0.8 \times 1500}$$ $$N = \frac{40{,}000}{600} = 66.67 \rightarrow 67 \text{ lamps (round up)}$$ **Step 3 — Verification (average illuminance check):** $$E_{avg} = \frac{N \cdot \Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF}{A} = \frac{67 \times 1500 \times 0.5 \times 0.8}{200}$$ $$E_{avg} = \frac{40{,}200}{200} = 201 \text{ lux } \approx 200 \text{ lux} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 4 — Spacing check:** Assume fixtures arranged in rows. With 67 lamps and 1 lamp per fixture, 67 fixtures. A typical industrial spacing-to-mounting-height ratio is $S/H_m \leq 1.5$. With $H_m = 5 - 1 = 4$ m, max spacing = 6 m. In a 20×10 room, a 4×3 grid (12 fixtures) with multiple tubes per fixture, or a 6×6 grid of fixtures (36) with 2 tubes each = 72 lamps — close to required. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns | Pattern Type | Key Formulae Used | Critical Step | |--------------|-------------------|---------------| | Find illuminance at a point | $E = (I \cos\theta)/d^2$ | Compute $d$ and $\theta$ from geometry | | Find distance for required $E$ | $d = \sqrt{I/E}$ | Inverse square law direct solve | | Find required lamp count | $N = EA/(UF \cdot MF \cdot \Phi)$ | Always include both factors | | Verify design | $E_{avg} = N\Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF / A$ | Should match specification | | Find luminance from illuminance | $L = \rho E / \pi$ | For perfectly diffuse surface | | Compare vertical vs horizontal | $E_v/E_h = \tan\alpha$ | Useful in daylighting problems | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Photometric Quantity Relationship Map ```mermaid flowchart TD Source["Luminous Source
Rated in Lumens Phi"] -->|emits| Flux["Luminous Flux Phi
Unit lm"] Flux -->|divided by solid angle omega| Intensity["Luminous Intensity I
Unit candela cd"] Flux -->|incident on area A| Illum["Illuminance E
Unit lux lx"] Intensity -->|through distance d| Illum Illum -->|times reflectance rho| Lumin["Luminance L
Unit cd per sq m"] Illum -->|inverse square law| Law1["E equals I over d squared"] Illum -->|cosine law| Law2["E equals I cos theta over d squared"] Law1 --> Illum Law2 --> Illum Source -->|power input| Efficacy["Luminous Efficacy eta
Unit lm per W"] Flux --> Efficacy Illum -->|UF and MF factors| Design["Lumen Method
N equals E times A over UF times MF times Phi"] Design --> Verif["Average Illuminance Check
E avg equals N Phi UF MF over A"] Verif --> Final["Optimised Lighting Layout"] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Lighting Design Methodology Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Identify Task and Required Illuminance
Per IS 3646 or IES Table"] --> Room["Measure Room Geometry
L W H and Work Plane Height"] Room --> Choice["Select Lamp Type and Luminaire
Incandescent Fluorescent LED"] Choice --> Mount["Decide Mounting Height and Spacing
S to H ratio less than 1.5"] Mount --> Calc["Apply Lumen Method
N equals E A over UF MF Phi"] Calc --> Decide{Count N acceptable?} Decide -->|No| Choice Decide -->|Yes| Point["Apply Point by Point Method
E p equals sum of I cos theta over d squared"] Point --> Quality["Check Quality Factors
Glare Contrast Uniformity CRI"] Quality --> Approve{All criteria met?} Approve -->|No| Mount Approve -->|Yes| Final["Final Layout and Energy Report"] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Visual Performance vs Illuminance Curve (KTU Board Sketch Adaptation) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Region A
Below 50 lux
Scotopic dominant
High error rate"] -->|Performance rising| B["Region B
50 to 500 lux
Mixed photopic scotopic
Steep performance gain"] B -->|Diminishing return starts| C["Region C
500 to 1500 lux
Photopic dominant
Recommended design zone"] C -->|Saturation| D["Region D
Above 1500 lux
Diminishing returns
Glare and energy waste"] note1["KTU Exam Tip
Design tasks land in Region C"] -.-> C ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Inverse Square + Cosine Law Geometry ```mermaid flowchart TD Lamp["Point Source S
Intensity I candela"] -->|distance d| PointP["Point P on Surface"] PointP --> Norm["Normal to surface
at point P"] Norm --> Angle["Angle theta between
incident ray and normal"] Angle -->|formula| Eformula["Illuminance at P
E equals I cos theta over d squared"] Eformula -->|horizontal surface| Eh["E horizontal equals I cos squared theta over h squared"] Eformula -->|normal surface| En["E normal equals I over d squared"] Eformula -->|vertical surface| Ev["E vertical equals I sin theta cos theta over h squared"] ``` ## 4.5 Lighting Quality Factors (Textual Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q["Lighting Quality Factors"] --> G["Glare Control
UGR less than 19 for offices"] Q --> C["Contrast
Weber ratio 0.4 to 0.6"] Q --> U["Uniformity
E min over E avg greater than 0.7"] Q --> CRI["Colour Rendering Index
CRI greater than 80 indoor"] Q --> S["Shadow Control
Diffuse plus directional mix"] Q --> F["Flicker
Frequency above 100 Hz"] Q --> Col["Colour Temperature
3000 K warm to 6500 K cool"] ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define the following photometric quantities and state their SI units:** (a) Luminous intensity (b) Luminance (c) Luminous efficacy **Model Answer:** (a) **Luminous Intensity ($I$):** The luminous flux emitted by a source per unit solid angle in a given direction. Mathematically $I = d\Phi / d\omega$. **Unit: candela (cd) = lm/sr.** (b) **Luminance ($L$):** The luminous intensity per unit projected area of a surface in a specified direction, representing perceived brightness. Mathematically $L = I / (A \cos\theta)$. **Unit: candela per square metre (cd/m²) or nit.** (c) **Luminous Efficacy ($\eta$):** The ratio of luminous flux emitted by a lamp to the electrical power consumed. It measures how efficiently electrical energy is converted to visible light. **Unit: lumens per watt (lm/W).** > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip:** Examiner expects the *formula + unit* for full 3 marks. Skip either and lose 1 mark. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **State and explain the Inverse Square Law of illumination. Mention its limitations.** **Model Answer:** **Statement:** *"The illuminance on a surface held normal to the rays from a point source is inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the source."* $$E \propto \frac{1}{d^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E = \frac{I}{d^2}$$ **Explanation:** Light from a point source spreads spherically. As the radius of the sphere doubles, the surface area quadruples ($A = 4\pi d^2$), so the flux per unit area becomes one-fourth. **Limitations:** 1. Valid only for a **point source** (source dimension $< d/10$). 2. Valid only when the receiving surface is **normal** to the incident rays. 3. Assumes a **clear medium** (no absorption, scattering, or reflection). 4. Not applicable for **line sources** (e.g., fluorescent tube) or **area sources** (e.g., luminous panel) where the geometry is different. ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]** **(a)** Derive the fundamental law of illumination combining the inverse square law and cosine law. State clearly the meaning of every symbol. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A workshop measures 30 m × 15 m with a mounting height of 6 m above the work plane. The required illuminance is 300 lux. Using the lumen method, calculate the number of 40 W fluorescent tubes (each giving 2400 lumens) required. Take $UF = 0.6$ and $MF = 0.75$. Verify your design by computing the average illuminance. **[7 Marks]** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of the Combined Law:** Let a point source $S$ have luminous intensity $I$ candela. Light falls on point $P$ on a surface. Let $d$ = slant distance from $S$ to $P$, and $\theta$ = angle between the incident ray and the *normal* to the surface at $P$. **Step 1:** Apply Inverse Square Law along the ray. Illuminance on a small area $dA_n$ held *normal* to the ray at distance $d$: $$E_n = \frac{I}{d^2}$$ **Step 2:** The horizontal (or tilted) surface has area $dA_h = dA_n / \cos\theta$ (the surface is larger when projected horizontally than along the normal). The same flux $\Phi = E_n \cdot dA_n$ now spreads over $dA_h$: $$E_h = \frac{\Phi}{dA_h} = \frac{E_n \cdot dA_n}{dA_n / \cos\theta} = E_n \cos\theta = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$$ **Step 3 — Combined Fundamental Law of Illumination:** $$\boxed{E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}}$$ Where: - $E$ = illuminance (lux) - $I$ = luminous intensity (candela) - $\theta$ = angle of incidence w.r.t. normal - $d$ = distance from source to point (m) **Special case:** $\theta = 0$ (normal incidence) → $E = I/d^2$ (pure inverse square). --- **(b) Lumen Method Calculation:** **Step 1 — Area of room:** $$A = 30 \times 15 = 450 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Number of lamps:** $$N = \frac{E \cdot A}{UF \cdot MF \cdot \Phi} = \frac{300 \times 450}{0.6 \times 0.75 \times 2400}$$ **Numerator:** $300 \times 450 = 135{,}000$ **Denominator:** $0.6 \times 0.75 \times 2400 = 1080$ $$N = \frac{135{,}000}{1080} = 125 \text{ lamps}$$ **Step 3 — Verification (average illuminance):** $$E_{avg} = \frac{N \cdot \Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF}{A} = \frac{125 \times 2400 \times 0.6 \times 0.75}{450}$$ $$E_{avg} = \frac{135{,}000}{450} = 300 \text{ lux} \quad \checkmark$$ **Design summary:** 125 fluorescent tubes of 40 W each, total electrical load = 5000 W (5 kW), average illuminance 300 lux achieved. The design satisfies the requirement. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting $UF$ or $MF$** — gives 125/0.6 = 208 or 125/0.75 = 167 lamps. Wrong by ~67%. **Marks deducted: 2–3.** > 2. **Rounding down** instead of up (using 124 instead of 125) — strictly speaking, fractional lamps must be rounded up; examiners accept either if explicitly stated. > 3. **Not verifying** the design by computing $E_{avg}$ — the *verification step* carries 2 marks; skipping it loses marks even if the lamp count is correct. > 4. **Unit mistakes:** Writing $E = 300$ without "lux" loses 0.5 mark; writing $\Phi$ in watts loses 1 mark. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Alternative]** **(a)** Explain the difference between **luminance** and **illuminance**. Why does the human eye respond to luminance, not illuminance, even though we measure illuminance for design purposes? **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A 200-candela lamp is suspended 4 m above a workbench. Calculate the illuminance: (i) directly below the lamp (ii) at a point 3 m horizontally from directly below the lamp (iii) at a vertical surface placed at the point in (ii) facing the lamp. **[7 Marks]** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Luminance vs Illuminance:** | Aspect | Illuminance ($E$) | Luminance ($L$) | |--------|-------------------|-----------------| | Definition | Flux incident on a surface per unit area | Flux emitted/reflected per unit *projected* area | | Symbol/Unit | lux (lm/m²) | cd/m² (nit) | | What it measures | Quantity of light *falling* on a surface | Quantity of light *leaving* a surface | | Measured by | Lux meter (placed on surface) | Cannot be measured directly; derived from intensity and geometry | | Eye perception | NOT directly perceived | **Directly perceived** as "brightness" | **Why the eye responds to luminance:** The retina receives light that *leaves* the object in the direction of the eye. This is luminance, not illuminance. Two surfaces under identical illuminance can have very different luminances depending on their reflectance (a black book vs a white paper under the same lamp). Hence, design uses illuminance as a *proxy* because it is easier to calculate and measure, but the final visual experience is determined by luminance. --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** Given: $I = 200$ cd, $h = 4$ m (mounting height). **(i) Directly below the lamp** ($\theta = 0$, $d = h = 4$ m): $$E_1 = \frac{I \cos 0}{d^2} = \frac{200 \times 1}{4^2} = \frac{200}{16} = 12.5 \text{ lux}$$ **(ii) At 3 m horizontally:** Slant distance: $$d = \sqrt{h^2 + x^2} = \sqrt{4^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \text{ m}$$ Angle $\theta$: $$\cos\theta = \frac{h}{d} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = 36.87°$$ Horizontal surface illuminance: $$E_2 = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^2} = \frac{200 \times 0.8}{25} = \frac{160}{25} = 6.4 \text{ lux}$$ **(iii) At a vertical surface at the same point:** A vertical surface has its normal horizontal, so the angle of incidence of the ray (measured from the *vertical* normal) is the *complement* of $\theta$ from the previous step. Alternatively: $$E_v = \frac{I \sin\theta \cos\theta}{h^2}$$ We have $\cos\theta = 0.8$ and $\sin\theta = 3/5 = 0.6$: $$E_3 = \frac{200 \times 0.6 \times 0.8}{16} = \frac{96}{16} = 6.0 \text{ lux}$$ **Summary of results:** - (i) 12.5 lux - (ii) 6.4 lux - (iii) 6.0 lux > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Question B:** > 1. **For part (iii):** Many students incorrectly use $E_3 = E_2$ (i.e., treat the vertical surface as horizontal). The correct geometry requires $\sin\theta \cos\theta$ or equivalently $E_v = E_h \tan\theta$ — **full 2 marks lost** if confused. > 2. **For part (i):** Students sometimes use $d = \sqrt{h^2 + x^2}$ instead of $d = h$ when $x = 0$. Always verify geometry first. > 3. **Angle convention:** Some texts use angle from horizontal, some from normal. State your convention explicitly. The KTU board usually uses **angle from normal**. ## 5.3 Additional High-Yield 3-Mark Conceptual Questions **Q3.** Distinguish between **photopic** and **scotopic** vision. Which type dominates in a normally lit office? **[3 Marks]** > **Answer:** Photopic vision is mediated by *cone* cells in the retina, operates at high luminance (above ~3 cd/m²), provides colour perception and high acuity, peaks at 555 nm. Scotopic vision uses *rod* cells, operates at low luminance (below ~0.001 cd/m²), is monochromatic, peaks at 507 nm. In a normally lit office (300–500 lux), photopic vision dominates. **Q4.** What is **glare**? Name and briefly define its two main types. **[3 Marks]** > **Answer:** Glare is the sensation of discomfort or reduction in visual performance caused by excessive brightness or inappropriate distribution of luminance in the field of view. Two types: (1) **Disability glare** — reduces visual performance (e.g., oncoming headlights at night). (2) **Discomfort glare** — causes annoyance and fatigue without necessarily reducing acuity (e.g., unshaded fluorescent tubes in peripheral vision). **Q5.** Define **Colour Rendering Index (CRI)**. What CRI value is recommended for office interiors? **[3 Marks]** > **Answer:** CRI is a measure (0–100) of how accurately a light source renders the colours of objects compared to a reference source of the same colour temperature. CRI = 100 = perfect colour rendering. **Recommended CRI for office interiors: $\geq 80$** (per IS 3646 / IES standards). ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Five photometric quantities:** Flux (lm), Intensity (cd), Illuminance (lx), Luminance (cd/m²), Efficacy (lm/W). Memorise symbols and units in order. - [x] **Fundamental law of illumination:** $E = (I \cos\theta)/d^2$ — combines both inverse square and cosine laws. - [x] **Inverse square law** valid for point sources only ($\text{size} < d/10$). Doubling distance → quarter illuminance. - [x] **Cosine law** applies for *diffuse* (Lambertian) surfaces. The horizontal, vertical, and normal illuminance components are different. - [x] **Lumen method equation:** $N = (E \cdot A) / (UF \cdot MF \cdot \Phi)$ — *always include both $UF$ and $MF$.* - [x] **Typical values:** $UF = 0.3$–$0.8$; $MF = 0.7$–$0.8$; $\eta_{\text{LED}} \approx 100$–$200$ lm/W; $\eta_{\text{incandescent}} \approx 10$–$15$ lm/W; $\eta_{\text{fluorescent}} \approx 50$–$80$ lm/W. - [x] **Luminance vs illuminance:** Eye perceives *luminance*; engineers measure/design using *illuminance* (easier). - [x] **Glare control:** UGR $\leq 19$ for offices; use diffusers, indirect lighting, and proper shielding angles. - [x] **Recommended illuminance (IS 3646):** General office 300–500 lx; precision work 750–1500 lx; corridors 100–150 lx; VDT work 300–500 lx. - [x] **Quality factors:** Uniformity ($E_{min}/E_{avg} \geq 0.7$), CRI ($\geq 80$), glare (UGR $\leq 19$), colour temperature (3000 K–6500 K), flicker-free (≥ 100 Hz). - [x] **Spacing-to-height ratio:** $S/H_m \leq 1.5$ for direct lighting, $\leq 1.0$ for indirect. - [x] **Conversion:** 1 fc = 10.76 lx (frequently asked in KTU). - [x] **Conversion of energy:** 1 W at 555 nm = 683 lm (theoretical max). - [x] **Critical Pitfalls to Avoid:** (1) Forgetting $UF$ or $MF$ in lumen method. (2) Using $d$ instead of $h$ in cosine law vertical/horizontal problems. (3) Confusing luminance with illuminance. (4) Treating all surfaces as horizontal. (5) Rounding lamp count down instead of up. - [x] **Visual performance curve:** Performance vs illuminance is *sigmoidal*, not linear. Optimal zone is 500–1500 lx for most tasks. - [x] **Photopic peak:** 555 nm; **Scotopic peak:** 507 nm — these are the wavelengths the eye is most sensitive to under each mode. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Board Advice:** In long-answer (14-mark) questions, the examiner allocates marks roughly as: 3 marks for the correct formula, 3 marks for substitution of values, 2 marks for final numerical answer with units, and 6 marks for *engineering interpretation* (verifying, commenting on design adequacy, suggesting improvements). Don't stop at the number — always add a one-line comment on whether the design is acceptable and why.

topics.title: Energy Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Human Factors in Engineering and Design**, an **Energy Source** refers to any physical, chemical, electrical, mechanical, thermal, or radiant form of energy present in a man-machine work system that possesses the *inherent potential to cause harm* to human operators, equipment, or the environment if released, transferred, or misapplied in an uncontrolled manner.

The concept is rooted in the **Energy Theory of Accident Causation**, originally proposed by **Gordon John Gibson (1961)** and later refined by **William Haddon Jr. (1963, 1968)** in his seminal *Haddon Matrix*. According to this framework, every occupational injury or accident is fundamentally an event in which **hazardous energy** reaches the human body (or a vulnerable system) at a rate and magnitude that exceeds the biological or structural damage threshold of that entity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Module 2, Energy Sources are treated not merely as physical quantities (as in basic Physics), but as **anthropocentric hazard vectors** — energy is only a "source" in the human factors sense when a human is exposed to it. Energy by itself in a vacuum is neutral; energy in the presence of humans is the study.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The River and the Dam"

Imagine a high-altitude mountain river carrying massive volumes of water (energy) downhill. 

- The water (energy) is **useful** — it can drive turbines, irrigate fields, and sustain life.
- The same water (energy) is **destructive** — it can drown people, erode towns, and destroy bridges.

A **dam** acts as a control mechanism. In human factors engineering, **safeguards, interlocks, lockout-tagout (LOTO) procedures, PPE, and ergonomic design** act as the "dams" that prevent hazardous energy from reaching the worker in harmful doses.

> **Key Insight:** Just as an engineer would never build a dam without a spillway, a human factors specialist would never design a machine with stored energy without an engineered energy-isolation pathway.

## 1.3 The Anthropocentric Energy Principle

$$E_{hazardous} = E_{released} - E_{tolerated}$$

This conceptual equation states that harm occurs only when the *released energy* exceeds the *tolerated (or absorbed)* energy of the human body or system.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

The following standard quantities govern energy hazards in human factors:

- **Threshold Limit Values (TLV)** — published by ACGIH
- **Permissible Exposure Limits (PEL)** — set by OSHA
- **IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health)** — NIOSH standard
- **g-force tolerance** of the human body: **≈ 6 g longitudinal, ≈ 3–4 g transverse**
- **Human pain threshold for electrical current**: **≈ 1 mA** (AC, 50/60 Hz)
- **Ventricular fibrillation threshold**: **≈ 100 mA** (AC) for 1 second
- **Skin burn threshold (thermal)**: **≈ 44 °C** (long exposure), **≈ 60 °C** (instant)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The 2024 scheme specifically tests whether students can *connect* physical quantities to human tolerance limits. Memorizing formulas without understanding human tolerance ranges is a common reason students lose marks in ESE.

---

## 1.5 Quick Classification Overview (Expanded in Section 2)

Energy sources in workplaces are classified into **seven canonical families**:

| # | Energy Family | Typical Work-Place Form | Human Damage Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Kinetic (Mechanical)** | Moving parts, vehicles, projectiles | Impact, crushing |
| 2 | **Potential (Gravitational / Elastic)** | Raised loads, springs, compressed gases | Falling, sudden release |
| 3 | **Electrical** | Mains supply, capacitors, batteries | Shock, burns, fibrillation |
| 4 | **Chemical** | Fuels, solvents, acids, dusts | Burns, poisoning, asphyxiation |
| 5 | **Thermal** | Furnaces, steam, molten metal, cryogens | Burns, frostbite |
| 6 | **Radiant (Ionising & Non-Ionising)** | Lasers, UV, X-rays, EMF, microwaves | Tissue damage, cancer |
| 7 | **Biological / Biomechanical** | Repetitive motion, vibration, posture | MSDs, fatigue |

---

## 1.6 Historical Framework Reference

The **Haddon Matrix** cross-tabulates three phases (Pre-event, Event, Post-event) against the four energy considerations (Host, Agent, Physical Environment, Socio-economic Environment). KTU 2024 expects students to be able to *place energy source controls into the correct cell* of this matrix.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Canonical Energy Sources — Analytical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Kinetic Energy (KE)

**Definition:** Energy possessed by an object due to its translational or rotational motion.

**Why it matters in Human Factors:** Most occupational impact injuries (struck-by, caught-in) are kinetic-energy transfer events.

**How it manifests:**
- Vehicle collisions in industrial yards
- Unguarded rotating shafts, pulleys, gears
- Flying chips, sparks, or fragments from machining
- Falling objects from elevation

**Human-factors mitigation levers:**
- Machine guarding (fixed, interlocked, adjustable)
- Speed-limiting governors
- Restraining barriers (light curtains, mats)
- PPE (helmets, steel-toe boots, face shields)

### 2.1.2 Potential Energy (PE)

**Definition:** Energy stored by virtue of position, configuration, or stress state.

**Forms relevant to workplaces:**
- *Gravitational PE* — elevated loads, workers on platforms
- *Elastic PE* — compressed springs, tensioned belts
- *Pneumatic / Hydraulic PE* — pressurised gas/liquid reservoirs
- *Residual electrical PE* — charged capacitors after isolation

**Why it matters:** Even after a machine is *electrically* de-energised, **stored (latent) energy** remains and is responsible for a large fraction of serious injuries during maintenance.

**Human-factors mitigation levers:**
- LOTO procedures (Lockout / Tagout / Try-out)
- Bleed-down valves, grounding sticks
- Mechanical blocking (jacks, chocks, pins)
- Stored-energy warning labels (per ISO 12100)

### 2.1.3 Electrical Energy

**Definition:** Energy associated with the flow of electric charge or its storage in electric fields.

**Why it matters:** Electrical injuries are uniquely unforgiving because the *current* passing through the body — not voltage alone — determines the physiological effect.

**Human-tolerance reference (per IEC 60479):**

| Current (mA, 50/60 Hz AC) | Physiological Effect |
|---|---|
| **0.5 – 1** | Perception threshold (tingling) |
| **1 – 5** | Mild shock, pain |
| **5 – 15** | Muscle "let-go" threshold |
| **15 – 100** | Severe shock, respiratory paralysis |
| **> 100 (> 0.5 s)** | Ventricular fibrillation → death |

### 2.1.4 Chemical Energy

**Definition:** Energy released during a chemical reaction (combustion, oxidation, neutralisation, polymerisation).

**Manifestations:**
- Fires and explosions (dust, vapour, gas)
- Acute toxicity (inhalation, skin absorption)
- Corrosive burns
- Chronic exposure (carcinogens, asphyxiants)

**Human-factors controls:** Ventilation, substitution (green chemistry), MSDS/SDS, intrinsically safe (Ex) equipment zoning, gas detectors, PPE.

### 2.1.5 Thermal Energy

**Definition:** Energy related to the kinetic energy of atoms/molecules; manifests as heat or cold.

**Burn severity (per ASTM C1055 reference scale):**
- *First-degree*: epidermal damage (≈ 44 °C, 6 h)
- *Second-degree*: dermal damage (≈ 55 °C, 30 s)
- *Third-degree*: full-thickness (≈ 70 °C, < 1 s)

**Cryogenic hazards** (e.g., liquid N₂ at −196 °C) cause tissue embrittlement and asphyxiation.

### 2.1.6 Radiant Energy

**Sub-classes:**
- *Ionising:* X-rays, gamma rays, alpha/beta particles
- *Non-ionising:* UV, visible, IR, microwave, radiofrequency, low-frequency EMF, laser

**Why it matters:** Radiation injuries are often *insidious* — no immediate pain, yet cellular damage accumulates. The **ALARA principle** (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) governs all radiation work.

### 2.1.7 Biomechanical Energy

A uniquely *human-factors* category. Energy is generated, transmitted, or absorbed by the worker's own musculoskeletal system through:
- Repetitive motion (carpal tunnel syndrome)
- Static posture (back loading, neck flexion)
- Vibration (Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome — HAVS)
- Forceful exertions (manual material handling)

**NIOSH Lifting Equation** quantifies biomechanical load and is a frequently tested concept in the KTU PEBMT742 syllabus.

---

## 2.2 The Energy Transfer Model (Gibson-Haddon Framework)

Haddon proposed that accidents can be analysed as **uncontrolled energy transfers** between four entities:

1. **Host** (the human)
2. **Agent** (the energy-bearing object)
3. **Physical Environment** (medium of transfer)
4. **Socio-economic Environment** (regulatory, cultural context)

**Ten Haddon Strategies for Energy Hazard Mitigation** (Critical for KTU Module 2):

| # | Strategy | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Prevent creation of hazard | Use non-flammable solvents |
| 2 | Reduce amount of hazard | Use smaller quantities of chemicals |
| 3 | Prevent release of hazard | Containment vessels, bunding |
| 4 | Reduce rate of release | Restrictive orifices, relief valves |
| 5 | Separate hazard from host in time/space | Machine guarding, barriers |
| 6 | Use barriers between hazard & host | Insulation, radiation shields |
| 7 | Modify basic qualities of hazard | Fire-retardant treatments |
| 8 | Strengthen host resistance | PPE, training, vaccination |
| 9 | Begin to counter damage already done | First aid, fire suppression |
| 10 | Stabilise, repair, rehabilitate host | Medical care, return-to-work programs |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** A common 14-mark question in ESE asks students to apply **Haddon's 10 strategies** to a specific industrial scenario (e.g., a robotic welding cell, a chemical reactor, a CNC machining centre). Memorising the list in order earns easy marks.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** Absolute-value bars `|x|` and similar `|` characters have been intentionally replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table layout.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning of Terms | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Translational Kinetic Energy | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | $m$ = mass, $v$ = velocity | J (kg·m²/s²) |
| 2 | Rotational Kinetic Energy | $E_{rot} = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ | $I$ = moment of inertia, $\omega$ = angular velocity | J |
| 3 | Gravitational PE | $E_{p} = m g h$ | $g \approx 9.81$ m/s², $h$ = height | J |
| 4 | Spring / Elastic PE | $E_{s} = \tfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}$ | $k$ = spring stiffness, $x$ = deformation | J |
| 5 | Electrical Power dissipation | $P = V I = I^{2} R$ | $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current, $R$ = resistance | W |
| 6 | Electrical Energy stored (capacitor) | $E_{cap} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | $C$ = capacitance, $V$ = voltage | J |
| 7 | Thermal Energy (sensible heat) | $Q = m c \Delta T$ | $c$ = specific heat capacity | J |
| 8 | Latent Heat of phase change | $Q_{L} = m L$ | $L$ = latent heat (fusion/vapour) | J |
| 9 | Mechanical Work done | $W = F \, d \cos\theta$ | $F$ = force, $d$ = displacement, $\theta$ = angle | J |
| 10 | Power (rate of energy transfer) | $P = \dfrac{dE}{dt} = F \cdot v$ | Scalar rate of energy flow | W |
| 11 | NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit | $RWL = LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM$ | Multipliers of Load Constant $LC = 23$ kg | kg |
| 12 | Lifting Index (Hazard flag) | $LI = \dfrac{\text{Actual Load}}{RWL}$ | LI > 1 ⇒ elevated risk | dimensionless |
| 13 | Vibration dose (partial) | $a_{hv} = \sqrt{\sum_{i} (W_i \, a_{hvi})^{2}}$ | Frequency-weighted RMS | m/s² |
| 14 | Total Energy Hazard Index | $HEI = \sum_{j} w_{j} \cdot \dfrac{E_{j}}{E_{tol,\,j}}$ | $w_j$ = weighting, $E_{tol}$ = tolerance | dimensionless |
| 15 | Acute injury severity (kinetic) | $E_{k,crit} \approx 7 \text{ J}$ (approx. skull fracture) | Empirical biological threshold | J |
| 16 | Voltage-to-current through body | $I_{body} = \dfrac{V_{source}}{Z_{body}}$ | $Z_{body} \approx 1\text{ k}\Omega$ wet, $\approx 100\text{ k}\Omega$ dry | A |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Safety:** Inherent Safer Design (ISD) uses energy minimisation (Strategies 1, 2) to shrink the magnitude of any potential accident — central to the **Dow Chemical Exposure Index** and **OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119**.
- **Product Liability:** Designers must demonstrate that foreseeable misuse cannot generate hazardous energy exposure beyond tolerable limits (referenced in **ISO 12100:2010** risk assessment).
- **Ergonomic Redesign:** The NIOSH Lifting Equation translates biomechanical energy exposure into a dimensionless Lifting Index that drives workstation redesign.
- **Forensic Engineering:** Reconstruction of accidents (e.g., forklift tip-overs, electrical arc-flash events) depends on back-calculating released energy using the equations above.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No truncation allowed.** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. Each transition is annotated with a one-line English explanation so a first-time reader can follow.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Falling Objects Hurt — Linking Height to Injury Severity

**Problem context:** A 5 kg tool is left on a 4 m high scaffold. A worker walks beneath. Determine the kinetic energy at impact and classify the expected injury.

### Step 1 — Identify the form of energy initially stored
The tool is stationary at height $h$. Its energy is *gravitational potential energy*, $E_p$.

$$E_{p} = m \, g \, h$$

**Logic:** At rest, $v = 0$, so $E_k = 0$. All the available energy is stored as $E_p$.

### Step 2 — Substitute numerical values

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{p} &= (5 \text{ kg}) \cdot (9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}) \cdot (4 \text{ m}) \\
     &= 5 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 4 \\
     &= 196.2 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Multiply mass by gravitational acceleration by height. The result is the energy available for *uncontrolled release*.

### Step 3 — Apply conservation of energy at the instant of impact
Just before hitting the worker, all $E_p$ has converted to $E_k$ (neglecting air drag for a short fall).

$$E_{k,\text{impact}} = E_{p} = 196.2 \text{ J}$$

**Logic:** Total mechanical energy is conserved when non-conservative forces (drag) are negligible. The "stored" potential becomes "released" kinetic energy.

### Step 4 — Compare with biological injury thresholds

| Body Region | Approximate Injury Threshold |
|---|---|
| Skull fracture (impact) | **≈ 7 J** |
| Rib fracture | **≈ 25–40 J** |
| Fatal head trauma | **≈ 80–100 J** |
| Skin laceration | **≈ 5–10 J** |

**Logic:** The released energy (196.2 J) is **> 2× the fatal head trauma threshold**, and **> 28× the skull-fracture threshold**. This is unambiguously a *fatal energy exposure* scenario.

### Step 5 — Human-factors conclusion

> **Haddon's countermeasure selection:**
> - *Strategy 5 (separate in time/space):* install toe-boards and debris nets on the scaffold.
> - *Strategy 3 (prevent release):* tether the tool with a lanyard (PRE-EX anchor).
> - *Strategy 8 (strengthen host):* mandate hard-hats rated to ANSI Z89.1 Class E (20 kV electrical + high-temperature; up to 5 kg falling object impact tested).
> - *Strategy 1 (prevent creation of hazard):* redesign the workflow so tools are not stored overhead.

### Step 6 — Useful derived metric: Equivalent Drop-Height for a Given Injury

Rearranging $E_p = m g h$ for $h$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{\text{critical}} &= \frac{E_{\text{threshold}}}{m \, g} \\
                    &= \frac{7 \text{ J}}{5 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}} \\
                    &= \frac{7}{49.05} \\
                    &\approx 0.143 \text{ m} \;\; (\approx 14.3 \text{ cm})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A 5 kg object falling even *15 cm* can fracture a skull. This counterintuitive result is the core reason KTU emphasises "dropped object" hazards in construction & maintenance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stored Capacitor Energy and the "After-Power-Off" Hazard

**Context:** A maintenance technician opens a high-voltage capacitor bank cabinet *believing* the system is dead. Estimate the danger from residual stored energy.

### Step 1 — State the stored-energy formula

$$E_{cap} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, C \, V^{2}$$

**Logic:** Energy in any capacitor is quadratic in voltage; small voltages store negligible energy, but high voltages (480 V, 5 kV, 35 kV) store dangerous quantities.

### Step 2 — Take a representative industrial value
Let $C = 100 \text{ μF}$, $V = 5\,000 \text{ V}$ (typical power-factor-correction capacitor).

### Step 3 — Compute the energy

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{cap} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}) \cdot (5\,000 \text{ V})^{2} \\
        &= 0.5 \cdot 10^{-4} \cdot 25 \times 10^{6} \\
        &= 0.5 \cdot 2500 \\
        &= 1\,250 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** 1 250 J is the kinetic energy of a **2 kg object moving at 35 m/s (≈ 126 km/h)**. The technician would receive an explosive, potentially fatal discharge.

### Step 4 — Human-factors translation
This single computation underpins the entire **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)** standard (OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147). The very act of "switching off" does not eliminate stored energy. The procedure must explicitly:
- Disconnect
- Isolate
- Discharge (bleed off the capacitor)
- Lock
- Verify zero-energy state

### Step 5 — Equivalent biological damage using the body-impedance model

$$I_{body} = \frac{V_{residual}}{Z_{body}} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{R_{body}^{2} + X_{c}^{2}}}$$

For a residual $V = 5\,000 \text{ V}$ and $Z_{body} \approx 1\,000 \, \Omega$ (wet skin):

$$I_{body} \approx \frac{5\,000}{1\,000} = 5 \text{ A} = 5\,000 \text{ mA}$$

**Logic:** 5 000 mA is **50× the ventricular-fibrillation threshold** (100 mA). The hazard is *not* "low voltage because the supply is off" — it is "stored energy inside a passive component."

---

## 3.3 Derivation: NIOSH Lifting Equation — Calculating the Lifting Index (LI)

### Step 1 — Write the Recommended Weight Limit equation

$$RWL = LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM$$

Where:
- $LC$ = Load Constant = **23 kg** (the weight recommended under ideal conditions)
- $HM$ = Horizontal Multiplier = $25 / H$ (with $H$ = horizontal distance in cm, measured from spine to hands)
- $VM$ = Vertical Multiplier = $1 - 0.003 \vert V - 75 \vert$ (with $V$ = vertical reach in cm)
- $DM$ = Distance Multiplier = $0.82 + 4.5 / D$ (with $D$ = vertical travel in cm)
- $AM$ = Asymmetry Multiplier = $1 - 0.0032 A$ (with $A$ = angular displacement in degrees)
- $FM$ = Frequency Multiplier (table lookup from lifts/min and duration)
- $CM$ = Coupling Multiplier (1.0 / 0.95 / 0.90 based on grip quality)

### Step 2 — Sample calculation

A worker lifts 18 kg from a shelf located:
- Horizontal distance $H = 50$ cm
- Vertical reach $V = 100$ cm
- Vertical travel $D = 80$ cm
- Asymmetry $A = 30°$
- Frequency 1 lift/min, ≤ 1 h (assume $FM = 1.0$)
- Coupling: good handles ($CM = 1.0$)

**Compute each multiplier:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HM &= \frac{25}{50} = 0.50 \\
VM &= 1 - 0.003 \vert 100 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \cdot 25 = 1 - 0.075 = 0.925 \\
DM &= 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{80} = 0.82 + 0.0563 = 0.8763 \\
AM &= 1 - 0.0032 \cdot 30 = 1 - 0.096 = 0.904 \\
FM &= 1.00 \quad (assumed) \\
CM &= 1.00 \quad (assumed)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute the RWL:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RWL &= 23 \cdot 0.50 \cdot 0.925 \cdot 0.8763 \cdot 0.904 \cdot 1.00 \cdot 1.00 \\
    &= 23 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.925 \cdot 0.8763 \cdot 0.904 \\
    &= 11.5 \cdot 0.925 \cdot 0.8763 \cdot 0.904 \\
    &= 10.6375 \cdot 0.8763 \cdot 0.904 \\
    &= 9.3215 \cdot 0.904 \\
    &\approx 8.43 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute the Lifting Index:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LI &= \frac{\text{Actual Load}}{RWL} = \frac{18 \text{ kg}}{8.43 \text{ kg}} \\
   &\approx 2.14
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** $LI > 1$ means the lift is *above recommended limits* and is associated with increased risk of musculoskeletal injury. $LI = 2.14$ indicates a *moderately hazardous* task that warrants ergonomic redesign.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Energy Sources to Human-Factors Controls

| Energy Source | Typical Worker Exposure | Primary Damage | Haddon Strategy (1–10) | Engineering Control | Administrative Control | PPE |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Kinetic (moving parts) | Conveyor belts, presses | Impact, crushing | 5, 6 | Machine guards, light curtains | SOP, training | Steel-toe boots |
| Potential (elevated loads) | Overhead cranes | Falling, struck-by | 3, 5 | Latches, anti-fall devices | Load charts | Helmets |
| Electrical (mains) | Switchgear, wiring | Fibrillation, burns | 4, 6 | Insulation, GFCIs, earthing | LOTO, permit-to-work | Insulated gloves |
| Chemical (solvents) | Cleaning operations | Poisoning, fire | 1, 2 | Substitution, ventilation | MSDS, training | Respirators |
| Thermal (steam lines) | Process plants | Burns | 6, 4 | Insulation, relief valves | PTW | Heat-resistant gloves |
| Radiant (laser) | Manufacturing, medical | Eye/skin damage | 1, 6 | Enclosures, interlocks | Laser safety officer | Laser goggles |
| Biomechanical (manual lift) | Warehousing | MSDs | 1, 7 | Lift-assist devices | Two-person lifts | Back belts (limited) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
