# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Define the term 'Hazardous Energy' as used in Human Factors Engineering. List any four canonical categories of energy sources found in industrial workplaces."**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks, ≈ 80 words):**
*Hazardous energy* is any form of energy in a workplace that, if released in an uncontrolled manner, can cause injury to personnel or damage to equipment. It is the central construct in Gibson's and Haddon's energy theory of accident causation. The four canonical categories are: (i) **mechanical** (kinetic and potential), (ii) **electrical**, (iii) **chemical**, and (iv) **thermal**. Radiant, biomechanical, and biological energy are additional categories frequently encountered in modern ergonomic practice.

> **Marking note:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for theoretical basis, 1 mark for listing the four categories. Examiner tolerance: students who give three correct categories plus one partially correct still receive full marks.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"What is meant by the 'Lifting Index (LI)' in the NIOSH Lifting Equation? State its significance."**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
The Lifting Index (LI) is a dimensionless number defined as the ratio of the **Actual Load** lifted to the **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** computed using six multipliers and a load constant. Mathematically, $LI = \dfrac{L_{actual}}{RWL}$. Its significance is that an $LI \leq 1$ indicates the task is within the recommended limits for nearly all healthy workers, whereas $LI > 1$ signals an *increased risk* of musculoskeletal injury. KTU Board examiners expect students to remember that $LI = 3$ is the upper limit used for designing new lifting tasks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

> As per KTU ESE 2024 regulation, the student answers **one question** out of a Module-level internal choice. We provide **Question A** and **Question B**; each contains two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 2, Set A]**

**(a)** Explain the **Energy Theory of Accident Causation** as proposed by William Haddon Jr. With the aid of a neat schematic, describe **Haddon's Ten Strategies** for mitigating hazardous energy. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A maintenance task requires a worker to replace a hydraulic cylinder rated at 210 bar (21 MPa) with an internal volume of 5 litres, fitted with a 0.5 m extending rod of mass 12 kg. The system is also supplied from a 480 V, 60 Hz electrical source and is mounted 3.5 m above floor level.

**Calculate:**
1. The **pressure-volume (P-V) energy** stored in the hydraulic cylinder.
2. The **gravitational potential energy** of the rod.
3. The **kinetic energy** of the rod if it were to free-fall and reach the worker.
4. The most appropriate combination of **Haddon strategies** and engineering controls to mitigate these hazards. *(7 marks)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

##### Part (a) — Haddon Energy Theory & Ten Strategies

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The Haddon Energy Theory posits that *all injuries and accidents are the result of uncontrolled transfer of one or more forms of energy to a host in quantities or rates exceeding biological tolerance*. Energy is treated as the "agent" of harm; the human is the "host."

**Three energy components (1.5 marks):**
1. *Pre-event energy* — energy at rest in the system.
2. *Event energy* — energy released during the accident.
3. *Post-event energy* — energy dissipated in the injury phase.

**Haddon's Ten Strategies (4 marks — 0.4 marks each):** Listed in Section 2.2 of these notes. A neat numbered table earns full credit.

##### Part (b) — Numerical & Engineering Analysis

**Step 1 — P-V energy of the hydraulic cylinder:**

$$E_{PV} = P \cdot V$$

where $P$ = absolute pressure and $V$ = volume.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{PV} &= (21 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}) \\
       &= 105 \times 10^{3} \text{ J} \\
       &= 105 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic (1 mark):** Pressurised fluids store enormous energy — equivalent to the kinetic energy of a 1.5-tonne car at 50 km/h.

**Step 2 — Gravitational PE of the rod:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{p} &= m \, g \, h \\
      &= 12 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 3.5 \\
      &= 411.42 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic (1 mark):** Stored purely from elevated position.

**Step 3 — Kinetic energy at impact (conservation of energy):**

$$
E_{k,impact} = E_{p} \approx 411.4 \text{ J}
$$

**Comparison with biological thresholds:** 411 J ≫ 100 J (fatal head trauma). The rod drop is lethal.

**Step 4 — Haddon strategy selection (2.5 marks):**
- *Strategy 3 — Prevent release of hazard:* install a **mechanical safety block** (pin or stop) on the cylinder to prevent the rod from extending under residual pressure.
- *Strategy 4 — Reduce rate of release:* fit a **pilot-operated relief valve** to bleed the cylinder slowly to atmospheric pressure.
- *Strategy 5 — Separate in time/space:* apply **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)** at both the electrical disconnect *and* the hydraulic shut-off valve.
- *Strategy 6 — Barrier between hazard and host:* install a **fixed guard** preventing access to the cylinder envelope while pressurised.
- *Strategy 8 — Strengthen the host:* ensure the technician wears **PPE** rated for hydraulic injection injury (≥ 10 bar puncture resistance) and is trained in stored-energy procedures.

> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
>
> > [!WARNING]
> > **Most-common mark loss (2–3 marks):** Students frequently compute only the electrical or only the hydraulic energy and forget the gravitational energy of the rod. KTU 2024 valuation keys specifically look for **all three** energy forms in a multi-source hazard problem. Always enumerate *every* form of energy present in the system — kinetic, potential (gravitational *and* elastic/pressurised), electrical, thermal, and chemical. Partial enumeration caps the answer at 50% of the marks.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 2, Set B]**

**(a)** Classify the **seven canonical families of energy sources** in industrial workplaces. For each family, provide one example, the associated human damage mechanism, and one primary engineering control. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A worker at a chemical plant handles 25 kg bags of a fine powdered reagent. The bags are manually lifted from a 30 cm-high pallet and placed onto a 90 cm-high conveyor. The horizontal reach is 60 cm, the vertical travel is 60 cm, and the lift is symmetric (asymmetry = 0°). Frequency is 2 lifts/min for 2 hours. The bags have poor handholds (coupling = 0.90).

**Calculate:**
1. The **NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)**.
2. The **Lifting Index (LI)**.
3. Recommend **three ergonomic redesign interventions** based on the result. *(7 marks)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

##### Part (a) — Seven Canonical Energy Families

| # | Energy Family | Example | Human Damage | Primary Engineering Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Kinetic (Mechanical)** | Rotating shaft | Impact, crushing | Fixed machine guard |
| 2 | **Potential (Gravitational / Elastic)** | Raised load, compressed spring | Falling, sudden release | Mechanical block, LOTO |
| 3 | **Electrical** | 415 V mains supply | Fibrillation, burns | Insulation, GFCI, earthing |
| 4 | **Chemical** | Solvent vapour | Poisoning, fire | Local exhaust ventilation |
| 5 | **Thermal** | Steam pipe (180 °C) | Burns | Insulation lagging |
| 6 | **Radiant** | Class 4 laser | Retinal burn | Enclosure, interlock |
| 7 | **Biomechanical** | Repetitive wrist motion | Carpal tunnel syndrome | Job rotation, tool redesign |

*Marking note: 1 mark for correct family classification + example, 0.5 marks for damage, 0.5 marks for control. Allocate 1 mark for a clear, tabular presentation.*

##### Part (b) — NIOSH Lifting Equation Application

**Step 1 — Compute each multiplier:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HM &= \frac{25}{H} = \frac{25}{60} = 0.4167 \\
VM &= 1 - 0.003 \vert V - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \cdot 60 = 0.82 \\
DM &= 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{D} = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{60} = 0.895 \\
AM &= 1 - 0.0032 \cdot 0 = 1.00 \\
FM &= \text{(table lookup, 2 lifts/min, 2 h duration)} \approx 0.95 \\
CM &= 0.90
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute RWL:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RWL &= LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM \\
    &= 23 \cdot 0.4167 \cdot 0.82 \cdot 0.895 \cdot 1.00 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 0.90 \\
    &\approx 5.88 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute LI:**

$$
LI = \frac{L_{actual}}{RWL} = \frac{25}{5.88} \approx 4.25
$$

**Step 4 — Three ergonomic redesign interventions (3 marks):**

1. **Reduce the actual load:** Limit the bag size to 15 kg (which would lower the LI below 3, the upper recommended design limit).
2. **Improve coupling:** Use bags with built-in handholds ($CM \to 1.0$), raising RWL by ~11%.
3. **Reduce horizontal reach and vertical travel:** Raise the pallet to 60 cm (using a scissor lift table) and bring the conveyor closer; this raises HM and DM, increasing RWL by a combined ~30–40%.
4. **Use mechanical aids:** A simple bag-dumping station with a hoist would eliminate the manual lift entirely (Strategy 1, Haddon — Eliminate the hazard).

> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
>
> > [!WARNING]
> > **Common student mistake (1–2 marks lost):** Students often use $LC = 25$ kg (the older 1981 NIOSH constant) instead of the **current $LC = 23$ kg** (revised 1991 equation). KTU 2024 valuation keys explicitly accept both, but the *updated* 23 kg constant is the *preferred* answer. Also, do not round $FM$ prematurely — use the published frequency table for the actual frequency and duration you are given.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this section on the night before the ESE.** Each bullet is a high-density recall point.

- Energy Sources in Human Factors = **anthropocentric hazard vectors**, not just physical quantities.
- **Gibson (1961)** formulated the original energy theory; **Haddon (1963, 1968)** operationalised it into the **Haddon Matrix** and the **Ten Strategies**.
- **Seven canonical families:** kinetic, potential, electrical, chemical, thermal, radiant, biomechanical.
- **KE formula:** $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ — energy is *quadratic* in velocity; doubling speed *quadruples* impact energy.
- **PE formula:** $E_p = m g h$ — a 5 kg object dropped 15 cm already exceeds the skull-fracture threshold of 7 J.
- **Capacitor energy:** $E_{cap} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ — *quadratic* in voltage; 100 μF at 5 kV stores 1.25 kJ.
- **Body current thresholds (50/60 Hz AC):** perception ≈ 1 mA; let-go ≈ 10–15 mA; fibrillation ≈ 100 mA (≥ 0.5 s).
- **Thermal burns:** 1st-degree ≈ 44 °C / 6 h; 2nd-degree ≈ 55 °C / 30 s; 3rd-degree ≈ 70 °C / < 1 s.
- **NIOSH Lifting Equation:** $RWL = LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM$ with $LC = 23$ kg.
- **Lifting Index (LI) = $L_{actual} / RWL$**; $LI \leq 1$ safe, $LI > 3$ redesign required.
- **Haddon's Ten Strategies** must be memorised in order; common KTU question = apply to a specific workplace scenario.
- **Hierarchy of controls:** Elimination > Substitution > Engineering > Administrative > PPE.
- **LOTO sequence:** Notify → Identify → Isolate → Lock/Tag → Bleed stored energy → Verify → Work → Re-verify → Remove lock → Restore → Notify.
- **Biological damage thresholds** (kinetic): skull fracture 7 J, rib 25–40 J, fatal head trauma 80–100 J.
- **Haddon Matrix axes:** Phases (Pre-event, Event, Post-event) × Entities (Host, Agent, Physical Env., Socio-economic Env.).
- **For human-factors energy analysis,** always enumerate *all* forms of energy present, never just the most obvious one.
- **ALARA principle** governs all radiation-energy work; non-threshold effects mean no exposure is "safe" — only "acceptably low."
- **Inherent Safer Design (ISD):** minimise, substitute, moderate, simplify (Kletz's four principles).
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147** is the U.S. regulation behind LOTO; **ISO 12100:2010** is the international risk-assessment standard.

> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** For 14-mark numericals, write down the *formula* first, then *substitute*, then *compute* — never skip a step. The Board examiner can only award partial marks for the steps you actually write.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Infection Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Infection Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛡️ Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Infection Control (IC)** is the disciplined application of engineered safeguards, administrative protocols, and behavioural design principles intended to **prevent or minimise the risk of transmission of infectious agents** (pathogens) within a defined human–system environment — typically a healthcare facility, laboratory, or industrial workspace.

From a **Human Factors Engineering (HFE)** standpoint, infection control is treated as a **closed-loop socio-technical problem** where the *human operator*, the *physical environment*, the *equipment interface*, and the *organisational workflow* interact. Failures in any of these nodes propagate into the chain of infection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Syllabus Terms (in bold):**
> - **Pathogen** — a biological agent capable of causing disease.
> - **Reservoir** — the habitat in which the infectious agent normally lives, grows, and multiplies.
> - **Host** — a person (or animal) who can harbour an infectious agent.
> - **Standard Precautions** — a universal set of infection-control practices applied to **all** patients regardless of suspected infection status.
> - **Hierarchy of Controls (HoC)** — a 5-tier framework (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE) prioritising the most effective interventions at the top.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of infection control as the **security system of a bank vault** 🏦:

| Bank Vault Element | Infection Control Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Outer perimeter wall | **Engineering Controls** (ventilation, isolation rooms) |
| Security guard at the gate | **Administrative Controls** (screening, triage protocols) |
| CCTV cameras & alarms | **Surveillance & Monitoring** (audit, contact tracing) |
| Lockers & gloves for tellers | **PPE** (masks, gowns, gloves) |
| Removing the vault entirely | **Elimination** (cancelling the procedure, telemedicine) |
| Replacing cash with digital | **Substitution** (non-touch faucets, automatic doors) |

> 🧠 **Mnemonic Tip:** *"The chain breaks at its weakest link."* — Infection control is about **breaking the chain of infection** at *any* point, and HFE helps you choose the *most ergonomic, least error-prone* point to break it.

---

## 1.3 Why Human Factors?

Most infection-transmission events are **not** caused by the pathogen alone — they occur because of:
1. **Cognitive slip** (forgetting to sanitise hands).
2. **Workflow mismatch** (poorly located hand-rub dispensers).
3. **Interface design flaws** (PPE that is too hot, fogs goggles, or takes 60 seconds to don).
4. **Alarm fatigue** (too many false alerts desensitise staff).
5. **Fatigue and shift work** degrading vigilance.

Human Factors Engineering systematically **redesigns the task, tool, and environment** to make the *safe behaviour the path of least resistance*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧪 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chain of Infection (Six Links)

The classical epidemiological model, mapped to engineering interventions:

1. **Infectious Agent (Pathogen)** — Bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites. *HFE angle:* surface-material selection (copper, antimicrobial coatings) to reduce viability.
2. **Reservoir** — Humans (HCWs, patients), equipment, water systems. *HFE angle:* workflow design to prevent reservoir overlap.
3. **Portal of Exit** — Respiratory tract, blood, skin, GI tract. *HFE angle:* cough-etiquette signage, mask design.
4. **Mode of Transmission** — Contact, Droplet, Airborne, Vector. *HFE angle:* spatial separation, ventilation design.
5. **Portal of Entry** — Mucous membranes, broken skin, invasive devices. *HFE angle:* sharps disposal engineering, needleless systems.
6. **Susceptible Host** — Immunocompromised, elderly, neonates. *HFE angle:* triage protocols, vaccination programmes.

> 🧠 **HFE Insight:** Breaking *any single link* interrupts transmission. The Hierarchy of Controls tells you **which link to break most effectively**.

---

## 2.2 Hierarchy of Controls (HoC) — Applied to Infection Control

Most → Least effective (top-down):

| Tier | Control | IC Example | HFE Question |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 🥇 | **Elimination** | Postpone elective procedures during outbreak | Can the hazardous task be *removed entirely*? |
| 🥈 | **Substitution** | Replace open suction with closed-system | Can a *safer alternative* be used? |
| 🥉 | **Engineering Controls** | Negative-pressure rooms, HEPA filters, touchless taps | Can the *environment* be redesigned? |
| 4️⃣ | **Administrative Controls** | Triage protocols, training, signage | Can *work practices* be changed? |
| 5️⃣ | **PPE** | Masks, gowns, gloves, face shields | Is the *last line of defence* usable? |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Pearl:** Examiners award higher marks if you justify *why* an intervention is at a particular tier. PPE is the **least reliable** tier because it depends on **human compliance** — a classic HFE pain point.

---

## 2.3 Standard vs. Transmission-Based Precautions

| Feature | Standard Precautions (SP) | Transmission-Based (TBP) |
|---|---|---|
| When applied | **Always**, all patients | When specific pathogen suspected |
| Components | Hand hygiene, PPE, sharps safety, injection safety, environmental cleaning | Added: Contact / Droplet / Airborne |
| HFE focus | Ergonomic design of sinks, dispenser placement | Spatial layout, ventilation design |
| Trigger | Universal | Diagnosis-driven |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all quantitative tools** you may need for derivations and numericals in this module. Use `\vert` (not `|`) for absolute-value notation to keep Markdown tables intact.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Application in IC |
|:-:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Basic Reproduction Number** | $R_0$ | Dimensionless; mean secondary cases per primary case | If $R_0 > 1$, outbreak grows; if $R_0 < 1$, dies out |
| 2 | **Effective Reproduction Number** | $R_e = R_0 \times S$ | $S$ = susceptible fraction (0–1) | Adjusts $R_0$ for immunity / vaccination |
| 3 | **Attack Rate** | $AR = \dfrac{\text{New cases}}{\text{Population at risk}} \times 100$ | Percent | Measures outbreak intensity |
| 4 | **Hand-Hygiene Compliance (HHC)** | $HHC = \dfrac{\text{Actual events}}{\text{Opportunities}} \times 100$ | Percent | WHO target: $\geq 70\%$ |
| 5 | **Air Changes per Hour (ACH)** | $ACH = \dfrac{Q}{V} \times 60$ | $Q$ = flow rate in m³/s; $V$ = room volume in m³ | Negative-pressure isolation: $ACH \geq 12$ (new) or $\geq 6$ (existing) |
| 6 | **HEPA Filtration Efficiency** | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{C_{downstream}}{C_{upstream}}$ | Dimensionless | $\eta \geq 0.9997$ at particle size **0.3 µm** |
| 7 | **Pressure Differential** | $\Delta P = P_{room} - P_{corridor}$ | Pascal (Pa) | Airborne isolation: $\Delta P \leq -2.5$ Pa |
| 8 | **Surface-Cleaning Efficacy** | $\text{Log Reduction} = -\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_{final}}{N_{initial}}\right)$ | Log units | $\geq 3$ log reduction acceptable for high-touch surfaces |
| 9 | **Fitts' Law (PPE Reach Time)** | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{D}{W}\right)$ | $D$ = distance, $W$ = target width | Predicts time to grasp PPE dispenser |
| 10 | **Risk Priority Number (FMEA)** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Severity × Occurrence × Detection (1–10) | Identifies highest-risk IC failure modes |

> 🧠 **HFE Real-World Utility:** The $R_0$ / $R_e$ pair drives **policy design** (lockdowns, vaccination thresholds). ACH and $\Delta P$ drive **facility design**. RPN drives **process redesign priorities** — exactly the type of cross-disciplinary problem an HFE engineer solves.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔢 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Although Infection Control is largely a systems-design subject, the **quantitative backbone** (epidemiology, ventilation, FMEA) appears regularly in KTU numericals. Below are **fully expanded derivations** with zero skipped steps.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Effective Reproduction Number $R_e$ from $R_0$

**Starting premise:** A pathogen with reproduction number $R_0$ spreads through a population in which a fraction $S$ is *still susceptible*. The remaining fraction $(1 - S)$ is immune (via prior infection or vaccination).

$$
R_e = R_0 \times S
$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

**Step 1 —** Each infected individual, in a *fully susceptible* population, generates on average $R_0$ new infections.

**Step 2 —** If only fraction $S$ of contacts are with susceptible individuals, only that fraction of transmission events succeeds.

**Step 3 —** Therefore, the *effective* average is the product:

$$
R_e = R_0 \times S
$$

**Step 4 — Herd-immunity threshold (HIT):** Outbreak dies out when $R_e \leq 1$.

$$
R_0 \times S \leq 1
$$

$$
S \leq \dfrac{1}{R_0}
$$

$$
\text{HIT} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{R_0}
$$

> 🧠 **Interpretation:** For measles ($R_0 = 15$), HIT $= 1 - \dfrac{1}{15} = 0.933$ → need **93.3 %** vaccine coverage. This is a classic KTU Board favourite.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Attack Rate Calculation

A 40-bed ward has 12 confirmed cases of a respiratory virus over a 14-day period, from a total of 38 exposed patients (2 beds unoccupied). Compute the Attack Rate.

**Given:**
- New cases $= 12$
- Population at risk $= 38$

**Step 1 —** Apply the formula:

$$
AR = \dfrac{\text{New cases}}{\text{Population at risk}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 —** Substitute:

$$
AR = \dfrac{12}{38} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 —** Evaluate:

$$
AR = 0.3158 \times 100 = 31.58 \%
$$

**Step 4 —** Interpret: **31.58 %** of exposed patients developed infection. WHO flags any $AR > 20\%$ as an **outbreak trigger**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Air Changes per Hour (ACH) for an Isolation Room

**Given:**
- Room volume $V = 4 \text{ m} \times 5 \text{ m} \times 3 \text{ m} = 60 \text{ m}^3$
- Air-extraction flow $Q = 0.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

**Step 1 —** Apply the formula:

$$
ACH = \dfrac{Q}{V} \times 60
$$

**Step 2 —** Substitute:

$$
ACH = \dfrac{0.2}{60} \times 60
$$

**Step 3 —** Evaluate:

$$
ACH = 0.2 \times 60 = 12 \text{ air changes/hour}
$$

**Step 4 —** Check against standard: ACH $= 12$ meets the **CDC/ASHRAE recommendation for new airborne-infection isolation rooms (AIIR)**. ✅

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — HEPA Filter Log-Reduction

A HEPA filter receives 100 000 colony-forming units (CFU) upstream; downstream count is 30 CFU.

**Step 1 —** Apply:

$$
\text{Log Reduction} = -\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_{final}}{N_{initial}}\right)
$$

**Step 2 —** Substitute:

$$
\text{Log Reduction} = -\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{30}{100\,000}\right) = -\log_{10}\!\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right)
$$

**Step 3 —** Evaluate:

$$
\text{Log Reduction} = -(\log_{10} 3 - 4) = -(0.477 - 4) = 3.523
$$

**Step 4 —** Interpret: **3.52-log reduction** (= 99.97 % removal) — meets HEPA specification. ✅

---

## 3.5 FMEA-Based Risk Priority Number (RPN) for a Hand-Hygiene Station

A hand-rub dispenser is located 8 m from the bed. Severity (S) if missed $= 8$. Occurrence (O) of missed event $= 5$. Detection (D) difficulty (higher = harder to detect) $= 6$.

**Step 1 —** Apply RPN:

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

**Step 2 —** Substitute:

$$
RPN = 8 \times 5 \times 6 = 240
$$

**Step 3 —** Threshold: RPN $> 150$ → **redesign required**. The dispenser should be relocated to $\leq 1.5$ m from the bed (per WHO guidelines) to lower O and D.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — HFE Interventions at Each Link

| Chain Link | HFE Engineering Intervention | Quantitative Lever |
|---|---|---|
| Infectious Agent | Antimicrobial surfaces, UV-C | Log reduction $\geq 3$ |
| Reservoir | Workflow zoning, no-touch bins | Separation distance $\geq 1.5$ m |
| Portal of Exit | Mask design, cough-etched signage | Filtration efficiency $\geq 95\%$ |
| Mode of Transmission | Ventilation, spatial layout | ACH $\geq 12$; $\Delta P \leq -2.5$ Pa |
| Portal of Entry | Needleless connectors, sharps containers | Needle-stick rate $\leq 5 / 1000$ HCW-days |
| Susceptible Host | Triage, vaccination drives | $R_e < 1$; HIT coverage |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!NOTE]

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks.
> - **Part B:** 1 question × 14 marks (with internal choice) = 14 marks.
> - Total module weightage as per syllabus.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the *Chain of Infection* and list its six links.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Chain of Infection** is a conceptual model describing how an infectious agent spreads from a reservoir to a susceptible host. The six links are:
1. **Infectious agent** (pathogen)
2. **Reservoir** (where the agent lives)
3. **Portal of exit** (route out of the reservoir)
4. **Mode of transmission** (contact / droplet / airborne / vector)
5. **Portal of entry** (route into the new host)
6. **Susceptible host** (person vulnerable to infection)

*(1 mark for definition; 2 marks for the six links — 0.5 each, OR 1 mark for concept + 2 marks for complete listing.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between *Standard Precautions* and *Transmission-Based Precautions*.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Standard Precautions | Transmission-Based Precautions |
|---|---|---|
| Applicability | **All** patients, always | Only when specific pathogen is suspected/confirmed |
| Components | Hand hygiene, PPE basics, sharps safety, environmental cleaning | Adds contact / droplet / airborne measures |
| Trigger | Universal | Diagnosis-driven |

*(1 mark for each correctly contrasted feature; 1 mark for example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Writing only textbook definitions** without HFE angle → loses 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Skipping diagrams** (Chain of Infection, HoC pyramid) → loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring numerical support** when the question says *"with examples"* → loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Putting PPE as the FIRST line of defence** → conceptual error, loses 2 marks.
> 5. **No mention of human factors / ergonomics** in a HFE-design course → 0 marks for application.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the *Chain of Infection* with a labelled diagram. Discuss how **human factors engineering** can be used to break the chain at *at least three* links. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An isolation room of dimensions $4 \text{ m} \times 5 \text{ m} \times 3 \text{ m}$ uses an air-extraction system delivering $Q = 0.15 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$.
    **(i)** Calculate the **Air Changes per Hour (ACH)**.
    **(ii)** State whether the room meets **CDC airborne-isolation standards** and justify.
    **(iii)** Recommend **one human-factors redesign** if compliance is poor. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Diagram (2 marks):** Draw a six-link chain of infection (refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1). Each link labelled 0.3 mark.

**Explanation of each link (1 mark each, choose 3 for 3 marks):**

- *Infectious Agent:* HFE intervention — **antimicrobial copper surfaces**; use of UV-C disinfection robots; quantified via log reduction $\geq 3$.
- *Mode of Transmission:* HFE intervention — **negative-pressure ventilation** with $ACH \geq 12$ and $\Delta P \leq -2.5$ Pa; spatial layout ensuring $\geq 1.5$ m separation.
- *Portal of Entry:* HFE intervention — **needleless IV connectors** and **engineered sharps containers** placed within arm's reach (≤ 1 m); training in neutral-hand technique.

**HFE synthesis (2 marks):** Infection control is most effective when *engineered controls* and *behavioural nudges* (e.g., alcohol-rub dispensers at bedside) work in tandem. Designers must align *workflow ergonomics* with *cognitive ergonomics* so that safe behaviour becomes the **default path**, not a deliberate choice.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) ACH Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
V = 4 \times 5 \times 3 = 60 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
ACH = \dfrac{Q}{V} \times 60 = \dfrac{0.15}{60} \times 60
$$

$$
ACH = 0.15 \times 60 = 9 \text{ air changes/hour}
$$

**(ii) Compliance Check (2 marks):**

CDC standard: $ACH \geq 12$ for **new** AIIR, $ACH \geq 6$ for **existing** rooms.
**Result:** $ACH = 9$ → **meets the existing-room standard but NOT the new-room standard.** Justification: the room is borderline; contaminant removal time is longer than ideal.

**(iii) Human-Factors Redesign (2 marks):**
- **Dispenser Placement:** Mount alcohol-rub dispensers at the **bedhead and room exit** (≤ 1.5 m from point of care) — nudges compliance.
- **PPE Station:** Position gown/gloves **outside the room in a visible, well-lit alcove** to reduce donning time (apply Fitts' Law: shorter distance + larger target = lower movement time).
- **Visual Management:** Use **colour-coded floor markings** for the contaminated/clean zone boundary — leverages pre-attentive processing.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **Hierarchy of Controls** as applied to infection prevention. Why is **PPE placed at the bottom** of the hierarchy from a human-factors perspective? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ward has 200 patients; 30 develop a new infection over 2 weeks. Hand-hygiene audits show 450 actual events against 1000 opportunities.
    **(i)** Calculate the **Attack Rate**.
    **(ii)** Calculate the **Hand-Hygiene Compliance (HHC)**.
    **(iii)** Using FMEA, the risk priority number for missed hand hygiene is $RPN = 240$. Suggest **two engineering redesigns** to bring RPN below 100. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Diagram (3 marks):** Five-tier pyramid (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE). Use the Mermaid in Section 4.2 as reference. Each tier 0.5 mark + arrow direction 0.5 mark.

**Explanation of each tier (1 mark each, choose 3 for 3 marks):**
- **Elimination:** Postpone elective surgery during outbreak → removes risk entirely.
- **Substitution:** Replace open suction with closed suction → reduces aerosol generation.
- **Engineering:** Negative-pressure rooms, HEPA filters → physically remove the hazard.
- **Administrative:** Triage protocols, training → change work practices.
- **PPE:** Last-line barrier → depends on human compliance.

**Why PPE is at the bottom (1 mark):** From an **HFE perspective**, PPE is the *least reliable* control because it relies on **continuous human compliance, correct sizing, correct donning/doffing sequence, and tolerance under fatigue**. Failure modes (forgetting, fogging, skin reactions, doffing contamination) are well-documented. Engineering and administrative controls, once designed in, work *passively* without human intervention.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Attack Rate (2 marks):**

$$
AR = \dfrac{30}{200} \times 100 = 15\%
$$

**(ii) Hand-Hygiene Compliance (2 marks):**

$$
HHC = \dfrac{450}{1000} \times 100 = 45\%
$$

This is **below** the WHO target of $\geq 70\%$.

**(iii) Two Engineering Redesigns to Lower RPN (3 marks):**

| Redesign | Lever Affected | Expected Change |
|---|---|---|
| **Bedside alcohol-rub dispensers** (≤ 1.5 m reach) | Lowers **Occurrence (O)** and improves **Detection (D)** | O: 5 → 2; D: 6 → 3 |
| **Automated compliance monitoring** (e-skin / IoT badges) | Improves **Detection (D)** | D: 6 → 2 |

**New RPN estimate:**

$$
RPN_{new} = 8 \times 2 \times 3 = 48 \quad (\text{well below 100}) \;\checkmark
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember 📌

- 🧬 **Chain of Infection** has **6 links**; breaking *any* link halts transmission.
- 🛡️ **Hierarchy of Controls** flows top-down: **Elimination > Substitution > Engineering > Administrative > PPE.**
- 👤 **PPE is the LAST line of defence**, not the first — it is the *least* reliable HFE control.
- 🧼 **Hand hygiene** is the single most effective intervention; **WHO target ≥ 70 % compliance**.
- 🌬️ **Negative-pressure AIIR** requires $ACH \geq 12$ (new) and $\Delta P \leq -2.5$ Pa.
- 🏥 **HEPA filter** removes $\geq 99.97\%$ of particles at **0.3 µm**.
- 📈 **$R_0$** dictates the *threshold* for outbreak control; HIT $= 1 - 1/R_0$.
- ⚖️ **Standard Precautions** apply to *all* patients; **Transmission-Based** are *pathogen-specific*.
- 🧠 **Human Factors** redesigns the *task*, *tool*, and *environment* so that **safe behaviour = default behaviour**.
- 🔁 **FMEA + RPN** quantitatively prioritises the highest-risk failure modes in the IC chain.

> 🎯 **Final KTU Pearl:** *Always* anchor your IC answer in the HFE framework — mention *cognitive ergonomics* (training, decision-making), *physical ergonomics* (PPE fit, dispenser reach), and *organisational ergonomics* (workflow, shift design) to score full marks on application-based questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies - Insulin Infusion Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Case Studies - Insulin Infusion Pump # 🩺 Case Studies — Insulin Infusion Pump ## 1.1 What is an Insulin Infusion Pump? (Formal Definition) > [!NOTE] > **Insulin Pump (KTU Definition):** A small, programmable, battery-powered **ambulatory electromechanical medical device** that delivers precise, continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion (CSII) to diabetic patients, replacing the need for multiple daily injections. It operates in *basal* (continuous micro-doses) and *bolus* (meal-time/ correction) modes, governed by user inputs through a graphical or keypad interface. From a **Human Factors (HF) and Ergonomics** standpoint, the insulin pump is one of the most widely studied **Class II/III active implantable/ambulatory medical devices** because: - It is operated primarily by **lay-users (patients)** in *uncontrolled environments* (home, workplace, travel). - It has **life-critical dosing** where a single keystroke error can lead to **hypoglycemia, diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), coma, or death**. - It integrates **multiple subsystems** — mechanical, electronic, software, alarm, and wireless — each offering a potential **human-error locus**. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The ATM That Saves Your Life" > [!TIP] > **Analogy:** Think of an insulin pump as an **ATM that dispenses life-saving money (insulin) directly into your body**, 24×7, based on buttons you press while half-asleep, distracted, or panicked. If the ATM has a confusing keypad, a dim screen, no "Confirm?" step, or rings a faint alarm while you are driving — the wrong "withdrawal" could be fatal. Now multiply that risk by **3.5 million users worldwide**. That is precisely why HF/UX design of insulin pumps is a board-favourite KTU case study. --- ## 1.3 Why Insulin Pumps are a *Canonical* HF Case Study | Reason | Explanation | |---|---| | **High Cognitive Workload** | Patient must calculate carbs → ratio → sensitivity → active insulin → final bolus | | **Multi-modal Interaction** | Touchscreen + keypad + audible + vibratory alarms + bolus buttons | | **High-Stakes Domain** | Errors cause morbidity / mortality, not just inconvenience | | **Lay-User Operation** | No clinical supervision during routine use | | **Regulatory Scrutiny** | FDA mandates HF/UE validation under **IEC 62366-1** and **FDA HF Guidance 2016** | | **Multiple Recalls** | Hundreds of FDA MAUDE reports cite *usability flaws*, not hardware failure | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** Insulin pump case studies illustrate **device-induced human error**, **alarm fatigue**, **mode confusion**, **poor feedback design**, and **the cost of ignoring HF principles** during product development. --- ## 1.4 Categories of Insulin Pumps Studied in HF Literature 1. **Conventional Pump (Tethered)** — e.g., *Medtronic MiniMed 670G*, *Tandem t:slim X2* 2. **Patch / Tubeless Pump** — e.g., *Insulet Omnipod* 3. **Hybrid Closed-Loop (HCL) / Artificial Pancreas** — auto-adjusts basal based on CGM 4. **Implantable Pump** — surgical intraperitoneal delivery (rare) All four are analysed in HF courses because **each generation has produced different error patterns**. --- ## 1.5 The Three Real-World Cases Examined in KTU > [!NOTE] > **Case 1 — The "Carb Ratio" Override Disaster (2008, Generic Pump)** > A 14-year-old entered `0.5` units instead of `5` units by mistyping the decimal on a small keypad; no confirmation screen appeared; pump delivered 10× overdose → fatal hypoglycemia. > **Case 2 — The "Stop / Silence" Alarm Mistake (Medtronic Paradigm Recall, 2009–2014)** > The *Rewind/Load* and *Menu/Silence* keys were adjacent. Patients in hypoglycemia pressed the wrong key, silenced the **No-Delivery** alarm, and went into DKA. **FDA recall issued.** > **Case 3 — The Omnipod "Failed Pod" Alarm (FDA MAUDE, 2012–2018)** > The *failed pod* alarm was identical in pitch to the *low-battery* and *delivery-confirmed* alarms. Result: **alarm desensitization** and missed occlusion alarms → DKA hospitalisations. # 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Human Factors Framework Applied to the Pump Every HF case study is analysed through **four lenses**: ### Lens A — *The User* (Who?) - **Age range:** 4 years → 90 years - **Cognitive state:** hypoglycemic (confused), asleep, stressed, distracted - **Training:** 2-hour hospital training (often forgotten in 6 months) - **Physical:** neuropathy, retinopathy (poor vision), arthritis (poor dexterity) ### Lens B — *The Task* (What?) 1. Power-on / Self-test 2. Program basal rate 3. Input carbohydrate amount 4. Calculate & deliver bolus 5. Suspend delivery (e.g., during exercise) 6. Respond to alarms ### Lens C — *The Device / UI* (How?) - Display type (LCD vs OLED) - Input modality (keypad, touchscreen) - Feedback channels (visual / auditory / haptic) - Labelling & iconography - Menu depth & terminology ### Lens D — *The Environment* (Where?) - Home, school, office, swimming pool, **low-light bedroom**, **shower (humidity)** --- ## 2.2 Common Human-Error Patterns Identified > [!WARNING] > **Five Killer Error Patterns Found Across All Three Cases:** > 1. **Slip vs Mistake** — *Slip:* pressed wrong adjacent key. *Mistake:* correct key, wrong mental model (e.g., believing pump was suspended when it wasn't). > 2. **Confirmation Absence** — no "Confirm 5.0 U?" screen before a *potentially fatal dose*. > 3. **Alarm Fatigue** — 8+ alarms per day → user silences → misses critical alarm. > 4. **Mode Confusion** — user thinks device is in "Manual" mode but it auto-delivers basal during HCL. > 5. **Decimal / Unit Confusion** — 1.0 U vs 10 U vs 0.1 U displayed identically on a 2-line LCD. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat-Sheet Table > [!IMPORTANT] > **These are the quantitative formulas students must memorise for any HF case study involving dosing devices.** | Symbol | Formula / Rule | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | Bolus Dose $B$ | $B = \dfrac{C}{CR} + \dfrac{(BG - T)}{ISF} - IOB$ | Total meal + correction bolus (units of insulin) | | Carbohydrate Ratio $CR$ | $CR = \dfrac{450}{TDI}$ | grams of carb covered by 1 unit insulin | | Insulin Sensitivity $ISF$ | $ISF = \dfrac{1700}{TDI}$ | mg/dL drop per 1 unit insulin ("1700 rule") | | Insulin-on-Board $IOB$ | $IOB(t) = D \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | Residual active insulin at time $t$ | | Time-in-Range $TIR$ | $TIR = \dfrac{t_{70-180}}{t_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % time glucose 70–180 mg/dL (target $\geq 70\%$) | | Error Probability $P_e$ | $P_e = \dfrac{N_{err}}{N_{op}}$ | Frequency of user errors per operation | | Likelihood Score $RPN$ | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | FMEA risk priority number (Severity × Occurrence × Detection) | | Bolus-Slip Error $E_b$ | $E_b = k \cdot \dfrac{1}{C_{size}}$ | Error inversely proportional to confirm-step size $C_{size}$ | | Reaction Time $RT$ | $RT = a + b \cdot H$ | Hick-Hyman law: $H = \log_2(n)$ choices | | Mean Time-to-Failure $MTTF$ | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Used in alarm reliability studies | > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook:** *"**C**arbs **C**overed **C**alculation"* — `CR = 450 / Total Daily Insulin`. The "450 rule" and "1700 rule" are tested every year. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Formulas - **FDA / IEC 62366-1 use** FMEA ($RPN$) to decide which use errors require *risk control*. - **Pump manufacturers** use *Hick-Hyman RT* to determine optimal menu depth (typically **≤ 3 levels**). - **Clinicians** use $TIR$ to compare usability outcomes between pump brands. - **HF Engineers** use $E_b \propto 1/C_{size}$ to argue for **larger confirmation dialogs** and **numeric entry verification**. --- ## 2.5 Why Each Error Type Matters in the KTU Exam | Error Type | Theoretical Construct | Famous Case | |---|---|---| | Adjacent-key slip | **Norman's Theory of Action** (gulf of execution) | Case 2 — Medtronic Recall | | Mode confusion | **Reason's Swiss-Cheese Model** | Case 3 — Omnipod alarms | | Decimal slip | **Reason's Generic Error Modelling System (GEMS)** | Case 1 — 0.5 vs 5.0 | | Alarm fatigue | **Signal Detection Theory** | Case 3 — Omnipod | | Wrong mental model | **Mental Model Theory (Norman)** | Case 2 — Stop vs Silence | # 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bolus Calculation (The Most-Tested Formula) A patient with **Total Daily Insulin $TDI = 50$ U** wants to eat **$C = 60$ g** of carbs. Current blood glucose $BG = 220$ mg/dL, target $T = 100$ mg/dL, $ISF = 34$, $CR = 9$, and **Insulin-on-Board $IOB = 2.5$ U**. **Step 1 — Bolus Calculation Formula:** $$ B \;=\; \frac{C}{CR} \;+\; \frac{(BG - T)}{ISF} \;-\; IOB $$ **Step 2 — Substitute Values:** $$ B \;=\; \frac{60}{9} \;+\; \frac{(220 - 100)}{34} \;-\; 2.5 $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate Each Term:** $$ \frac{60}{9} \;=\; 6.667 \text{ U (meal bolus)} $$ $$ \frac{(220 - 100)}{34} \;=\; \frac{120}{34} \;=\; 3.529 \text{ U (correction bolus)} $$ **Step 4 — Combine and Subtract IOB:** $$ B \;=\; 6.667 \;+\; 3.529 \;-\; 2.5 $$ $$ B \;=\; 10.196 \;-\; 2.5 \;=\; 7.696 \text{ U} $$ **Step 5 — Round to Pump Granularity (0.05 U):** $$ B_{\text{pump}} \;\approx\; 7.70 \text{ U} $$ > [!NOTE] > **HF Insight:** Now imagine the patient accidentally types `77.0 U` instead of `7.70 U` because the **decimal point is on the same key as zero** — this is exactly the *decimal-confusion slip* of **Case 1**. Without a confirmation step showing `7.70 U ✓`, a `77.0 U` overdose is a **10× fatal error**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — FMEA Risk Priority Number for "No Confirmation Screen" Using **FMEA**, the HF team scores the use error of *missing dose-confirmation*. | Factor | Score (1–10) | Justification | |---|---|---| | **Severity $S$** | 9 | Patient death from overdose | | **Occurrence $O$** | 5 | Happens ~ once / month in general population | | **Detection $D$** | 8 | Pump gives no audible "are you sure?" prompt | $$ RPN \;=\; S \times O \times D $$ $$ RPN \;=\; 9 \times 5 \times 8 \;=\; 360 $$ > [!WARNING] > **RPN $\geq 200$ → Mandatory Risk Control.** Manufacturers must add a *confirmation screen* (lowering $D$ from 8 → 2): $$ RPN_{\text{new}} \;=\; 9 \times 5 \times 2 \;=\; 90 $$ This is precisely how IEC 62366-1 forces *human-factors-driven design changes*. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hick-Hyman Decision Time for Alarm Identification The pump emits **$n = 6$ distinct alarm tones** (No Delivery, Low Battery, Occlusion, Pod Failure, Cartridge Empty, System Error). Time per cognitive step $b = 200$ ms, base $a = 150$ ms. **Hick-Hyman Law:** $$ RT \;=\; a + b \cdot \log_2(n) $$ $$ RT \;=\; 150 + 200 \cdot \log_2(6) $$ $$ \log_2(6) \;=\; \log_{10}(6) / \log_{10}(2) \;=\; 0.778 / 0.301 \;\approx\; 2.585 $$ $$ RT \;=\; 150 + 200 \cdot 2.585 $$ $$ RT \;=\; 150 + 517 \;=\; 667 \text{ ms} $$ > [!TIP] > **HF Interpretation:** If alarms are indistinguishable in pitch (Case 3 — Omnipod), users take **longer to identify** the critical one, increasing **alarm response time** and risk. Designing **uniquely-pitched alarms** reduces the cognitive lookup time and improves $D$ in FMEA. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Insulin-on-Board Decay (GEMS / Pharmacokinetics) Insulin action decays exponentially with rate $\lambda = \ln(2) / t_{1/2}$ where $t_{1/2} = 4$ hours. $$ \lambda \;=\; \frac{\ln(2)}{4} \;=\; 0.1733 \text{ hr}^{-1} $$ For a delivered dose $D = 5$ U at time $t = 3$ hours ago: $$ IOB(3) \;=\; 5 \cdot e^{-0.1733 \cdot 3} $$ $$ IOB(3) \;=\; 5 \cdot e^{-0.5199} $$ $$ IOB(3) \;=\; 5 \cdot 0.5948 \;=\; 2.974 \text{ U} $$ **This is why patients must NOT re-bolus — the pump itself subtracts $IOB$, preventing "insulin stacking".** > [!IMPORTANT] > **HF Insight:** When patients ignore the $IOB$ warning screen, *workload is too high*. The correct HF solution is **visualising the $IOB$ as a shrinking pie-chart on the home screen** so the patient understands *why* a bolus was reduced — supporting *Situational Awareness* (Endsley's 3-level model). --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping the Three Cases to HF Theory | HF Theory | Case 1 (Decimal) | Case 2 (Medtronic) | Case 3 (Omnipod) | |---|---|---|---| | **Norman's Action Cycle** | Gulf of execution | Gulf of execution + evaluation | Gulf of evaluation | | **Reason's Swiss Cheese** | Patient layer failed | UI + alarm layers failed | Alarm + training layers failed | | **GEMS** | Action slip (Rasmussen Skill-Rule-Knowledge) | Rule-based mistake | Knowledge-based mistake | | **Alarm-Fatigue Model** | — | — | Habituation (SDT) | | **FMEA RPN** | 360 | 280 | 420 | | **Cost of Failure** | Patient death | Multi-million $ recall | Class-action lawsuit | | **HF Redesign Fix** | Confirm-screen + big fonts | Tactile separators + unique alarm tones | Differentiated pitches + haptic | # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Bolus Delivery Workflow (with HF Failure Points) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User Decides to Eat Carbs] --> B[Opens Bolus Menu on Pump] B --> C{Decimal Point Visible?} C -- Yes --> D[Enters Carbohydrate Amount] C -- No --> E[Enters WRONG Decimal Value] D --> F[Calculates Recommended Bolus] F --> G{Confirmation Screen Present?} G -- Yes --> H[User Reviews and Confirms] G -- No --> I[Pump Delivers UNCONFIRMED Dose] H --> J[Pump Delivers Confirmed Dose] E --> K[Fatal Overdose or Underdose] I --> K J --> L[IOB Tracking Active] L --> M[Safe Outcome] K --> N[Adverse Event - FDA Report] style A fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064 style K fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style M fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style N fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Reason's Swiss-Cheese Model Applied to Insulin Pump ```mermaid flowchart LR L1[Organisational Layer
No HFE Validation Plan] --> L2 L2[Supervisory Layer
Skipped Summative Usability Test] --> L3 L3[Preconditions Layer
Patient in Hypoglycemia] --> L4 L4[Unsafe Act Layer
Wrong Key Pressed] --> E[Patient Harm] style L1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style L2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style L3 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 style L4 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style E fill:#d32f2f,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Hick-Hyman Alarm Identification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Alarm Sounds] --> Q1{Is the Tone Unique?} Q1 -- Yes --> ID1[User Identifies Alarm in 300 ms] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Check Display Icon} Q2 -- Icon Clear --> ID2[Identified in 600 ms] Q2 -- Icon Ambiguous --> Q3[Guessing - Possible Mistake] Q3 --> Fail[Missed Occlusion - DKA Risk] ID1 --> Safe[Timely Response] ID2 --> Safe style Safe fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style Fail fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Endsley's 3-Level Situational Awareness in Pump Use ```mermaid flowchart TD L1P[Level 1 - Perception
Patient sees ICON and HEARS tone] --> L2P L2P[Level 2 - Comprehension
Patient UNDERSTANDS this is occlusion] --> L3P L3P[Level 3 - Projection
Patient PREDICTS glucose will rise in 30 min] --> Action[Correct Response - Replace Set] L1F[Patient only sees icon, tone silenced] --> L2F L2F[Patient thinks it is low battery] --> L3F L3F[Patient takes no action] --> Fail[DKA Event] style Action fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style Fail fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c ``` # 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"List any three human factors issues commonly observed in insulin pump design."** **Model Answer (3 points):** 1. **Lack of dose confirmation screen** — leading to accidental `10×` decimal overdose errors (Case 1). 2. **Indistinguishable alarm tones** — causing alarm fatigue and missed occlusion events (Case 3). 3. **Adjacent-key confusion** between critical functions such as *Stop* and *Silence* (Case 2 – Medtronic recall). 4. *Bonus point:* Poor display legibility under low light / for elderly users with retinopathy. > [!TIP] > Examiner's Tip: Always cite the **case + the human factor principle** (e.g., "alarm fatigue + signal detection theory") to score full marks. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **"What is alarm fatigue? How does it relate to insulin pump safety?"** **Model Answer (3 points):** 1. **Alarm fatigue** is the *desensitisation* of a user to frequent, non-critical, or repetitive auditory alarms — they begin to ignore, silence, or delay responding to them. 2. In insulin pumps, an average user receives **8–12 non-critical alerts per day** (low reservoir, calibration reminder, CGM warm-up, etc.), which causes the user to **silence critical occlusion alarms** reflexively. 3. **Real-world consequence:** FDA MAUDE database records multiple DKA events because patients silenced an "Occlusion Detected" alarm believing it was the routine low-battery tone. 4. *HF remedy:* Differentiate alarm **pitch, urgency, and haptic pattern**; require **acknowledgement tap** for critical alarms. --- ## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** > *"A leading insulin pump was recalled because users frequently pressed the wrong key when silencing alarms, leading to missed occlusion events and diabetic ketoacidosis.* > *(a) Apply Norman's Theory of Action and Reason's Swiss-Cheese Model to analyse this case. (7 marks)* > *(b) Propose a redesigned interface with at least FOUR human-factors-driven improvements and justify each using FMEA. (7 marks)"* #### ✅ Model Solution **Part (a) — Analysis (7 marks)** **1. Norman's Theory of Action** (3 marks) The user intends to *silence the alarm* (Goal). They form an *intention* (Execute), then specify an *action sequence* (Find the Silence key). The user encounters the **Gulf of Execution** — the Silence and Stop keys are visually and tactilely identical. They press *Stop*, evaluating (Perceptual + Interpretive) that the alarm is silenced — but it was only the *No-Delivery alarm* that was muted, not the actual occlusion alarm. The **Gulf of Evaluation** is also crossed incorrectly because the pump's display icon did not change. **2. Reason's Swiss-Cheese Model** (4 marks) | Layer | Hole in this case | |---|---| | Organisational | HF validation team was not consulted during UI redesign | | Supervision | Summative usability test skipped to meet launch deadline | | Preconditions | Patient in hypoglycemic state (cognitive impairment) | | Unsafe Act | Pressed wrong adjacent key | | Outcome | DKA hospitalisation | The "holes" aligned because **all four layers failed simultaneously** — a classic Swiss-Cheese event. **Part (b) — Redesign Proposal (7 marks)** | # | Redesign | FMEA Justification | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Tactile separator + colour-coded keys** (Stop = Red, Silence = Yellow, Confirm = Green) | Reduces $O$ (occurrence) from 5 → 2 | | 2 | **Mandatory 2-second long-press for Stop** during an active alarm | Increases $D$ (detection difficulty) from 8 → 9 (harder to do by mistake) | | 3 | **Confirmation dialog**: "Are you sure? This will stop ALL insulin delivery" | Lowers $D$ from 8 → 2 | | 4 | **Unique alarm pitches** for each of 6 alarm classes (NIST 700-Hz standard) | Reduces alarm-fatigue + Hick-Hyman RT from 667 ms → 380 ms | | 5 | *Bonus:* **Haptic differentiation** (continuous vs pulse vibration) | Improves recognition in noisy environments | **New RPN Calculation:** $$ RPN_{\text{new}} \;=\; 9 \times 2 \times 2 \;=\; 36 $$ This represents a **90% reduction** in residual risk — well within IEC 62366-1 acceptable limits. --- ### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** > *(a) Explain with a flowchart how a hierarchical task analysis (HTA) would be performed for the task of "delivering a meal-time bolus" using an insulin pump. (7 marks)* > *(b) A patient mis-keys the decimal and enters 0.5 U instead of 5.0 U. Using the bolus formula, show step-by-step how this affects the patient and propose two interface changes that could have prevented this. (7 marks)* #### ✅ Model Solution **Part (a) — HTA Flowchart (7 marks)** **Hierarchical Task Analysis for "Meal-Time Bolus":** ``` 0. Deliver Meal-Time Bolus 1. Open Bolus Menu 1.1 Press MENU key 1.2 Select "BOLUS" 1.3 Select "CARB" or "MANUAL" 2. Enter Carbohydrate Amount 2.1 Type grams using keypad 2.2 Press ENTER 3. Confirm Blood Glucose (optional) 3.1 Enter BG reading or accept CGM value 4. Review Calculated Bolus 4.1 Read recommended dose on screen 4.2 Verify against expected range 5. Confirm and Deliver 5.1 Press CONFIRM 5.2 Listen for delivery click 5.3 Check "DELIVERED" message ``` > **HTA Principles Used:** > - **Decomposition** of high-level goal into sub-tasks > - **Plans** (P1: If BG > 250, take correction bolus first) > - **Allocation** of sub-tasks to *human* (1.1–2.2) vs *device* (4.1, 5.2) **Part (b) — Decimal Error Numerical Analysis (7 marks)** **Given:** Patient intends to deliver $B = 5.0$ U but types $0.5$ U. **Using the bolus formula:** $$ B_{\text{intended}} = \frac{C}{CR} + \frac{(BG - T)}{ISF} - IOB $$ $$ 5.0 \;=\; \frac{C}{CR} + \frac{(BG - T)}{ISF} - IOB $$ If the **decimal is missing** (i.e., $B = 0.5$ U), the pump delivers: $$ B_{\text{actual}} = 0.5 \text{ U (under-dose)} $$ **Result:** $$ \text{Missed Dose} = 5.0 - 0.5 = 4.5 \text{ U} $$ **Clinical Consequence:** - 4.5 U of missing insulin over a 60 g carb meal ≈ **$BG$ rises 120–150 mg/dL within 90 minutes** → **hyperglycemia** → potential DKA if repeated. - Conversely, if the patient types `50` instead of `5.0` → **10× overdose → severe hypoglycemia → seizure / coma**. **Proposed Interface Changes (4 marks):** 1. **Big-font confirmation screen** showing `5.0 U ✓ OK?` with the *decimal point highlighted in red* to prevent decimal misreads. 2. **Pre-defined dose presets** (Small = 2 U, Medium = 5 U, Large = 10 U) selected via a single large button — eliminating decimal entry entirely. 3. *Bonus:* **Sliding scale input** using `+` / `-` buttons instead of typed digits → eliminates decimal errors completely. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks in Insulin Pump Case Studies:** > 1. ❌ **Writing generic HF principles** without *naming the case* (Case 1/2/3). Always cite the specific recall/event. > 2. ❌ **Forgetting FMEA math** — Examiner expects at least one **RPN calculation** in 14-mark answers. > 3. ❌ **Skipping the "Why"** — Don't just list *tactile keys*. Explain *why* they reduce Occurrence (O) in FMEA. > 4. ❌ **Ignoring IEC 62366-1 / FDA HF Guidance** — Citing these standards is a guaranteed 2-mark bonus. > 5. ❌ **No mention of alarm fatigue + Signal Detection Theory** in alarm-related questions — a topper's differentiator. > 6. ❌ **Decimal errors in numerical problems** — Use the formula correctly: $B = C/CR + (BG-T)/ISF - IOB$. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Insulin Pump Case Studies:** - ⭐ **Three landmark cases:** Decimal Overdose (2008) · Medtronic Recall (2009–14) · Omnipod Alarm Fatigue (2012–18). - ⭐ **Five error patterns:** Slip · Mistake · Mode confusion · Alarm fatigue · Decimal/unit confusion. - ⭐ **Master formula:** $B = \dfrac{C}{CR} + \dfrac{(BG-T)}{ISF} - IOB$ - ⭐ **CR = 450/TDI ; ISF = 1700/TDI ; IOB = D·e^(-λt)** — these are board favourites. - ⭐ **FMEA:** $RPN = S \times O \times D$ ; $RPN \geq 200$ → mandatory risk control. - ⭐ **Hick-Hyman:** $RT = a + b \log_2(n)$ — used to justify alarm-tone differentiation. - ⭐ **Swiss-Cheese Model** has *four layers*: Organisational → Supervision → Preconditions → Unsafe Act. - ⭐ **Gulf of Execution vs Gulf of Evaluation** — Norman's theory is *always* asked. - ⭐ **Standards:** IEC 62366-1 (Usability Engineering) + FDA HF Guidance 2016. - ⭐ **HF Redesign Fixes:** Confirmation screens, tactile separators, unique alarm pitches, haptic feedback, large fonts, decimal highlighting, pre-set dose buttons. - ⭐ **One-liner for exam:** *"An insulin pump is the perfect case study because the user is stressed, the task is cognitive, the device is critical, and the environment is uncontrolled — every layer of Reason's Swiss Cheese can fail."*

topics.title: Special Environments: Mobile Medical Device Design Principles - Weight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Special Environments: Mobile Medical Device Design Principles - Weight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mobile Medical Device Weight (Human Factors Definition):**
> In the context of human factors engineering and design, *weight* refers to the total gravitational load (expressed in Newtons or kilogram-force) imposed by a portable/mobile medical device on the operator's musculoskeletal system during carrying, lifting, transporting, or sustained handling operations. It is a critical ergonomic variable that directly governs user safety, fatigue accumulation, postural stability, and the overall acceptability of the device in clinical environments.

The KTU 2024 Scheme course descriptor (PEBMT742) treats *weight* as a primary *anthropometric-physiological constraint* governing the design of mobile medical equipment such as portable ventilators, handheld ultrasound scanners, mobile X-ray units, defibrillators (AEDs), ambulatory infusion pumps, and wearable patient monitoring systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Heavy School Bag vs. The Smart Backpack"**
> Imagine two students walking 2 km to school daily. Student A carries a **9 kg** leather satchel slung over one shoulder. After a week, he develops shoulder pain, back spasms, and a tilted gait. Student B carries the same books in a **7 kg** ergonomic backpack with padded straps, hip belt, and load distributed evenly across the back. He feels no strain. The *weight was the same*, but the *human-factors design of weight handling* was completely different. Mobile medical devices follow exactly the same principle: **it is not just how heavy a device is, but how that weight is carried, gripped, balanced, and held that determines whether a clinician can use it safely for 12 hours straight.**

### 1.3 Why Weight is a Special-Environment Issue

Mobile medical devices operate in *special environments* — ambulances (vibrating, tilted, moving), disaster zones (uneven terrain), ICUs (continuous repositioning), home care (elderly caregivers), and battlefield medevac (extreme conditions). Each environment multiplies the cost of poor weight design.

### 1.4 Governing International Standards & Constants

- **NIOSH Lifting Equation (1991, Revised 2007)** — defines Recommended Weight Limit (RWL).
- **ISO 11226:2000** — Assessment of static working postures.
- **ISO 11228-1:2003** — Manual lifting and carrying.
- **MIL-STD-1472G** — Military human factors (carrying loads up to 30 kg).
- **IEC 60601-1-9** — Environmental design for medical electrical equipment.
- Key anthropometric constant: **5th percentile female static strength (one-handed carry) ≈ 9–11 kg**.
- Key anthropometric constant: **95th percentile male lift strength ≈ 25 kg** (close to body, two-handed).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Weight is treated as a *cross-cutting ergonomic variable* affecting *Usability (CO1), Safety (CO2), and Accessibility (CO3)* outcomes of mobile medical devices.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Theory — Why Weight Matters Step-by-Step

The human factors impact of device weight is decomposed into the following structured logic chain:

1. **Force Transmission:** Any carried mass $m$ exerts gravitational force $F_g = m \cdot g$ on the operator's musculoskeletal frame.
2. **Moment Arm Generation:** When the device is held *away* from the body's center of gravity (CoG), a torque $\tau$ is created at the shoulder, elbow, or lumbar spine: $\tau = F_g \cdot d$, where $d$ is the horizontal distance from the joint axis.
3. **Muscular Recruitment:** To counteract this torque, specific muscle groups (deltoid, trapezius, erector spinae) must generate counter-forces. Greater torque → greater muscle activation → earlier fatigue.
4. **Fatigue Accumulation:** Sustained static muscle loading above **15–20% Maximum Voluntary Contraction (MVC)** leads to intramuscular pressure occlusion of blood vessels, anaerobic metabolism, and rapid fatigue onset.
5. **Postural Compensation:** Operators subconsciously alter posture (lean, twist, hunch) to reduce torque, often causing Musculoskeletal Disorders (MSDs).
6. **Task Interruption:** Heavy/unbalanced devices cause micro-pauses, dropping risk, and reduced procedural accuracy.

### 2.2 Categories of Weight in Mobile Medical Device Design

| Weight Category | Typical Range | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Handheld** | < 2 kg | Pulse oximeter, handheld Doppler |
| **Portable (one-hand carry)** | 2 – 7 kg | Portable ultrasound, AED |
| **Mobile (two-hand cart)** | 7 – 30 kg | Portable ventilator, defibrillator on trolley |
| **Transportable** | 30 – 100 kg | Mobile X-ray, mobile C-arm |
| **Wheeled Stationary-mobile** | > 100 kg | CT scanner, MRI |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` (not `\vert` typed as `|`) inside any LaTeX math within table cells to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variable Meaning | Units | KTU Exam Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gravitational Force | $F_g = m \cdot g$ | $m$ = mass, $g = 9.81\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$ | Newtons (N) | Compute device load on operator |
| 2 | Joint Moment / Torque | $\tau = F_g \cdot d \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | $d$ = horizontal distance to joint, $\theta$ = trunk angle | N·m | Determine spinal loading |
| 3 | NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit | $RWL = LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM$ | $LC = 23\ \mathrm{kg}$, multipliers dimensionless | kg | Compute safe one-person lift |
| 4 | Lifting Index | $LI = \dfrac{\text{Actual Load}}{RWL}$ | $LI > 1.0 \Rightarrow$ hazardous | unitless | Risk classification |
| 5 | Static Load Endurance Limit | $T_{\max} = \dfrac{60}{F_{\%MVC} - 15}$ (Rohmert's curve) | $F_{\%MVC}$ = % of Max Voluntary Contraction | minutes | Predict fatigue time |
| 6 | Center of Gravity Offset Effect | $\tau_{eff} = m \cdot g \cdot \Delta x_{CoG}$ | $\Delta x_{CoG}$ = offset from neutral axis | N·m | Predict torque from imbalance |
| 7 | Snook–Ciriello Workload Guide | $\%pop\ capable = f(W,\ F,\ H,\ V,\ A)$ | Population-based capacity tables | % | Design acceptance % |
| 8 | Energy Expenditure (carrying) | $EE \approx 1.5 \cdot W_{body} + 2 \cdot W_{load}$ (walking) | $W_{body}$ = body weight, $W_{load}$ = load | W | Sustained carry limit |
| 9 | Acceptable Carry Duration | $t_{carry} \leq \dfrac{12}{k \cdot m}$ (rule of thumb) | $k$ = task difficulty factor | minutes | Mobile equipment field use |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Weight design principles are critical in:
- **Ambulance defibrillators** (must be carried up stairs by a single paramedic within seconds).
- **Portable ventilators for disaster response** (operated by nurses wearing PPE for hours).
- **Military medevac equipment** (loaded onto helicopters with extreme vibration).
- **Home-care oxygen concentrators on carts** (pushed by elderly relatives).
- **Wearable insulin pumps and patient monitors** (worn 24/7 against the skin).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state weight in **both kg AND Newtons** in design calculations; engineers must convert via $g = 9.81\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Joint Torque from a Handheld Medical Device

**Problem Setup:** A clinician holds a portable ultrasound probe of mass $m = 1.5\ \mathrm{kg}$ with the arm extended horizontally by $d = 35\ \mathrm{cm}$ from the shoulder joint. Compute the torque at the shoulder for $\theta = 0^\circ$ (horizontal) and $\theta = 30^\circ$ (arm angled downward).

**Step 1 — Identify Forces**

The device exerts a downward force equal to its weight.

$$
F_g = m \cdot g
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Known Values**

$$
F_g = 1.5\ \mathrm{kg} \times 9.81\ \mathrm{m/s^2}
$$

$$
F_g = 14.715\ \mathrm{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Moment Arm for Horizontal Hold ($\theta = 0^\circ$)**

When the arm is horizontal, the entire force vector is perpendicular to the moment arm, so $\cos(0^\circ) = 1$.

$$
\tau_1 = F_g \cdot d \cdot \cos(\theta)
$$

$$
\tau_1 = 14.715\ \mathrm{N} \times 0.35\ \mathrm{m} \times 1.0
$$

$$
\tau_1 = 5.15\ \mathrm{N \cdot m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Moment Arm for Tilted Hold ($\theta = 30^\circ$)**

When the arm tilts downward at $30^\circ$, the perpendicular component of force on the shoulder is reduced by $\cos(30^\circ)$.

$$
\tau_2 = 14.715\ \mathrm{N} \times 0.35\ \mathrm{m} \times \cos(30^\circ)
$$

$$
\tau_2 = 14.715 \times 0.35 \times 0.8660
$$

$$
\tau_2 = 4.46\ \mathrm{N \cdot m}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the Result**

The shoulder torque is reduced from $5.15\ \mathrm{N \cdot m}$ to $4.46\ \mathrm{N \cdot m}$, a **13.4% reduction** simply by tilting the arm. This is a foundational human-factors design rule: **devices should be operable close to the body's sagittal plane** to minimize joint torque.

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) for Mobile Device Lift

**Problem Setup:** A nurse must lift a portable defibrillator ($m = 9.0\ \mathrm{kg}$) from the floor to a cart at waist height. Horizontal distance = 25 cm, vertical lift = 50 cm, asymmetric angle = $30^\circ$, single lift, good coupling.

**Step 1 — Load Constant (LC)**

The NIOSH LC for adults is fixed.

$$
LC = 23\ \mathrm{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Horizontal Multiplier (HM)**

$$
HM = \dfrac{25}{H}\ \text{ where } H \text{ is in cm, clamped } 25 \le H \le 63
$$

$$
HM = \dfrac{25}{25} = 1.00
$$

**Step 3 — Vertical Multiplier (VM)**

$$
VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert
$$

For $V = 50\ \mathrm{cm}$ (lift ends at 50 cm above floor — midway between floor and 75 cm optimum):

$$
VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 50 - 75 \vert
$$

$$
VM = 1 - 0.003 \times 25
$$

$$
VM = 1 - 0.075
$$

$$
VM = 0.925
$$

**Step 4 — Distance Multiplier (DM)**

$$
DM = 0.82 + \dfrac{4.5}{D}\ \text{, clamped } D \ge 25\ \mathrm{cm}
$$

For $D = 50\ \mathrm{cm}$ (lift travel = 50 cm):

$$
DM = 0.82 + \dfrac{4.5}{50}
$$

$$
DM = 0.82 + 0.09
$$

$$
DM = 0.91
$$

**Step 5 — Asymmetric Multiplier (AM)**

$$
AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A
$$

For $A = 30^\circ$:

$$
AM = 1 - 0.0032 \times 30
$$

$$
AM = 1 - 0.096
$$

$$
AM = 0.904
$$

**Step 6 — Frequency Multiplier (FM)**

For a single lift, $F \le 0.2$ lifts/min, $V < 75\ \mathrm{cm}$: $FM = 1.00$.

$$
FM = 1.00
$$

**Step 7 — Coupling Multiplier (CM)**

Good hand-grip coupling: $CM = 1.00$.

$$
CM = 1.00
$$

**Step 8 — Compute RWL**

$$
RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM
$$

$$
RWL = 23 \times 1.00 \times 0.925 \times 0.91 \times 0.904 \times 1.00 \times 1.00
$$

$$
RWL = 23 \times 0.7617
$$

$$
RWL \approx 17.52\ \mathrm{kg}
$$

**Step 9 — Compute Lifting Index (LI)**

$$
LI = \dfrac{\text{Actual Load}}{RWL}
$$

$$
LI = \dfrac{9.0}{17.52}
$$

$$
LI \approx 0.51
$$

**Step 10 — Interpretation Matrix**

| LI Range | Risk Level | Design Action |
|---|---|---|
| $LI < 1.0$ | Acceptable | Device safe to lift as configured |
| $1.0 \le LI < 2.0$ | Marginal | Redesign grips or reduce weight |
| $LI \ge 2.0$ | Hazardous | Mandatory redesign or two-person lift |

Our $LI = 0.51 < 1.0$ → **Acceptable**, but design should still target reducing horizontal reach for prolonged tasks.

---

### 3.3 Derivation C — Fatigue Time Using Rohmert's Curve

**Problem Setup:** A handheld infusion pump trigger is held at $25\%$ MVC. Predict endurance time.

**Step 1 — Apply Rohmert's Equation**

$$
T_{\max} = \dfrac{60}{F_{\%MVC} - 15}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$
T_{\max} = \dfrac{60}{25 - 15}
$$

$$
T_{\max} = \dfrac{60}{10}
$$

$$
T_{\max} = 6\ \mathrm{minutes}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation**

Beyond 6 minutes, the operator cannot sustain the contraction; device redesign (e.g., trigger lock, voice activation) is mandatory if task exceeds this duration.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Weight Design Decisions

| Design Decision | Quantitative Basis | Acceptable Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| One-hand carry mass | 5th percentile female grip + arm strength | $\le 4\ \mathrm{kg}$ (continuous), $\le 7\ \mathrm{kg}$ (intermittent) |
| Two-hand lift | NIOSH LI | $LI \le 1.0$ |
| Wheeled cart push force | ANSI/HFES 100 push force (initial) | $\le 23\ \mathrm{kgf}$ (75th percentile female) |
| Backpack / harness load | MIL-STD-1472G | $\le 30\%$ body weight |
| Wearable mass | ISO/TR 7250 | $\le 2\ \mathrm{kg}$ for limb-mounted |
| Vibration-compensated mass | ISO 2631 | Effective load $\times$ vibration factor |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Weight Design Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify User Population] --> B[Select Strength Percentile]
    B --> C{Weight Threshold}
    C -- Less than 4 kg --> D[One Handed Carry OK]
    C -- 4 to 9 kg --> E[Two Handed Carry]
    C -- 9 to 30 kg --> F[Cart or Harness]
    C -- Greater than 30 kg --> G[Mechanical Aid Required]
    D --> H[Evaluate Moment Arm]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute Joint Torque]
    I --> J{Torque within MVC limits}
    J -- Yes --> K[Design Acceptable]
    J -- No --> L[Redesign Grip or Balance CoG]
    L --> H
    K --> M[Field Validation with Target Users]
    M --> N[Final Weight Design Locked]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Weight-Related Design Influences on a Mobile Medical Device

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[User and Task Inputs]
        U1[User Strength Data]
        U2[Task Duration]
        U3[Environment Terrain]
        U4[Posture Requirements]
    end

    subgraph DEVICE[Device Weight System]
        D1[Gross Mass]
        D2[Center of Gravity]
        D3[Grip and Handle Geometry]
        D4[Distribution of Mass]
        D5[Wheel and Cart System]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Human Factors Outcomes]
        O1[Joint Torque]
        O2[Fatigue Time]
        O3[Drop Risk]
        O4[MSD Probability]
        O5[Task Completion Rate]
    end

    U1 --> D1
    U2 --> D1
    U3 --> D5
    U4 --> D2
    D1 --> O1
    D2 --> O1
    D3 --> O1
    D4 --> O1
    D1 --> O2
    D4 --> O2
    D5 --> O3
    O1 --> O4
    O2 --> O4
    O3 --> O5
    O4 --> O5
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Fatigue Interaction

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as Operator
    participant Dev as Mobile Device
    participant MS as Musculoskeletal System
    Op->>Dev: Lift device mass m
    Dev->>MS: Apply Fg = m times g
    MS->>Op: Generate counter-torque
    loop Sustained Hold
        MS->>MS: MVC percentage rises
        MS->>Op: Blood flow occludes
    end
    Op->>Dev: Release or shift posture
    Note over Op,Dev: Trigger design change
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define the term "weight" in the context of mobile medical device design from a human factors perspective. List two international standards that govern weight-based design limits.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Weight, in human factors engineering of mobile medical devices, is the total gravitational load (in Newtons or kilogram-force) imposed by the device on the operator's musculoskeletal system during handling, lifting, carrying, or sustained holding, and which directly determines user fatigue, postural stress, and safety.

Two governing standards (any two): **(1 mark each)**

1. **NIOSH Lifting Equation (1991, revised 2007)** — sets the Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) of 23 kg under ideal conditions.
2. **ISO 11228-1:2003** — manual lifting and carrying limits.
3. **MIL-STD-1472G** — military human engineering carrying-load limits.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*State Rohmert's equation for static endurance and explain its significance in mobile medical device weight design.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Rohmert's curve predicts the maximum time a muscle can sustain a static contraction:

$$
T_{\max} = \dfrac{60}{F_{\%MVC} - 15}
$$

where $F_{\%MVC}$ is the percentage of Maximum Voluntary Contraction.

**Significance (2 marks):**
- It quantifies how long a clinician can safely hold or carry a device without fatigue-induced errors.
- If holding a device exceeds **20% MVC**, fatigue onset is rapid (a few minutes), forcing the designer to reduce weight, add grips, provide shoulder straps, or implement mechanical support.
- It links weight design directly to **task duration** and **MSD prevention**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model – Module 2 Choice 1]**

**(a)** *Explain, with a neat sketch, the NIOSH Lifting Equation. Compute the Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) and Lifting Index (LI) for a portable patient monitor of mass 11 kg being lifted from the floor to a bedside table. The lift origin is at 25 cm horizontal, 0 cm vertical; the lift destination is at 30 cm horizontal, 75 cm vertical; asymmetric angle = 0°; lift frequency < 0.2 lifts/min; coupling is poor (CM = 0.95).* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Discuss how the centre of gravity of a mobile medical device influences operator torque and fatigue. Propose at least three design strategies to mitigate CoG-related ergonomic hazards in devices weighing between 5 kg and 15 kg.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

Step 1 — Identify load constant:
$LC = 23\ \mathrm{kg}$ — **(1 mark)**

Step 2 — Horizontal multiplier ($H$ = 30 cm, clamped to $\ge 25$):
$HM = 25/30 = 0.833$ — **(1 mark)**

Step 3 — Vertical multiplier ($V = 75$ cm — optimum, so no penalty):
$VM = 1.0$ — **(0.5 mark)**

Step 4 — Distance multiplier ($D = 75 - 0 = 75$ cm):
$DM = 0.82 + 4.5/75 = 0.82 + 0.06 = 0.88$ — **(1 mark)**

Step 5 — Asymmetric multiplier ($A = 0^\circ$):
$AM = 1.0$ — **(0.5 mark)**

Step 6 — Frequency multiplier ($F < 0.2$ lifts/min, $V \ge 75$):
$FM = 1.0$ — **(0.5 mark)**

Step 7 — Coupling multiplier (poor coupling):
$CM = 0.95$ — **(0.5 mark)**

Step 8 — Compute RWL:
$RWL = 23 \times 0.833 \times 1.0 \times 0.88 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.95 = 16.0\ \mathrm{kg}$ — **(1 mark)**

Step 9 — Compute Lifting Index:
$LI = 11/16.0 = 0.69 < 1.0$ → **Acceptable lift.** — **(1 mark)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

Centre of Gravity (CoG) influence (4 marks):
- A CoG offset from the natural carrying axis produces a torque $\tau = m \cdot g \cdot \Delta x_{CoG}$ at the shoulder, lumbar spine, or wrist.
- Greater offset → greater muscle recruitment → earlier fatigue per Rohmert's curve.
- Asymmetric CoG causes postural sway, twisting, and unilateral loading → chronic MSDs.
- CoG offset also increases **drop probability** during ambulation or stair-climbing.

Design strategies (3 marks — any three):

1. **Centralize mass:** Place the heaviest internal components (battery, transformer, motor) directly below or behind the primary grip.
2. **Modular battery placement:** Allow the user to slide battery packs to rebalance CoG based on grip style.
3. **Multi-handle design:** Provide top, side, and bottom handles so the user can always choose a CoG-aligned grip.
4. **Symmetric dual-handle bar:** For devices 5–15 kg, force two-handed lift with handles placed at the actual CoG.
5. **Tilt-and-roll handles:** Allow CoG adjustment during transport on uneven terrain.

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model – Module 2 Choice 2]**

**(a)** *For a handheld pulse oximeter of mass 0.3 kg held 20 cm from the wrist, calculate the torque at the wrist joint for horizontal and 45°-down holds. Comment on the fatigue implications using Rohmert's curve.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Compare and contrast the human-factors design considerations for (i) handheld devices (< 2 kg), (ii) portable one-hand-carry devices (2–7 kg), and (iii) wheeled mobile devices (7–30 kg). Provide one example medical device for each category.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

Step 1 — Compute force:
$F_g = m \cdot g = 0.3 \times 9.81 = 2.943\ \mathrm{N}$ — **(1 mark)**

Step 2 — Horizontal hold ($\theta = 0^\circ$):
$\tau_1 = 2.943 \times 0.20 \times \cos(0^\circ) = 0.589\ \mathrm{N \cdot m}$ — **(2 marks)**

Step 3 — 45° downward hold:
$\tau_2 = 2.943 \times 0.20 \times \cos(45^\circ) = 2.943 \times 0.20 \times 0.7071 = 0.416\ \mathrm{N \cdot m}$ — **(2 marks)**

Step 4 — Fatigue implication:
Lower torque at $45^\circ$ means lower $\%MVC$, hence longer endurance. Using Rohmert: if $\tau_2$ corresponds to $10\%MVC$, then $T_{\max} = 60/(10-15)$ → undefined (no fatigue); if $15\%MVC$, $T_{\max} \to \infty$ sustained. Designers prefer tilted grips to maximize endurance. — **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

| Category | Mass | Key HF Concerns | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Handheld | < 2 kg | Pinch grip, trigger force, single-hand balance | Pulse oximeter |
| (ii) Portable one-hand | 2–7 kg | Power grip, wrist posture, fatigue during prolonged use | Portable ultrasound |
