| (iii) Wheeled mobile | 7–30 kg | Push force, cart stability, threshold negotiation | Portable ventilator on trolley |

*(Distribute 7 marks across categories, comparison depth, and example accuracy.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Weight Design Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kg to N** ($F = mg$). Always show the multiplication by $g = 9.81$.
> 2. **Using bare pipe `\vert`** inside markdown tables — this breaks the table. Always use `\vert` in LaTeX.
> 3. **Omitting $\cos\theta$** in torque calculations when the arm is tilted. Examiners deduct full marks for this.
> 4. **Stating only "reduce the weight"** in design answers. KTU expects *quantitative* strategies: torque reduction, CoG alignment, grip redesign, frequency reduction, mechanical aids.
> 5. **Ignoring population strength percentiles** — always specify whether design targets 5th percentile female, 50th percentile, or 95th percentile male.
> 6. **Confusing *mass* and *weight*** — engineers must use Newtons; clinicians often use kg. State both.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Weight = gravitational load on the user**, not just device mass on a spec sheet.
- ✅ Always convert to **Newtons** using $F_g = m \cdot g$, $g = 9.81\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$.
- ✅ **NIOSH LC = 23 kg** is the baseline; apply 6 multipliers for real conditions.
- ✅ **Lifting Index $LI < 1.0$** is the safety acceptance criterion.
- ✅ **Joint torque** $\tau = F_g \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta$ depends on horizontal reach and trunk angle.
- ✅ **Rohmert's curve** predicts fatigue — keep $\%MVC \le 15\%$ for indefinite holding.
- ✅ Design for the **5th percentile female** strength, not average users.
- ✅ **Center of Gravity alignment** is as important as raw mass.
- ✅ Apply **ANSI/HFES 100** for cart-push forces ($\le 23$ kgf initial).
- ✅ Mobile device weight categories: handheld, portable, mobile, transportable, wheeled-stationary.
- ✅ Use **multipliers**, **population strength tables**, and **fatigue curves** — never rules of thumb alone.
- ✅ For every design solution, cite a **standard (NIOSH, ISO, MIL-STD, IEC 60601-1-9)** to earn full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moving Parts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Moving Parts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Human Factors in Engineering and Design**, *Moving Parts* refers to the systematic study of human interaction with all mechanical components, sub-assemblies, and control interfaces that possess relative motion (rotary, linear, reciprocating, or combined) within a man-machine system. This sub-domain of ergonomics integrates **anthropometry**, **biomechanics**, **cognitive ergonomics**, and **occupational safety engineering** to ensure that human operators can effectively, comfortably, and safely initiate, control, monitor, and halt the motion of machine elements.

According to the **ISO 12100:2010** framework (Safety of Machinery — General Principles for Design — Risk Assessment and Risk Reduction) and aligned with **KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcome CO2** (Analyse human-machine interaction in dynamic systems), moving parts are classified as primary **mechanical hazards** that require engineered control measures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 2 Focus:**
> Moving Parts bridges the gap between *static anthropometry* (Module 1) and *dynamic workplace design* (Module 3). Students must master both the **design of moving controls** (knobs, levers, wheels, pedals) and the **defensive design against hazardous motion** (guarding, interlocks, emergency stops).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine driving a **manual transmission car**. Your hands operate the **gear lever (a moving control)**, your feet actuate the **clutch and brake pedals (reciprocating moving parts)**, and your eyes monitor the **speedometer and tachometer (sensing moving outputs)**. Meanwhile, the engine's **crankshaft, camshafts, and connecting rods** are high-energy moving parts that are completely inaccessible to you — protected by **guards, shrouds, and interlocks**.

In this everyday activity:
- The driver is **consciously interacting** with low-energy moving parts (controls).
- The driver is **passively protected** from high-energy moving parts (engine internals).
- The driver's body dimensions, strength, reaction time, and perception are all critical to the design.

**This dual role — operator of friendly motion and protector from hostile motion — is the essence of "Moving Parts" in human factors engineering.**

## 1.3 Classification of Moving Parts (Foundational Taxonomy)

Moving parts are categorized along three orthogonal axes in the KTU 2024 framework:

| Classification Axis | Categories | Engineering Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Type of Motion** | Rotary, Linear, Reciprocating, Oscillating, Combined | Lathe spindle, hydraulic ram, piston, windshield wiper, robot arm |
| **Energy Domain** | Manual (human-powered), Powered (motor/actuator), Hybrid | Hand crank, electric motor, power steering |
| **Hazard Potential** | Friendly (intended interaction), Hostile (guarded), Neutral | Steering wheel, flywheel, mounting bracket |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics** used in KTU board examinations:
> - **Reaction Time (RT) ≈ 250 ms** for simple auditory/visual stimuli (Hick's Law baseline)
> - **Maximum Voluntary Grip Force ≈ 400 N** (95th percentile male, dominant hand)
> - **Maximum Push/Pull Force ≈ 230 N (push), 180 N (pull)** for seated operators
> - **Standard Emergency Stop Response Time ≤ 0.5 s** (per ISO 13850)
> - **Minimum Safety Distance** calculated using **ANSI B11.19** formula
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Four Pillars of Moving Parts Design

The ergonomic design of systems involving moving parts rests upon four interlocking pillars. Each pillar addresses a specific failure mode when human meets machine.

### Pillar 1 — Anthropometric Compatibility (Static & Dynamic)

Every moving control must be operable by the **5th percentile female** to the **95th percentile male** of the target user population (per **NIOSH Anthropometric Manual** and **DIN 33402**).

- **Static (Structural) Anthropometry** defines the *envelope* of body dimensions: standing height, sitting height, arm reach, grip span, leg length.
- **Dynamic (Functional) Anthropometry** defines the *motion envelope*: reach while seated with back supported, reach while standing on tiptoes, maximum grip aperture, functional legroom.

The **clearance** around any moving part must accommodate the dynamic body envelope, not the static one. A worker reaching across a conveyor belt does not occupy their static shoulder width — they extend, rotate, and lean, enlarging their effective swept volume.

### Pillar 2 — Biomechanical Load Compatibility

Human actuation of moving parts generates **forces, torques, and moments** that must remain within safe physiological limits. Key biomechanical thresholds:

- **Recommended Maximum Sustained Force (RMSN) per NIOSH:**
  - Two-handed push (horizontal): **230 N**
  - Two-handed pull (horizontal): **180 N**
  - One-handed push: **120 N**
  - One-handed pull: **100 N**
- **Maximum Torque on a Hand Wheel:** **~60 N·m** for continuous operation; **~200 N·m** for emergency/cranking.
- **Maximum Foot Pedal Force (sustained):** **~320 N**; emergency brake pedal: **~680 N**.
- **Joint Angle Constraints:** Wrist deviation should not exceed **15°** from neutral; shoulder flexion should not exceed **120°** during repetitive operation.

### Pillar 3 — Cognitive & Perceptual Compatibility

The operator must perceive, interpret, decide, and act upon the state of moving parts within a time window compatible with human reaction time.

- **Hick-Hyman Law:** Reaction time increases logarithmically with the number of stimulus-response alternatives.
- **Fitts' Law:** Movement time to acquire a target scales with the log of distance-to-target-size ratio.
- **Recognition-Reaction Time** (per AASHTO and ISO 2631): driver perception-brake-reaction time = **2.5 s** (95th percentile, expected).
- **Three-Stage Information Processing Model:** Stimulus → Cognition → Response.

### Pillar 4 — Safety Integrity (Defensive Design)

The hierarchy of controls (per **ISO 12100:2010** and **ANSI B11.0**) applied to moving parts:

1. **Inherent Safe Design** — eliminate the hazard at source (e.g., reduce power, substitute safer motion).
2. **Safeguarding** — fixed guards, interlocked guards, adjustable guards, two-hand controls.
3. **Complementary Protective Measures** — emergency stop devices, presence-sensing mats, light curtains.
4. **Information for Use** — warning signs, training, procedures (lowest priority).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark-Worthy Concept:** The **stopping performance** of a machine is determined by three sequential latencies: **Human Reaction Time (t₁)** + **Machine Hydraulic/Mechanical Lag (t₂)** + **Brake Engagement Time (t₃)**. Designers must guarantee that the *combined stopping distance* is less than the *minimum operator-to-hazard distance*, otherwise guarding alone cannot prevent injury.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Definition | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_s = v \cdot (t_h + t_m) + C$ | $D_s$ = min. safety distance; $v$ = approach velocity; $t_h$ = human reaction time; $t_m$ = machine stopping time; $C$ = intrusion depth | m, m/s, s, m | ANSI B11.19 / EN ISO 13855 safety mat calculation |
| 2 | $T = F \cdot r$ | Torque from force on a wheel/lever | N·m | Hand-wheel and crank design |
| 3 | $R = \rho \cdot L \cdot A$ | Linear resistance: $\rho$ = coefficient; $L$ = length; $A$ = area | N | Hydraulic/pneumatic cylinder friction |
| 4 | $\text{MT} = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | Hick-Hyman: MT = movement time, $n$ = choices | s, bits | Reaction-time-based control layout |
| 5 | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/S + 1)$ | Fitts' Law: $D$ = distance, $S$ = target size | s, bits | Control knob/button spacing design |
| 6 | $a_c = v^2 / (2 \cdot d)$ | Deceleration to stop within distance $d$ | m/s² | Emergency stop kinematics |
| 7 | $P = F \cdot v$ | Mechanical power transferred to/from operator | W | Hand tool ergonomics |
| 8 | $\eta = W_{out} / W_{in}$ | Ergonomic efficiency ratio | dimensionless | Operator energy expenditure |
| 9 | $L_{95} = \bar{L} + 1.645 \cdot \sigma$ | 95th percentile dimension | m, mm | Anthropometric upper bound design |
| 10 | $L_{05} = \bar{L} - 1.645 \cdot \sigma$ | 5th percentile dimension | m, mm | Anthropometric lower bound design |
| 11 | $v_{max} = \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot \mu \cdot s}$ | Max safe approach speed (friction-limited) | m/s | Pedestrian-machinery separation |
| 12 | $t_{brake} = J \cdot \omega / \tau_{brake}$ | Brake stopping time: $J$ = inertia, $\omega$ = angular velocity, $\tau$ = brake torque | s | Flywheel / rotating mass stopping |

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Consistency Reminder:** In KTU board papers, failure to convert mm to m (or vice versa) in the safety distance formula is the single most common source of lost marks. Always state units explicitly.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The moving-parts framework is applied universally across:

- **Automotive Engineering:** Steering geometry, pedal placement, gear-shift ergonomics, airbag deployment timing.
- **Industrial Robotics:** Collaborative robot (cobot) speed-and-separation monitoring per **ISO/TS 15066**, with **biomechanical limits** of **150 N quasi-static contact force** and **112 N/s dynamic force**.
- **Aerospace:** Cockpit control yokes and throttle quadrants must withstand **±400 N** stick force with **30–60 Hz** natural frequency.
- **Medical Devices:** Surgical robot master controllers, MRI-compatible non-ferrous moving parts.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Haptic feedback in touchscreens, force-displacement curves in keyboards.

The unifying principle: **human limits, not machine limits, define the design envelope.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Safety Distance for a Movable Guard with Presence-Sensing Device

The **ANSI B11.19 / EN ISO 13855** safety distance formula is the single most important derivation in Module 2. We derive it from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A robotic cell is fitted with a **light curtain** (electro-sensitive protective device, ESPE). An operator can approach the hazard. We must determine the **minimum horizontal distance** $D_s$ that the light curtain must be placed from the hazard zone, so that the operator's hand is detected **before** the machine can cause injury.

### 3.1.2 Variables and Symbols

- $D_s$ = minimum safety distance (mm)
- $K$ = approach speed constant of the operator's hand (typically **1600 mm/s** for upper limbs in industrial settings, per ISO 13855)
- $T$ = total stopping time = $(t_1 + t_2)$ where $t_1$ = reaction time of the safety circuit, $t_2$ = machine stopping time
- $C$ = intrusion distance — how far the operator's hand can penetrate past the detection plane toward the hazard before the machine actually halts (default **C = 850 mm** for non-reach-through scenarios)

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the physical constraint.**
The operator's hand can travel no further than the safety distance in the time available before the machine stops. Express this as the kinematic inequality:

$$
D_s \;\geq\; \text{(distance hand travels during machine stop)} + \text{(intrusion allowance)}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the distance traveled by the hand.**
Assuming the hand approaches at a constant velocity $K$, the distance covered during the stopping interval $T$ is simply:

$$
d_{hand} = K \cdot T
$$

**Step 3 — Express the machine's contribution.**
The machine itself has a finite stopping time $t_2$. During the safety circuit's reaction time $t_1$, the machine continues its motion. Adding both gives total stopping performance time:

$$
T = t_1 + t_2
$$

**Step 4 — Combine and add intrusion factor.**
The hand not only reaches the detection plane, it can also penetrate a small additional distance $C$ before being struck. This is the **intrusion distance**, based on the assumption that even after the light curtain triggers, the hand/body has momentum.

$$
D_s = K \cdot T + C
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute standard values to obtain a numerical example.**
Let $K = 1600$ mm/s, $T = 0.4$ s (combined safety relay + brake), $C = 850$ mm.

$$
D_s = (1600 \;\text{mm/s}) \times (0.4 \;\text{s}) + 850 \;\text{mm}
$$

$$
D_s = 640 \;\text{mm} + 850 \;\text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_s = 1490 \;\text{mm}}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**
The light curtain must be installed at least **1.49 m** from the nearest hazard point. If the operator can reach over the curtain (a "reach-over" scenario), the formula becomes more complex:

$$
D_s = K \cdot T + C - (R \cdot h)
$$

where $R$ is the reach factor and $h$ is the height of the hazard above the floor.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The KTU board often tests whether students can articulate **why $C$ exists**. The answer: $C$ is the *minimum additional distance* a body part can travel after the protective device is triggered but before the machine is mechanically safe. It is **not** a fudge factor — it has a specific biometric origin based on **hand/arm anthropometry and reflex withdrawal latency**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Brake Stopping Time of a Rotating Mass

When a flywheel, spindle, or rotor must be stopped by an operator-initiated or automatic brake, the stopping time is derived from Newton's second law for rotation.

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A **lathe spindle** of moment of inertia $J = 2.5$ kg·m² is rotating at $n = 1500$ rpm. A **band brake** applies a friction torque of $\tau_b = 50$ N·m. Find the stopping time.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert rotational speed to angular velocity.**

$$
\omega = \frac{2 \pi n}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60}
$$

$$
\omega = 157.08 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the rotational form of Newton's second law.**
The angular deceleration $\alpha$ is constant because the brake torque is (assumed) constant:

$$
\tau_b = J \cdot \alpha
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{\tau_b}{J} = \frac{50 \;\text{N·m}}{2.5 \;\text{kg·m}^2}
$$

$$
\alpha = 20 \;\text{rad/s}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Use the kinematic equation for deceleration from $\omega$ to 0.**

$$
\omega_f = \omega_i - \alpha \cdot t_{brake}
$$

Setting $\omega_f = 0$:

$$
0 = 157.08 - 20 \cdot t_{brake}
$$

$$
t_{brake} = \frac{157.08}{20}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{brake} = 7.854 \;\text{seconds}}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy check (verification).**
The kinetic energy of the flywheel at the start is:

$$
KE = \frac{1}{2} J \omega^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 2.5 \times (157.08)^2
$$

$$
KE = 0.5 \times 2.5 \times 24674.14 = 30842.7 \;\text{J}
$$

The energy dissipated by the brake in time $t$ at constant power is $P \cdot t$, where $P = \tau_b \cdot \omega_{avg}$. This provides a useful cross-check for KTU problems that include thermal limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **rotational inertia $J$** (in kg·m²) with **mass $m$** (in kg) when applying formulas. Always check units: $J$ involves $r^2$, while $m$ does not. A solid disc of mass $m$ and radius $r$ has $J = \frac{1}{2} m r^2$, not $m r^2$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Control Types

| Control Type | Best For | Maximum Sustainable Force | Wrist Posture Risk | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Push Button** | Binary on/off, low force | **20–50 N** | Low | Very High |
| **Toggle Switch** | Mode selection | **5–15 N** | Moderate | High |
| **Rotary Knob** | Continuous adjustment | **20–40 N·m** torque | Wrist deviation | High |
| **Hand Wheel** | Heavy machine feed | **60 N·m** continuous | High | Medium |
| **Lever (vertical)** | High-force linear motion | **230 N** push | Shoulder flexion | Medium |
| **Joystick** | 2D positioning | **10 N** continuous | Low | Medium |
| **Foot Pedal** | Hands-busy tasks | **320 N** sustained, **680 N** burst | Leg/ankle | High |
| **Trackball/Mouse** | Cursor control | **5 N** | Repetitive strain | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Bloom's Level:** This matrix is tested at the **Analyze** level (Bloom Level 4). Students are expected to *compare and contrast* control types and *defend* a design choice with biomechanical justification.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Human-Moving-Part Interaction Loop

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **closed-loop control model** linking an operator to a machine's moving parts. Each block represents a measurable ergonomic variable, and each arrow represents an information or energy flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Operator Perception]:::perception -->|Visual, Auditory, Tactile Stimulus| B[Cognitive Processing]:::cognition
    B -->|Decision: HICK HYMAN Law| C[Neuromuscular Actuation]:::actuation
    C -->|Force, Torque, Displacement| D[Machine Moving Part]:::machine
    D -->|Kinematic Response| E[Workpiece or Environment]:::work
    E -->|Feedback: Position, Force, Vibration| A
    D -->|Hazardous Motion| F[Safeguard Subsystem]:::safety
    F -->|Stop Signal, Interlock, Light Curtain| D
    G[Anthropometric Envelope 5th to 95th Percentile]:::anthro --> A
    G --> C
    H[Standards: ISO 12100, ANSI B11, ISO 13850]:::standards --> F

    classDef perception fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cognition fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef actuation fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef machine fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef work fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef safety fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef anthro fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef standards fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation for KTU Examinations:**
> - The **outer loop** (Operator ↔ Machine ↔ Workpiece) is the **productive** interaction.
> - The **inner loop** (Anthropometric Envelope) is the **biomechanical constraint**.
> - The **safety branch** (Safeguard Subsystem) is the **defensive overlay** per ISO 12100.
> - Standards act as a **design input** (top-down), not a feedback signal.

## 4.2 Figure 2 — Decision Tree for Selecting a Moving-Part Safeguard

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Identify Hazardous Moving Part]):::start --> Q1{Frequency of Access?}
    Q1 -->|Frequent, e.g. every cycle| Q2{Production Rate Critical?}
    Q1 -->|Infrequent, e.g. maintenance| Q3{Access Duration > 30s?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| PathA[Use Interlocked Guard with Door Switch]:::guard
    Q2 -->|No| PathB[Use Fixed Guard with Tools-Required Removal]:::guard
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Light Curtain Available?}
    Q3 -->|No| PathC[Use Fixed Guard]:::guard
    Q4 -->|Yes| PathD[Calculate D_s using ISO 13855 Formula]:::calc
    Q4 -->|No| PathE[Use Pressure-Sensitive Safety Mat]:::guard
    PathD --> End([Documented, Validated, Signed Off]):::end
    PathA --> End
    PathB --> End
    PathC --> End
    PathE --> End

    classDef start fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef guard fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef end fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Figure 3 — Force-Angle Envelope for Hand-Wheel Operation

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **recommended operating zone** for a hand wheel, mapped against human biomechanical limits.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z1[Green Zone: 0 to 15 deg wrist deviation, Force 0 to 60 N]:::safe --> Z2[Yellow Zone: 15 to 30 deg, Force 60 to 120 N]:::caution
    Z2 --> Z3[Red Zone: over 30 deg, Force over 120 N]:::danger
    Z3 --> Z4[Injury Risk: Tendonitis, Carpal Tunnel, Muscle Strain]:::injury

    classDef safe fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef caution fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef danger fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef injury fill:#8B0000,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Literacy Tip:** When a board question asks for a "sketch" or "diagram," the Mermaid block diagrams above satisfy the requirement if rendered in text. For hand-drawn examinations, reproduce the **boxes with arrows** and **label each block with the ergonomic variable** it represents.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define "Moving Parts" in the context of Human Factors Engineering. List any two types of motion exhibited by moving parts in industrial machinery.**

**Model Answer (Marks Distribution: 1 + 1 + 1):**

*Definition (1 mark):* Moving Parts, in Human Factors Engineering, refers to all mechanical components of a man-machine system that possess relative motion — rotary, linear, reciprocating, or oscillating — and which either (a) are directly actuated/controlled by a human operator, or (b) constitute a hazard to a human operator requiring safeguarding.

*Two types of motion (1 mark each):*
1. **Rotary motion** — e.g., lathe spindle, hand wheel, motor shaft.
2. **Linear motion** — e.g., hydraulic ram, conveyor belt, sliding carriage.

*(Acceptable alternatives: reciprocating, oscillating, combined/curvilinear.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the standard formula for minimum safety distance as per ISO 13855 and explain the significance of the intrusion distance $C$.**

**Model Answer (Marks Distribution: 2 + 1):**

*Formula (2 marks):*

$$
D_s = K \cdot T + C
$$

where $D_s$ is the minimum safety distance (mm), $K$ is the approach speed (typically 1600 mm/s for hands), $T = t_1 + t_2$ is the total stopping time (s), and $C$ is the intrusion distance (mm).

*Significance of $C$ (1 mark):* The intrusion distance $C$ accounts for the **additional penetration** of a body part (typically a hand) toward the hazard *after* the protective device has been triggered but *before* the machine has physically come to a safe state. It incorporates human reflex withdrawal latency and the biomechanical depth of the hand/finger. Standard default is $C = 850$ mm for non-reach-through installations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **four pillars of ergonomic design** for systems involving moving parts. Discuss how each pillar contributes to operator safety and performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **robotic welding cell** is guarded by a **light curtain**. The operator's hand approach speed is **1600 mm/s**, the safety relay reaction time is **20 ms**, and the robot's mechanical stopping time is **380 ms**. The light curtain cannot be placed at the same height as the hazard. Calculate the **minimum safety distance** using the standard ISO 13855 formula. Discuss what modifications you would recommend if the operator must frequently reach **over** the light curtain to access the workpiece. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Four Pillars (7 marks):**

The four pillars of ergonomic design for moving parts are:

1. **Anthropometric Compatibility (2 marks):** Design must accommodate the **5th–95th percentile** of the operator population. Static anthropometry governs clearances and dimensions; dynamic anthropometry governs motion envelopes. Example: a control lever must be reachable by a **5th-percentile seated female** (forward reach ≈ 700 mm) without stretching, and operable by a **95th-percentile standing male** without cramping.

2. **Biomechanical Load Compatibility (2 marks):** Forces, torques, and joint angles must remain within physiological limits. NIOSH-recommended maxima: push = **230 N**, pull = **180 N**, hand-wheel torque = **60 N·m** (sustained). Violations cause musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs) such as tendonitis, carpal tunnel syndrome, and lower-back injury.

3. **Cognitive & Perceptual Compatibility (1.5 marks):** Control layout must respect **Hick-Hyman** and **Fitts' Laws**. Number of controls, their grouping (by function), and their discriminability (shape, color, texture) determine reaction time and error rate. An emergency stop must be **red, palm-shaped, mushroom-head, on a yellow background**, per ISO 13850.

4. **Safety Integrity (1.5 marks):** Per ISO 12100, the **hierarchy of controls** — inherent safe design → safeguarding → complementary measures → information for use — must be applied. The safety distance formula $D_s = K \cdot T + C$ ensures that **safeguarding is geometrically validated**, not just procedurally assumed.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical + Design (7 marks):**

**Numerical calculation (4 marks):**

Given:
- $K = 1600$ mm/s
- $t_1 = 20$ ms $= 0.020$ s (safety relay reaction)
- $t_2 = 380$ ms $= 0.380$ s (robot stopping time)
- $C = 850$ mm (default for non-reach-through)

Compute total stopping time:

$$
T = t_1 + t_2 = 0.020 + 0.380 = 0.400 \;\text{s}
$$

Apply the ISO 13855 formula:

$$
D_s = K \cdot T + C = 1600 \times 0.400 + 850
$$

$$
D_s = 640 + 850
$$

$$
\boxed{D_s = 1490 \;\text{mm} \;\approx\; 1.49 \;\text{m}}
$$

**Design discussion for reach-over scenario (3 marks):**

When the operator must reach **over** the light curtain, the hazard can be approached from above without the curtain detecting the hand. The standard formula must be modified to:

$$
D_s = K \cdot T + C - (R \cdot h)
$$

where $R$ is a reach factor (mm/mm) and $h$ is the height of the hazard above the floor (mm).

**Recommended modifications:**
1. **Use multiple horizontal light curtains** at staggered heights to create a "curtain wall" effect.
2. **Reduce the approach speed** $K$ by physically constraining the operator (e.g., fixed perimeter rail).
3. **Add a top-mounted horizontal light curtain** to detect reach-over.
4. **Switch to a safety mat** at floor level if reach-over is unavoidable.
5. **Lower $T$** by upgrading to a faster robot controller (e.g., safety-rated soft-stop with **20 ms** instead of 380 ms).

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative Selection) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the aid of a **labeled diagram**, describe the **closed-loop human-machine interaction model** for a moving-part system. Identify the four measurable ergonomic variables in the loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **lathe spindle** of moment of inertia $J = 3.0$ kg·m² rotates at $n = 1200$ rpm. A friction brake applies a constant torque of $\tau = 75$ N·m. Calculate the **stopping time** and the **number of revolutions** the spindle makes before coming to rest. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Diagram + Description (7 marks):**

**Diagram (3 marks):** Draw a block diagram with the following labeled blocks in a loop:

`Operator Perception` → `Cognitive Processing (Hick-Hyman)` → `Neuromuscular Actuation` → `Machine Moving Part` → `Workpiece/Environment` → (feedback) → `Operator Perception`

Also include a parallel **Safeguard Subsystem** tapping into the machine block.

**Four measurable ergonomic variables (4 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Reaction Time (RT)** — measured in seconds, from stimulus onset to response initiation. Reference: simple visual RT ≈ **250 ms**.
2. **Applied Force / Torque** — measured in newtons or newton-metres, from operator to control. Reference: max hand-wheel torque **60 N·m**.
3. **Movement Time (MT)** — duration of the limb motion, predicted by Fitts' Law.
4. **Error Rate** — proportion of incorrect responses; should be **< 1 in 10,000** for safety-critical controls.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert rotational speed to angular velocity (1.5 marks):**

$$
\omega = \frac{2 \pi n}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1200}{60} = 125.66 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate angular deceleration (1.5 marks):**

$$
\alpha = \frac{\tau}{J} = \frac{75}{3.0} = 25.0 \;\text{rad/s}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate stopping time (2 marks):**

Using $\omega_f = \omega_i - \alpha t$ with $\omega_f = 0$:

$$
t_{brake} = \frac{\omega_i}{\alpha} = \frac{125.66}{25.0}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{brake} = 5.026 \;\text{s}}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate number of revolutions (2 marks):**

Using $\theta = \omega_i t - \frac{1}{2} \alpha t^2$:

$$
\theta = 125.66 \times 5.026 - 0.5 \times 25.0 \times (5.026)^2
$$

$$
\theta = 631.57 - 315.79 = 315.78 \;\text{rad}
$$

Convert to revolutions:

$$
N = \frac{\theta}{2 \pi} = \frac{315.78}{6.2832}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 50.26 \;\text{revolutions}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit error in $D_s$ formula:** Mixing mm and s incorrectly yields answers off by **factor of 1000**. Always write units at every step.
> 2. **Confusing $J$ and $m$:** Inertia $J$ has units of kg·m²; mass $m$ has kg. Many students write $J = m r$ instead of $J = \frac{1}{2} m r^2$ for a solid disc.
> 3. **Forgetting the intrusion term $C$:** Omitting $C$ from the safety distance formula is a **deduction of 2 marks minimum** because it is structurally essential to the answer.
> 4. **Confusing linear $K$ and angular $K$:** ISO 13855's $K$ is a linear approach speed (mm/s), not an angular velocity. Do not use $\omega$ in its place.
> 5. **Skipping the verification step:** Examiners reward students who **sanity-check** the numerical result (e.g., "1.49 m is reasonable for a robotic cell — passes the back-of-envelope test").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 — Moving Parts: High-Density Summary**

- ✅ **Definition:** Moving Parts = mechanical components with relative motion that either *interact with* or *threaten* a human operator.
- ✅ **Four Pillars:** Anthropometry, Biomechanics, Cognition, Safety Integrity.
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $D_s = K \cdot T + C$ with $K = 1600$ mm/s and $C = 850$ mm (default).
- ✅ **Reach-over Variant:** $D_s = K \cdot T + C - R \cdot h$ — always state this distinction explicitly.
- ✅ **Anthropometric Range:** Design for **5th–95th percentile**; use $\bar{L} \pm 1.645 \sigma$ to compute.
- ✅ **Biomechanical Limits:** Push 230 N, Pull 180 N, Hand-wheel 60 N·m, Pedal 320 N (sustained) / 680 N (burst).
- ✅ **Reaction Time Baseline:** Simple stimulus ≈ **250 ms**; complex (Hick-Hyman) increases with $\log_2(n+1)$.
- ✅ **Fitts' Law:** MT grows with $\log_2(D/S + 1)$ — larger targets and shorter distances improve speed.
- ✅ **Stopping Time:** $t_{brake} = J \omega / \tau_{brake}$ for rotation; $D = v^2/(2a)$ for linear.
- ✅ **Standards Toolkit:** ISO 12100 (hierarchy), ISO 13850 (E-stop), ISO 13855 (safety distance), ANSI B11.19 (guards), ISO/TS 15066 (cobots).
- ✅ **Cobot Limit:** **150 N** quasi-static contact force, **112 N/s** dynamic.
- ✅ **Hierarchy:** Inherent → Safeguarding → Complementary → Information.
- ✅ **E-stop:** Red, palm, mushroom-head, yellow background, normally-closed, fail-safe.
- ✅ **Exam Hot Topics:** Derive $D_s$ from first principles; calculate brake stopping time; compare control types in a matrix; critique a given safeguard scheme.
- ✅ **Unit Discipline:** State units at every step; convert rpm to rad/s by multiplying by $2\pi/60$.

> **End of Module 2 — Moving Parts Notes** | **Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme PEBMT742**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Size and Shape
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Size and Shape

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 2):**
> Under the broader unit *"Anthropometry and Biomechanics"*, the sub-topic **Size and Shape** deals with the measurement of human body dimensions, the statistical treatment of those measurements across populations, and the translation of this data into engineering design parameters (clearances, reaches, clearances, and adjustability ranges).

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Human Factors Engineering (HFE), **Size** refers to the measurable linear, circumferential, or volumetric dimensions of the human body (e.g., stature, shoulder width, hip breadth, hand length), while **Shape** refers to the geometric and morphological configuration of body segments and the body as a whole (e.g., the curvature of the spine, the cross-sectional profile of the torso, the aspect ratio of limbs).

The *American National Standards Institute / Human Factors and Ergonomics Society (ANSI/HFES 100-2007)* standard for computer workstations explicitly defines this: the human body exhibits both **static (structural) dimensions**—measured with the subject fixed in standardized postures—and **dynamic (functional) dimensions**—measured during movement. The two are not interchangeable.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are designing an **office chair for a population that ranges from a 5th-percentile teenage girl to a 95th-percentile adult male**. The *size* of each user is just a number on a tape measure—their height, thigh length, buttock-to-knee length. The *shape* is the way their spine curves when seated, how their weight distributes across the seat pan, and how their shoulders flare out from the torso.

**If you design only for size (length)**, you might get the seat depth right, but the user will still be uncomfortable because the lumbar curve is wrong. **If you design only for shape**, you might match the curvature but the chair is too deep for short users. Ergonomic design must integrate **both**—size gives the *envelope*; shape gives the *contour* inside that envelope.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Very High Yield for KTU):**
> - **Size** = *How big* (magnitude, scalar dimensions, percentile-based).
> - **Shape** = *How configured* (geometry, contour, posture-dependent).

### 1.3 The Two Sub-Domains of Size

| Sub-Domain | Description | Engineering Use |
|------------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Static (Structural) Anthropometry** | Body dimensions measured with the subject motionless in standardized postures (e.g., standing erect, sitting upright). | Used for **clearances** (door heights, cockpit ceilings, cabin widths) and **static fit**. |
| **Dynamic (Functional) Anthropometry** | Body dimensions measured during motion (e.g., maximum forward reach while seated, overhead reach while standing on tiptoes). | Used for **reach envelopes**, **control placement**, and **workspace layout**. |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exam:** *"Static = Standing Still = Structure. Dynamic = Doing = Function."*

---

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in KTU 2024

- **Stature** — vertical distance from the floor to the top of the head (vertex), measured with the subject standing erect. Units: **millimetres (mm)** or **centimetres (cm)**.
- **Sitting Height (Erect Sitting Height)** — vertical distance from the sitting surface to the top of the head.
- **Eye Height (Sitting)** — sitting surface to the inner canthus (corner) of the eye; critical for visual display placement.
- **Shoulder Breadth (Biacromial Breadth)** — maximum horizontal distance between the lateral deltoid muscles.
- **Hip Breadth (Sitting)** — maximum horizontal breadth of the hips when seated; determines minimum seat width.
- **Buttock-to-Knee Length** — horizontal distance from the rearmost point of the buttocks to the front of the kneecap; determines seat depth.
- **Popliteal Length (Knee Height, Sitting)** — vertical distance from the footrest (or floor) to the underside of the thigh just behind the knee; determines seat height.
- **Elbow-to-Fingertip Length (Functional Forward Reach)** — horizontal distance from the backrest to the fingertip with the upper arm hanging and the forearm extended horizontally forward.

---

### 1.5 The Concept of Percentiles (Foundation for Size)

Because a single "average" body does not exist, the design population is statistically represented as a distribution. **Percentiles** are the units the designer uses to select a user boundary.

$$P_k = \text{the value below which } k\% \text{ of the population falls}$$

- **5th percentile (P5)** — the smaller user. Used to define the *minimum* dimension that the *smallest* users can still operate (e.g., reach of a brake pedal).
- **50th percentile (P50)** — the median user. Useful when designing for the "average" or majority.
- **95th percentile (P95)** — the larger user. Used to define the *maximum* clearance that the *largest* user can still fit into (e.g., cockpit width).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU Valuation):** Students frequently reverse P5 and P95. Mnemonic: *P5 = small person = 5 cm out of 100, so a small number; P95 = large person.* Use **P5 for minimum reach** (small person must reach) and **P95 for maximum clearance** (large person must fit).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Statistical Basis of Anthropometric Data

Human body dimensions, when measured across a large sample of a defined population (e.g., Indian male industrial workers, USAF pilots, Japanese female office workers), approximately follow a **normal (Gaussian) distribution** within the central range, although extreme tails often show slight positive skew (longer right tail due to a few very tall individuals).

For a normally distributed variable $X$ with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, the density is:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

The cumulative distribution function gives the probability of finding a person with dimension less than or equal to $x$:

$$F(x) = P(X \le x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\, dt$$

The **z-score** (standard score) is the bridge between a percentile and the raw measurement:

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

For example, the 5th percentile corresponds to $z = -1.645$, and the 95th percentile to $z = +1.645$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5%–95% Rule (KTU Favourite):** Approximately **90% of a normally distributed population** lies between $z = -1.645$ and $z = +1.645$. Hence the 5th–95th percentile range is the standard accommodation band for general-purpose design.

### 2.2 Steps in Anthropometric Design (Why and How)

The application of size-and-shape data to engineering design follows a structured decision sequence:

1. **Define the user population** — age, sex, ethnicity, occupational group. (Different populations have different $\mu$ and $\sigma$.)
2. **Identify the design criterion** — Is it clearance (accommodation of size) or reach (accommodation of function)?
3. **Select the critical percentile**:
   - *Clearance* → use the **larger** dimension (P95 male or P95 female, whichever defines the design boundary).
   - *Reach* → use the **smaller** dimension (P5 of the population that must reach).
4. **Apply the appropriate correction factors**:
   - Add **clothing allowance** (e.g., +25 mm for seated hip breadth to allow winter clothing).
   - Add **shoe allowance** (e.g., +25 mm for stature-based eye height with shoes).
   - Add **safety margin** (typically 10–25 mm for clearance dimensions).
5. **Decide on adjustability** — For dimensions that conflict (a cockpit that fits a P95 male is too big for a P5 female), provide adjustable features (seat sliders, pedal boxes, steering column reach).
6. **Validate with mock-ups or CAD manikins** — Use software manikins (e.g., JACK, RAMSIS, SAFEWORK) populated with P5 and P95 dimensions to check fit.

### 2.3 Body Shape Considerations

While size is univariate (one number per dimension), **shape** is multivariate and posture-dependent. Key shape parameters:

- **Spinal curvature (lordosis/kyphosis):** The natural S-curve of the human spine. A seated posture that flattens the lumbar lordosis (slouching) is a primary cause of lower back pain. Chair backrests must support the lumbar curve.
- **Torso cross-section:** Approximately elliptical, but the *anterior-posterior* depth increases from chest to waist to hip. This non-uniformity is why a contoured seat pan differs from a flat plank.
- **Limb segment ratios:** The ratio of upper-arm to forearm length, and of thigh to lower-leg length, varies across populations and even between sexes. This affects the optimal placement of controls in a workstation.
- **Body Volume / Body Surface Area (BSA):** Important for thermal ergonomics, cockpit pressurization, and life-support sizing.
- **Body Mass Index (BMI):** Used as a coarse shape metric:
$$BMI = \frac{m}{h^2}$$
where $m$ is body mass in kg and $h$ is stature in metres. The WHO classifies adults as underweight ($BMI < 18.5$), normal ($18.5 \le BMI < 25$), overweight ($25 \le BMI < 30$), and obese ($BMI \ge 30$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shape vs. Size in Design (Very High Yield):**
> - **Size** problems are solved by *percentile selection* and *adjustability*.
> - **Shape** problems are solved by *contouring* (lumbar support, seat pan shape, handle grips) and *posture accommodation*.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` (or `\mid`) for absolute-value separators in tables — never the bare pipe `|`.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---------|----------------|----------------|------|
| 1 | Mean of dimension | $\mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | Sample mean of dimension | mm |
| 2 | Standard deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2}$ | Spread of dimension | mm |
| 3 | Z-score (for percentile) | $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Standardised position of $x$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Percentile value from z-score | $x_k = \mu + z_k \cdot \sigma$ | $k$-th percentile | mm |
| 5 | P5 z-score | $z_{5} = -1.645$ | — | — |
| 6 | P50 z-score | $z_{50} = 0$ | — | — |
| 7 | P95 z-score | $z_{95} = +1.645$ | — | — |
| 8 | P99 z-score | $z_{99} = +2.326$ | — | — |
| 9 | Body Mass Index | $BMI = \dfrac{m}{h^2}$ | $m$ = mass (kg), $h$ = stature (m) | kg/m² |
| 10 | Body Surface Area (Dubois) | $BSA = 0.007184 \cdot m^{0.425} \cdot h^{0.725}$ | $m$ in kg, $h$ in cm | m² |
| 11 | Design clearance (lower) | $D_{min} = P5_{\text{dim}} + \text{allowance}$ | Allowance ≈ 10–25 mm | mm |
| 12 | Design clearance (upper) | $D_{max} = P95_{\text{dim}} + \text{safety margin}$ | Safety margin ≈ 10–25 mm | mm |
| 13 | Design reach | $R = P5_{\text{reach}} - \text{grip thickness}$ | Subtract grip/tool thickness | mm |
| 14 | Coefficient of variation | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu} \times 100\%$ | Relative variability | % |

> [!TIP]
> **CV insight:** If two populations have the same mean stature but different CVs, the one with the higher CV needs a wider accommodation range. Indian and North-East Asian populations often have lower CVs than heterogeneous Western samples for some dimensions.

### 2.5 Engineering Applications (Why This Matters in the Real World)

- **Automotive seat design:** Seat pan depth is set by P95 buttock-to-knee length *minus* clothing allowance. Seat width is set by P95 hip breadth *plus* clothing allowance.
- **Aircraft cockpit layout:** A fighter pilot must fit within an ejection-seat envelope. Eye position (P5) must align with the sight reticle; the same cockpit must accommodate pilots from the 5th to 95th percentile in stature, so the seat is power-adjustable.
- **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE):** Hard hats, gloves, and coveralls are sized in S/M/L/XL based on P5–P95 ranges of relevant dimensions.
- **Public infrastructure:** Door heights, handrail heights, stair risers, and counter heights use anthropometric data so that the largest practicable fraction of the public is accommodated.
- **Medical devices:** MRI bores, hospital beds, and surgical tool grips all rely on patient size data.
- **Consumer products:** The dimensions of mobile phone grips, laptop keyboards, and gaming controllers depend on hand-size data.

### 2.6 Misconceptions to Avoid

- **"Designing for the average works."** It does not. The average person does not exist; designing to the 50th percentile of *every* dimension simultaneously produces a person who is statistically almost non-existent. Use the 5th–95th range and adjustability.
- **"Male data covers the design."** Not for general-public products. Women typically have a lower $\mu$ for many dimensions, and a unisex design must combine male and female distributions or use a unisex user model.
- **"Static and dynamic dimensions are the same."** Forward reach (dynamic) is shorter than arm length (static) because the body pivots; overhead reach (dynamic) is greater than the static shoulder-to-fingertip length because the body extends and rises on tiptoes.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Converting Percentile to Dimension (and Vice Versa)

**Problem Statement:** The sitting height of a population of male Indian industrial workers has a mean $\mu = 870$ mm and a standard deviation $\sigma = 36$ mm, and is normally distributed. Find (a) the 5th-percentile and 95th-percentile sitting heights, and (b) the height of a worker whose sitting height is 940 mm (in percentiles).

#### Step 1 — Identify the governing equation

The percentile of a dimension is found from the z-score using:

$$x_k = \mu + z_k \cdot \sigma$$

where $z_k$ is the standard-normal z-score corresponding to the $k$-th percentile.

#### Step 2 — Look up the z-score for the required percentile

From the standard normal table:
- $z_{5} = -1.645$ (since $P(Z \le -1.645) = 0.05$)
- $z_{95} = +1.645$ (since $P(Z \le +1.645) = 0.95$)

#### Step 3 — Calculate the 5th-percentile sitting height

Substitute the values into the equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{5} &= \mu + z_{5} \cdot \sigma \\
&= 870 \text{ mm} + (-1.645)(36 \text{ mm}) \\
&= 870 \text{ mm} - 59.22 \text{ mm} \\
&= 810.78 \text{ mm} \\
&\approx \mathbf{810.8 \text{ mm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpretation: 5% of male Indian industrial workers have a sitting height of 810.8 mm or less. This is the lower boundary of the typical accommodation range.

#### Step 4 — Calculate the 95th-percentile sitting height

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{95} &= \mu + z_{95} \cdot \sigma \\
&= 870 \text{ mm} + (1.645)(36 \text{ mm}) \\
&= 870 \text{ mm} + 59.22 \text{ mm} \\
&= 929.22 \text{ mm} \\
&\approx \mathbf{929.2 \text{ mm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpretation: 95% of male Indian industrial workers have a sitting height of 929.2 mm or less. This is the upper boundary for clearance-type design.

#### Step 5 — Convert an observed dimension back to a percentile

For $x = 940$ mm, first compute the z-score:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z &= \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} \\
&= \frac{940 \text{ mm} - 870 \text{ mm}}{36 \text{ mm}} \\
&= \frac{70}{36} \\
&= 1.944
\end{aligned}
$$

Then look up the cumulative probability:

$$P(Z \le 1.944) = 0.9740$$

So a worker with a 940 mm sitting height lies at approximately the **97.4th percentile** — i.e., taller than about 97.4% of the reference population.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The two-way conversion (dimension $\leftrightarrow$ percentile) is the single most-tested KTU numerical problem on this topic. Master Steps 1–5.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Design of a Doorway Height (Clearance-Type Problem)

**Problem Statement:** Design the minimum internal height of a doorway in a public building to accommodate at least 95% of adult male users. Use $\mu_{stature} = 1720$ mm and $\sigma_{stature} = 70$ mm. Include a 25 mm shoe allowance and a 20 mm safety margin above the head.

#### Step 1 — Identify the design criterion

A doorway is a **clearance** problem. The largest 95% of the population must pass through without ducking. Therefore we use the **95th percentile** of stature.

#### Step 2 — Find the 95th-percentile stature

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{95} &= \mu + z_{95} \cdot \sigma \\
&= 1720 \text{ mm} + (1.645)(70 \text{ mm}) \\
&= 1720 \text{ mm} + 115.15 \text{ mm} \\
&= 1835.15 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Add the shoe allowance

People pass through a doorway wearing shoes, adding to their effective stature:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{95,\text{shoes}} &= 1835.15 \text{ mm} + 25 \text{ mm} \\
&= 1860.15 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Add the safety margin (clearance above the head)

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{door}} &= 1860.15 \text{ mm} + 20 \text{ mm} \\
&= 1880.15 \text{ mm} \\
&\approx \mathbf{1880 \text{ mm}} \quad (\text{rounded up to nearest 10 mm})
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — State the design specification

> **Design Specification:** The doorway internal height shall be **1880 mm** to accommodate at least 95% of adult male users (shoes and clearance included).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 1880 mm and not a "standard" 2100 mm?** Because adding unnecessary height costs material, increases door weight, and raises the lintel. Anthropometric design minimises over-engineering while meeting the user-accommodation criterion.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Design of a Bracket Reach (Reach-Type Problem)

**Problem Statement:** A wall-mounted emergency shut-off button must be reachable by 95% of standing adult female workers. Female stature has $\mu = 1600$ mm and $\sigma = 65$ mm. Forward reach from the wall (functional arm extension at shoulder height) is given by the empirical relation $R = 0.38 \cdot \text{stature}$ for a P5 reach. Find the maximum horizontal distance from the wall at which the button may be placed.

#### Step 1 — Identify the design criterion

A reach is the *opposite* of clearance: the *smallest* users in the target population must still be able to reach the control. We therefore use the **5th percentile of stature** for adult females.

#### Step 2 — Find the 5th-percentile female stature

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{5} &= \mu + z_{5} \cdot \sigma \\
&= 1600 \text{ mm} + (-1.645)(65 \text{ mm}) \\
&= 1600 \text{ mm} - 106.925 \text{ mm} \\
&= 1493.075 \text{ mm} \\
&\approx 1493.1 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the reach using the empirical relation

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 0.38 \cdot x_{5} \\
&= 0.38 \cdot 1493.075 \text{ mm} \\
&= 567.37 \text{ mm} \\
&\approx \mathbf{567 \text{ mm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — State the design specification

> **Design Specification:** The button's centreline shall be no more than **~567 mm** horizontally from the wall, at the same height as the standing shoulder, to be reachable by 95% of the standing adult female workforce.

> [!TIP]
> **Note on the empirical constant 0.38:** This is a *dimensionless ratio* (reach-to-stature) derived from large-population studies. It varies with posture, clothing, and task. KTU exam problems may give this constant; if not, the default value is **0.36–0.42** depending on the type of reach (forward, overhead, sideward).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Correct Percentile

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is a "must-memorise" KTU 2024 high-yield tool.**

| Design Intent | Required Action | User Boundary | Percentile to Use | Adjustment |
|---------------|-----------------|---------------|-------------------|------------|
| Minimum door width | Everyone must pass | Largest user fits | **P95** (larger) | *Minus* safety margin if hinged-swing |
| Maximum reach to a control | Smallest user reaches | Smallest user reaches | **P5** (smaller) | *Minus* grip thickness |
| Minimum seat height | Tallest user's feet touch floor | Shortest user's feet rest | **P5** (smaller) | *Plus* shoe allowance |
| Maximum seat height (clearance under desk) | Tallest user fits legs | Tallest user fits | **P95** (larger) | *Plus* thigh clearance |
| Standing eye height (for display) | Centroid of population | Middle user | **P50** (median) | *Plus* shoe allowance |
| Overhead clearance (ceiling) | Tallest user fits | Tallest user fits | **P95** (larger) | *Plus* hard-hat allowance if industrial |
| Forward reach (control) | Smallest user reaches | Smallest user reaches | **P5** (smaller) | *Minus* tool thickness |
| Headroom in a car (helmet) | Largest user fits | Tallest with helmet | **P95** + helmet | — |
| Hand-grip diameter | Average comfort | Middle user | **P50** | None |
| Stair riser height (descent) | All climb safely | Whole range | P5 *to* P95 (range design) | Velocity & safety factors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern Recognition (Exam Hack):** Whenever the wording is *"...must be able to..."* and the user is constrained (reach, see, operate), use the **P5** boundary. Whenever the wording is *"...must fit inside / must not hit / must clear..."* and the user is the obstacle, use the **P95** boundary.

### 3.5 Worked Example — BMI and Shape (Composite)

**Problem:** A 70 kg worker has a stature of 1.75 m. Calculate his BMI and classify him. Estimate his Body Surface Area using the Dubois formula.

#### Step 1 — Compute the BMI

$$
\begin{aligned}
BMI &= \frac{m}{h^2} \\
&= \frac{70}{(1.75)^2} \\
&= \frac{70}{3.0625} \\
&= 22.86 \text{ kg/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Classify

Since $18.5 \le 22.86 < 25$, the worker is in the **normal weight** category.

#### Step 3 — Compute the BSA (Dubois)

Convert $h$ to cm: $h = 175$ cm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
BSA &= 0.007184 \cdot m^{0.425} \cdot h^{0.725} \\
&= 0.007184 \cdot (70)^{0.425} \cdot (175)^{0.725}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute the exponents:
- $70^{0.425} \approx 5.84$
- $175^{0.725} \approx 47.10$

$$
\begin{aligned}
BSA &\approx 0.007184 \cdot 5.84 \cdot 47.10 \\
&\approx 0.007184 \cdot 275.06 \\
&\approx 1.976 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Interpret

A BSA of $\sim 1.98$ m² is typical for an adult male and is used, for example, in designing cooling garments, chemical protective suits, and life-support oxygen consumption calculations.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — The Anthropometric Design Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **block-level functional architecture** of how raw size-and-shape data flows through an engineering design process. This replaces a physical drawing of a person on a tape measure with a clearer information-flow schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PopDB[(Population Database<br/>Mean mu and Sigma)] ] --> B[Step1: Define Target User Group]
    B --> C[Step 2: Identify Design Intent]
    C --> D{Is it Clearance or Reach?}
    D -- Clearance --> E[Use P95 Dimension<br/>Larger Boundary]
    D -- Reach --> F[Use P5 Dimension<br/>Smaller Boundary]
    E --> G[Apply Allowances<br/>Clothing, Shoes, Helmet]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Apply Safety Margin<br/>Plus or Minus 10 to 25 mm]
    H --> I[Step 3: Check Adjustability Needs]
    I --> J{P5 to P95 Range<br/>Conflict?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Provide Adjustable Feature<br/>Seat Slider, Pedal Box]
    J -- No --> L[Step 4: Validate with Manikin]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Step 5: Final Design Specification]
    A2[Shape Parameters<br/>Spinal Curve, Torso, BMI] --> N[Contour Design<br/>Backrest, Seat Pan, Grip]
    N --> M
    M --> O[Final Anthropometrically<br/>Optimised Product]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The two parallel rails (top: *Size* via percentiles; bottom: *Shape* via contouring) converge at **Step 5**, the final specification. A design that handles size but ignores shape ends at the top rail only — incomplete.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — How a CAD Manikin Validates the Design

Modern manikin software (JACK, RAMSIS, SAFEWORK, SantosHuman) is the bridge between percentiles and verified design. The following block diagram explains the data flow inside such a tool.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputBlock[Input Layer]
        P1[P5 Dimensions] --> MergeA
        P2[P50 Dimensions] --> MergeA
        P3[P95 Dimensions] --> MergeA
    end
    MergeA --> ManikinCore[Digital Manikin Core<br/>3D Linked Segment Model]
    PostureLib[Posture Library<br/>Standing, Seated, Reached] --> ManikinCore
    ManikinCore --> TaskSim[Workspace Task Simulation]
    TaskSim --> ClashCheck[Clash and Clearance Check]
    TaskSim --> ReachCheck[Reach Envelope Check]
    TaskSim --> VisionCheck[Line of Sight Check]
    ClashCheck --> Report[Engineering Design Report]
    ReachCheck --> Report
    VisionCheck --> Report
```

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Answers:** A simple labelled block diagram like this, drawn during an exam, can be worth **2–3 marks** if the candidate explains the role of each block in 1–2 lines. Always include the P5/P50/P95 input layer to show statistical coverage.

### 4.3 Schematic of a Seated Workspace — Static vs Dynamic Dimensions

The figure below (rendered as a Mermaid block diagram because physical drawings cannot be drawn in plain text) shows a seated office workstation with **static** dimensions on the left rail and **dynamic** reach/clearance on the right rail.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph StaticRail[Static Dimensions - Subject Motionless]
        S1[Stature mm]
        S2[Sitting Height mm]
        S3[Popliteal Length mm]
        S4[Buttock to Knee Length mm]
        S5[Hip Breadth mm]
        S6[Shoulder Breadth mm]
    end
    subgraph DynamicRail[Dynamic Dimensions - Subject in Motion]
        D1[Forward Reach mm]
        D2[Overhead Reach mm]
        D3[Sideward Reach mm]
        D4[Vertical Sight Line mm]
    end
    subgraph DesignObj[Design Object - Office Workstation]
        W1[Seat Height = P5 Popliteal + Shoes]
        W2[Seat Depth = P95 Buttock Knee minus 25 mm]
        W3[Seat Width = P95 Hip Breadth + 25 mm]
        W4[Desk Reach = P5 Forward Reach minus 50 mm]
        W5[Eye to Screen = P50 Eye Height adjusted]
    end
    S3 --> W1
    S4 --> W2
    S5 --> W3
    D1 --> W4
    S2 --> W5
    S1 --> W5
    S6 --> W3
    D2 --> W4
    D4 --> W5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why P5 for seat height?** Because a P5 (short) person must have their feet flat on the floor; if the seat is set for the P95 popliteal length, the short user's legs dangle, increasing pressure behind the thighs. Therefore *minimum* functional seat height is the *smallest* user's popliteal length (plus shoe allowance), and the *maximum* is the *largest* user's popliteal length.

### 4.4 Diagram — Percentile Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Design Question] --> R{Does the user have to<br/>reach or operate?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Use P5 of User Population]
    R -- No --> T{Does the user have to<br/>fit inside or under?}
    T -- Yes --> U[Use P95 of User Population]
    T -- No --> V{Is it a comfort<br/>dimension?}
    V -- Yes --> W[Use P50 of User Population]
    V -- No --> X[Re-examine Design Intent]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **This 3-branch decision tree** is the single most efficient exam answer for the question *"Which percentile do you use, and why?"*

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
> - **Part A:** 3-mark short-answer questions (definitions, one-line derivations, label diagrams).
> - **Part B:** 14-mark questions with internal choice, typically split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.
> - The questions below follow the actual KTU 2024 cognitive-level distribution: **Remember (L1)**, **Understand (L2)**, **Apply (L3)**, and **Analyse (L4)**.

---

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q1. (3 marks)** *Differentiate between static and dynamic anthropometry. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Static Anthropometry | Dynamic Anthropometry |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Definition | Body dimensions measured with the subject motionless in a standardised posture. | Body dimensions measured while the subject is in motion or assuming a working posture. |
| Posture | Fixed, e.g., standing erect or sitting upright. | Variable, e.g., reaching, bending, twisting. |
| Example 1 | Stature (standing height). | Functional forward reach while seated. |
| Example 2 | Sitting height. | Overhead reach while standing on tiptoes. |
| Engineering use | Clearances, structural fit. | Workspace layout, control placement. |

> *Award 1 mark per row, 3 rows for full marks.*

---

**Q2. (3 marks)** *Why is it incorrect to design a product "for the average person"? Explain using the concept of percentiles.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The "average" (50th percentile) of *every* dimension simultaneously does not correspond to a real individual — it represents a *composite* of different body parts from different people. A person who is 50th-percentile in stature, 50th in arm length, 50th in hip breadth, and 50th in torso depth is statistically extremely rare (probability $\approx 0.5^4 = 0.0625$, or 6.25%, even for only four dimensions; for 20 dimensions it is vanishingly small). *(1 mark)*
2. Designing for the 50th percentile means that **50% of the population cannot use the product** for every dimension. This is unacceptable for general-public goods. *(1 mark)*
3. The correct approach is the **5th–95th percentile accommodation range**, with adjustability for dimensions that conflict across the range. This guarantees 90% of the population can use the product. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Anthropometric Design Problem

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Pattern, Module 2]**

**Q A (a). (7 marks)** *The stature of a population of male industrial workers is normally distributed with $\mu = 1710$ mm and $\sigma = 68$ mm. Determine:*
*(i) The stature corresponding to the 5th percentile.* *(2 marks)*
*(ii) The stature corresponding to the 95th percentile.* *(2 marks)*
*(iii) The percentage of workers taller than 1820 mm.* *(3 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{5} &= \mu + z_{5} \cdot \sigma \\
&= 1710 + (-1.645)(68) \\
&= 1710 - 111.86 \\
&= \mathbf{1598.14 \text{ mm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{95} &= \mu + z_{95} \cdot \sigma \\
&= 1710 + (1.645)(68) \\
&= 1710 + 111.86 \\
&= \mathbf{1821.86 \text{ mm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (iii):**

For $x = 1820$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z &= \frac{1820 - 1710}{68} \\
&= \frac{110}{68} \\
&= 1.6176
\end{aligned}
$$

From the standard normal table, $P(Z \le 1.62) \approx 0.9474$.

Therefore, the percentage of workers **taller than** 1820 mm:

$$P(Z > 1.6176) = 1 - 0.9474 = 0.0526 \approx \mathbf{5.26\%}$$

So approximately **5.26%** of the workers are taller than 1820 mm. *(Full 3 marks for correct z, correct table look-up, and correct complement.)*

---

**Q A (b). (7 marks)** *Design the minimum overhead clearance for a doorway in an industrial plant such that 95% of adult male workers (wearing safety helmets) can pass through without ducking. Use the data from part (a). Add 30 mm for helmet height and 25 mm safety margin.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The relevant dimension is **stature**. The doorway is a *clearance* problem — the tallest 95% of workers must fit. Use **P95 of stature**. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2:** From part (a)(ii), $x_{95} = 1821.86$ mm. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3:** Add the helmet allowance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{95,\text{helmet}} &= 1821.86 + 30 \\
&= 1851.86 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4:** Add the safety clearance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{clearance}} &= 1851.86 + 25 \\
&= \mathbf{1876.86 \text{ mm}} \\
&\approx 1880 \text{ mm (rounded up to nearest 10 mm)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 5:** **Design Specification:** The minimum internal overhead clearance of the industrial doorway shall be **1880 mm**. *(2 marks — for statement and engineering reasoning.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Pattern, Module 2]**

**Q B (a). (7 marks)** *Explain the difference between "Size" and "Shape" in the context of human factors engineering. With suitable examples, describe how each influences the design of a product.*

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** *Size* refers to the scalar, measurable dimensions of the human body (lengths, breadths, depths, circumferences). *Shape* refers to the geometric configuration, contour, and posture-dependent morphology of the body and its segments.

**Size Examples and Design Influence (3 marks):**

- *Stature* → determines minimum door heights, aircraft cabin heights, and the range of adjustable seat heights.
- *Hip breadth (sitting)* → determines seat-pan width; P95 hip breadth + clothing allowance sets the *minimum* seat width.
- *Buttock-to-knee length* → determines seat-pan depth; P95 buttock-knee length minus a 25 mm popliteal clearance sets the *maximum* seat depth.
- These are all solved by **percentile selection** and **adjustability** (e.g., seat slider).

**Shape Examples and Design Influence (3 marks):**

- *Lumbar lordosis* → requires a *contoured* backrest with lumbar support, not a flat plank.
- *Hand cross-section (cylindrical grip)* → requires a grip diameter of 30–50 mm to fit the average closed-hand circumference, plus a *shape*-appropriate contour (e.g., wing-grip on a screwdriver).
- *Torso anterior-posterior depth variation* → a contoured seat pan follows the thigh-to-buttock profile rather than being flat.

> *Award marks for clearly distinguishing size (numbers/percentiles) from shape (contour/geometry) and for giving at least one design example of each.*

---

**Q B (b). (7 marks)** *A worker has a body mass of 78 kg and a stature of 1.78 m. Calculate his BMI, classify the result, and estimate his body surface area using the Dubois formula. Discuss the ergonomic implications of the BMI category.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — BMI Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BMI &= \frac{m}{h^2} \\
&= \frac{78}{(1.78)^2} \\
&= \frac{78}{3.1684} \\
&= 24.62 \text{ kg/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Classification (1 mark):**

Since $18.5 \le 24.62 < 25$, the worker is classified as **Normal Weight**.

**Step 3 — BSA (Dubois) (2 marks):**

Convert stature to cm: $h = 178$ cm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
BSA &= 0.007184 \cdot m^{0.425} \cdot h^{0.725} \\
&= 0.007184 \cdot (78)^{0.425} \cdot (178)^{0.725}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute the sub-expressions:
- $78^{0.425} \approx 6.225$
- $178^{0.725} \approx 47.71$

$$
\begin{aligned}
BSA &\approx 0.007184 \cdot 6.225 \cdot 47.71 \\
&\approx 0.007184 \cdot 296.99 \\
&\approx \mathbf{2.13 \text{ m}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Ergonomic Implications (2 marks):**

- A *normal-weight* worker typically fits standard workstation envelopes and PPE sizes (M/L coveralls, size-9–10 gloves).
- A BSA of $\sim 2.13$ m² implies higher convective and evaporative heat loss than a smaller-framed person; for hot-work environments (foundries, kilns), this is an advantage.
- If the worker's BMI were to increase into the *overweight* or *obese* range, seat-pan width, harness sizes, and reach envelopes would need to be re-sized; PPE sizing bands would shift upward.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in KTU Module 2 Answers:**
> 1. **Wrong percentile chosen** — students default to P50. The examiner will deduct 2 marks immediately if the wrong percentile is used for a clearance vs reach problem.
> 2. **Forgetting the +25 mm clothing/shoe allowance** — for clearance-type problems, the calculated P95 alone is incomplete. A full-credit answer must mention and apply at least one allowance.
> 3. **Omitting the safety margin** — the final design value must always include the safety margin, stated explicitly.
> 4. **Z-score arithmetic error** — sign errors on $z_{5} = -1.645$ (vs $+1.645$ for P95) are very common; examiners mark the *final value*, not the z-score, so the mark loss is severe.
> 5. **Confusing static and dynamic dimensions** — if the question says "reach", students must NOT use the static arm length; they must use the *functional* reach formula (e.g., $0.38 \cdot$ stature).
> 6. **Shape ignored** — when asked to "design" a product, students often give only numbers (size) and forget the contour (shape). Both must be addressed.
> 7. **BMI formula errors** — height must be in **metres** for BMI (kg/m²), but in **centimetres** for the Dubois BSA formula. Mixing up units is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save This Before the Exam):**

- **Size vs Shape:** Size is *how big* (a number); shape is *how configured* (a contour). Use percentiles for size; use contouring and posture accommodation for shape.
- **Static vs Dynamic:** Static dimensions are for *clearances*; dynamic dimensions are for *reach and workspace*.
- **Percentiles (MEMORISE):**
  - $P5: z = -1.645$ → use for *reach* (smallest user must operate).
  - $P50: z = 0$ → use for *comfort* (middle user).
  - $P95: z = +1.645$ → use for *clearance* (largest user must fit).
- **Conversion equation:** $x_k = \mu + z_k \cdot \sigma$. Always state this explicitly in derivations.
- **Allowances to add (in order):**
  1. Calculate the boundary percentile.
  2. Add shoe allowance (~25 mm) if stature-based.
  3. Add clothing allowance (~25 mm) if girth/breadth-based.
  4. Add helmet allowance (~30 mm) if headroom-based.
  5. Add safety margin (10–25 mm) for clearance.
- **The 5%–95% rule:** Approximately 90% of a normal population lies within $\pm 1.645\sigma$ of the mean. This is the **default accommodation range** for general-public design.
- **BMI formula:** $BMI = m/h^2$ (kg/m²). **WHO bands:** Underweight $< 18.5$; Normal $18.5$–$24.9$; Overweight $25$–$29.9$; Obese $\ge 30$.
- **Dubois BSA formula:** $BSA = 0.007184 \cdot m^{0.425} \cdot h^{0.725}$ (m, kg; h, cm).
- **Reach ratios (typical values):**
  - Forward reach (seated): $0.36$–$0.42 \times$ stature.
  - Overhead reach (standing): $1.20$–$1.30 \times$ shoulder height.
- **Adjustability is the cure for percentile conflict** — when the P5 and P95 values produce an infeasible single dimension, provide an adjustable feature (seat slider, pedal box, telescoping column).
- **Female vs male data:** For unisex public design, use the *female* distribution for reach and the *male* distribution for clearance, or use a unisex population dataset.
- **Anthropometric surveys cited in KTU textbooks:** NASA Man-Systems Integration Standards (NASA-STD-3000), ANSI/HFES 100, ISO 7250, and Indian reference data from the Defence Institute of Physiology and Allied Sciences (DIPAS).

> **End of Notes — Module 2, Topic: Size and Shape.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Warnings and Labels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Warnings and Labels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Warnings

A **Warning** is a communication of safety-critical information that alerts a user to the presence of a hazard, the nature of the potential harm, and the consequences of non-compliance. According to the ANSI Z535.6 (2011) standard, a warning is formally defined as a "statement that calls attention to the possibility of personal injury or death" associated with the use of a product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Syllabus Aligned):**
> A *Warning* is any sensory signal (visual, auditory, tactile) that conveys hazard information rapidly enough to permit the user to take corrective action. A *Label* is the printed, affixed, or displayed information that supplements the warning with identification, instructions, or supplementary safety text.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine driving on a busy Kerala highway. You see a yellow diamond-shaped sign showing a person digging — that **sign** is the *warning*. It instantly communicates a hazard ahead. Below it, a small textboard might read *"Road work in progress. Reduce speed to 30 km/h"* — that **textboard** is the *label* (instructional). 

- The **warning** grabs attention: "Danger exists."
- The **label** explains context: "What the danger is, and what to do."

Together, they form the **Warning-Label System (WLS)** of any product, machine, or workspace.

## 1.2 Formal Definition — Labels

A **Label** is a piece of text, symbol, or graphic affixed to a product or its packaging, providing identification, content information, instructions for use, precautions, or supplementary hazard information. Labels differ from warnings in that labels may also convey non-safety information (such as batch number, manufacturer details, or operating instructions).

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Rule:** Not all labels are warnings, but **every effective warning contains label-level textual information** describing the hazard, consequence, and avoidance behavior. This is called the *Hazard × Consequence × Avoidance (HCA) triplet* and is the gold-standard structure in the **ANSI Z535.4** and **ISO 3864** standards.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Hazard** | Any source of potential harm | A hot stove surface |
| **Risk** | Probability × Severity of harm | High probability of burn |
| **Signal Word** | The attention-grabbing word on a warning | "DANGER", "WARNING", "CAUTION" |
| **Pictogram** | A graphical symbol that conveys information | ☠ Skull and crossbones |
| **Affordance** | A design feature that signals how to use an object | A handle shaped like a grip |
| **Compliance** | The user taking the recommended action | Wearing ear protection in a noisy zone |

## 1.4 Importance of Warnings and Labels in Engineering Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, this topic directly supports **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Design human-centered systems with appropriate warnings, controls, and displays."*

- **Legal Compliance:** Products without proper warnings face product-liability lawsuits.
- **Accident Prevention:** The U.S. Consumer Product Safety Commission (CPSC) estimates that effective warnings could prevent up to **40%** of avoidable injuries annually.
- **Information Transfer:** Warnings bridge the gap between designer intent and user behavior.
- **Universal Communication:** Pictograms transcend language barriers — critical for global product markets.

## 1.5 Warning vs. Label — A Critical Distinction

| Feature | Warning | Label |
|---------|---------|-------|
| **Purpose** | Alerts to imminent danger | Provides identification, instructions, or supplementary info |
| **Urgency Level** | High (safety-critical) | Variable (informational to safety) |
| **Visual Elements** | Signal word + color + pictogram | Text, symbol, barcode, etc. |
| **Failure Consequence** | Personal injury/death | Confusion, misuse, or no injury |
| **Example** | "DANGER: Electric Shock Risk" on a transformer | Manufacturer logo + serial number on the same transformer |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU exam, students often confuse "warning" and "label" as synonyms. **Remember:** A *warning* is a *type* of *label*, but not every *label* is a *warning*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Functions of an Effective Warning

According to the **Human Factors literature (Wogalter, 2006)** and KTU Module 2 syllabus, a warning must perform **four sequential functions** for it to be effective:

1. **Attention Capture (Noticing):** The warning must attract the user's visual or auditory focus.
2. **Hazard Identification (Comprehension):** The user must understand *what* the danger is.
3. **Consequence Explanation (Belief):** The user must believe that ignoring the warning will cause harm.
4. **Avoidance Instruction (Compliance):** The warning must tell the user *what to do* to avoid harm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** If **any one** of these four functions fails, the entire warning system fails. This is the **Wogalter Warning Effectiveness Model (WAM)**, and it is a guaranteed 7-mark question in KTU exams.

## 2.2 Design Principles for Warnings (KTU Formula Sheet)

The following table consolidates the key design parameters, principles, and quantitative thresholds that are frequently tested in the KTU 2024 scheme ESE.

| Principle | Quantitative Rule | KTU Application |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **Signal Word Selection** | Use **DANGER** (imminent, death) → **WARNING** (possible, serious) → **CAUTION** (minor injury) | Hierarchy based on severity |
| **Color Coding (ANSI Z535)** | **Red** = Danger, **Orange** = Warning, **Yellow** = Caution | Universal color semantics |
| **Minimum Letter Height** | $H_{min} = \dfrac{D}{25}$ where $D$ = reading distance (in same units) | Letter height for legibility |
| **Contrast Ratio** | Minimum **4.5:1** (WCAG 2.0 standard) for text vs. background | Visibility threshold |
