2. **Denial** — *Example:* A worker continues using a damaged ladder despite visible cracks.
3. **Projection** — *Example:* A stressed supervisor accuses subordinates of being "lazy."
4. **Displacement** — *Example:* A scolded worker kicks a toolbox.
5. **Rationalization** — *Example:* "I bypassed the safety check because production was behind."
6. **Reaction Formation** — *Example:* An anxious safety officer becomes excessively rigid about PPE.
7. **Sublimation** — *Example:* A competitive, aggressive worker becomes a top athlete in a company sports team.
8. **Regression** — *Example:* A senior engineer throws a tantrum when a design is rejected.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Describe the **sensory protective mechanisms** of the human body, covering the **visual system (blink, pupillary)**, the **auditory system (acoustic reflex)**, and the concept of **sensory adaptation**. Include relevant numerical thresholds.

**(b)** Explain the **role of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** and the **hierarchy of engineering controls** in compensating for the **limitations of human protective mechanisms**.

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Sensory Protective Mechanisms (7 marks)**

1. **Visual — Blink Reflex (1.5 marks)**
   - Trigger: corneal touch, bright light, sudden approach.
   - Latency: **100 – 400 ms**.
   - Function: spreads tear film and protects cornea.

2. **Visual — Pupillary Light Reflex (1.5 marks)**
   - Pathway: retina → pretectal nucleus → **Edinger-Westphal nucleus** → ciliary ganglion → iris sphincter.
   - Latency: **~200 ms**.
   - Function: regulates retinal illumination, protects photoreceptors.

3. **Auditory — Acoustic Reflex (2 marks)**
   - Trigger level: **70 – 90 dB SPL** above threshold.
   - Attenuation: **10 – 15 dB** at low frequencies.
   - Limitation: ineffective for impulse noise.

4. **Sensory Adaptation (2 marks)**
   - Reduced sensitivity to a **constant** stimulus.
   - Example: a worker no longer notices the persistent hum of a ventilation fan after 10 minutes.
   - **HFE implication:** continuous warning signals (steady buzzers) are less effective than **pulsed or varying** signals.

**(b) PPE and Hierarchy of Controls (7 marks)**

**Hierarchy of Controls (most effective → least):**
1. **Elimination** — remove the hazard entirely.
2. **Substitution** — replace with a less hazardous process/material.
3. **Engineering Controls** — guards, interlocks, ventilation, isolation.
4. **Administrative Controls** — training, job rotation, signage.
5. **PPE** — last line of defense: helmets, earplugs, gloves, respirators.

**Why PPE is the last line (1 mark):** PPE relies on **operator compliance**, which is undermined by psychological defense mechanisms like **denial** and **rationalization**.

**Compensation for Reflex Limitations (3 marks):**
- Acoustic reflex latency (25–150 ms) is too slow for impulse noise → PPE (muffs + plugs) provide **25–35 dB combined attenuation**.
- Withdrawal reflex latency (30–70 ms) cannot prevent crush injuries at high machine speeds → **machine guarding** and **light curtains** are mandatory.
- Pupillary reflex (200 ms) is too slow for arc-flash → **auto-darkening helmets** (response time < 1 ms) are used.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions**
> - ❌ Confusing **sensory** vs **physiological** reflexes (Sensory = eye/ear; Physiological = spinal/autonomic).
> - ❌ Stating that the acoustic reflex "protects against all loud noises" — it **does NOT** protect against impulse noise.
> - ❌ Listing psychological defense mechanisms without giving **workplace examples** (each example carries 0.5 marks).
> - ❌ Forgetting the **HPA axis** (cortisol) in the discussion of stress — many students only mention the acute sympathetic response.
> - ❌ Using **denominator 2** instead of **5-dB exchange rate** in OSHA calculations → wrong dose values.
> - ❌ In the hierarchy of controls, putting PPE first — it is the **least preferred**, not the first option.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Protective Mechanisms**

- ✅ Protective mechanisms are **layered**: sensory → physiological → psychological → behavioral.
- ✅ **Reflex arc** = Receptor → Afferent → Integration → Efferent → Effector.
- ✅ **Withdrawal reflex latency** = 30 – 70 ms (faster than pain perception at 400–500 ms).
- ✅ **Acoustic reflex** activates at **70 – 90 dB SPL**, attenuates **10 – 15 dB**, but is **useless for impulse noise**.
- ✅ **Blink reflex latency** = 100 – 400 ms; **Pupillary reflex** = ~200 ms.
- ✅ **Sympathetic response** = fast (seconds); **HPA axis (cortisol)** = slow (minutes to hours).
- ✅ Anna Freud's **8 defense mechanisms**: Repression, Denial, Projection, Displacement, Rationalization, Reaction Formation, Sublimation, Regression.
- ✅ Defense mechanisms are **unconscious** — not deliberate lies.
- ✅ **OSHA PEL** for noise = **90 dB(A) TWA over 8 hours**, with a **5-dB exchange rate**.
- ✅ OSHA Dose formula: $D\,\% = \dfrac{C \cdot 2^{(L-90)/5}}{8} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls**: Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- ✅ HFE design should **augment, not fight**, innate protective mechanisms.
- ✅ **Reaction time** = Detection time + Motor time (~100–300 ms + ~200 ms).
- ✅ **PPE** is the **last line** of defense — its effectiveness is undermined by human denial and rationalization.
- ✅ Acoustic reflex provides **insufficient protection** for industrial noise — **engineering controls and PPE** are mandatory above **85 dB(A)** (NIOSH REL).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mounting and Security Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mounting and Security Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Mounting** in the context of Human Factors Engineering refers to the systematic process of physically installing, attaching, or supporting equipment, displays, controls, tools, or workstations in a manner that is **ergonomically optimized** for the human operator. It encompasses the geometric placement, orientation, adjustability, and structural support of hardware relative to the human body and the work environment.

**Security Mechanisms** are the engineered hardware provisions (mechanical, electrical, or hybrid) that ensure mounted equipment remains **firmly attached, vibration-resistant, tamper-proof, and fail-safe** under all operational conditions, while still permitting deliberate removal or adjustment when required by the user.

Together, these two concepts form the **physical interface layer** between the human operator and the engineered system, governing accessibility, stability, and operational safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** *Mounting and Security Mechanisms* is a sub-topic under "Anthropometric Applications in Design." Examiners expect students to link anthropometric data (reach, stature, clearance) directly to mounting decisions and justify security provisions using human reliability and safety frameworks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **driver's seat, steering wheel, and rear-view mirror of a car**:
- The **mounting** is what positions the steering wheel within the driver's reach envelope and the mirror within the line of sight. A poorly mounted mirror forces the driver to twist awkwardly, while a properly mounted one allows glance-reading in <1 second.
- The **security mechanism** is the **locking clamp** that holds the steering column in place, the **detent pin** that holds the seat adjustment, and the **latch** that keeps the seatbelt fastened. These mechanisms must be secure enough to survive a crash, yet releasable in <2 seconds during an emergency.

**In industry**, this translates to:
- Mounting a **CNC control pendant** on an articulated arm at shoulder height.
- Using a **captive thumb-screw** to secure a server in a rack.
- Providing a **safety tether** on a handheld inspection tool used at height.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "5-Second Rule" of Mounting:** A well-designed mount should allow an operator to position, use, and recover the equipment without exceeding ergonomic tolerance limits (e.g., the **NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit of 23 kg** for lifting, and a reach envelope confined within the **5th to 95th percentile** of the operator population).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

Key anthropometric constants used in mounting design (from standard **ANSI/HFES 100-2007** and **ISO 7250** databases):

- **5th percentile female reach (forward, shoulder-flexed):** ≈ **580 mm**
- **95th percentile male reach (forward, shoulder-flexed):** ≈ **840 mm**
- **Standard seated eye height (5th–95th percentile range):** **685 mm – 855 mm**
- **Preferred mounting height for visual displays:** **1.10 × seated eye height** (i.e., **753 mm – 940 mm**)
- **Maximum recommended static push/pull force (one-handed):** **≤ 230 N** (male), **≤ 150 N** (female)
- **Standard bolt-torque range for vibration-resistant mounting:** **15 Nm – 95 Nm** (M6 to M12 fasteners)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Mounting Systems

Mounting systems are classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

**A. By Adjustability**
- **Fixed Mounting:** Permanently attached, no in-service adjustment (e.g., bulkhead-mounted cockpit gauges).
- **Adjustable Mounting:** Allows repositioning for individual users (e.g., height-adjustable monitor arms).
- **Detachable Mounting:** Allows complete removal for portability (e.g., quick-release camera plates).
- **Stowable Mounting:** Folds/retracts when not in use (e.g., aircraft fold-down jump seats).

**B. By Load Path**
- **Direct Mount:** Equipment bolted directly to a structural member.
- **Indirect Mount:** Equipment attached via a frame, bracket, or compliant isolator (e.g., rubber grommets for vibration damping).

**C. By Orientation**
- **Vertical Mount** (top-down, e.g., ceiling-suspended displays).
- **Horizontal Mount** (e.g., dashboard consoles).
- **Tilted/Angled Mount** (e.g., 15°–30° inclination for keyboard trays).

**D. By Population Coverage**
- **Single-User Optimized:** Tuned to a specific percentile (e.g., a pilot's ejection seat).
- **Multi-User Adjustable:** Range covers the 5th to 95th percentile.
- **Universal:** One-size-fits-most (e.g., 5th-percentile-female to 95th-percentile-male envelope).

### 2.2 Why and How of Mounting Decisions

- **Why adjustability?** The human body dimensions span a range of roughly **1.4:1** between the smallest adult female and the largest adult male. A single fixed mount cannot serve both without forcing awkward posture.
- **How is it done?** By providing adjustment ranges based on **percentile differences**:
  $$\Delta L = P_{95} - P_{5}$$
  This range is then distributed across **discrete steps** (e.g., 5 positions) or **continuous adjustment** (e.g., friction-slide with detent).
- **The "Why" of Security:** Equipment subjected to shock, vibration, or operator-induced forces must remain fastened. The **MIL-STD-810H** vibration test specifies **7.7 g RMS** for aircraft equipment. A loose mount under such conditions becomes a **projectile hazard**.

### 2.3 Security Mechanism Taxonomy

| Security Type | Operating Principle | Typical Use Case | Failure Mode Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Captive Fasteners** | Retained screws/pins (cannot fall out) | Aircraft panels, server racks | Vibration loosening |
| **Quick-Release Pins** | Spring-loaded detent ball | Machinery guards, removable seats | Accidental release |
| **Safety Latches** | Over-center cam action | Doors, oven chambers | Incomplete engagement |
| **Torque-Lock Bolts** | Pre-loaded to yield point | Engine mounts, heavy machinery | Over/under-torque |
| **Wire-Safety Tethers** | Secondary mechanical link | Handheld tools at height | Cable fatigue |
| **Magnetic Locks** | Solenoid-released holding force | Security panels, access covers | Power loss release |
| **Pneumatic/Hydraulic Clamps** | Fluid pressure holds mount | Aircraft weapon pylons | Pressure decay |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Principle:** A *good* security mechanism must satisfy the **3-S rule:** **Safe (fail-safe), Simple (one-hand operation), and Sure (positive indication of engagement).**

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Description | Standard / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_k = \mu + k\sigma$ | Percentile value where $\mu$ = mean, $\sigma$ = SD, k from Z-table | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $L_{adj} = P_{95} - P_{5}$ | Total adjustment range required | mm |
| 3 | $n_{steps} = \lceil L_{adj} / \Delta L_{step} \rceil$ | Number of discrete adjustment steps | Integer |
| 4 | $F_{preload} = 0.75 \cdot A_s \cdot \sigma_y$ | Bolt preload (75% of proof load) | N |
| 5 | $T = K \cdot d \cdot F_{preload}$ | Torque-Tension relation (K ≈ 0.20 for lubricated steel) | N·m |
| 6 | $a_{vib,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T a^2(t)\,dt}$ | RMS vibration acceleration | m/s² |
| 7 | $f_{nat} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency of mount (must be ≪ forcing frequency) | Hz |
| 8 | $M_{reach} = H_{seated} + 0.86 L_{arm}$ | Maximum functional reach, seated operator | mm |
| 9 | $H_{display,opt} = 1.10 \cdot H_{eye,seated}$ | Optimal display mounting height | mm |
| 10 | $SA = \frac{\pi D_{grip}^2}{4}$ | Grip cross-section for handle/tether design | mm² |
| 11 | $\eta_{spring} = \frac{U_{stored}}{U_{input}} = 0.5$ for linear | Quick-release pin energy efficiency | Dimensionless |

**Critical boundary conditions to remember:**

- The **5th–95th percentile** design range covers **90%** of the working population.
- Bolt preload must NOT exceed **90% of proof load**, or fatigue failure occurs.
- For a mount to be **vibration-isolated**, the natural frequency must be **less than 1/√2** of the forcing frequency.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Mounting inertial navigation units on **damped elastomer isolators** to survive launch vibration profiles.
- **Medical Devices:** Mounting infusion pumps on **IV-pole quick-release brackets** that allow single-hand operation.
- **Automotive:** Mounting child seats using **ISOFIX/LATCH** standardized anchors — a direct human-factors-driven security standard.
- **Industrial Control Rooms:** Mounting touchscreens on **gas-spring articulated arms** for 5th-to-95th percentile adjustability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Adjustment Range for a Monitor Mount

**Problem:** An engineering team is designing an adjustable monitor mount for a control room. The relevant anthropometric data is the seated eye height. Given:
- 5th percentile female seated eye height: $H_{5} = 685$ mm
- 95th percentile male seated eye height: $H_{95} = 855$ mm
- The display should be mounted at 1.10× seated eye height.

**Required:** (a) The total adjustment range, and (b) the number of discrete steps if each step is 25 mm.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Calculate the lower bound of the mounting height.

$$H_{min} = 1.10 \times H_5$$

$$H_{min} = 1.10 \times 685$$

$$H_{min} = 753.5 \text{ mm}$$

Step 2: Calculate the upper bound of the mounting height.

$$H_{max} = 1.10 \times H_{95}$$

$$H_{max} = 1.10 \times 855$$

$$H_{max} = 940.5 \text{ mm}$$

Step 3: Compute the total adjustment range $L_{adj}$.

$$L_{adj} = H_{max} - H_{min}$$

$$L_{adj} = 940.5 - 753.5$$

$$L_{adj} = 187.0 \text{ mm}$$

Step 4: Determine the number of discrete steps.

$$n_{steps} = \left\lceil \frac{L_{adj}}{\Delta L_{step}} \right\rceil$$

$$n_{steps} = \left\lceil \frac{187.0}{25} \right\rceil$$

$$n_{steps} = \lceil 7.48 \rceil$$

$$n_{steps} = 8 \text{ discrete positions}$$

**Interpretation:** The mount must offer at least 8 click-stop positions. A continuous slide would also satisfy the requirement but provides no positive lock; a 3-position mount would leave 32% of the population poorly served.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bolt Preload and Torque for a Vibration-Resistant Mount

**Problem:** An M8 (nominal diameter $d = 8$ mm) steel bolt (proof strength $\sigma_p = 600$ MPa, tensile stress area $A_s = 36.6$ mm²) is to be used to mount a vibration-sensitive instrument. Compute the recommended preload and tightening torque using the K-factor method.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute the recommended preload force (75% of proof load, the KTU-accepted safe fraction).

$$F_{preload} = 0.75 \times A_s \times \sigma_p$$

$$F_{preload} = 0.75 \times 36.6 \times 600$$

$$F_{preload} = 0.75 \times 21960$$

$$F_{preload} = 16470 \text{ N} \approx 16.47 \text{ kN}$$

Step 2: Compute the tightening torque (using K = 0.20 for lubricated steel).

$$T = K \cdot d \cdot F_{preload}$$

$$T = 0.20 \times 8 \times 16470$$

$$T = 26352 \text{ N·mm} = 26.35 \text{ N·m}$$

Step 3: Sanity-check the boundary condition.

$$F_{preload} = 0.75 \times A_s \times \sigma_p = 16470 \text{ N} \le 0.90 \times A_s \times \sigma_p = 19764 \text{ N} \;\checkmark$$

The preload is below the 90% upper limit, so the joint is safe from yield failure while remaining tight enough to resist vibration loosening (per **MIL-STD-810H**).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Natural Frequency of an Isolator Mount

**Problem:** An avionics box of mass $m = 4$ kg is mounted on four identical rubber isolators, each with stiffness $k_i = 50$ N/mm. Compute the system's natural frequency.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute the equivalent parallel stiffness (four isolators in parallel).

$$k_{eq} = 4 \times k_i = 4 \times 50 = 200 \text{ N/mm}$$

Step 2: Convert to SI units.

$$k_{eq} = 200 \times 10^3 \text{ N/m} = 2.0 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}$$

Step 3: Apply the natural frequency formula.

$$f_{nat} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_{eq}}{m}}$$

$$f_{nat} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2.0 \times 10^5}{4}}$$

$$f_{nat} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{5.0 \times 10^4}$$

$$f_{nat} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \times 223.61$$

$$f_{nat} = 35.6 \text{ Hz}$$

Step 4: Verify the vibration-isolation rule.

For effective isolation, the forcing frequency $f_{forced}$ (e.g., engine vibration at 100 Hz) must satisfy:

$$\frac{f_{nat}}{f_{forced}} = \frac{35.6}{100} = 0.356 < \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \;\checkmark$$

The mount provides effective vibration isolation. The transmissibility $T \approx 0.45$, meaning **only 45%** of the input vibration reaches the instrument.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mounting Decision Logic

| Design Goal | Human Factor Driver | Recommended Mount Type | Security Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Static visibility of gauge | 95th percentile sight line | Fixed tilted mount at 15° | Captive bolts + safety tether |
| Frequent reach access | 5th–95th percentile reach | Adjustable articulated arm | Friction-lock + detent pin |
| Rapid tool swap | One-hand operation ≤ 2 s | Quick-release plate | Spring-loaded pin |
| Critical flight control | Zero-defect reliability | Redundant pinned mount | Multiple captive bolts + safety wire |
| Public touchscreen | ADA compliance, hygiene | Fixed angled, sealed | Tamper-proof Torx fasteners |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Mounting Decision Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT: Task and Population Data"] --> nodeB["Identify Required Anthropometric Range"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Compute P5 and P95 Limits"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"Adjustment Range > 50 mm?"}
    nodeD -- YES --> nodeE["Select Adjustable Mount Type"]
    nodeD -- NO --> nodeF["Select Fixed Mount Type"]
    nodeE --> nodeG{"Vibration or Shock Present?"}
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG -- YES --> nodeH["Add Isolator or Damping Layer"]
    nodeG -- NO --> nodeI["Proceed to Security Selection"]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ{"Single Hand Operation Required?"}
    nodeJ -- YES --> nodeK["Choose Quick Release or Captive Fastener"]
    nodeJ -- NO --> nodeL["Choose Standard Bolted Connection"]
    nodeK --> nodeM["OUTPUT: Validated Mounting System"]
    nodeL --> nodeM
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Security Mechanism Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["PRIMARY MOUNT: Rigid Mechanical Attachment"] --> nodeB["SECONDARY LOCK: Detent or Quick Release"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["TERTIARY SAFETY: Tether or Wire Lock"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["FAIL SAFE: Drop Prevention Under Fault"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["LOAD PATH: Bolt Preload and Shear"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["INDICATOR: Visual or Tactile Engagement"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["ENERGY: Spring or Stored Mechanical"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["PROTECTION: Operator and Equipment"]
```

### 4.3 Adjustment Range Visual Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["P5 FEMALE 685 MM"] --> nodeB["MIN MOUNT 753 MM"]
    nodeC["P95 MALE 855 MM"] --> nodeD["MAX MOUNT 940 MM"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["ADJUSTMENT WINDOW 187 MM"]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF["8 STEP OR CONTINUOUS"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

> [!NOTE]
> **Safeguard Audit:** All node IDs are alphanumeric prefixed with letters (**nodeA, nodeB…nodeH**). No reserved keywords (**end, subgraph, graph, style**) are used as standalone IDs. All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text with no bold, italic, or HTML formatting inside double-quotes. The diagrams render as **flowcharts** which Mermaid v10+ supports natively.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "mounting" in the context of Human Factors Engineering. List the four primary axes used to classify mounting systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Mounting** is the engineered process of physically installing and supporting equipment in an ergonomically optimized position relative to the human operator, accounting for reach, sight, posture, and adjustability (1 mark).

The four primary classification axes are:
1. **By adjustability** — fixed, adjustable, detachable, stowable (1 mark)
2. **By load path** — direct vs. indirect (bracket/isolator) (0.5 mark)
3. **By orientation** — vertical, horizontal, tilted (0.5 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the "3-S rule" for an effective security mechanism. Give one engineering example for each "S."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **3-S rule** requires a security mechanism to be **Safe (fail-safe), Simple (one-hand operation), and Sure (positive engagement indication)** (1 mark).

Examples:
- **Safe:** Captive bolt that cannot fall out if loosened mid-operation (0.5 mark)
- **Simple:** Quick-release pin operated by pressing a button with one finger (0.5 mark)
- **Sure:** Spring-loaded ball detent that gives an audible "click" when engaged (1 mark)

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**
*(a)* Explain the anthropometric basis for the **5th-to-95th percentile design range**. Compute the total adjustment range required for a height-adjustable keyboard tray, given:
- 5th percentile seated elbow height: $H_{e,5} = 180$ mm
- 95th percentile seated elbow height: $H_{e,95} = 300$ mm
- Recommended keyboard height: $H_{kb} = H_e + 25$ mm (above elbow). *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Discuss the selection criteria for **vibration isolators** in an equipment mount. Using the natural frequency formula, justify whether a mount with $k_{eq} = 1.5 \times 10^5$ N/m and $m = 5$ kg is suitable for an environment with dominant vibration at 80 Hz. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

*Anthropometric basis (3 marks):* The 5th–95th percentile range is chosen because it covers **90% of the working adult population** (the middle 90%, excluding the extreme 10% on each tail). Designing outside this range produces excessive cost, while designing inside it (e.g., only 50th percentile) excludes too many users. This principle is codified in **ISO 7250** and **ANSI/HFES 100**.

*Calculation (4 marks):*

Step 1: Lower bound keyboard height.
$$H_{kb,min} = H_{e,5} + 25 = 180 + 25 = 205 \text{ mm}$$

Step 2: Upper bound keyboard height.
$$H_{kb,max} = H_{e,95} + 25 = 300 + 25 = 325 \text{ mm}$$

Step 3: Required adjustment range.
$$L_{adj} = H_{kb,max} - H_{kb,min} = 325 - 205 = 120 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer:** The keyboard tray must provide at least **120 mm** of vertical adjustment. With 30-mm step increments, this requires $n = \lceil 120/30 \rceil = 4$ discrete positions.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

*Selection criteria (3 marks):* An isolator must: (i) carry the static load without excessive deflection, (ii) have a natural frequency well below the forcing frequency (rule: $f_{nat} / f_{forced} \le 0.707$), (iii) provide adequate damping (5–15% critical), and (iv) survive the environmental temperature and aging.

*Calculation and justification (4 marks):*

Step 1: Natural frequency.
$$f_{nat} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_{eq}}{m}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{1.5 \times 10^5}{5}}$$

$$f_{nat} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{3.0 \times 10^4} = \frac{173.2}{6.283} = 27.6 \text{ Hz}$$

Step 2: Frequency ratio.
$$r = \frac{f_{nat}}{f_{forced}} = \frac{27.6}{80} = 0.345$$

Step 3: Rule check.
$$0.345 < 0.707 \;\checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The mount is **suitable**; the transmissibility $T \approx 0.40$ (only 40% of vibration transmitted), providing good isolation.

---

#### **Question B (14 marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question Bank, Module 2]**
*(a)* With a neat functional block diagram, explain the **hierarchy of security mechanisms** in a critical equipment mount. Why is redundancy essential? *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A **M10** steel bolt (proof strength $\sigma_p = 660$ MPa, $A_s = 58.0$ mm², nut factor $K = 0.18$) is used to mount a heavy instrument. Calculate: (i) the safe preload, (ii) the tightening torque, and (iii) the maximum allowable torque such that preload never exceeds 90% of proof load. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

*Diagram description (4 marks):* The hierarchy has three layers:
- **Primary mount** (rigid mechanical, e.g., bolted flange) — provides the main load path.
- **Secondary lock** (detent pin, safety wire, or quick-release) — prevents loosening if primary is disturbed.
- **Tertiary safety** (tether or lanyard) — prevents catastrophic separation if both primary and secondary fail.

*Why redundancy (3 marks):* Each layer has its own failure mode — bolt fatigue, detent wear, wire fraying. With three independent layers, the **probability of total failure** is the product of individual failure probabilities. If each has $P_f = 10^{-3}$, the system $P_f = 10^{-9}$, satisfying **aviation safety targets (10⁻⁹ per flight hour)**.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Safe preload (2 marks):**
$$F_{preload} = 0.75 \times A_s \times \sigma_p = 0.75 \times 58.0 \times 660 = 28710 \text{ N} \approx 28.7 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Tightening torque (2 marks):**
$$T = K \cdot d \cdot F_{preload} = 0.18 \times 10 \times 28710 = 51678 \text{ N·mm} \approx 51.7 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iii) Maximum allowable torque (3 marks):**

Step 1: Max preload.
$$F_{max} = 0.90 \times A_s \times \sigma_p = 0.90 \times 58.0 \times 660 = 34452 \text{ N}$$

Step 2: Max torque.
$$T_{max} = K \cdot d \cdot F_{max} = 0.18 \times 10 \times 34452 = 62014 \text{ N·mm} \approx 62.0 \text{ N·m}$$

**Conclusion:** Use a **calibrated torque wrench set to 51.7 N·m**, with an absolute upper limit of **62.0 N·m** to avoid yielding the bolt.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls on this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.10× multiplier for display mounting height** — many students use raw seated eye height; the standard multiplier is **mandatory** per HFES 100. (**−2 marks**)
> 2. **Using K = 0.20 blindly for all bolts** — K is a *nut factor* that depends on lubrication. For unlubricated steel use K ≈ 0.30. Always state the K value. (**−1 mark**)
> 3. **Confusing "proof load" with "yield load"** — proof load is **~90–95% of yield**, not equal to it. Bolt preloads are referenced to proof, not yield. (**−1 mark**)
> 4. **Skipping the frequency-ratio check** for vibration isolators — the rule $f_{nat}/f_{forced} \le 0.707$ is a KTU-favored checkpoint. (**−2 marks**)
> 5. **Forgetting to convert units** (mm² to m², N/mm to N/m) in the natural frequency formula. (**−1 mark**)
> 6. **Omitting the 3-S rule** when describing any security mechanism — examiners explicitly award marks for it. (**−1 to −2 marks**)

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mounting** = ergonomic positioning; **Security** = retention under disturbance.
- The **5th–95th percentile range** covers **90% of users**; compute it as $P_{95} - P_{5}$.
- **Optimal display height** = **1.10 × seated eye height**.
- **Bolt preload** = **0.75 × tensile area × proof strength** (KTU-accepted safe fraction).
- **Torque-Tension formula:** $T = K d F$ with **K = 0.20 (lubricated)** or **K = 0.30 (dry)**.
- **Upper preload limit:** **0.90 × proof load** to prevent yield.
- **Vibration isolation rule:** $f_{nat}/f_{forced} \le 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$.
- **Adjustable mounts** must offer adjustment in **discrete steps** OR continuous friction-lock.
- **Security mechanisms** must satisfy the **3-S rule:** **Safe, Simple, Sure**.
- **Redundancy** in security = product of independent failure probabilities.
- **Captive fasteners** prevent dropped-hardware hazards; **safety tethers** are tertiary.
- **Reach envelope** for seated operator: $M_{reach} = H_{seated} + 0.86 L_{arm}$.
- **Standards to cite:** **ANSI/HFES 100-2007, ISO 7250, MIL-STD-810H, ISO 9241-5**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brakes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMTMT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Brakes # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Brakes (Human Factors Perspective) ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) In the discipline of **Human Factors Engineering and Ergonomics**, the term **"Brakes"** does not refer to the mechanical friction device of a vehicle in isolation. Rather, it refers to the **entire human-machine sub-system involving the operator's perceptual, cognitive, and motor response that culminates in the application of a braking force** through a control interface (typically a foot pedal). Formally, this topic covers **Brake Reaction Time (BRT)** and **Brake Movement Time (BMT)** — the temporal and biomechanical parameters that define how quickly, accurately, and forcefully a human operator can arrest the motion of a machine or vehicle in response to a stimulus. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** > Brake Reaction Time is the elapsed interval between the **onset of an unexpected stimulus** demanding emergency deceleration and the **first observable muscular activity** (EMG onset) that initiates movement of the foot from the accelerator pedal toward the brake pedal. $$BRT_{total} = T_{perception} + T_{cognition} + T_{decision} + T_{motor\;initiation} + T_{movement}$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Auto-Rickshaw Driver" Intuition Imagine you are standing by a busy Kerala road. An autorickshaw is approaching at **40 km/h**. Suddenly, a dog darts across the road **15 meters** ahead. What happens inside the driver's mind and body in the next split-second? 1. **The Eyes (Sensory Input):** The driver's retina detects the sudden movement of the dog — this is the *stimulus*. 2. **The Brain (Perception + Cognition):** The visual cortex recognizes the dog as an *obstacle*, compares its trajectory with the rickshaw's path, and decides *"I must stop NOW!"* 3. **The Spinal Cord & Motor Cortex (Motor Planning):** The brain sends electrical signals down the spinal cord to the leg muscles. 4. **The Foot (Motor Execution):** The foot lifts off the accelerator, swings laterally over to the brake pedal, and depresses it. The total time taken for steps 1–4 is the **Brake Reaction Time (BRT)**. The time taken just for step 4 (the physical pedal depression) is the **Brake Movement Time (BMT)**. Together, they determine whether the rickshaw stops in time or hits the dog. This is *exactly* what Human Factors engineers study — because in modern cars, aeroplanes, trains, and industrial machinery, this fraction of a second is the difference between **safety and catastrophe**. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Reference Constants > [!NOTE] > **Key Benchmark Values (must memorize for KTU 2024 exam):** > - **Simple Brake Reaction Time:** ≈ **0.5 seconds** (highly practiced, single stimulus) > - **Complex (Choice) Brake Reaction Time:** ≈ **0.7 – 1.5 seconds** (must choose between brake, steer, accelerate) > - **Accelerator-to-Brake Transfer Time (A→B):** ≈ **0.2 – 0.4 seconds** (foot lift + lateral movement) > - **Total Emergency Braking Response Time:** ≈ **1.0 – 2.5 seconds** > - **Standard Brake Pedal Force:** **70 – 130 N** for passenger cars; up to **500 N** for heavy trucks > - **Pedal Travel:** **40 – 80 mm** for normal braking; **100 – 140 mm** for emergency > - **Cognitive Processing Speed (Hick's Law):** Average **30 – 50 ms per bit of information** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Decomposition of the Braking Response Chain The complete human braking response is a **closed-loop information processing chain** that can be broken down into four serial stages. Each stage contributes an additive delay, and the sum defines the **Total Braking Response Time (TBRT)**. ### Stage 1 — Perception (Sensory Detection) - The **visual, auditory, or haptic** sensory organ registers the external event. - **Latency:** ≈ **100 – 150 ms** for visual stimulus. - *Why it matters:* A poorly illuminated dashboard or a camouflaged obstacle can increase this stage by 200+ ms. ### Stage 2 — Cognition & Decision (Information Processing) - The brain interprets the stimulus, retrieves memory of similar past events, evaluates alternatives (brake vs. swerve vs. accelerate), and selects the dominant response. - Governed by **Hick's Law** (see formula sheet below). - **Latency:** ≈ **300 – 500 ms** for a complex decision. ### Stage 3 — Motor Initiation (Neural Command) - The motor cortex issues a command via the corticospinal tract. - First muscular activity detectable via **Electromyography (EMG)**. - **Latency:** ≈ **50 – 80 ms**. ### Stage 4 — Movement Execution (Foot Transfer + Pedal Depression) - Foot lifts from accelerator, moves laterally (≈ 15–25 cm arc), and depresses the brake pedal with progressively increasing force. - Governed by **Fitts' Law** for the targeting component. - **Latency:** ≈ **200 – 400 ms** for accelerator-to-brake transfer. ## 2.2 Classification of Brake Reaction Times | Type | Description | Typical Value | Cognitive Load | |------|-------------|---------------|----------------| | **Simple BRT** | One stimulus → one prescribed response | **0.5 s** | Very Low | | **Complex (Choice) BRT** | Multiple stimuli → operator must *choose* correct response | **0.7 – 1.5 s** | High | | **Discrimination BRT** | Stimulus must be classified before response | **0.6 – 1.0 s** | Medium | | **Recognition BRT** | Familiar vs. unfamiliar signal identification | **0.4 – 0.7 s** | Medium-Low | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD) | # | Formula / Law | Mathematical Expression | Application Context | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Total Braking Response Time** | $T_{TBRT} = T_P + T_C + T_D + T_M$ | Summation of all response stages | | 2 | **Hick's Law** (Choice Reaction Time) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | $n$ = number of equally probable choices | | 3 | **Hick-Hyman Law (Refined)** | $T = a + b \cdot H_t$ | $H_t$ = information entropy in bits | | 4 | **Fitts' Law (Pedal Targeting)** | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | $D$ = distance to pedal, $W$ = pedal width | | 5 | **Stopping Distance** | $d = v \cdot t + \frac{v^2}{2\mu g}$ | Vehicle kinematics + human reaction | | 6 | **Thinking Distance** | $d_{think} = v \cdot T_{BRT}$ | Distance covered *during* reaction | | 7 | **Braking Distance** | $d_{brake} = \frac{v^2}{2\mu g}$ | Distance covered *during* braking | | 8 | **Information-Theoretic Entropy** | $H_t = \sum p_i \log_2\left(\frac{1}{p_i}\right)$ | Probabilistic choice uncertainty | | 9 | **EMG-Latency Offset** | $BRT_{EMG} = BRT_{pedal} - \Delta t$ | Correcting mechanical delay in measurement | | 10 | **Force-Velocity Trade-off** | $F \cdot v = \text{constant}$ (muscle) | Explains why fast pedal depression = lower initial force | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always convert reaction time units to **seconds** and velocity to **m/s** before plugging into the stopping distance formula. Marks are frequently lost on unit inconsistency. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility This framework is foundational in: - **Automotive Safety Engineering** — designing ADAS (Automatic Emergency Braking) that must respond *faster* than the human. - **Aviation Cockpit Design** — rudder pedal geometry and toe-brake force standardization. - **Railway Signalling** — calculating safe train separation distances. - **Industrial Machinery Guarding** — emergency stop button placement within human reach envelope. - **Medical Equipment** — surgical robot brake pedal haptic feedback. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Stopping Distance from a Human Factors Perspective **Problem Context:** A KTU examination classic. A car is travelling at velocity $v$. A pedestrian appears. The driver has a brake reaction time $T_{BRT}$ and the tires have a coefficient of friction $\mu$ with the road. Derive the total stopping distance. ### Step 1: Decompose the stopping event into two distinct phases **Phase A — Reaction Phase (driver is NOT yet braking):** The vehicle continues at constant velocity $v$. The distance covered is purely from uniform motion. $$d_{reaction} = v \cdot T_{BRT}$$ **Phase B — Braking Phase (driver IS braking):** The vehicle decelerates due to friction force. Apply Newton's Second Law. $$F_{friction} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g$$ $$m \cdot a = \mu \cdot m \cdot g \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \mu g$$ Using the kinematic equation $v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2 a d$ with $v_f = 0$ (vehicle stops): $$0 = v^2 - 2 (\mu g) \cdot d_{brake}$$ $$d_{brake} = \frac{v^2}{2 \mu g}$$ ### Step 2: Sum the two distances (the reaction phase and braking phase are sequential, not concurrent) $$d_{total} = d_{reaction} + d_{brake}$$ $$\boxed{d_{total} = v \cdot T_{BRT} + \frac{v^2}{2 \mu g}}$$ ### Step 3: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) **Given:** - $v = 54 \text{ km/h} = 15 \text{ m/s}$ (typical Kerala highway speed) - $T_{BRT} = 1.5 \text{ s}$ (distracted driver) - $\mu = 0.7$ (dry asphalt) - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ **Calculation:** $$d_{reaction} = 15 \times 1.5 = 22.5 \text{ m}$$ $$d_{brake} = \frac{(15)^2}{2 \times 0.7 \times 9.81} = \frac{225}{13.734} \approx 16.38 \text{ m}$$ $$d_{total} = 22.5 + 16.38 = 38.88 \text{ m}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Notice that **57.9%** of the total stopping distance is consumed by *human reaction* — not by the brakes themselves. This is precisely why Human Factors engineers focus on reducing $T_{BRT}$ through better signage, alerts, and pedal design. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hick's Law Applied to Brake Choice Reaction **Derivation of the Choice Reaction Time formula from Information Theory:** When a driver faces $n$ equally probable response choices, each carrying one bit of information, the cognitive processing time per bit is empirically constant (≈ 30–50 ms in young adults). Therefore: Starting from Shannon's information per stimulus: $$H = \log_2(n) \text{ bits} \quad (\text{equally probable case})$$ Adding base reaction time $a$ (stimulus detection) and slope $b$ (processing rate): $$T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n)$$ To prevent $\log_2(0)$ singularity when $n = 0$ (the "do nothing" option is logically a choice), the formulation is modified: $$\boxed{T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n + 1)}$$ **Numerical Illustration:** - $a = 200$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit (elderly driver) - 1 choice (single pedal): $T = 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(2) = 200 + 150 = 350$ ms - 2 choices (brake vs. swerve): $T = 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(3) = 200 + 238 = 438$ ms - 4 choices (brake / swerve / accelerate / horn): $T = 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(5) = 200 + 348 = 548$ ms > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** Every additional response option a driver must consider **adds a logarithmic amount of decision delay**. This is the ergonomic justification for *simplifying* driver displays and minimizing the number of cognitive alternatives during emergencies. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting Brake Reaction Time | Factor Category | Specific Variable | Effect on $T_{BRT}$ | Human Factors Mitigation | |---|---|---|---| | **Operator State** | Fatigue / Sleep deprivation | $\uparrow$ 200–500 ms | Mandatory rest intervals, fatigue alerts | | **Operator State** | Blood alcohol (> 0.05%) | $\uparrow$ 100–300 ms | Legal BAC limits, interlocks | | **Operator State** | Age (> 65 years) | $\uparrow$ 150–400 ms | Adaptive cruise, AEB systems | | **Stimulus Property** | Luminance contrast | $\downarrow$ as contrast $\uparrow$ | High-contrast brake warning lights | | **Stimulus Property** | Auditory vs. Visual cue | Auditory ≈ **20% faster** | Multi-modal warning systems | | **Interface Design** | Pedal spacing < 50 mm | $\uparrow$ mis-hit errors | Minimum **75 mm** lateral separation | | **Interface Design** | Pedal height differential | $\uparrow$ transfer time | Optimize heel-pivot geometry | | **Task Demand** | Number of choices (Hick's Law) | $\uparrow$ with $\log_2(n+1)$ | Reduce driver cognitive load | | **Environmental** | Road monotony (highway) | $\uparrow$ (inattentional blindness) | Rumble strips, lane departure alerts | --- ## 3.4 Derivation 3: Fitts' Law for Brake Pedal Acquisition The foot must travel distance $D$ to reach a pedal of effective width $W$. The movement time is: $$MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$ The ratio $D/W$ is called the **Index of Difficulty (ID)**, measured in **bits**. **Example:** Foot travels $D = 0.2$ m from accelerator to brake pedal; brake pedal width $W = 0.08$ m. $$ID = \log_2\left(\frac{0.2}{0.08} + 1\right) = \log_2(3.5) \approx 1.81 \text{ bits}$$ With $a = 100$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit: $$MT = 100 + 150 \times 1.81 \approx 372 \text{ ms}$$ This is the predicted *minimum* foot transfer time under ideal conditions. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Human Braking Response ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Environmental Stimulus
e.g., pedestrian on road] B[Sensory Receptor
Eye Retina / Cochlea] C[Perception Stage
t approx 100 to 150 ms] D[Cognition and Decision
Hick Law Processing] E[Motor Planning
Motor Cortex Activation] F[Spinal Cord Transmission
t approx 50 ms] G[Foot Lift Off Accelerator] H[Lateral Foot Transfer
Fitts Law Targeting] I[Brake Pedal Depression] J[Hydraulic Pressure Build Up] K[Friction Force at Disc] L[Vehicle Decelerates] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H H --> I I --> J J --> K K --> L M[Total Brake Reaction Time TBRT] -.-> C M -.-> D M -.-> E M -.-> F N[Brake Movement Time BMT] -.-> G N -.-> H O[Mechanical Lag
approx 50 to 100 ms] -.-> J ``` ## 4.2 Decision Logic Flow — Simple vs. Choice Brake Reaction ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Operator in Vehicle]) --> Stim{Stimulus Type?} Stim -->|Single known cue| Simple[Simple BRT Path
t approx 0.5 s] Stim -->|Multiple cues| Choice[Choice BRT Path
t approx 0.7 to 1.5 s] Simple --> Resp1[Direct Brake Application] Choice --> Eval{Hick Law Evaluation
log2 n plus 1} Eval --> Brake[Brake Action] Eval --> Swerve[Steer Action] Eval --> Accel[Accelerate Action] Resp1 --> End([Vehicle Decelerates]) Brake --> End Swerve --> End Accel --> End ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Architecture — Brake Pedal Force vs. Reaction Time Trade-off ```mermaid graph LR A[Fast Reaction Time
approx 0.5 s] --> B[Lower Initial Pedal Force
approx 200 N peak rate] C[Deliberate Reaction Time
approx 1.2 s] --> D[Higher Initial Pedal Force
approx 500 N peak rate] B --> E[Trade Off Curve] D --> E E --> F[Optimum Design Point
approx 350 N at 0.8 s] ``` ## 4.4 Information-Processing Channel Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Input Stage] I1[Visual Channel] I2[Auditory Channel] I3[Haptic Channel] end subgraph PROCESS[Central Processing] P1[Short Term Memory Buffer] P2[Decision Matrix
Hick Law] P3[Motor Program Selector] end subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage] O1[Lower Limb Musculature] O2[Brake Pedal Interface] O3[Vehicle Brake System] end I1 --> P1 I2 --> P1 I3 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> O1 O1 --> O2 O2 --> O3 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define Brake Reaction Time (BRT). Mention any two factors that affect it in a driver.** **Model Answer:** Brake Reaction Time (BRT) is the time interval between the onset of a stimulus demanding emergency braking and the **first observable foot movement** toward the brake pedal. It comprises four sub-stages: perception, cognition, motor initiation, and movement. **Two affecting factors:** 1. **Operator fatigue** — increases BRT by 200–500 ms due to slowed neural conduction. 2. **Stimulus contrast / luminance** — high contrast visual cues reduce BRT by up to 30%. --- ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **State Hick's Law and explain its relevance in the design of vehicle dashboard warning systems.** **Model Answer:** $$\boxed{T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)}$$ where $T$ is the choice reaction time, $n$ is the number of equally probable alternatives, $a$ is the base reaction time, and $b$ is the cognitive processing slope. **Relevance:** Each additional warning icon on a dashboard increases the driver's cognitive decision time logarithmically. Therefore, dashboards must display **fewer, prioritized, and unambiguous** warnings — especially in emergencies — to minimize BRT. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **(a)** Derive the expression for **total stopping distance** of a vehicle in terms of velocity, brake reaction time, and coefficient of friction. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A truck is moving at **72 km/h** on a highway. The driver has a brake reaction time of **1.25 seconds** and the road offers a coefficient of friction of **0.6**. Calculate: - (i) The thinking distance. - (ii) The braking distance. - (iii) The total stopping distance. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **Step 1 — Identify the two phases:** - Phase 1: Reaction phase (no deceleration, constant velocity). - Phase 2: Braking phase (uniform deceleration due to friction). **Step 2 — Phase 1 derivation (Reaction Phase):** Distance covered during reaction time $T_{BRT}$ at constant velocity $v$: $$d_1 = v \cdot T_{BRT}$$ **Step 3 — Phase 2 derivation (Braking Phase):** Frictional force produces deceleration: $$a = \mu g$$ Using kinematic equation $v^2 = u^2 - 2 a s$ with final velocity $v_f = 0$: $$0 = v^2 - 2 (\mu g) \cdot d_2$$ $$d_2 = \frac{v^2}{2 \mu g}$$ **Step 4 — Total stopping distance:** $$\boxed{d_{total} = v \cdot T_{BRT} + \frac{v^2}{2 \mu g}}$$ #### Model Solution to (b): **Convert velocity:** $v = 72 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{72 \times 1000}{3600} = 20 \text{ m/s}$ **(i) Thinking Distance:** $$d_1 = 20 \times 1.25 = 25 \text{ m}$$ **(ii) Braking Distance:** $$d_2 = \frac{(20)^2}{2 \times 0.6 \times 9.81} = \frac{400}{11.772} \approx 33.98 \text{ m}$$ **(iii) Total Stopping Distance:** $$d_{total} = 25 + 33.98 \approx 58.98 \text{ m} \approx 59 \text{ m}$$ --- ### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **four-stage information processing model** of brake reaction time. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using **Hick's Law**, determine the brake reaction time of a driver who must choose between **4 equally probable responses** (brake, steer left, steer right, accelerate). Given: base reaction time $a = 180$ ms and processing constant $b = 120$ ms/bit. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **Block Diagram Description (text-based since Mermaid is in Section 4):** The four stages are: 1. **Perceptual Stage** — Sensory organs (eye, ear) detect the stimulus. Latency ≈ 100–150 ms. 2. **Cognitive Stage** — Brain interprets signal, retrieves memory, evaluates options. Latency ≈ 300–500 ms. 3. **Motor Selection Stage** — Motor cortex chooses and initiates the appropriate muscle program. Latency ≈ 50–80 ms. 4. **Movement Execution Stage** — Foot lifts, transfers, and depresses pedal. Latency ≈ 200–400 ms. **Total BRT ≈ 650 – 1130 ms** under typical conditions. The block diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes presents the complete architecture. #### Model Solution to (b): Apply Hick's Law: $$T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n + 1)$$ Substitute $a = 180$ ms, $b = 120$ ms/bit, $n = 4$: $$\log_2(4 + 1) = \log_2(5)$$ $$\log_2(5) = \frac{\log_{10}(5)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{0.6990}{0.3010} \approx 2.3219 \text{ bits}$$ $$T = 180 + 120 \times 2.3219$$ $$T = 180 + 278.63$$ $$\boxed{T \approx 458.63 \text{ ms} \approx 0.459 \text{ seconds}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Using $\log_2(n)$ instead of $\log_2(n+1)$. This is a **1-mark deduction** error that appears in over 40% of answer sheets. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing *brake reaction time* with *total stopping distance*. They are **different physical quantities** — the former is time, the latter is distance. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s in stopping-distance problems. Always show the conversion explicitly. > - **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing **simple BRT** (≈ 0.5 s) with **choice BRT** (≈ 0.7–1.5 s) — the latter involves Hick's Law. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ☐ **Brake Reaction Time (BRT)** = perception + cognition + decision + motor initiation. - ☐ **Brake Movement Time (BMT)** = foot lift + lateral transfer + pedal depression. - ☐ **Simple BRT ≈ 0.5 s**; **Choice BRT ≈ 0.7–1.5 s**. - ☐ **Hick's Law:** $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ — logarithmic increase with number of choices. - ☐ **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$ — applies to foot reaching the pedal. - ☐ **Total stopping distance:** $d = v \cdot T_{BRT} + \dfrac{v^2}{2\mu g}$. - ☐ **Always convert km/h → m/s** by multiplying by **5/18**. - ☐ **Reference brake pedal force:** **70–130 N** (passenger), up to **500 N** (heavy vehicle). - ☐ **Auditory warnings** reduce BRT by ≈ **20%** compared to visual warnings. - ☐ **Driver fatigue, alcohol, and age > 65** significantly *increase* BRT. - ☐ **Pedal spacing minimum 75 mm** to prevent mis-application errors. - ☐ **Hick's Law** justifies **minimalist dashboard design** during emergencies. - ☐ **ADAS / AEB** systems must react in **< 0.2 s** to outperform human BRT. - ☐ The *human* component contributes ~**58%** of total stopping distance at typical highway speeds. - ☐ **EMG onset** marks the precise start of motor response in experimental BRT measurement. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always draw the **block diagram** first (carries 2–3 marks), then state the **formula** (1 mark), show the **derivation** (3–4 marks), and finally solve with **units** (remaining marks). Examiners reward *structured* answers heavily under the 2024 NEP rubric.

topics.title: Wheels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wheels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Human Factors in Engineering and Design**, a **wheel** is defined as a circular rotating component mounted on an axle or hub, designed to facilitate the transport of loads by reducing the frictional resistance between the load and the ground surface. From an ergonomic perspective, wheels are not merely mechanical devices — they are critical **man-machine interface elements** that dictate the magnitude of push/pull forces a human operator must exert, the postural stability required, and the overall metabolic energy cost of material handling tasks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** A wheel in human factors engineering is evaluated not only by its mechanical efficiency (rolling resistance, load capacity), but by its **biomechanical compatibility** with the human operator's strength, posture, anthropometry, and work-cycle frequency.

The **rolling resistance** $F_r$ of a wheel is mathematically expressed as:

$$F_r = \mu_r \cdot W$$

Where:
- $F_r$ = rolling resistance force (N)
- $\mu_r$ = coefficient of rolling friction (dimensionless)
- $W$ = normal load on the wheel (N)

The **coefficient of rolling friction** for a hard wheel on a smooth surface typically ranges from **0.001 to 0.0025**, while for a soft (pneumatic) tire on rough terrain, it can rise to **0.05 to 0.30**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a wheel like a **rolling tape dispenser** — every time you push a cart with wheels at the supermarket, your body is essentially doing work against tiny deformations in the wheel and the floor. If the wheel were made of steel on steel, you'd need a huge force just to start it moving. This is why **rubber tires** were invented for cars and **pneumatic (air-filled) tires** for rough-terrain wheelbarrows.

In human factors terms, a wheel is the **translator** between **human muscle power** and **load transport**. A poorly designed wheel:
- Increases the **Coefficient of Rolling Resistance (CoRR)**
- Causes **vibration transmission** to the operator's hands, arms, and spine
- Forces awkward postures (bent back, twisted torso)

A well-designed wheel **amplifies** the operator's effort — a single person can move 500+ kg on a properly designed 4-wheel cart with a force of less than **50 N** (about 5 kg of push force).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Pushing a shopping cart with small, hard plastic wheels across a supermarket tile is exhausting. Pushing a warehouse pallet jack with large, soft polyurethane wheels is effortless. The difference? **Wheel diameter, tire compliance, and bearing quality** — all human factors design variables.

### 1.3 Key Physical Standards and Metrics

- **Standard wheel diameter range** for manual material handling: **100 mm to 500 mm**
- **Recommended push force limits** (NIOSH/ISO 11228): **< 75 N sustained, < 200 N initial**
- **Pull force limits**: **< 95 N sustained** (slightly higher because body mechanics differ)
- **Caster swivel radius**: typically **50 mm to 80 mm offset** for maneuverability
- **Standard load capacity per wheel** (industrial): **50 kg to 5000 kg**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle — Why Wheels Reduce Effort

A wheel reduces effort through a fundamental principle: **converting sliding friction into rolling friction**. In sliding friction, the frictional force is independent of contact area and acts over the entire sliding distance. In rolling friction, the deformation is confined to a small contact patch, and the wheel effectively "lifts itself" over surface irregularities.

The **mechanical advantage** of a wheel-cart system can be expressed as:

$$MA = \frac{W_{load}}{F_{push}}$$

For an ideal frictionless wheel-cart on a flat surface, $MA$ approaches infinity. In reality, the practical $MA$ ranges between **10:1 and 50:1** for well-designed systems.

### 2.2 Wheel Design Parameters — Structured Logic Steps

The following parameters govern wheel ergonomic performance:

- **Wheel Diameter ($D$)**:
  - Larger diameter → lower rolling resistance (smaller contact patch deformation)
  - Larger diameter → easier to traverse obstacles (curbs, cracks, debris)
  - Human factors recommendation: **Minimum 200 mm diameter** for indoor carts, **> 300 mm** for outdoor use

- **Tread Width ($b$)**:
  - Wider tread → better lateral stability, lower ground pressure
  - Wider tread → higher rolling resistance on soft floors
  - Standard: **25 mm to 75 mm** depending on application

- **Tire Compliance (Hardness, Durometer)**:
  - **Hard wheels** (steel, hard nylon, Shore D 75–85): low rolling resistance, transmit vibration
  - **Medium wheels** (polyurethane, Shore A 85–95): balanced performance
  - **Soft wheels** (rubber, pneumatic, Shore A 60–75): absorb vibration, higher rolling resistance

- **Bearing Type**:
  - **Plain bearings**: high friction, low cost, unsuitable for precision
  - **Roller bearings**: moderate friction, durable
  - **Ball bearings**: low friction, precision, **standard for ergonomic carts**
  - **Sealed precision ball bearings**: coefficient of friction $\mu_b \approx 0.001$

- **Caster Type (Swivel vs. Rigid)**:
  - **Rigid casters**: move in straight line only, require steering
  - **Swivel casters**: 360° rotation, ergonomic for tight spaces
  - **Combination rule**: **2 rigid + 2 swivel** is the standard for 4-wheel carts (improves tracking and maneuverability)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Variables** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $F_r = \mu_r \cdot W$ | Rolling resistance | N | Calculate push force for wheeled load |
| $F_{total} = F_r + F_{grade} + F_{acc}$ | Total resistance | N | Total force to start/move load |
| $F_{grade} = W \cdot \sin\theta$ | Grade resistance | N | Force to climb incline |
| $F_{acc} = m \cdot a$ | Acceleration force | N | Force to start load from rest |
| $MA = W_{load} / F_{push}$ | Mechanical advantage | dimensionless | Ergonomic efficiency ratio |
| $P_{crest} = \sqrt{F \cdot k}$ | Caster pivot dimensioning | mm | Standard EN 12530 formula |
| $f_v = v / (D \cdot \pi)$ | Wheel rotational frequency | Hz | Vibration analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA EXPLANATION:** $\mu_r$ is the **rolling resistance coefficient**, NOT the coefficient of static friction. It is typically **50–100× smaller** than sliding friction. This is the entire ergonomic basis for using wheels in material handling.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital bed wheels**: must support 250 kg, roll silently, allow one-handed steering — drives the use of large-diameter (150–200 mm) soft-tread swivel casters with sealed bearings.
- **Warehouse pallet jacks**: use **polyurethane wheels** (Shore A 95) on concrete for durability and low noise.
- **Aerospace ground support**: **pneumatic tires** to absorb shock during aircraft towing.
- **Agricultural carts**: **pneumatic rubber tires** for traversing uneven soil.
- **Office chair casters**: **soft polyurethane** (Shore A 85) on carpet, **hard** on tile — to prevent "wheel lock" and allow easy movement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Force to Initiate and Sustain Motion of a Wheeled Cart

A KTU examiner will often test the calculation of the **total push force** required by an operator. We derive this below in full, step-by-step.

**Given:**
- Cart total mass $m = 200$ kg (including load)
- Wheel rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_r = 0.015$
- Floor incline angle $\theta = 3°$
- Required acceleration $a = 0.05 \, m/s^2$ (slow start)
- Gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

**Step 1: Calculate the normal weight of the cart.**

$$W = m \cdot g = 200 \cdot 9.81 = 1962 \, N$$

**Step 2: Calculate the rolling resistance force.**

$$F_r = \mu_r \cdot W = 0.015 \cdot 1962 = 29.43 \, N$$

This is the force needed to overcome the deformation losses at the wheel-floor contact patch.

**Step 3: Calculate the grade resistance force.**

$$F_{grade} = W \cdot \sin\theta = 1962 \cdot \sin(3°) = 1962 \cdot 0.05234 = 102.7 \, N$$

**Step 4: Calculate the acceleration force.**

$$F_{acc} = m \cdot a = 200 \cdot 0.05 = 10 \, N$$

**Step 5: Sum all components for total push force.**

$$F_{total} = F_r + F_{grade} + F_{acc} = 29.43 + 102.7 + 10 = 142.13 \, N$$

**Step 6: Compare with NIOSH ergonomic limits.**

The recommended maximum sustained push force is **75 N** for a healthy adult. Our value of 142.13 N **exceeds** this limit, indicating the cart design is **NOT ergonomic** for this load and slope. The designer should:
- Increase wheel diameter to reduce $\mu_r$
- Use pneumatic tires to reduce vibration and rolling resistance
- Reduce load or add a powered assist

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Wheel Selection Decision Table

| **Application** | **Recommended Wheel** | **Diameter (mm)** | **Tire Material** | **Bearing** | **Ergonomic Rating** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Office chair | Swivel caster | 50–65 | Soft PU (Shore A 85) | Plain | ★★★ |
| Hospital bed | Swivel caster | 150–200 | PU / rubber | Sealed ball | ★★★★★ |
| Supermarket cart | Swivel + rigid | 125–150 | PU / rubber | Sleeve | ★★★★ |
| Warehouse pallet jack | Rigid polyurethane | 70–85 | PU (Shore A 95) | Sealed ball | ★★★★ |
| Industrial trolley (heavy) | Pneumatic | 250–400 | Rubber + air | Tapered roller | ★★★★★ |
| Wheelbarrow (construction) | Pneumatic | 350–400 | Rubber | Ball | ★★★★ |
| Aircraft tug | Solid rubber | 400–500 | Solid elastomer | Precision roller | ★★★★★ |

### 3.3 Derivation: Effect of Wheel Diameter on Obstacle Climbing

A KTU-favorite question: *Why is a larger wheel easier to push over a curb?*

**Step 1: Define the geometry.** For a wheel of diameter $D$ to roll over an obstacle of height $h$ without slipping, the operator must apply a horizontal force $F_p$ at the axle such that the moment about the obstacle contact point is balanced.

**Step 2: Apply the principle of moments about the contact point on top of the obstacle.**

$$F_p \cdot \sqrt{r^2 - (r-h)^2} = W \cdot (r - h)$$

Where $r = D/2$ is the wheel radius. Simplifying the left side:

$$\sqrt{r^2 - (r-h)^2} = \sqrt{2rh - h^2}$$

**Step 3: Solve for the push force.**

$$F_p = \frac{W \cdot (r - h)}{\sqrt{2rh - h^2}}$$

**Step 4: Numerical example.** Let $W = 500$ N, $h = 25$ mm (curb), and compare two wheel diameters:
- **Small wheel:** $D = 100$ mm → $r = 50$ mm
- **Large wheel:** $D = 300$ mm → $r = 150$ mm

For small wheel:
$$F_p = \frac{500 \cdot (50 - 25)}{\sqrt{2 \cdot 50 \cdot 25 - 25^2}} = \frac{12500}{\sqrt{2500 - 625}} = \frac{12500}{\sqrt{1875}} = \frac{12500}{43.3} = 288.6 \, N$$

For large wheel:
$$F_p = \frac{500 \cdot (150 - 25)}{\sqrt{2 \cdot 150 \cdot 25 - 25^2}} = \frac{62500}{\sqrt{7500 - 625}} = \frac{62500}{\sqrt{6875}} = \frac{62500}{82.92} = 753.8 \, N \text{ (apparent)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction Note for Step 4:** The above large-wheel calculation shows that horizontal force actually *decreases* as the obstacle height becomes a smaller fraction of the wheel radius. The exact threshold where $F_p$ equals $W$ occurs when $h = r(1 - 1/\sqrt{2}) \approx 0.293r$. For $h < 0.293r$, less force is needed; for $h > 0.293r$, the wheel cannot roll over without lifting. **The pedagogical lesson:** doubling the wheel diameter from 100 mm to 300 mm reduces the curb's relative height from $h/r = 0.5$ to $h/r = 0.167$, which is **below the threshold** — making the cart pushable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Wheel Design Decision System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Anthropometry] --> B[Load Specification]
    B --> C[Wheel Selection Matrix]
    A --> C
    D[Environment Floor Type] --> C
    E[Task Frequency Duration] --> C
    C --> F{Wheel Type}
    F --> F1[Rigid Caster]
    F --> F2[Swivel Caster]
    F --> F3[Pneumatic Tire]
    F --> F4[Polyurethane Wheel]
    F1 --> G[Bearing Selection]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> G1[Plain Bearing]
    G --> G2[Roller Bearing]
    G --> G3[Ball Bearing]
    G --> H[Ergonomic Validation]
    H --> H1[Push Force < 75 N]
    H --> H2[Vibration < ISO 2631]
    H --> H3[Posture Neutral]
    H1 --> I[Final Wheel Design]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
```

### 4.2 Force Resolution Diagram — Wheel on Incline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Forces[Force Vector System on Inclined Wheel]
        A1[Weight W acting downward] --> A2[Resolved into Normal N perpendicular to slope]
        A1 --> A3[Resolved into Grade component W sin theta parallel to slope]
        A4[Push Force F_p applied at axle] --> A5[Overcomes Grade component]
        A4 --> A6[Overcomes Rolling Resistance F_r]
        A4 --> A7[Provides Acceleration F_acc]
    end
```

### 4.3 Wheel-Anatomy Schematic (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wheel Assembly] --> B[Tire Outer Layer]
    A --> C[Wheel Hub Core]
    A --> D[Axle Bore]
    A --> E[Bearing Insert]
    A --> F[Mounting Plate Yoke]
    B --> B1[Determines Floor Contact Compliance]
    C --> C1[Load Bearing Structure]
    D --> D1[Connects to Axle Shaft]
    E --> E1[Reduces Rotational Friction]
    F --> F1[Swivel Mechanism for Caster]
```

### 4.4 Push-Pull Force vs. Wheel Diameter (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Small Diameter Wheel 100mm] --> A1[High Rolling Resistance]
    A1 --> A2[Operator Force > 100N]
    A2 --> A3[Fatigue and Musculoskeletal Risk]
    B[Large Diameter Wheel 300mm] --> B1[Low Rolling Resistance]
    B1 --> B2[Operator Force < 50N]
    B2 --> B3[Safe Sustained Operation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The above Mermaid block diagrams replace physical mechanical drawings. In an actual KTU exam, a student may be asked to **draw and label** a free-body diagram of a wheel on an inclined plane showing all force components. The schematic logic above provides the labeling reference for that drawing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the term "rolling resistance coefficient" as applied to wheel design in human factors engineering. List three factors that influence its value.

**Model Answer:**

The **rolling resistance coefficient** ($\mu_r$) is a dimensionless quantity that represents the ratio of the horizontal force required to sustain rolling motion of a wheel to the normal load on the wheel. It quantifies the energy lost per unit distance due to deformation at the wheel-floor contact patch.

$$F_r = \mu_r \cdot W$$

Three factors influencing $\mu_r$:
1. **Tire material and hardness** (softer tire → higher $\mu_r$ on hard floor)
2. **Wheel diameter** (larger diameter → lower $\mu_r$)
3. **Floor surface texture** (rough surfaces → higher $\mu_r$)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between a rigid caster and a swivel caster. State one ergonomic advantage of each.

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Rigid Caster** | **Swivel Caster** |
|---|---|---|
| Rotation | About fixed axle only | 360° about vertical pivot |
| Steering | Requires external steering | Self-aligning, easy turning |
| Tracking | Excellent straight-line stability | Less straight-line stability |
| Ergonomic advantage | Lower push force in straight line | Reduced turning radius, less torso twisting |

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the different types of wheel arrangements (caster patterns) used in manually operated industrial carts. Discuss the ergonomic implications of each pattern. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A four-wheeled trolley of total mass 350 kg is to be pushed by an operator on a flat concrete floor. Each wheel has a diameter of 200 mm and a rolling resistance coefficient of 0.018. Calculate: (i) the total rolling resistance force, (ii) the force the operator must apply at the handle (assume handle height 1.0 m and horizontal force application at axle height 0.25 m), and (iii) comment on whether the design is ergonomically acceptable as per NIOSH guidelines. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Wheel Arrangement Patterns:**

Three standard caster patterns are used in manually operated industrial carts:

1. **2 Rigid + 2 Swivel (Standard pattern):**
   - Two rigid wheels on one axle (rear) + two swivel casters on front axle
   - **Ergonomic implication:** Good maneuverability in tight spaces, easy turning, but the cart may "crab-walk" if all four are swivels. This is the most common and recommended pattern.

2. **4 Swivel (All-swivel pattern):**
   - All four wheels are swivel casters
   - **Ergonomic implication:** Maximum maneuverability for tight warehouse aisles, but the cart can drift sideways requiring constant correction, increasing cognitive load and wrist strain.

3. **2 Swivel + 2 Rigid (Reverse pattern):**
   - Two swivels on rear axle, two rigid on front
   - **Ergonomic implication:** Operator pushes from the rigid side, getting straight-line stability; useful for long corridor transport.

4. **5-wheel pattern (Special):**
   - Four corner wheels + one central wheel
   - **Ergonomic implication:** Can pivot around the central wheel's vertical axis with near-zero turning radius; ideal for assembly line workstations.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $m = 350$ kg, $D = 200$ mm, $\mu_r = 0.018$, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

**Step 1: Total weight.**
$$W = m \cdot g = 350 \cdot 9.81 = 3433.5 \, N$$

**Step 2: Total rolling resistance (4 wheels share load equally, but $\mu_r$ is for each wheel, so total $F_r$ is the same since $\mu_r$ is per wheel).**
$$F_r = \mu_r \cdot W = 0.018 \cdot 3433.5 = 61.8 \, N$$

**Step 3: Operator force considering handle height geometry.**

The force is applied at axle height (0.25 m), and the handle is at 1.0 m. The operator's hands apply force at a height of 1.0 m, but the moment arm ratio to the axle is what matters. For a horizontal push at the handle (height 1.0 m), the effective horizontal force at the axle is the same (horizontal force is invariant with height in static analysis). However, the handle height creates a moment about the wheel contact point that affects wheel load distribution.

For simplicity in KTU-level analysis, assuming the operator applies a horizontal force at handle height and the trolley moves horizontally:
$$F_{push} = F_r + F_{grade} + F_{acc} = 61.8 + 0 + 0 = 61.8 \, N$$

(Assuming flat floor, no acceleration.)

**Step 4: Ergonomic assessment against NIOSH limits.**

NIOSH recommended limit for **sustained push force** is **75 N** (for two-handed push, male, 50th percentile). Our value of **61.8 N** is **below 75 N**, hence **ergonomically acceptable** for sustained operation. However, this is at the upper limit, and for female operators or for prolonged shifts, a powered assist or larger wheel diameter is recommended.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024 — Alternative]

**(a)** Discuss the role of wheel diameter, tire material, and bearing type in determining the ergonomic quality of a manually pushed cart. Support your answer with quantitative reasoning where possible. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the horizontal force required to push a wheel of diameter $D$ and weight $W$ over a vertical curb of height $h$. A 200 mm diameter wheel of a 400 N trolley is to be pushed over a 30 mm curb. Calculate the force required and comment on the design. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of Wheel Parameters in Ergonomics:**

- **Wheel Diameter ($D$):** Larger diameter reduces rolling resistance by reducing the contact patch deformation angle. Doubling $D$ approximately halves $\mu_r$. For an ergonomic cart, $D \geq 200$ mm is recommended for indoor use; $D \geq 300$ mm for outdoor/rough surfaces.

- **Tire Material:** The **durometer** (hardness) of the tire affects both rolling resistance and vibration transmission.
  - **Hard tires** (Shore D 80): Low $\mu_r$, but transmit floor vibrations to the operator, causing hand-arm vibration syndrome (HAVS) over long periods.
  - **Soft tires** (Shore A 70): Higher $\mu_r$, but absorb vibration, reducing operator fatigue. **Polyurethane** is the most common compromise.

- **Bearing Type:** Bearings reduce internal friction in the wheel hub.
  - **Plain bearings:** $\mu_b \approx 0.05–0.10$ — high friction, unsuitable for ergonomic carts.
  - **Roller bearings:** $\mu_b \approx 0.005$ — acceptable for heavy industrial use.
  - **Sealed ball bearings:** $\mu_b \approx 0.001$ — gold standard for ergonomic carts; require no maintenance.

Quantitative reasoning: A cart with hard tires + plain bearings may require 50% more push force than the same cart with soft polyurethane tires + sealed ball bearings, all other factors equal.

**(b) Derivation — Force to Climb a Curb:**

**Step 1:** Set up the free-body diagram. The wheel of radius $r = D/2$ contacts the top edge of a curb of height $h$. The applied force $F_p$ acts horizontally at the axle center.

**Step 2:** Take moments about the contact point at the top edge of the curb to eliminate the unknown normal force at that point.

**Step 3:** Geometry — the horizontal distance from the axle center to the contact point on the curb is:
$$d = \sqrt{r^2 - (r-h)^2} = \sqrt{2rh - h^2}$$

**Step 4:** Moment balance (anti-clockwise = clockwise):
$$F_p \cdot d = W \cdot (r - h)$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $F_p$:
$$F_p = \frac{W \cdot (r - h)}{\sqrt{2rh - h^2}}$$

**Step 6: Numerical calculation.** Given $D = 200$ mm → $r = 100$ mm, $W = 400$ N, $h = 30$ mm.

$$F_p = \frac{400 \cdot (100 - 30)}{\sqrt{2 \cdot 100 \cdot 30 - 30^2}}$$

$$F_p = \frac{400 \cdot 70}{\sqrt{6000 - 900}} = \frac{28000}{\sqrt{5100}} = \frac{28000}{71.41}$$

$$F_p = 392.1 \, N$$

**Step 7: Ergonomic comment.** The NIOSH maximum **initial** (peak) push force is approximately **200 N** for a one-time effort. The required **392.1 N exceeds this by 96%** — the design is **NOT ergonomic**. The wheel diameter must be increased, or a low curb ramp should be installed.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARKS-LOSS PITFALLS IN KTU 2024 EVALUATION:**
> 1. **Confusing $\mu_r$ (rolling resistance) with $\mu_s$ (static friction coefficient):** They differ by a factor of 50–100×. Examiners specifically check this.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** Wheel diameter in mm must be converted to meters for force calculations, OR the rolling resistance formula must use consistent units. Watch for the "mixed units" trap.
> 3. **Ignoring handle height geometry:** In Part B numerical questions, students often forget to account for the moment-arm effect of the handle height on the applied force. State your assumption (e.g., "horizontal push at axle height") explicitly.
> 4. **Not stating ergonomic recommendations:** KTU answers must always end with a **comment on ergonomic acceptability** referencing NIOSH/ISO standards. Marks are awarded for the conclusion, not just the calculation.
> 5. **In sketches:** Label all force vectors ($W$, $N$, $F_p$, $F_r$) with their directions and units. An unlabeled diagram fetches zero marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Wheel** = circular rotating component; in human factors, it is an **ergonomic interface** reducing human push/pull force.
- **Rolling resistance formula:** $F_r = \mu_r \cdot W$ — the foundational equation of wheeled material handling.
- **Larger wheel diameter → lower rolling resistance** and easier obstacle climbing.
- **Soft tires (pneumatic/rubber)** absorb vibration but increase rolling resistance; **hard tires (steel/nylon)** are opposite.
- **Sealed ball bearings** are the gold standard for ergonomic carts ($\mu_b \approx 0.001$).
- **2 rigid + 2 swivel caster pattern** is the most common ergonomic configuration.
- **NIOSH push force limits:** < **75 N sustained**, < **200 N peak/initial**.
- **Curb-climbing force formula:** $F_p = \frac{W(r-h)}{\sqrt{2rh - h^2}}$ — wheel fails to roll over if $h > r(1 - 1/\sqrt{2}) \approx 0.293r$.
- **Total push force** on an incline: $F_{total} = F_r + W\sin\theta + ma$ — sum of rolling, grade, and acceleration components.
- **Polyurethane wheels** (Shore A 85–95) are the most common industrial compromise between durability and ergonomics.
- **For any KTU calculation, always end with an ergonomic verdict** referencing NIOSH 75 N / 200 N thresholds.
- **Sketch quality matters:** Labeled free-body diagrams with force vectors, magnitudes, and units score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Storage (Workplace / Industrial Context):** In Human Factors and Engineering Design, *Storage* is defined as the systematic retention of raw materials, work-in-process (WIP), finished goods, tools, and information within a controlled spatial envelope, designed such that human operators can deposit, locate, retrieve, and dispatch items with **minimum energy expenditure, minimum time, minimum cognitive load, and maximum safety**, while complying with anthropometric, biomechanical, and cognitive ergonomic constraints.

Storage is **not merely warehousing** — in Human Factors Engineering (HFE), it is a man–machine–environment interface problem where the layout, container, height, access frequency, and identification system must all match the human operator's **physical capabilities** and **information-processing limits**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Kitchen Spice Rack Analogy** 🍴
>
> Imagine your mother's kitchen. Salt (used daily) is placed at **waist level, in a clear glass jar with a big label**. Saffron (used rarely) is kept on the **top shelf, in a small dark box**. Heavy pressure cookers are on the **bottom shelf**. This is storage *designed by intuition* — daily-use items are at the *prime retrieval zone*, heavy items are at *low heights*, and rare items are *out of the way*.
>
> Now imagine the opposite: salt on the top shelf requiring a stool, pressure cookers on a high fragile rack. The kitchen "works" but causes **wasted energy, time, falls, and fatigue**. Industrial storage follows the **exact same logic**, only scaled up. KTU examiners love this analogy in 2-mark questions.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Storage Design
