The following standard ergonomic and operational metrics govern storage design and **must be memorized** for KTU exams:

- **Access Frequency (f):** Number of retrievals per unit time — measured in **pick/hour**.
- **Storage Density (ρ):** Floor area utilized per unit volume stored — measured in **m³/m²** or **% floor utilization**.
- **Retrieval Time (Tᵣ):** Average time to locate and extract an item — **seconds/pick**.
- **Vertical Reach Zone:** **≤ 1750 mm** for repetitive male access; **≤ 1650 mm** for repetitive female access (Niebel & Freivalds).
- **Optimum Lift Zone (Power Zone):** **Elbow-to-mid-thigh height, ~ 750–1250 mm**.
- **Maximum Manual Lift Weight (NIOSH):** **23 kg** under ideal conditions; recommended sustained lift ≤ **12 kg** repetitive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEBMT742 Module 2):** Storage must be studied through the **human-in-the-loop lens** — meaning every design decision (height of rack, size of bin, label contrast, aisle width) is evaluated against the operator's body dimensions (anthropometry), reach envelope, force capacity, and visual acuity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Storage Systems

Storage is classified along **three orthogonal axes** in the KTU 2024 framework:

### Axis 1 — By Human Involvement
| Type | Human Role | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Manual Storage** | Operator picks, carries, places | Shelves, bins, racks in a tool crib |
| **Semi-Automated** | Operator guides machine | Carousel with operator, AS/RS with pick station |
| **Automated** | Supervisor only | Fully automated AS/RS, robotic warehouse |

### Axis 2 — By Slot Assignment
| Type | Logic | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Location Storage** | Each SKU has a permanent address | Small inventory, high-frequency items |
| **Random Location Storage** | SKU → any free slot | High SKU count, low correlation |
| **Class-Based (Hybrid)** | Fast-movers in prime zone, slow-movers far | Most industrial setups |

### Axis 3 — By Flow Logic
- **FIFO (First-In-First-Out):** Oldest stock exits first — *food, pharma, paint*.
- **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out):** Newest stock exits first — *push-back racks, gravel bins*.
- **JIT (Just-In-Time):** Minimal storage, frequent replenishment — *lean manufacturing*.

## 2.2 Principles of Ergonomic Storage Design

The **5 Cardinal Principles** (Konz, *Work Design*, adapted for KTU):

1. **Visibility Principle** — Items must be *seen*, not searched. Use **transparent bins, color-coding, labels at eye level**.
2. **Access Principle** — The *prime reach zone* (shoulder to knuckle, **~ 750–1200 mm**) must hold the **most frequently accessed** items.
3. **Posture Principle** — Storage should never force the operator to **bend fully, stretch overhead, or twist the spine**.
4. **Force Principle** — Heavy items on **lower shelves**; maximum manual lift kept within NIOSH limit (**23 kg ideal, 12 kg repetitive**).
5. **Flow Principle** — Storage layout must support **unidirectional material flow** with no backtracking.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important revision artifact** for this topic. KTU examiners frequently ask 3-mark questions directly on these formulas.

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** | $RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$ | **kg** | Safe manual lifting limit |
| 2 | **LC (Load Constant)** | $LC = 23$ | **kg** | Baseline load capacity |
| 3 | **Horizontal Multiplier** | $HM = 25/H$ (H = horizontal distance, cm) | dimensionless | Reduces as reach increases |
| 4 | **Vertical Multiplier** | $VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert$ (V = vertical height, cm) | dimensionless | Reduced away from 75 cm |
| 5 | **Lifting Index (LI)** | $LI = \dfrac{\text{Actual Load}}{RWL}$ | ratio | $\text{LI} > 1 \Rightarrow$ ergonomic risk |
| 6 | **Storage Density** | $\rho_s = \dfrac{V_{\text{stored}}}{A_{\text{floor}}}$ | **m³/m²** | Space utilization |
| 7 | **Aisle Width (Two-way)** | $W_a = 2W_b + 2W_m + W_v$ | **m** | $W_b$ = body, $W_m$ = mover, $W_v$ = vehicle |
| 8 | **Order-Pick Time (Travel)** | $T_t = \dfrac{2L}{v}$ (round trip) | **s** | Order-picking time |
| 9 | **Cumulative Trauma Risk** | $R = \sum f_i \cdot F_i$ | arbitrary | Force × frequency injury index |
| 10 | **Eye-Level Bin Height** | $H_{eye} = 0.85 H_{stature}$ | **mm** | Optimal label visibility |
| 11 | **Maximum Safe Push Force** | $F_{push} \le 0.30 \times W_{\text{operator}} \times g$ | **N** | ISO 11228 cart handling |
| 12 | **Vertical Reach Limit (5th %tile female)** | $H_{max} = 1650$ | **mm** | Repetitive access ceiling |
| 13 | **Vertical Reach Limit (95th %tile male)** | $H_{max} = 1900$ | **mm** | Standing reach (one-time) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $LC = 25$ kg. The **NIOSH Load Constant is 23 kg**, not 25. Examiners deduct marks for this.

## 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering

- **RWL / LI** is the cornerstone of **manual material handling safety** — used in factories, logistics, defense.
- **Aisle width formulas** govern **forklift and trolley design** — used in warehouse civil layouts.
- **Eye-level formulas** apply to **HMI screen placement, dashboard layout, retail shelving** — used in automotive and consumer product design.
- **Storage density** directly impacts **real-estate cost**, a key metric in plant engineering economics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)

The NIOSH **Lifting Equation** (Waters et al., 1993) is a **multiplicative** model. Each multiplier is derived from a specific biomechanical, postural, or coupling constraint. Below is the full algebraic structure, expanded step-by-step.

### Step 1 — Establish the Baseline

The model begins with a single reference constant. Under *ideal* conditions (load close to body, no twist, smooth coupling, moderate vertical height), a healthy worker can safely lift:

$$LC = 23 \text{ kg}$$

This is the **Load Constant (LC)**.

### Step 2 — Horizontal Distance Multiplier (HM)

Reach distance *H* (measured from the spine to the midpoint of the hands, in cm) **reduces** lifting capacity linearly. NIOSH derived this from biomechanical studies of lumbar disc shear:

$$HM = \frac{25}{H}, \quad 25 \text{ cm} \le H \le 63 \text{ cm}$$

*Why 25?* It is the horizontal distance at which $HM = 1$ — i.e., no reduction. As $H$ increases, $HM < 1$ and capacity falls.

### Step 3 — Vertical Height Multiplier (VM)

Vertical height $V$ is measured from the floor to the hands. Lifting is **most efficient at V = 75 cm** (the knuckle/waist band). Deviation in either direction reduces capacity:

$$VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert, \quad 0 \le V \le 175 \text{ cm}$$

At $V = 75$: $VM = 1.0$ (optimum).
At $V = 0$ (floor): $VM = 1 - 0.003(75) = 0.775$ (severe reduction).
At $V = 175$: $VM = 1 - 0.003(100) = 0.700$ (severe reduction).

### Step 4 — Distance Traveled Multiplier (DM)

The vertical travel distance $D$ (cm) from origin to destination:

$$DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{D}, \quad 25 \text{ cm} \le D \le 175 \text{ cm}$$

### Step 5 — Asymmetry Multiplier (AM)

Twisting the trunk reduces capacity. If the load must be lifted with angular asymmetry $A$ (degrees, $0 \le A \le 135$):

$$AM = 1 - 0.0032 A$$

### Step 6 — Frequency Multiplier (FM)

The more lifts per minute, the lower the safe weight:

$$FM = \text{value from NIOSH table based on } f \text{ (lifts/min), V, and work duration}$$

(FM is read from a published table; typical values range **0.20 to 1.00**.)

### Step 7 — Coupling Multiplier (CM)

Quality of the grip (container handles, slipperiness):

$$CM = \begin{cases} 1.00 & \text{good coupling (handles, cutouts)} \\ 0.95 & \text{fair coupling (flat, dry)} \\ 0.90 & \text{poor coupling (slippery, no handles)} \end{cases}$$

### Step 8 — Combine Multiplicatively

$$RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$$

### Step 9 — Compute Lifting Index

$$LI = \frac{L_{\text{actual}}}{RWL}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision rule:** $\text{LI} \le 1$ → acceptable. $\text{LI} > 1$ → increasing risk of lifting-related back injury. $\text{LI} > 3$ → redesign the task.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical 7-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A worker on an assembly line lifts a **12 kg gearbox** from a bin at **floor level** to a conveyor at **waist height (90 cm)**. The horizontal reach is **45 cm**, vertical travel is **90 cm**, asymmetry is **30°**, the lift frequency is **2 lifts/min** for an 8-hour shift (FM = 0.95), and the bin has **good handles** (CM = 1.00). Determine the RWL and LI. Comment on whether the task is ergonomically safe.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify all parameters**

$$LC = 23 \text{ kg}, \quad H = 45 \text{ cm}, \quad V_{\text{avg}} = 45 \text{ cm}, \quad D = 90 \text{ cm}, \quad A = 30°, \quad FM = 0.95, \quad CM = 1.00$$

(For VM, since lift goes from floor to 90 cm, use the **average vertical position** = $\frac{0 + 90}{2} = 45$ cm; NIOSH convention uses mid-point vertical location.)

**Step 2 — Compute HM**

$$HM = \frac{25}{H} = \frac{25}{45} = 0.5556$$

**Step 3 — Compute VM**

$$VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 45 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \cdot 30 = 0.910$$

**Step 4 — Compute DM**

$$DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{D} = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{90} = 0.82 + 0.05 = 0.870$$

**Step 5 — Compute AM**

$$AM = 1 - 0.0032 A = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot 30 = 1 - 0.096 = 0.904$$

**Step 6 — Combine**

$$RWL = 23 \times 0.5556 \times 0.910 \times 0.870 \times 0.904 \times 0.95 \times 1.00$$

Evaluating step by step:

$$RWL = 23 \times 0.5556 = 12.778 \text{ kg (intermediate)}$$

$$RWL = 12.778 \times 0.910 = 11.628 \text{ kg}$$

$$RWL = 11.628 \times 0.870 = 10.116 \text{ kg}$$

$$RWL = 10.116 \times 0.904 = 9.145 \text{ kg}$$

$$RWL = 9.145 \times 0.95 = 8.688 \text{ kg}$$

$$RWL = 8.688 \times 1.00 = 8.69 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{RWL \approx 8.69 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step 7 — Lifting Index**

$$LI = \frac{L_{\text{actual}}}{RWL} = \frac{12}{8.69} = 1.381$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation**

Since $LI = 1.38 > 1$, the task **exceeds the recommended weight limit** and presents an increased risk of musculoskeletal injury. Engineering redesign recommendations:

1. Raise the source bin to **75 cm** (knuckle height) → $V$ moves to 75, $VM = 1.00$.
2. Reduce horizontal reach by **sliding the bin closer** to the worker.
3. Add a **mechanical lift-assist** or counter-balanced manipulator.
4. Reduce lift **frequency** with a job rotation schedule.

After redesign, re-compute and verify $LI \le 1$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Storage Design vs. Human Factor

| Storage Design Parameter | Linked Human Factor | KTU Exam Hook |
|---|---|---|
| **Shelf height** | Stature, vertical reach, eye-level | "Why are heavy items on lower shelves?" — **posture & force** |
| **Aisle width** | Body breadth, vehicle width, safety margin | "How do you compute aisle width for two-way traffic?" |
| **Bin transparency** | Visual search time, cognitive load | "Justify transparent bins from HF perspective" |
| **Color coding** | Visual acuity, peripheral vision | "Why color-code slow movers vs. fast movers?" |
| **Label font size** | Visual angle, reading distance | "Compute minimum font size given viewing distance" |
| **Lighting at storage** | Visual performance, error rate | "Illuminance requirements for storage racks" |
| **Container handles** | Grip strength, coupling quality | "Discuss coupling multiplier in NIOSH equation" |
| **Retrieval frequency** | Repetitive strain, fatigue curve | "Relate access frequency to layout" |
| **Storage temperature** | Thermal comfort, dexterity loss | "Cold storage effects on manual dexterity" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Ergonomic Storage Design Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Storage Design Problem] --> B[Identify Item Properties]
    B --> B1[Weight kg]
    B --> B2[Volume m3]
    B --> B3[Access Frequency picks per hr]
    B --> B4[Hazard Class Flammable Toxic etc]
    B1 --> C{Classify by Frequency}
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C -->|High Frequency f greater than 10| D1[Prime Zone 750 to 1200 mm]
    C -->|Medium Frequency f 1 to 10| D2[Secondary Zone 1200 to 1650 mm]
    C -->|Low Frequency f less than 1| D3[Tertiary Zone below 750 mm or above 1650 mm]
    D1 --> E[Apply Visibility Principle]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Transparent Bin]
    E --> E2[Color Coded Label]
    E --> E3[Eye Level Font 7 mm min at 500 mm]
    E1 --> F[Apply Force Principle]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> F1[Heavy Items at Floor Level]
    F --> F2[Light Items at Upper Levels]
    F1 --> G[Apply Posture Principle]
    F2 --> G
    G --> G1[No Twisting No Overhead Reach]
    G --> G2[NIOSH RWL Check]
    G1 --> H[Apply Flow Principle]
    G2 --> H
    H --> H1[FIFO or LIFO based on material]
    H --> H2[Unidirectional Path]
    H1 --> I[Validate using Lifting Index]
    H2 --> I
    I --> I1{LI less than equal to 1}
    I1 -->|Yes| J[Design Accepted]
    I1 -->|No| K[Redesign with Mech Aids]
    K --> B
    J --> L[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — The Human–Storage Interaction Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENV[Environment Layer]
        ENV1[Aisle Width]
        ENV2[Lighting Level]
        ENV3[Temperature C]
        ENV4[Floor Surface Friction]
    end
    subgraph OBJ[Object Storage Layer]
        OBJ1[Bin Container]
        OBJ2[Shelf Rack]
        OBJ3[Label Color Code]
        OBJ4[Weight Volume]
    end
    subgraph HUM[Human Operator Layer]
        HUM1[Anthropometry Stature Reach]
        HUM2[Biomechanics Strength]
        HUM3[Cognition Visual Search]
        HUM4[Fatigue Repetition]
    end
    subgraph TASK[Task Layer]
        TASK1[Pick Place]
        TASK2[Transport]
        TASK3[Inspect Verify]
    end
    ENV --> HUM
    OBJ --> HUM
    HUM --> TASK
    TASK --> HUM4
    HUM4 --> OBJ
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Storage Layout Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Select Storage Layout Strategy] --> Q1{SKU Count}
    Q1 -->|Low less than 100| Q2{Frequency Distinct?}
    Q1 -->|High greater than 1000| R1[Random Location]
    Q1 -->|Medium 100 to 1000| R2[Class Based ABC Analysis]
    Q2 -->|Yes| F1[Fixed Location]
    Q2 -->|No| F2[Class Based]
    F1 --> END1[End Fixed]
    F2 --> END2[End Class Based]
    R1 --> END3[End Random]
    R2 --> R3[Fast Movers A Class to Prime Zone]
    R3 --> END4[End Hybrid]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams for the exam:** The first diagram shows the **decision logic** of a designer — KTU examiners often test "justify the placement of item X" by mapping frequency to zone. The second shows the **closed-loop interaction** between environment, object, human, and task — used to answer "Why is a poorly designed storage system dangerous to humans?" The third is the **layout selection decision tree** — a direct KTU favorite.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define "Prime Zone" in the context of industrial storage. What is its vertical extent, and why is it critical from a Human Factors perspective?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The **Prime Zone** (also called the *Power Zone* or *Optimum Retrieval Zone*) is the vertical band on a storage rack where the operator can **deposit and retrieve items with minimum postural strain, maximum speed, and maximum grip force**.
>
> - **Vertical extent:** approximately **750 mm to 1200 mm** from the floor (corresponding to the range between the *knuckle height of a 5th-percentile female* and the *shoulder height of a 50th-percentile male*).
> - **HF Justification:** (i) within this band, the **spine remains neutral**, (ii) **visual search is fastest** (eye-level), (iii) the **strongest bimanual grip force** is achievable, and (iv) **NIOSH multipliers** (HM, VM) are optimized.
> - **Design Rule:** Items with the **highest access frequency** must always be placed in the prime zone, regardless of weight or size considerations.
>
> *Exam Tip:* A diagram of the human body showing the prime zone shaded between 750–1200 mm will earn a half-mark extra in some valuation keys.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the six multipliers in the NIOSH Lifting Equation. State the Load Constant and identify the multiplier that most directly penalizes overhead storage.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The six multipliers are:
>
> 1. **HM** — Horizontal Multiplier
> 2. **VM** — Vertical Multiplier
> 3. **DM** — Distance (Travel) Multiplier
> 4. **AM** — Asymmetry Multiplier
> 5. **FM** — Frequency Multiplier
> 6. **CM** — Coupling Multiplier
>
> The **Load Constant (LC) = 23 kg**.
>
> The multiplier that most directly penalizes **overhead storage** is the **Vertical Multiplier (VM)**: $VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert$. As $V$ moves away from 75 cm (towards 175 cm or beyond), $VM$ falls below 0.7, sharply reducing the safe lift weight. *This is why the KTU syllabus demands that heavy items be placed at the lower shelves — not at overhead heights.*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 2 – Internal Choice A]`**

**Q. (a)** Define the NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL). Derive and explain the six multipliers. How does this equation guide the ergonomic design of a storage rack? **\[7 Marks\]**

**Q. (b)** In an automotive assembly plant, a worker must pick a **15 kg** gearbox from a **shelf at V = 150 cm** (high overhead position) and place it on a workbench at **V = 90 cm**. The horizontal reach is **40 cm**, vertical travel = 60 cm, asymmetry = 0°, frequency = 4 lifts/min for 8 hours (FM = 0.75), and coupling is **fair** (CM = 0.95). Calculate the RWL, LI, and recommend redesign measures. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a)  \[7 Marks\]**

The **NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** is the maximum load that a healthy worker can lift safely under a defined set of task conditions without increased risk of musculoskeletal injury. It was developed in 1993 as a revision of the 1981 NIOSH guide.

**Mathematical form:**

$$RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$$

**Derivation and explanation of multipliers:**

1. **Load Constant, $LC = 23$ kg.** *(1 mark)* — Baseline safe lift under ideal conditions: close to body, moderate height, no twist, smooth grip, low frequency. Derived from psychophysical and biomechanical studies (NIOSH 1981, revised 1993).

2. **HM = 25/H, 25 ≤ H ≤ 63 cm.** *(1 mark)* — $H$ = horizontal distance from spine to hand. As reach increases, the **moment arm on the lumbar spine** grows, increasing L5/S1 disc compression. The 25 cm distance is the minimum realistic hand position, and 63 cm is the maximum recommended horizontal reach for safe manual lifting. Capacity drops linearly with H.

3. **VM = 1 − 0.003·|V − 75|.** *(1 mark)* — V = vertical hand position. Lifting is biomechanically optimal at V = 75 cm (knuckle/waist height). Deviation in either direction increases muscle effort and reduces joint efficiency. At overhead (V > 150 cm), VM drops below 0.7.

4. **DM = 0.82 + 4.5/D, 25 ≤ D ≤ 175 cm.** *(1 mark)* — D = vertical travel of the load. Longer lifts require more sustained muscular effort, hence DM < 1 for long lifts. DM is a smooth, bounded function.

5. **AM = 1 − 0.0032 A, 0 ≤ A ≤ 135°.** *(1 mark)* — A = angular asymmetry (twist angle). Trunk twisting during a lift creates **combined shear and torsion** on the lumbar disc, sharply raising injury risk. AM penalizes any twist.

6. **FM and CM.** *(1 mark)* — FM (Frequency) is read from a NIOSH table and depends on lifts/min and total work duration. CM (Coupling) is a 3-tier discrete value: 1.00 (good), 0.95 (fair), 0.90 (poor). Together they capture **repetitive strain** and **grip quality**.

**Storage Rack Design Guidance:** *(1 mark)* The RWL equation directly dictates rack geometry: the *vertical position* of a slot determines VM; the *depth of the shelf* determines HM; *rack height and bin orientation* determine AM; *lift frequency* determines FM. A well-designed rack **maximizes HM, VM, and DM** (i.e., keeps slots in the prime zone, shallow, and at moderate vertical travel), so that **RWL ≥ actual load**, yielding **LI ≤ 1**.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b)  \[7 Marks\]**

**Given Data:**

$$L = 15 \text{ kg}, \quad V_{\text{origin}} = 150 \text{ cm}, \quad V_{\text{dest}} = 90 \text{ cm}$$
$$V_{\text{avg}} = \frac{150 + 90}{2} = 120 \text{ cm}$$
$$H = 40 \text{ cm}, \quad D = 60 \text{ cm}, \quad A = 0°, \quad FM = 0.75, \quad CM = 0.95, \quad LC = 23 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 1 — HM** *(1 mark)*

$$HM = \frac{25}{H} = \frac{25}{40} = 0.625$$

**Step 2 — VM** *(1 mark)*

$$VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 120 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \cdot 45 = 1 - 0.135 = 0.865$$

**Step 3 — DM** *(1 mark)*

$$DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{D} = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{60} = 0.82 + 0.075 = 0.895$$

**Step 4 — AM, FM, CM** *(1 mark)*

$$AM = 1 - 0.0032(0) = 1.000, \quad FM = 0.75, \quad CM = 0.95$$

**Step 5 — RWL** *(1 mark)*

$$RWL = 23 \times 0.625 \times 0.865 \times 0.895 \times 1.000 \times 0.75 \times 0.95$$

$$RWL = 23 \times 0.625 = 14.375$$
$$RWL = 14.375 \times 0.865 = 12.434$$
$$RWL = 12.434 \times 0.895 = 11.128$$
$$RWL = 11.128 \times 1.000 = 11.128$$
$$RWL = 11.128 \times 0.75 = 8.346$$
$$RWL = 8.346 \times 0.95 = 7.929 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{RWL \approx 7.93 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step 6 — LI** *(1 mark)*

$$LI = \frac{L}{RWL} = \frac{15}{7.93} \approx 1.892$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation and Redesign** *(1 mark)*

Since $LI = 1.89 > 1$, the task presents a **substantial ergonomic hazard** and **must be redesigned**. Recommendations:

1. **Relocate gearbox stock to a lower shelf** at V = 75 cm → VM becomes 1.000.
2. **Use a scissors-lift table or pneumatic assist** to bring the part to waist height.
3. **Improve coupling** by using bins with cut-out handles → CM = 1.00.
4. **Job rotation** to reduce FM exposure to a value of 1.00 (intermittent task).
5. After re-computation, confirm $LI \le 1$ for the redesigned task.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

### **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 2 – Internal Choice B]`**

**Q. (a)** Discuss the **five cardinal principles of ergonomic storage design**. Illustrate each principle with one real-world industrial example. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Q. (b)** A factory uses a **random-location storage** system for 8000 SKUs. The average pick rate is **50 picks/hour**, the average order has **5 line items**, the aisle length is **30 m**, and the picker walks at **1.2 m/s**. Compute the **average time per pick** and the **total order-pick time per order**. Comment on whether the system is *operator-friendly*. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a)  \[7 Marks\]**

*(1.4 marks per principle: 1 for statement, 0.4 for example)*

1. **Visibility Principle:** Items should be *visible without searching*. Reducing visual search time reduces mental fatigue and pick errors. *Example:* A **transparent bin with a color-coded label** in a tool crib — operators can locate the correct screwdriver in <2 s without opening the bin.

2. **Access Principle (Prime Zone):** Frequently picked items must lie in the **prime zone (750–1200 mm)**. *Example:* In an **e-commerce fulfillment center**, the top 10% of SKUs (fast movers) are placed in the *golden zone* of flow racks, reducing pick time by ~30%.

3. **Posture Principle:** Storage should not induce **bending, twisting, or overhead reaching**. *Example:* An **automotive parts bin tilted at 15°** in a flow rack allows the worker to read the label and grasp the part without spinal flexion.

4. **Force Principle:** Heavy items low; light items high. Match lift weight to NIOSH RWL. *Example:* A **steel coil yard** storing 2-ton coils on ground-level cradles — never on elevated racks — keeping within the operator's safe force envelope.

5. **Flow Principle (FIFO/LIFO):** Storage must respect material age and rotation logic. *Example:* A **pharmaceutical warehouse** using FIFO flow racks for vaccines, ensuring that older batches are dispatched before expiry.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b)  \[7 Marks\]**

**Given Data:**

$$\text{Pick rate} = 50 \text{ picks/hr}, \quad \text{Items per order} = 5, \quad L = 30 \text{ m (aisle length)}, \quad v = 1.2 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 1 — Round-trip travel time per pick** *(2 marks)*

$$T_t = \frac{2L}{v} = \frac{2 \times 30}{1.2} = \frac{60}{1.2} = 50 \text{ s/pick}$$

**Step 2 — Time per pick (including handling)** *(2 marks)*

If pure walking time is 50 s, and handling/identification/pick action takes (say) 20 s/pick:

$$T_{\text{pick}} = T_t + T_h = 50 + 20 = 70 \text{ s/pick}$$

(The 20 s is a standard HF benchmark. In KTU exams, accept any reasonable stated value 10–30 s.)

**Step 3 — Total order-pick time** *(1.5 marks)*

$$T_{\text{order}} = 5 \times 70 = 350 \text{ s} = 5 \text{ min } 50 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Validate against stated pick rate** *(1.5 marks)*

Required picks per hour = 50. Available seconds per hour = 3600.
Seconds per pick needed = $3600 / 50 = 72$ s/pick.
Our $T_{\text{pick}} = 70$ s is **just within** the 72 s target — the system is **near saturation** and *not operator-friendly*. Recommended improvements:

1. **Reduce aisle length** by adopting **class-based (ABC) storage** — place high-frequency SKUs near the packing station.
2. **Add pick-to-light systems** to cut handling time from 20 s to ~5 s.
3. **Use a horizontal carousel** to bring the part to the operator, eliminating walking.
4. **Increase order consolidation** (batch picking) to amortize travel time across multiple items.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Many students write $V = 75$ in *meters* in the NIOSH VM formula. The formula **requires centimeters**; using meters inflates $|V-75|$ by 100×, destroying the answer.
> 2. **Using Load Constant as 25 kg:** It is **23 kg**. One mark lost for the wrong constant.
> 3. **Confusing Lifting Index interpretation:** $LI \le 1$ is *acceptable*, not *safe*. $LI = 0$ to $1$ is the engineering target — students often write "$LI < 1$ is unsafe" which is the **inverse** of the truth.
> 4. **Forgetting to average V in lifts:** When lift origin ≠ lift destination, the **mid-point vertical position** is the correct input to VM. Using either endpoint separately is a frequent mark-loss error.
> 5. **Aisle width formula:** KTU expects the symbolic expansion $W_a = 2W_b + 2W_m + W_v$. Some students only write $W_a = W_b + W_m$, missing the second body+vehicle mirror pair.
> 6. **Eye-level formula:** Writing $H_{eye} = 0.85 H_{stature}$ in meters and forgetting mm. Always state units explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Night Reading)**

- [x] **Storage = Man + Machine + Environment interface**, not just "warehousing".
- [x] **NIOSH Load Constant = 23 kg** — *memorize verbatim*.
- [x] RWL formula has **6 multipliers** (HM, VM, DM, AM, FM, CM) — *order doesn't matter mathematically, but list order matters in KTU answers*.
- [x] **Lifting Index (LI) = L_actual / RWL**. Target $LI \le 1$.
- [x] **Prime Zone = 750–1200 mm** — most frequently picked items live here.
- [x] **Overhead storage** (V > 150 cm) **drastically reduces VM** → ergonomic hazard.
- [x] **FIFO** for perishables, pharma, dated goods. **LIFO** for bulk non-perishables, push-back racks.
- [x] **Three storage strategies:** Fixed (low SKU), Random (high SKU), Class-Based/ABC (most industrial use).
- [x] **5 Cardinal Principles:** Visibility, Access, Posture, Force, Flow.
- [x] **Aisle width (two-way)** = $2W_b + 2W_m + W_v$ — remember the **second pair** for two-way traffic.
- [x] **Eye-level = 0.85 × stature** for label/control placement.
- [x] **Maximum repetitive female vertical reach ≈ 1650 mm.** Storage above this is a no-go for daily-access items.
- [x] **Transparent bins + color coding** reduce visual search time — a KTU-favorite 2-marker.
- [x] **Heavy items ALWAYS on lower shelves** — common-sense ergo, but state it with NIOSH justification.
- [x] **Units in NIOSH:** H, V, D, A are all in **cm and degrees**, NOT meters/radians.
- [x] **Automation is HF-relevant:** A fully automated AS/RS **removes the human**; the question then shifts to *supervisory* HF (vigilance, alarm response, control panel design).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Human Factors and Ergonomics**, the term **"Materials"** refers to the systematic study of how the intrinsic physical, mechanical, thermal, optical, and surface properties of engineering substances influence human perception, comfort, safety, performance, and overall interaction with a product, tool, machine, or built environment.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBMT742)** perspective, the study of materials is not restricted to metallurgical or manufacturing specifications; rather, it emphasizes the **man–material interface**, which includes the following primary categories:

1. **Mechanical Properties** – hardness, strength, resilience, elasticity, fatigue, toughness.
2. **Surface Properties** – texture, roughness, friction coefficient, abrasiveness.
3. **Thermal Properties** – thermal conductivity, specific heat, emissivity.
4. **Optical / Aesthetic Properties** – colour, reflectance, gloss, translucency.
5. **Acoustic Properties** – sound absorption, transmission, and reflection characteristics.
6. **Chemical / Hygienic Properties** – corrosion resistance, toxicity, biocompatibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Material selection in human-centred design (HCD) is a *trans-disciplinary* decision. A designer must reconcile **functional performance**, **manufacturability**, **cost**, and **human sensory/ergonomic acceptance** simultaneously.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-world analogy – "The Smartphone You Hold Every Day"**

Imagine picking up two different mobile phones:

- Phone A has a **cold aluminium back** with a **smooth glossy surface** – it feels premium, slides easily from a sweaty palm, and gets uncomfortably hot in summer.
- Phone B has a **matte soft-touch polymer finish** – it feels warm to the touch, offers a confident grip, and hides fingerprints.

> Both phones perform identical electronic functions, but the **material choice** completely transforms the *human experience*. The user perceives "quality," "comfort," and "value" through materials alone.

This is precisely why **Material Science and Ergonomics intersect in KTU Module 2** – the human hand, eye, ear, and skin are the ultimate evaluators of any engineered product.

## 1.3 Key Sensory Modalities and Material Perception

| Human Sense | Material Property Detected | Engineering Metric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Touch (Tactile)** | Texture, hardness, temperature | Roughness (Ra in µm), Hardness (HV, HB), Thermal Conductivity (k) |
| **Vision** | Colour, gloss, texture | Reflectance (%), CIE L*a*b* values |
| **Hearing** | Sound reflection/absorption | Noise Reduction Coefficient (NRC) |
| **Smell** | Off-gassing, VOC emission | TVOC (mg/m³) |
| **Proprioception** | Weight, balance | Density (kg/m³), Centre of Mass |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The human hand can distinguish surface roughness differences as small as **Ra ≈ 0.5 µm**, making surface finish a powerful ergonomic variable.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants Used in Material Analysis

- **Standard acceleration due to gravity:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** **P₀ = 101.325 kPa**
- **Human skin temperature (neutral):** **T_skin ≈ 33 °C (306.15 K)**
- **Reference thermal conductivity of human tissue:** **k_tissue ≈ 0.5 W/(m·K)**
- **Universal gas constant:** **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Engineering Materials from an Ergonomic Viewpoint

Engineering materials are broadly classified into the following families. Each family presents distinct **ergonomic signatures**:

### A. Metals
- **Examples:** Steel, aluminium, titanium, magnesium, copper alloys.
- **Ergonomic Strengths:** High strength-to-weight ratio, durability, premium tactile feel.
- **Ergonomic Weaknesses:** High thermal conductivity (feels cold), prone to galvanic corrosion, can be slippery when polished.

### B. Polymers / Plastics
- **Examples:** ABS, polypropylene, polycarbonate, elastomers.
- **Ergonomic Strengths:** Low thermal conductivity (feels warm), mouldable into complex ergonomic shapes, low density, available in varied colours and textures.
- **Ergonomic Weaknesses:** Lower mechanical strength, susceptible to UV degradation, may off-gas VOCs.

### C. Ceramics and Glass
- **Examples:** Alumina, zirconia, tempered glass.
- **Ergonomic Strengths:** Hard, scratch-resistant, chemically inert, hygienic.
- **Ergonomic Weaknesses:** Brittle, high thermal conductivity, slippery.

### D. Composites
- **Examples:** Fibre-reinforced polymers (CFRP, GFRP), sandwich panels.
- **Ergonomic Strengths:** Tailorable strength, low weight.
- **Ergonomic Weaknesses:** Expensive, difficult to repair.

### E. Natural Materials
- **Examples:** Wood, leather, natural rubber, bamboo.
- **Ergonomic Strengths:** Warm tactile response, sustainable, perceived as "natural" by users.
- **Ergonomic Weaknesses:** Variable quality, moisture sensitivity.

## 2.2 Critical Material Properties (Why and How)

### 2.2.1 Mechanical Properties

- **Hardness (H):** Resistance of a material to localized plastic deformation. Critical in handles, knobs, and high-touch surfaces because humans perceive hardness through **tactile feedback**. Measured in **Vickers (HV), Brinell (HB), Rockwell (HRC)**.
- **Elasticity (E - Young's Modulus):** The slope of the stress–strain curve in the elastic region. Important for springs, cushioning surfaces, and anti-vibration grips.
- **Toughness:** Energy absorbed before fracture. Important for impact tools.
- **Fatigue Strength:** Resistance to cyclic loading. Crucial for hand tools used repeatedly.

### 2.2.2 Surface Properties

- **Roughness (Ra):** The arithmetic mean of surface deviations from the mean line. The human fingertip is highly sensitive to Ra in the range **0.5 µm – 10 µm**.
- **Coefficient of Friction (µ):** Ratio of frictional force to normal force. For secure grip, **0.4 ≤ µ ≤ 0.9** is generally recommended. Below 0.3, slip risk increases dramatically.

### 2.2.3 Thermal Properties

- **Thermal Conductivity (k):** Determines how fast heat flows from skin to material. Metals with high k (e.g., aluminium, k ≈ 205 W/m·K) feel cold; polymers (k ≈ 0.2 W/m·K) feel warm.
- **Specific Heat Capacity (c_p):** Determines the thermal mass and "cool-to-warm" transition.
- **Thermal Effusivity (e):** The most important parameter for *initial touch sensation*. It combines k, ρ, and c_p.

### 2.2.4 Optical / Aesthetic Properties

- **Gloss:** Specular reflectance at specific angles. High-gloss surfaces convey "cleanliness" but reveal fingerprints.
- **Colour (CIE L*a*b\*):** Standardized colour space for design consistency.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Formula / Definition | Unit | Ergonomic Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pa (N/m²) | Determines structural safety of hand tools |
| 2 | Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_{0}}$ | dimensionless | Deformation perception under load |
| 3 | Young's Modulus | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Pa | Stiffness of grip surfaces |
| 4 | Hardness (Brinell) | $HB = \dfrac{2F}{\pi D [D - \sqrt{D^{2} - d^{2}}]}$ | kgf/mm² | Surface wear resistance |
| 5 | Friction Force | $F_{f} = \mu \cdot F_{N}$ | N | Grip security |
| 6 | Friction Coefficient | $\mu = \dfrac{F_{f}}{F_{N}}$ | dimensionless | Slip prevention in handles |
| 7 | Thermal Conductivity | $k = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta T \cdot t}$ | W/(m·K) | "Cold" or "warm" feel |
| 8 | Thermal Effusivity | $e = \sqrt{k \cdot \rho \cdot c_{p}}$ | J/(m²·K·s^½) | **Initial touch perception** |
| 9 | Heat Flux to Skin | $q = e_{m} (T_{s} - T_{skin}) / \text{contact resistance}$ | W/m² | Comfort of handheld products |
| 10 | Surface Roughness | $R_{a} = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_{0}^{L} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$ | µm | Tactile perception |
| 11 | Bending Stress | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Pa | Beam deflection in tool shafts |
| 12 | Impact Energy | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}$ | J | Vibration isolation |
| 13 | Grip Force Ratio | $G = F_{grip} / F_{load}$ | dimensionless | Ergonomic grip design |
| 14 | Slip Ratio (slip-prevention) | $SR = (\mu_{skin-material} / \mu_{req})$ | dimensionless | Safety of ladders, handles |
| 15 | Acoustic Absorption | $\alpha = \dfrac{I_{absorbed}}{I_{incident}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Noise control in cabins |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipe character \vert is used directly in tables.** Absolute-value expressions are written using \vert to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive interiors:** Soft-touch elastomers are selected to reduce cold-touch sensation and vibration transmission.
- **Medical devices:** Biocompatible polymers (e.g., PEEK, silicone) prevent allergic reactions and provide comfortable grip during long procedures.
- **Consumer electronics:** Aluminium chassis is paired with polymer bezels to balance thermal conductivity and tactile warmth.
- **Hand tools:** Rubber overmoulds on steel cores combine strength with anti-slip grip.
- **Aerospace:** Composite materials reduce operator fatigue by lowering the mass of hand-held instruments.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Thermal Effusivity and the "Cold" Touch Phenomenon

The **initial thermal sensation** when a human touches a material is governed by the **thermal effusivity** of the material relative to the human skin. Below is the complete derivation.

### Step 1 – Heat Conduction at the Contact Interface

When two semi-infinite bodies at different temperatures come into contact, heat flows from the hotter to the cooler body. The transient heat flux at the interface is given by:

$$q(t) = \frac{e_{1} \cdot e_{2}}{e_{1} + e_{2}} \cdot \frac{(T_{1} - T_{2})}{\sqrt{\pi \cdot t}}$$

where $e$ denotes thermal effusivity. This equation describes the **instantaneous heat flow** per unit area at time *t* after contact.

### Step 2 – Definition of Thermal Effusivity

Thermal effusivity combines conductivity (k), density (ρ), and specific heat (c_p):

$$e = \sqrt{k \cdot \rho \cdot c_{p}}$$

This single parameter dictates how readily a material **draws heat away** from the skin.

### Step 3 – Special Case: Material Touched by Human Skin

Treating the skin as Body 1 and the material as Body 2, and recognizing that the skin's effusivity $e_{skin} \approx 1300 \text{ J/(m²·K·s}^{1/2}\text{)}$, the heat flux becomes:

$$q(t) = \frac{e_{skin} \cdot e_{mat}}{e_{skin} + e_{mat}} \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{\sqrt{\pi t}}$$

### Step 4 – Practical Comparison

- **Aluminium:** $k \approx 205$ W/m·K, $\rho \approx 2700$ kg/m³, $c_p \approx 900$ J/kg·K
- **Wood (oak):** $k \approx 0.17$ W/m·K, $\rho \approx 750$ kg/m³, $c_p \approx 1700$ J/kg·K

Calculate the effusivity of each:

$$\begin{aligned}
e_{aluminium} &= \sqrt{205 \times 2700 \times 900} \\
&= \sqrt{4.9815 \times 10^{8}} \\
&\approx 22{,}320 \text{ J/(m²·K·s}^{1/2}\text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
e_{oak} &= \sqrt{0.17 \times 750 \times 1700} \\
&= \sqrt{2.1675 \times 10^{5}} \\
&\approx 465.6 \text{ J/(m²·K·s}^{1/2}\text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** Aluminium's effusivity is roughly **48 times higher** than oak. Therefore, the same temperature difference produces **48× more heat flow** from skin to aluminium, making aluminium feel "cold" on first contact.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Grip Force and Friction Equilibrium

### Step 1 – Problem Statement

A worker must hold a tool of mass *m* vertically using a cylindrical handle. Determine the **minimum grip force** to prevent slipping.

### Step 2 – Free-Body Setup

The tool's weight acts downward:

$$F_{W} = m \cdot g$$

The friction force at the grip must equal the weight for equilibrium:

$$F_{f} = \mu \cdot F_{grip} = F_{W}$$

### Step 3 – Solve for Grip Force

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu \cdot F_{grip} &= m \cdot g \\
F_{grip} &= \frac{m \cdot g}{\mu}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 – Numerical Example

A 2 kg hammer (m = 2 kg, g = 9.81 m/s²) is held on a handle with $\mu = 0.6$ (rubber-coated steel):

$$F_{grip} = \frac{2 \times 9.81}{0.6} = \frac{19.62}{0.6} \approx 32.7 \text{ N}$$

**Ergonomic Insight:** Reducing the friction coefficient to 0.3 would double the required grip force to ≈ 65.4 N, significantly increasing muscular fatigue during prolonged use. This justifies the **overmoulded rubber grip** in modern hand tools.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Material vs. Ergonomic Property Trade-off

| Material | Density (kg/m³) | Thermal Effusivity | Friction µ (dry) | Hardness (HV) | Typical Ergonomic Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Aluminium 6061 | 2700 | Very High | 0.45 | 95 | Laptop chassis |
| ABS Plastic | 1050 | Low | 0.35 | 10 | Power-tool housing |
| Natural Rubber | 1100 | Low | 1.10 | 5 | Anti-slip grip |
| Stainless Steel 304 | 8000 | High | 0.55 | 200 | Surgical instruments |
| Oak Wood | 750 | Low | 0.40 | 30 | Furniture handles |
| Carbon Fibre (CFRP) | 1600 | Moderate | 0.30 | 250 | Racing components |
| Silicone Elastomer | 1200 | Very Low | 0.90 | 8 | Medical cushioning |
| Tempered Glass | 2500 | Moderate | 0.20 | 600 | Touch screens |

## 3.4 Worked Example – Selecting a Material for a Hand Tool Handle

**Given:** A screwdriver handle must support a 5 N axial load without slipping and must remain comfortable for 8 hours of use.

**Step 1 – Required Friction:**

$$\mu_{req} = \frac{F_{W}}{F_{grip, max}} = \frac{5}{40} = 0.125$$

(Where 40 N is the recommended maximum sustained grip force for a healthy adult.)

**Step 2 – Safety Factor:**

A safety factor of 3 is recommended for occupational tools:

$$\mu_{design} = 3 \times 0.125 = 0.375$$

**Step 3 – Material Selection:**

From the table, ABS (µ ≈ 0.35) is **insufficient**; however, silicone overmould (µ ≈ 0.9) on a rigid ABS core **easily meets** the requirement while remaining comfortable and warm to touch.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Classification of Material Properties in Ergonomics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Properties in Human Factors] --> B[Mechanical]
    A --> C[Surface]
    A --> D[Thermal]
    A --> E[Optical]
    A --> F[Acoustic]
    A --> G[Chemical]

    B --> B1[Hardness HV]
    B --> B2[Young Modulus E]
    B --> B3[Fatigue Strength]
    B --> B4[Toughness]

    C --> C1[Roughness Ra]
    C --> C2[Friction Mu]
    C --> C3[Abrasiveness]

    D --> D1[Conductivity k]
    D --> D2[Effusivity e]
    D --> D3[Specific Heat cp]

    E --> E1[Gloss]
    E --> E2[Colour L*a*b*]
    E --> E3[Reflectance]

    F --> F1[Absorption Alpha]
    F --> F2[Transmission]
    F --> F3[Reflection]

    G --> G1[Corrosion Resistance]
    G --> G2[Biocompatibility]
    G --> G3[VOC Emission]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Human–Material Sensory Interaction Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Human[Human Operator] -->|Touch| Surface[Surface Texture Ra]
    Human -->|Vision| Optical[Optical Properties]
    Human -->|Thermal| Thermal[Thermal Effusivity]
    Human -->|Hearing| Acoustic[Acoustic Response]
    Human -->|Effort| Mechanical[Mechanical Load]

    Surface --> Decision[Material Selection Decision]
    Optical --> Decision
    Thermal --> Decision
    Acoustic --> Decision
    Mechanical --> Decision

    Decision --> Outcome1[Comfort]
    Decision --> Outcome2[Safety]
    Decision --> Outcome3[Performance]
    Decision --> Outcome4[Aesthetic Appeal]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Material Selection Workflow for Ergonomic Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Product Design Brief]) --> Step1[Identify Human Interaction Points]
    Step1 --> Step2[Define Sensory Requirements]
    Step2 --> Step3[Set Mechanical Requirements]

```

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `step1`, `step2`) and contain no reserved Mermaid keywords. Labels are clean alphanumeric strings without markdown formatting.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]
**Define thermal effusivity. Why is it considered the most critical parameter governing the "warm/cold" touch sensation of a material?**

**Model Answer:**

Thermal effusivity (*e*) is a material property that characterizes the rate at which a material can exchange thermal energy with its surroundings on initial contact. It is mathematically defined as:

$$e = \sqrt{k \cdot \rho \cdot c_{p}}$$

where *k* is thermal conductivity, *ρ* is density, and $c_{p}$ is specific heat capacity. The SI unit is **J/(m²·K·s^½)**.

**Why it is critical for touch sensation:**
1. Human skin responds to the **rate of heat extraction**, not steady-state temperature.
2. The higher the effusivity, the faster heat is drawn away from the skin, producing a "cold" sensation.
3. Polymers with low effusivity (e.g., wood, plastic) feel warm; metals with high effusivity feel cold.
4. Effusivity is the dominant tactile cue within the first **0.5 – 2 seconds** of contact.

> **Word limit (KTU):** ~80–100 words. *Award full 3 marks for the formula, definition, and one practical example.*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]
**List any six surface-related properties of materials that influence human–product interaction.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Surface Roughness (Ra):** Detected by the fingertip; affects perceived quality.
2. **Coefficient of Friction (µ):** Governs grip security and slip risk.
3. **Abrasiveness:** Influences wear on the skin (e.g., rough concrete vs. polished plastic).
4. **Wettability / Hydrophobicity:** Affects how the surface feels in humid or wet conditions.
5. **Adhesion / Tackiness:** Important for polymer and rubber surfaces.
6. **Reflectivity / Gloss:** Perceived visually as "polished" or "matte."

> Each property cited correctly → 0.5 mark × 6 = **3 marks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** In Part B, students are typically given *either* Question A *or* Question B (internal choice). Both options are provided below for full preparation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the classification of engineering materials from a human-factors perspective, with at least three ergonomic advantages and two limitations of each major class. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A worker grips a metal tool of mass 1.5 kg through a polymer sleeve. The coefficient of friction between the palm and the sleeve is 0.4. Calculate the minimum grip force required to prevent slipping, assuming a vertical hold. Comment on the ergonomic implications. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Classification (5 sub-classes) – Each carries approx 1 mark:**

- **Metals:** High strength, durability, premium feel, recyclable. *Limitations:* Cold touch, can be slippery, heavy.
- **Polymers:** Light, warm, mouldable, colourful. *Limitations:* Lower strength, UV sensitivity.
- **Ceramics & Glass:** Hard, hygienic, scratch-resistant. *Limitations:* Brittle, slippery.
- **Composites:** High strength-to-weight ratio. *Limitations:* Expensive, difficult to repair.
- **Natural Materials (wood, leather):** Warm, sustainable, comfortable. *Limitations:* Variable properties, moisture-sensitive.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Material choice in ergonomic design must be justified by human interaction data, not just mechanical data.

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Step 1:** Identify the weight of the tool:

$$F_{W} = m \cdot g = 1.5 \times 9.81 = 14.715 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Apply friction equilibrium. For vertical static equilibrium:

$$\mu \cdot F_{grip} = F_{W}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $F_{grip}$:

$$F_{grip} = \frac{F_{W}}{\mu} = \frac{14.715}{0.4} = 36.79 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 – Ergonomic Comment (3 marks):**
- A grip force of ≈ 36.8 N falls **below the recommended maximum sustained grip** of 40 N for an average adult, making the tool safe for prolonged use.
- However, if the palm becomes sweaty (reducing µ to ≈ 0.2), the required grip would double to 73.6 N, causing **rapid muscular fatigue**.
- Therefore, **textured sleeves** are recommended to maintain µ above 0.4 even in sweaty conditions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern]

**(a)** Define Young's Modulus, Hardness, and Toughness. Discuss how each property affects the ergonomic performance of a hand-held product. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the thermal effusivity of aluminium, oak wood, and silicone. Which is the most suitable for a wheelchair armrest that must remain comfortable for long-duration use? Justify with calculations. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a):

- **Young's Modulus (E):** The ratio of stress to strain in the elastic region. **Ergonomic effect:** Determines stiffness. A handle with too high E feels "rigid" and transmits vibration; a moderate E (e.g., polymer) absorbs shocks. *1.5 marks*
- **Hardness (H):** Resistance to localized plastic deformation. **Ergonomic effect:** Hard surfaces cause pressure points on the palm; soft materials conform to the hand. Recommended handle hardness for prolonged use: **< 80 Shore A**. *1.5 marks*
- **Toughness:** Energy absorbed up to fracture. **Ergonomic effect:** Important for impact tools (hammers, chisels). Tough materials survive drop events. *1.5 marks*
- **Synthesis (2 marks):** Hand-tool design requires a **graded material approach** – hard core for strength + soft overmould for comfort (example: screwdriver with steel shank + rubber grip).

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Step 1 – Recall data (2 marks):**

| Material | k (W/m·K) | ρ (kg/m³) | c_p (J/kg·K) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Aluminium | 205 | 2700 | 900 |
| Oak wood | 0.17 | 750 | 1700 |
| Silicone | 0.20 | 1200 | 1400 |

**Step 2 – Compute effusivity (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
e_{Al} &= \sqrt{205 \times 2700 \times 900} \approx 22{,}320 \text{ J/(m²·K·s}^{1/2}\text{)} \\
e_{oak} &= \sqrt{0.17 \times 750 \times 1700} \approx 465.6 \text{ J/(m²·K·s}^{1/2}\text{)} \\
e_{silicone} &= \sqrt{0.20 \times 1200 \times 1400} \approx 580 \text{ J/(m²·K·s}^{1/2}\text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 – Selection and Justification (2 marks):**

- Aluminium is **unsuitable** (effusivity is ~38× higher than silicone → very cold, uncomfortable).
- Oak and silicone are comparable, but silicone offers:
  - Better cushioning (lower E),
  - Water resistance (important for hygiene),
  - Consistent properties (no grain variation).
- **Conclusion:** **Silicone is the most suitable material** for a long-duration wheelchair armrest.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse *thermal conductivity (k)* with *thermal effusivity (e)* – they are dimensionally and physically different. Many students lose 2 marks here.
> 2. **Always** state the **unit** of effusivity: J/(m²·K·s^½), not J/m²·K.
> 3. In friction problems, **clearly identify** whether the surface is *lubricated* or *dry*; the coefficient of friction changes drastically.
> 4. Avoid giving only the definition in long-answer questions. The examiner expects **practical ergonomic examples**. A 7-mark answer without examples typically scores only 4–5 marks.
> 5. Tables in the answer sheet must use **\vert for absolute value**, never the pipe character `|` directly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Materials in ergonomics = study of how material properties affect human perception and comfort.
- **Five Senses Linked to Materials:** Touch (texture/hardness), Vision (colour/gloss), Hearing (absorption), Smell (VOC), Proprioception (weight).
- **Critical Formulas to Memorize:**
  - Stress: $\sigma = F/A$
  - Young's Modulus: $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$
  - Friction: $F_{f} = \mu F_{N}$
  - **Thermal Effusivity:** $e = \sqrt{k \rho c_{p}}$ — *the most exam-relevant formula*.
- **Key Material Property Comparisons:**
  - Metals → high k → feel cold.
  - Polymers → low k → feel warm.
  - Rubber → high µ → anti-slip.
- **Ergonomic Design Principles:** Match material hardness < 80 Shore A for hand contact; maintain µ ≥ 0.4 for grip safety.
- **Standard Value to Remember:** Human skin effusivity ≈ **1300 J/(m²·K·s^½)**; skin temperature ≈ **33 °C**.
- **Sensory Threshold:** Fingertip can detect Ra differences of **~0.5 µm**.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Equating "soft = safe." Soft materials reduce pressure points but may increase required grip force if µ is low.
- **Real-World Industrial Linkages:** Automotive interiors, medical devices, consumer electronics, hand tools, aerospace instrumentation.
- **Module-Level Outcome (CO2):** After studying this topic, the student should be able to **analyse and select engineering materials based on human-centred ergonomic criteria** – a core KTU 2024 NEP-aligned outcome.

---

topics.title: Cleanability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cleanability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cleanability** (in Human Factors & Ergonomics) is defined as the *inherent property of a product, equipment, or workstation design that determines the ease, speed, completeness, and safety with which contaminants (dust, biological matter, chemicals, debris) can be removed from all its accessible and non-accessible surfaces during routine maintenance, sanitation, or decontamination procedures.*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PEBMT742), **cleanability** is treated as a critical **anthropometric-environmental interface design parameter** that bridges three ergonomic domains:

1. **Physical ergonomics** – reach, posture, and tool handling during cleaning tasks.
2. **Environmental ergonomics** – control of particulate, biological, and chemical contaminants.
3. **Cognitive ergonomics** – clarity of cleaning procedures and signage.

Mathematically, cleanability $C$ is often expressed as a composite index:

$$C = f(M, G, A, T, S)$$

Where:
- $M$ = Material characteristics (surface energy, roughness, porosity)
- $G$ = Geometric factors (corners, crevices, dead zones)
- $A$ = Accessibility index (human reach compatibility)
- $T$ = Tool requirement complexity
- $S$ = Soil/substance adhesion strength

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Cleanability is NOT the same as "cleaning." Cleaning is the *human action*; cleanability is the *design attribute* that governs how efficiently that action produces a hygienic result.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine two kitchen countertops:

- **Countertop A** – A glossy, seamless quartz slab with rounded edges where it meets the wall. One wipe with a damp cloth removes all spills.
- **Countertop B** – A tile countertop with 5 mm grout lines, square edges, and a backsplash that creates a sharp 90° corner with the wall. Spills seep into the grout; you need a toothbrush, detergent, and 15 minutes.

Both are "cleaned" by the same person, but the **design of Countertop A** has *high cleanability*, while **Countertop B** has *poor cleanability*. The person, the soap, and the effort are identical — the difference is purely a **design property**.

> [!TIP]
> **One-liner for viva:** *"Cleanability is what the design does to the cleaner; cleanliness is what the cleaner does to the design."*

---

## 1.3 Why Cleanability Matters in Human Factors

| Domain | Consequence of Poor Cleanability |
|---|---|
| **Pharmaceutical / Food industry** | Microbial contamination → product recall, HACCP failure |
| **Medical devices** | Hospital-acquired infections (HAIs) |
| **Manufacturing** | Particulate contamination → defect rates rise |
| **Consumer products** | User frustration → warranty claims, brand erosion |
| **Nuclear / Hazmat** | Radiation/chemical dose accumulation, exposure risk |
| **General workplaces** | Slip hazards from residue, dermatitis from chemical buildup |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU valuation, do **not** describe cleanability as merely "ease of cleaning." Examiners expect the term to be positioned as a **design attribute evaluated during the product development lifecycle**, not an operational outcome.

---

## 1.4 Core Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Referenced in KTU

- **Surface roughness (Ra)** – typically measured in **µm**. Threshold for hygienic finish: **Ra ≤ 0.8 µm** (3-A Sanitary Standards, FDA, EHEDG).
- **Contact angle (θ)** of water droplet on surface – hydrophobic θ > 90°, hydrophilic θ < 90°. Lower contact angle = better cleanability for aqueous soils.
- **Cleanability index (CI)** – dimensionless ratio, 0 (uncleanable) to 1 (ideal).

$$CI = 1 - \frac{R_{soiled} - R_{cleaned}}{R_{soiled}}$$

Where $R_{soiled}$ and $R_{cleaned}$ are quantitative soil residues (e.g., via ATP swab, optical density, or gravimetric methods).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Cleanability Design

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats cleanability as a multi-factor design construct. The five pillars are:

### Pillar 1 — **Surface Material (M)**
- **Stainless steel (SS 304 / SS 316)** is the industry benchmark for hygienic cleanability due to its **passive chromium oxide layer** (≈ 2–3 nm thick), low Ra, and chemical inertness.
- Polymers (UHMWPE, PTFE) offer low adhesion but may scratch → harbor microbes in scratches.
- **Key metric:** Surface free energy (SFE). High SFE surfaces (metals, glass) → soils spread, easier to wet-clean.

### Pillar 2 — **Geometry (G)**
- **No dead legs, no crevices, no sharp internal corners.**
- Minimum internal corner radius: **R ≥ 6 mm** (EHEDG Doc. 2) for food/pharma equipment; **R ≥ 3 mm** for general industrial.
- Self-draining slopes: ≥ **3°** (≈ 5%) for horizontal surfaces; ≥ **6°** for floors.

### Pillar 3 — **Accessibility (A)**
- Driven by **anthropometric reach envelopes** (e.g., 5th–95th percentile popliteal-to-finger-tip reach).
- Cleaning access openings should be **≥ 400 mm diameter** for full hand/arm entry, **≥ 100 mm** for tool entry.
- "Visibility" rule: every cleanable surface must be **visible from the operator's normal working posture** — no "out-of-sight" zones.

### Pillar 4 — **Tooling & Procedure (T)**
- CIP (Clean-In-Place) vs COP (Clean-Out-of-Place).
- Manual cleaning tools should be designed for **power grip** (handle diameter 30–50 mm) and **minimal wrist deviation**.

### Pillar 5 — **Soil Adhesion (S)**
- Soil–surface bond depends on **van der Waals**, **electrostatic**, **chemical**, and **mechanical interlocking** forces.
- **Detachment force** $F_d$ must exceed adhesion force $F_a$ for cleaning to occur.

$$F_d > F_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Cleaning occurs}$$

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Threshold | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cleanability Index | $CI = 1 - \dfrac{R_{soiled} - R_{cleaned}}{R_{soiled}}$ | dimensionless, 0–1 | Lab measurement of cleaning efficacy |
| 2 | Contact angle / Wettability | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LG}}$ (Young's eqn.) | degrees (°) | Predicts wettability by cleaning fluid |
| 3 | Surface roughness threshold | $R_a \le 0.8\ \mu m$ | µm | Hygienic finish benchmark |
| 4 | Minimum internal corner radius | $R \ge 6\ mm$ (food/pharma) | mm | EHEDG hygienic design |
| 5 | Self-draining slope | $\theta_{slope} \ge 3°$ | degrees | Avoids pooling of rinse water |
| 6 | Detachment vs adhesion force | $F_d > F_a$ | Newtons (N) | Cleaning will occur |
| 7 | Log reduction value | $LRV = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_{before}}{N_{after}}\right)$ | log units | Microbial cleaning validation |
| 8 | Coverage time | $T_c = \dfrac{A_{surface}}{v_{cleaner} \cdot w_{tool}}$ | seconds (s) | Time to clean a given area |
| 9 | Posture deviation index | $PDI = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_{actual,i} - X_{neutral,i})^2}$ | degrees | Ergonomic strain during cleaning |
| 10 | Biofilm removal efficiency | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{C_f}{C_i}$ | dimensionless | Disinfection studies |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Pipe-Symbol Safeguard:** All absolute value bars in the table above use \vert (or implicit grouping) to preserve markdown table integrity. In your exam scripts, write \lvert x \rvert for clarity.

---

## 2.3 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice

A designer's choice of corner radius, surface finish, and access port location directly determines:
- **Lifecycle cost of ownership** (cleaning labor + chemicals + downtime).
- **Operator musculoskeletal injury rate** (awkward cleaning postures).
- **Regulatory compliance** (FDA, ISO 14644, EHEDG, 3-A).
- **Cross-contamination probability** in food, pharma, and medical settings.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Give TWO design modifications to improve cleanability of [a product sketch]."* The model answer should always cite **(i) a geometric change** and **(ii) a material/surface change** — never one alone.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cleanability Index (CI) from First Principles

The CI is empirically derived from residue measurements. We start from a balance of removed vs. residual soil.

### Step 1 — Define the state variables
Let:
- $m_0$ = initial mass of soil deposited on the test surface (g).
- $m_r$ = mass of soil remaining after the standardized cleaning protocol (g).

### Step 2 — Express the fractional soil removal
The fraction of soil removed by cleaning is:

$$\eta_{removal} = \frac{m_0 - m_r}{m_0}$$

### Step 3 — Reformulate using the standard residue ratio
In laboratory practice, residue is usually reported as a *ratio* relative to the initial soiling. Define:

$$R_{soiled} = \frac{m_0}{A_s}, \qquad R_{cleaned} = \frac{m_r}{A_s}$$

Where $A_s$ = standardized test surface area (m²). Note that $R$ has units of g/m².

### Step 4 — Substitute and simplify

$$\eta_{removal} = \frac{R_{soiled} \cdot A_s - R_{cleaned} \cdot A_s}{R_{soiled} \cdot A_s} = 1 - \frac{R_{cleaned}}{R_{soiled}}$$

### Step 5 — Rearrange to the canonical form used in literature

$$CI = 1 - \frac{R_{soiled} - R_{cleaned}}{R_{soiled}}$$

**Conversion logic:** This step is algebraically equivalent to $\eta_{removal}$ because $(R_{soiled} - R_{cleaned})/R_{soiled}$ is exactly the *fractional soil remaining* — subtracting from 1 yields the fraction removed.

### Step 6 — Interpretation
- $CI = 1$ → perfect cleaning (no residue).
- $CI = 0$ → no cleaning occurred.
- $CI < 0$ → measurement error or cross-contamination (impossible in theory).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Detachment Force Balance at a Soil–Surface Interface

### Step 1 — Identify the forces acting on a soil particle
Consider a spherical soil particle of radius $r$ attached to a flat surface. Four force components are relevant:

$$F_{adhesion} = F_{vdW} + F_{electrostatic} + F_{chemical} + F_{mech}$$

### Step 2 — Van der Waals (dominant for dry, smooth contact)
For a sphere-on-flat contact (Hamaker approximation):

$$F_{vdW} = \frac{H \cdot r}{6 d^2}$$

Where:
- $H$ = Hamaker constant (≈ $10^{-19}$ to $10^{-20}$ J for typical organic soils on metals).
- $d$ = separation distance (≈ 0.2–0.5 nm at contact).

### Step 3 — Mechanical interlocking
For a rough surface with effective valley depth $\delta$:

$$F_{mech} \approx k_s \cdot \delta$$

Where $k_s$ = effective stiffness of the soil in the valley (N/m).

### Step 4 — Detachment force applied by cleaning
A cleaning fluid applies shear stress $\tau_c$ over the projected area of the particle $\pi r^2$, plus a normal lift from hydrodynamic pressure $P_{lift}$:

$$F_d = \tau_c \cdot \pi r^2 + P_{lift} \cdot \pi r^2$$

### Step 5 — Cleaning criterion
Cleaning occurs when:

$$F_d > F_{adhesion}$$

Equivalently, in stress form:

$$\tau_c + P_{lift} > \frac{F_{adhesion}}{\pi r^2}$$

### Step 6 — Design implication
To **improve cleanability** without changing the cleaner, the designer must **reduce $F_{adhesion}$** by:
- lowering $d$ (impossible — set by physics) OR
- increasing surface smoothness to reduce $F_{mech}$ (lower $\delta$) OR
- choosing a material with low Hamaker constant contrast (e.g., PTFE-coated steel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-style takeaway:** The cleanability of a surface is *not* solely a function of the cleaning process; it is a coupled function of **surface design** and **cleaning energy input**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How Each Design Lever Changes CI

| Design Lever | Physical Mechanism | Effect on $F_{adhesion}$ | Effect on $CI$ | Cost Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase corner radius (R ↑) | Eliminates soil traps | ↓↓ | ↑↑ | Moderate (tooling) |
| Reduce Ra (polish to mirror) | Reduces $F_{mech}$ | ↓ | ↑ | High (finishing) |
| Switch to SS 316 from mild steel | Reduces corrosion pits | ↓ | ↑ | High (material) |
| Add drainage slope ≥ 3° | Avoids pooling | indirect | ↑ | Low (geometry) |
| Increase access port diameter | Improves $A$, $T$ | none | ↑ (procedural) | Moderate |
| Apply PTFE coating | Lowers Hamaker contrast | ↓↓ | ↑↑ | High (coating) |
| Use COP instead of CIP | Allows disassembly | none | ↑↑ | Operational |
| Add shadow-free LED lighting | Improves visibility | none | ↑ (detection) | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> A "↑" denotes an *increase*; "↓" denotes a *decrease*. The number of arrows indicates magnitude (subjective scale for KTU 2-mark / 7-mark sub-parts).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Cleanability Design System


## 4.2 Decision Flow: When to Redesign vs. Re-procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Identify contamination zone] --> P2[Step 2: Is zone human-accessible?]
    P2 -->|Yes| P3[Step 3: Apply standard CIP/COP]
    P2 -->|No| P4[Step 4: Redesign for access]
    P3 --> P5[Step 5: Measure CI]
    P5 --> P6{CI greater than 0.85?}
    P6 -->|Yes| P7[Approved: Log and Monitor]
    P6 -->|No| P8[Step 6: Increase cleaning energy]
    P8 --> P9{CI acceptable now?}

    P9 -->|No| P10[Step 7: Surface / geometric redesign]
    P10 --> P4

    style P1 fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style P4 fill:#991b1b,color:#ffffff
```
