## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]

**(a)** With a neat labeled diagram, explain the **Archard wear equation**. Derive the relation and discuss the significance of each variable in the design of human-handled tools. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A steel tool handle is subjected to a normal grip force of 50 N over a sliding distance of 10 km during its service life. The hardness of the handle material is 2 GPa, and the wear coefficient against human skin is $k = 5 \times 10^{-5}$. Calculate the total wear volume. Comment on whether this volume is acceptable for ergonomic use. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Statement of Archard's Equation:**

$$
V = k \cdot \dfrac{F \cdot s}{H}
$$

where $V$ = wear volume (m³), $k$ = dimensionless wear coefficient, $F$ = normal load (N), $s$ = sliding distance (m), $H$ = material hardness (Pa).

**Step 2 — Diagram Description (refer to Section 4.2):** A labeled schematic should show the normal load $F$ acting on a hemispherical asperity sliding across a softer counterface, generating wear particles.

**Step 3 — Derivation Outline:**

- Real area of contact: $A_r = \dfrac{F}{H}$ (from the definition of hardness).
- Volume of hemispherical asperity penetration: $V_{particle} = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi x^3$.
- Total volume over sliding distance $s$: $V = k_{geom} \cdot A_r \cdot s$.
- Substituting $A_r$ and defining $k = k_{geom}$:

$$
V = k \cdot \dfrac{F \cdot s}{H}
$$

**Step 4 — Significance for Human-Handled Tools:**

- **Higher $H$** (hardness) → less wear → durable grip surface.
- **Lower $k$** (use of soft overmolds like TPE) → reduces skin abrasion.
- **Lower $F$** (ergonomic grip redesign) → minimizes repetitive strain and wear.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:**
- $F = 50$ N
- $s = 10$ km $= 10{,}000$ m
- $H = 2 \text{ GPa} = 2 \times 10^{9}$ Pa
- $k = 5 \times 10^{-5}$

**Step 1 — Substitute into Archard's equation:**

$$
V = k \cdot \dfrac{F \cdot s}{H}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute numerator:**

$$
F \cdot s = 50 \times 10{,}000 = 5 \times 10^{5} \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by hardness:**

$$
\dfrac{F \cdot s}{H} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{5}}{2 \times 10^{9}}
$$

$$
\dfrac{F \cdot s}{H} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by wear coefficient:**

$$
V = 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
V = 12.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{V = 1.25 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3 = 12.5 \text{ mm}^3}
$$

**Step 5 — Ergonomic Acceptability Comment:**

A wear volume of 12.5 mm³ over a 10 km sliding distance is exceptionally small. This corresponds to a uniform removal of less than 0.1 mm from the contact surface, well below the human tactile detection threshold (~0.025 mm for fingertips under light load). Therefore, the wear is **acceptable** for ergonomic use, and the chosen material is durable for the intended service life.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**(a)** Discuss the **four pillars of material durability** with specific reference to human factors engineering. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A prosthetic limb component is made of an aluminum alloy with $\sigma_u = 450$ MPa. During normal walking, it experiences a stress amplitude of 150 MPa. The fatigue strength coefficient is $\sigma_f' = 900$ MPa and $b = -0.1$. Determine:
   (i) The endurance limit $\sigma_e$.
   (ii) The fatigue life $N_f$.
   (iii) Whether the component has infinite life. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Pillar 1 — Mechanical Durability:**
Encompasses static strength, fatigue endurance, and impact toughness. In human factors, this ensures the product can bear static loads (a chair supporting a 100 kg user), repeated cycles (a prosthetic limb during walking, ~2 million cycles/year), and impact shocks (a dropped mobile phone).

**Pillar 2 — Tribological Durability:**
Wear resistance of moving or contacting surfaces. Critical for tool grips, shoe soles, and keyboard surfaces. Archard's equation quantifies this.

**Pillar 3 — Chemical/Electrochemical Durability:**
Resistance to corrosion from sweat, humidity, cleaning agents, and bodily fluids. Crucial for implants, surgical tools, and wearables.

**Pillar 4 — Environmental Durability:**
Resistance to UV, thermal cycling, ozone, and biological agents. Important for outdoor furniture and transportation interiors.

> The four pillars must be **simultaneously satisfied** in any human-centered design.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**(i) Endurance Limit:**

$$
\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \cdot \sigma_u
$$

$$
\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \times 450 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_e \approx 225 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**(ii) Fatigue Life** (Basquin's equation):

$$
N_f = \left( \dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_f'} \right)^{1/b}
$$

$$
N_f = \left( \dfrac{150}{900} \right)^{1/(-0.1)}
$$

$$
N_f = \left( \dfrac{1}{6} \right)^{-10}
$$

$$
N_f = 6^{10}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_f = 6.05 \times 10^{7} \text{ cycles}}
$$

**(iii) Infinite Life Assessment:**

Since $\sigma_a = 150$ MPa $< \sigma_e = 225$ MPa, the applied stress is below the endurance limit. The component has **theoretically infinite life** under ideal conditions. With ~2 million cycles per year of walking, this represents **>30 years of service** — well beyond typical prosthetic replacement intervals (3–5 years due to fit changes, not material failure). The aluminum alloy is therefore a safe and durable choice for this application.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (As per KTU 2024 Valuation Keys):**
> 1. **Missing units** in final numerical answers → lose 1 mark per occurrence. Always include SI units.
> 2. **Skipping the derivation assumption statement** (e.g., "assuming hemispherical wear particles") → lose 1 mark in derivations.
> 3. **Failing to link material property to human factor outcome** → lose up to 2 marks in application questions. Always end with "this means for the user..."
> 4. **Using $k$ as dimensional** without clarification → lose 0.5 mark. State explicitly that $k$ is dimensionless when $H$ is in Pa.
> 5. **Confusing fatigue limit with fatigue strength** — $\sigma_e$ is the *endurance limit* (for steels); for non-ferrous metals, use the *fatigue strength at $N = 10^7$ or $10^8$ cycles*.
> 6. **Neglecting safety factor** in real design → industrial practice uses a safety factor of 1.5–3 on top of theoretical life.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for PEBMT742 Module 4 — Durable Materials**

- ☐ **Definition**: Durable materials = materials retaining functionality over service life under human use conditions.
- ☐ **Four Pillars**: Mechanical, Tribological, Chemical, Environmental — must all be addressed in design.
- ☐ **Hardness unit**: GPa or HV/HRC; higher $H$ reduces wear per Archard.
- ☐ **Archard's Equation**: $V = k \cdot \dfrac{F \cdot s}{H}$ — **memorize variables and units**.
- ☐ **Empirical Fatigue Limit (Steels only)**: $\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \, \sigma_u$.
- ☐ **Basquin's Equation**: $N_f = \left( \dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_f'} \right)^{1/b}$ for high-cycle fatigue.
- ☐ **Wear Mechanisms**: Adhesive, Abrasive, Fatigue, Corrosive — know at least one example each.
- ☐ **Corrosion Types**: Uniform, Galvanic, Pitting, SCC — especially Pitting for implants.
- ☐ **Human-Factor Linkage**: Always answer "so what?" — explain how the property affects the **user**, not just the material.
- ☐ **Typical Material Choices**: TPE for grips, CFRP for prosthetics, PC for helmets, 440C SS for scalpels, HR foam for seating.
- ☐ **Standards to remember**: ASTM G154 (UV), ISO 10993 (biocompatibility), ASTM D2240 (hardness).
- ☐ **Safety Factor**: Apply 1.5–3× on theoretical calculations in real product design.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — PEBMT742 Durable Materials**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Legibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Legibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **Legibility (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> **Legibility** is defined as the characteristic of a visual display (text, numerals, symbols, or signs) that enables the observer to identify or recognize the individual elements (letters, digits, or symbols) rapidly, accurately, and with minimal effort. It is a perceptual attribute that quantifies the **discriminability** between a character and its surrounding background, and among adjacent characters.

In the context of Human Factors Engineering and Design (HFED), legibility is treated as a **systems-level design parameter** — not merely a typographic choice — because it directly governs the efficiency, accuracy, and safety of human–machine interaction in displays, signage, instruments, control panels, screens, and printed documentation.

> [!NOTE] **Difference Between Legibility and Readability — A Frequently Tested Distinction**
> - **Legibility** = can you *recognize each individual character*?
> - **Readability** = can you *comprehend the meaning of the full word/sentence*?
> Legibility is a prerequisite for readability. A page may be legible (you can see every letter) but not readable (the font or layout is uncomfortable for sustained comprehension).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to read the **license plate of a car parked 50 meters away at dusk**. Some characters are clear, others blur into each other. The *legibility* of that plate depends on:

- The **height of the digits** printed on it,
- The **thickness** of each stroke of the digit,
- The **contrast** between the white background and the black digits,
- The **spacing** between digits so they don't run together,
- And the **ambient lighting** falling on the plate.

Now shift that scenario to a Boeing 737 cockpit display at 35,000 feet. The pilot must read engine parameters, navigation waypoints, and warning indicators **in fractions of a second**. If the legibility is poor, lives are at stake. **Legibility, therefore, is the engineering discipline of ensuring that information arrives at the human eye in the most recognizable, error-free, and low-effort form possible.**

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEBMT742 – Module 4)**
> Legibility is grouped with **Coding, Symbol Design, and Visual Display Terminals (VDTs)** as a sub-topic of *Information Display and Communication*. Expect short 3-mark questions on definitions and 14-mark questions combining legibility with coding principles.

---

### 1.3 Primary Design Factors Influencing Legibility

The legibility of any visual display is governed by **six principal interacting variables**:

1. **Character Size (height & width)** — the physical dimensions of the glyph.
2. **Stroke Width** — the thickness of the lines forming the character.
3. **Contrast (Figure-Ground Luminance)** — the brightness difference between text and background.
4. **Font / Typeface Geometry** — serif, sans-serif, block, dot-matrix, etc.
5. **Spacing** — inter-character (tracking), inter-word, and inter-line (leading).
6. **Viewing Conditions** — distance, angle, illumination, vibration, and observer's visual acuity.

These factors are not independent. A change in one (say, increasing stroke width) demands compensating adjustments in others (e.g., increasing inter-character spacing) to preserve optimum legibility.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Seeing a Character — Visual Angle

The human eye resolves a character not by its physical height alone, but by the **angular subtense** it creates at the eye. This angular size is called the **visual angle (θ)** and is the fundamental currency of legibility theory.

For an object of vertical height **h** placed at a perpendicular viewing distance **d** from the observer's eye:

$$\theta = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\frac{h}{2d}\right)$$

For most engineering design calculations, the small-angle approximation holds because distances greatly exceed character dimensions ($\theta$ is typically only a few minutes of arc). Under this approximation:

$$\theta \;\approx\; \frac{h}{d} \quad \text{(radians)}$$

To convert to **minutes of arc** (the unit used in optometry and display design):

$$\theta_{\text{arcmin}} \;=\; \frac{h}{d} \times \frac{180}{\pi} \times 60 \;\approx\; 3438 \cdot \frac{h}{d}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why Visual Angle Matters**
> The retina cannot resolve two points that subtend an angle smaller than the eye's **minimum angular resolution** (≈ 1 minute of arc for a young, healthy eye under high contrast). Therefore, a character must subtend *at least* this angle to be seen. Practical engineering margins push this minimum to **5–10 minutes of arc** for comfortable reading.

---

### 2.2 Snellen's Acuity Principle Applied to Display Design

Snellen, who invented the familiar **20/20 vision** standard, established that the **just-legible detail** (such as the gap between two strokes of the letter "E") must subtend a visual angle of at least **1 minute of arc**, while the entire character should subtend approximately **5 minutes of arc** for reliable recognition.

$$\theta_{\text{character}} \;\geq\; 5 \text{ arc-minutes}$$

$$\theta_{\text{stroke-gap}} \;\geq\; 1 \text{ arc-minute}$$

This gives us the **engineering design rule for minimum character height**:

$$h_{\min} \;=\; d \cdot \tan(5') \;\approx\; \frac{d}{685}$$

> [!NOTE] **Worked Mental Check**
> At a viewing distance $d = 685 \text{ mm}$, the minimum legible character height is roughly **1 mm**. At $d = 6.85 \text{ m}$, the minimum becomes **10 mm**. This linear scaling is why road signs, cockpit displays, and overhead gantry signs must grow with viewing distance.

---

### 2.3 Stroke Width Ratio

The ratio of stroke width ($W$) to character height ($H$) is a critical legibility parameter. Research by Tinker and others established the optimal range:

$$\frac{W}{H} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{6} \text{ to } \frac{1}{8}$$

- Ratios **thinner than 1/10** → strokes appear broken or thread-like.
- Ratios **thicker than 1/5** → characters appear bold and crowded, with internal counters (closed loops) filling in.

---

### 2.4 Michelson Contrast

Contrast is the **luminance difference** between the foreground (text/symbol) and background. The standard **Michelson contrast formula** is used:

$$C_{M} \;=\; \frac{L_{\max} - L_{\min}}{L_{\max} + L_{\min}}$$

Where:
- $L_{\max}$ = luminance of the brighter element (in cd/m²).
- $L_{\min}$ = luminance of the darker element.

A value of $C_M = 1$ means **maximum contrast** (pure black on pure white); $C_M = 0$ means **zero contrast** (invisible).

> [!WARNING] **KTU Common Mistake**
> Students often confuse **Michelson contrast** with the simpler **Weber contrast** $C_W = (L_{\text{bg}} - L_{\text{fg}})/L_{\text{bg}}$. For display design problems in KTU exams, always check which formula is implied by the context. *Legibility of text on a screen uses Michelson's; visibility of an object against an adapting background uses Weber's.*

---

### 2.5 Recommended Illumination Levels (Lux) for Reading Tasks

| Task / Display Type | Recommended Illumination (lux) |
|---|---|
| Fine print reading (books, manuals) | 300 – 500 |
| Ordinary office reading | 200 – 300 |
| LCD / VDT screens | 150 – 250 (avoid glare) |
| Instrument panels (aircraft, vehicles) | 50 – 100 (low-light adaptation) |
| Highway sign at night (retro-reflective) | 5 – 30 (with vehicle headlights) |
| Cinema / dark auditorium safety signs | 1 – 5 (photoluminescent) |

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Visual angle (exact) | $\theta = 2\arctan(h/2d)$ | rad | Exact trigonometric form |
| 2 | Visual angle (small-angle approx.) | $\theta \approx h/d$ | rad | Valid when $d \gg h$ |
| 3 | Visual angle (arc-minutes) | $\theta = 3438 \cdot h/d$ | arc-min | Conversion factor used in optometry |
| 4 | Snellen minimum character angle | $\theta_{\min} = 5$ arc-min | arc-min | Empirical minimum for recognition |
| 5 | Minimum character height | $h_{\min} = d / 685$ | mm | For $d$ in mm; assumes 5 arc-min |
| 6 | Stroke-width-to-height ratio | $W/H \in [1/8,\; 1/6]$ | dimensionless | Tinker's optimum |
| 7 | Michelson contrast | $C_M = (L_{\max}-L_{\min})/(L_{\max}+L_{\min})$ | dimensionless | Range $[0,1]$ |
| 8 | Weber contrast | $C_W = \Delta L / L_{\text{bg}}$ | dimensionless | Range $[0,\infty)$ in principle |
| 9 | Inter-character spacing | $\geq 1 \times$ stroke width | mm | Minimum to avoid crowding |
| 10 | Line spacing (leading) | $\geq 1.5 \times$ character height | mm | For body text |
| 11 | Vernier acuity (minimum) | $\approx 4$ arc-seconds | arc-sec | Best human visual resolution |
| 12 | Snellen fraction conversion | $V = d_{\text{test}} / d_{\text{normal}}$ | unitless | E.g., 20/20, 6/6, 6/60 |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Exam Tip**
> In KTU numericals, the most frequently tested combinations are:
> (a) Given viewing distance, find minimum character height.
> (b) Given character height and viewing distance, find visual angle in arc-minutes.
> (c) Given two luminances, find Michelson contrast and comment on legibility.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: Cockpit primary flight displays (PFDs) are engineered so that the *minimum critical character* subtends at least 15 arc-minutes — a triple margin above the Snellen minimum — because pilots glance at them for under one second.
- **Road Sign Design**: MUTCD and IRC standards prescribe character heights of **200 mm for expressway signs at 30 m** and **400 mm at 100 m**, following Snellen scaling.
- **Pharmaceutical Packaging**: FDA requires a minimum 12-point font with $C_M \geq 0.7$ on drug labels to reduce medication errors.
- **Mobile UI Design**: Apple and Google Human Interface Guidelines specify a minimum 11 pt "comfortable" body text, and minimum 17 pt on a watch face at 30 cm viewing distance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Visual Angle Approximation

We start from exact geometry. Place a vertical character of height **h** at distance **d** in front of the eye. The character subtends an angle $\theta$ at the eye's nodal point, bounded by the two lines of sight from the top and bottom of the character.

By the tangent half-angle relationship:

$$\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) = \frac{h/2}{d} = \frac{h}{2d}$$

Therefore:

$$\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{h}{2d}\right) \qquad \text{(exact)}$$

Now apply the small-angle approximation $\arctan(x) \approx x$ for $x \ll 1$ (in radians):

$$\theta \;\approx\; 2 \cdot \frac{h}{2d} \;=\; \frac{h}{d} \qquad \text{(radians, small-angle approx.)}$$

To convert from radians to **arc-minutes**, we use the identity:

$$1 \text{ rad} = \frac{180 \times 60}{\pi} \text{ arc-min} \approx 3437.75 \text{ arc-min}$$

Therefore:

$$\theta_{\text{arc-min}} \;=\; \frac{h}{d} \cdot \frac{10800}{\pi} \;\approx\; 3438 \cdot \frac{h}{d}$$

This is the conversion factor used universally in optometry and visual ergonomics.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Minimum Character Height on a Dashboard

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A driver views the speedometer at a distance of 800 mm. According to the Snellen criterion, what should be the minimum height of the digital speed numerals (in mm) so that the digits are reliably legible?

**Step 1 — Identify the given data and the desired output.**

Given:
- Viewing distance: $d = 800 \text{ mm}$.
- Required minimum visual angle: $\theta_{\min} = 5 \text{ arc-min}$.

To find: $h_{\min}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the small-angle formula in arc-minutes.**

$$\theta = 3438 \cdot \frac{h}{d}$$

Solve for $h$:

$$h = \frac{\theta \cdot d}{3438}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numbers.**

$$h_{\min} = \frac{5 \times 800}{3438} \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate.**

$$h_{\min} = \frac{4000}{3438} \approx 1.163 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Apply engineering safety margin.**

For moving-vehicle displays, a 2× safety factor is recommended:

$$h_{\text{design}} \approx 2 \times 1.163 = 2.33 \text{ mm}$$

> **Answer:** The minimum character height is approximately **1.16 mm**; a practical design height is **2.3 mm**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Michelson Contrast on a VDT Screen

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A laptop screen displays text with foreground luminance $L_{\text{fg}} = 180 \text{ cd/m}^2$ and background luminance $L_{\text{bg}} = 15 \text{ cd/m}^2$. Compute the Michelson contrast and comment on the legibility.

**Step 1 — State the formula.**

$$C_M = \frac{L_{\max} - L_{\min}}{L_{\max} + L_{\min}}$$

**Step 2 — Identify maximum and minimum.**

Here $L_{\max} = 180$, $L_{\min} = 15$.

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$C_M = \frac{180 - 15}{180 + 15} = \frac{165}{195}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate.**

$$C_M \approx 0.846$$

**Step 5 — Interpret against the legibility benchmark.**

| $C_M$ Range | Legibility Verdict |
|---|---|
| $0.9 - 1.0$ | Excellent (printed text standard) |
| $0.7 - 0.9$ | Good (typical LCD) |
| $0.4 - 0.7$ | Marginal (acceptable in low ambient light) |
| $< 0.4$ | Poor — avoid for sustained reading |

> **Answer:** $C_M = 0.846$ indicates **good legibility** — within the typical LCD range. For prolonged reading under bright office lighting, raising $L_{\text{bg}}$ toward 30–40 cd/m² by reducing screen brightness would push $C_M$ closer to 0.85 and reduce eye strain.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Stroke Width Calculation

> **Problem:** A control-panel label uses uppercase characters 6 mm tall, designed to be read at 1.2 m. Compute the recommended stroke width using the Tinker $W/H = 1/7$ rule.

**Step 1 — Apply the ratio.**

$$W = \frac{H}{7} = \frac{6}{7} \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate.**

$$W \approx 0.857 \text{ mm}$$

> **Answer:** Stroke width should be approximately **0.86 mm**. A rounded manufacturing value of **0.9 mm** is acceptable.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Design Variable on Legibility

| Design Variable | Increase → Effect on Legibility | Decrease → Effect on Legibility | Interaction with Other Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| Character height ($h$) | ↑ Recognizability at longer distances | ↓ Becomes harder to read | Must scale with viewing distance |
| Stroke width ($W$) | ↑ Boldness, but crowding at high $W/H$ | ↓ Strokes appear thin, fragmented | Coupled to character height |
| Contrast ($C_M$) | ↑ Sharp figure-ground separation | ↓ "Ghosting," washed-out appearance | Falls with ambient glare |
| Viewing distance ($d$) | ↓ Angular size, ↓ legibility | ↑ Angular size, ↑ legibility | Inverse with $h$ |
| Illumination (lux) | ↑ Up to optimum (~500 lux) | ↓ Below 100 lux, legibility collapses | Couples with $C_M$ |
| Inter-character spacing | ↓ Too tight → confusability of letters like "rn"/"m" | ↓ Too wide → breaks word Gestalt | Coupled to stroke width |
| Line spacing (leading) | ↓ Too wide → eye loses line; too tight → vertical crowding | (reciprocal) | Independent |
| Typeface (serif vs sans-serif) | Serifs aid sustained reading on paper; sans-serif on screens | (depends on dot pitch) | Couples with viewing medium |

---

### 3.6 Comparative Table — Serif vs Sans-Serif vs Dot-Matrix

| Property | Serif | Sans-Serif | Dot-Matrix |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stroke uniformity | Variable | Uniform | Discrete dots |
| Best for | Printed body text | Screens, signs, displays | Legacy indicators, LEDs |
| Recognizability at distance | Good | Better | Fair |
| Crowding susceptibility | Moderate | Low | High |
| KTU Exam Recommendation | Note as "good for paper" | Note as "preferred for VDTs" | Note as "inferior for HFED" |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Factors Influencing Legibility

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[LEGIBILITY DESIGN SYSTEM]
    A0 --> B1[Character Geometry]
    A0 --> B2[Optical Environment]
    A0 --> B3[Observer Attributes]
    A0 --> B4[Display Medium]

    B1 --> C1A[Height H]
    B1 --> C1B[Stroke Width W]
    B1 --> C1C[Width-to-Height Ratio]
    B1 --> C1D[Inter-Character Spacing]
    B1 --> C1E[Line Spacing Leading]

    B2 --> C2A[Luminance of Foreground]
    B2 --> C2B[Luminance of Background]
    B2 --> C2C[Ambient Illumination Lux]
    B2 --> C2D[Glare and Reflections]
    B2 --> C2E[Color Temperature]

    B3 --> C3A[Visual Acuity Snellen]
    B3 --> C3B[Age of Observer]
    B3 --> C3C[Contrast Sensitivity]
    B3 --> C3D[State of Adaptation]

    B4 --> C4A[Paper Print]
    B4 --> C4B[LCD or LED Screen]
    B4 --> C4C[Retro-Reflective Sign]
    B4 --> C4D[Self-Luminous Display]

    C1A --> D1[Visual Angle theta]
    C1B --> D1
    C2A --> D2[Michelson Contrast CM]
    C2B --> D2
    C3A --> D3[Observer Resolution Limit]
    C3B --> D3

    D1 --> E1[LEGIBILITY VERDICT]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
```

---

### 4.2 Flow Diagram — Engineering Procedure for Sizing Display Characters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Step 1: Define Viewing Distance d] --> F2[Step 2: Choose Target Visual Angle]
    F2 --> F3[Step 3: Compute Minimum Height h equals d slash 685]
    F3 --> F4[Step 4: Apply Safety Factor 1.5x to 3x]
    F4 --> F5[Step 5: Compute Stroke Width h divided by 7]
    F5 --> F6[Step 6: Set Inter-Character Spacing]
    F6 --> F7[Step 7: Verify Michelson Contrast greater than 0.7]
    F7 --> F8{Contrast Acceptable?}
    F8 -- No --> F9[Step 8: Adjust Luminance or Background]
    F9 --> F7
    F8 -- Yes --> F10[Step 9: Finalize Design Spec]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Visual Angle Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G0[Visual Angle Geometry Schematic]
    G0 --> G1[Observer Eye at Origin]
    G1 --> G2[Line of Sight to Top of Character]
    G1 --> G3[Line of Sight to Bottom of Character]
    G2 --> G4[Angle theta is Subtended at Eye]
    G3 --> G4
    G4 --> G5[Character Height h Perpendicular at Distance d]
    G5 --> G6[Formula theta equals 3438 times h divided by d arc-minutes]
```

---

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing Font / Typeface

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Choose Typeface for Display] --> H2{Reading Duration?}
    H2 -- Short Glance under 2 sec --> H3[Sans-Serif Bold Block Letter]
    H2 -- Long Sustained over 30 sec --> H4{Medium?}
    H4 -- Paper Print --> H5[Serif Font e.g. Times]
    H4 -- VDT Screen --> H6[Sans-Serif Regular e.g. Helvetica]
    H3 --> H7[Verify Stroke Width Ratio W over H in 1 by 6 to 1 by 8]
    H5 --> H7
    H6 --> H7
    H7 --> H8[Run Contrast Check Michelson greater than 0.7]
    H8 --> H9[Approve Design]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q:** Define *legibility*. Distinguish it from *readability*.

**Model Answer:**

> **Legibility** is the ease with which individual characters, symbols, or numerals can be identified and recognized. It is determined by the physical characteristics of the character (size, stroke width, contrast) and the viewing conditions.
>
> **Readability**, on the other hand, refers to the ease with which the *meaning* of words, sentences, or passages can be comprehended as a whole — it depends on layout, line length, vocabulary, and reader motivation.
>
> **Key distinction:** Legibility is a *visual / perceptual* property of the symbol; readability is a *cognitive / linguistic* property of the text. Legibility is a prerequisite but does not guarantee readability.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for legibility definition, 1 mark for readability definition, 1 mark for the distinction.)*

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q:** State Snellen's principle of minimum visual angle for character recognition. What is the practical design implication?

**Model Answer:**

Snellen's principle states that for reliable recognition, a character (such as the letter "E") must subtend a visual angle of at least **5 minutes of arc** at the observer's eye, while the **critical detail** (e.g., the gap between strokes) must subtend at least **1 minute of arc**.

> **Practical implication:** This gives the engineering design rule $h_{\min} = d / 685$, where $h_{\min}$ is the minimum character height and $d$ is the viewing distance. Designers use this to size road signs, cockpit displays, gauges, and screen fonts. In critical applications (aviation, nuclear control rooms), a safety factor of 2×–3× is applied, raising the design angle to 10–15 arc-minutes.

*(3 marks: 1 mark stating the principle, 1 mark the 1' and 5' values, 1 mark design implication.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Module 4 carries an internal choice. **Attempt either Question A OR Question B in full.**

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Set A]`

**Q:** Legibility is the foundation of effective visual communication in man-machine systems.

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **physical and optical factors** that affect the legibility of textual and symbolic displays. (7 marks)

**(b)** A road sign on a national highway must be readable by a driver whose eye-level is **15 m** away from the sign. Using Snellen's criterion, determine (i) the **minimum character height** required, and (ii) the **stroke width** using the Tinker $W/H = 1/7$ rule. Recommend a design height after applying a **2× safety factor**, and state its significance. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Part (a): 7 Marks**

The physical and optical factors that affect legibility can be grouped into **four categories**:

1. **Character Geometry**
   - *Character height* — larger characters subtend a larger visual angle and are easier to recognize.
   - *Character width-to-height ratio* — typically 0.6 to 0.8 for digits and 0.5 to 0.7 for uppercase letters; too wide creates spacing issues.
   - *Stroke width-to-height ratio* — Tinker's optimum of $W/H = 1/7$ balances boldness and crowding.
   - *Inter-character spacing* — minimum of one stroke width prevents adjacent letters from merging.

2. **Contrast (Figure-Ground)**
   - **Michelson contrast** $C_M = (L_{\max}-L_{\min})/(L_{\max}+L_{\min})$ must exceed **0.6** for minimum acceptable legibility and **0.7–0.9** for sustained reading.
   - Color contrast (e.g., black on yellow, white on blue) follows WCAG-style luminance ratios.

3. **Viewing Conditions**
   - *Viewing distance and angle* — affects required $h_{\min}$.
   - *Illumination level* — optimum 200–500 lux for paper, 100–300 lux for VDTs.
   - *Glare* — reduces effective contrast.
   - *Vibration and motion* — dynamic viewing reduces effective acuity.

4. **Observer Characteristics**
   - *Visual acuity* (Snellen fraction) — must be ≥ 20/40 for most tasks.
   - *Age* — acuity declines after ~40 years (presbyopia), requiring larger characters.
   - *State of adaptation* — dark-adapted eyes are more sensitive but lose acuity for detail.

**(7 marks awarded: 2 marks for geometry, 2 marks for contrast, 2 marks for viewing conditions, 1 mark for observer.)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b): 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Viewing distance $d = 15 \text{ m} = 15{,}000 \text{ mm}$.
- Snellen's minimum visual angle $\theta_{\min} = 5 \text{ arc-min}$.
- Stroke ratio $W/H = 1/7$.
- Safety factor $k = 2$.

**(i) Minimum Character Height**

Using the arc-minute formula:

$$h_{\min} = \frac{\theta_{\min} \cdot d}{3438}$$

Substitute the values:

$$h_{\min} = \frac{5 \times 15{,}000}{3438} = \frac{75{,}000}{3438} \text{ mm}$$

$$h_{\min} \approx 21.81 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Stroke Width**

$$W = \frac{h_{\min}}{7} = \frac{21.81}{7} \approx 3.12 \text{ mm}$$

**Apply 2× Safety Factor:**

$$h_{\text{design}} = 2 \times 21.81 \approx 43.62 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{h_{\text{design}} \approx 44 \text{ mm}, \quad W_{\text{design}} \approx 6.2 \text{ mm}}$$

**Significance of the Safety Factor:**
The 2× margin compensates for (i) driver variability in visual acuity (presbyopia, fatigue), (ii) adverse weather conditions (fog, rain, headlight glare), (iii) vehicle vibration, and (iv) the brief ~0.5 s glance time available to drivers at highway speeds. It also brings the effective visual angle to **10 arc-minutes**, comfortably above the Snellen minimum, ensuring legibility for the *worst-case 95th-percentile observer*.

**(7 marks awarded: 2 marks (i) setup + answer, 1 mark (ii) stroke width, 1 mark safety factor calculation, 3 marks significance discussion.)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Set B]`

**Q:** Modern display design relies on quantifiable legibility metrics.

**(a)** Explain the **Snellen principle**, the **Michelson contrast formula**, and the **Tinker stroke-width rule**. State the design implication of each. (7 marks)

**(b)** A cockpit Primary Flight Display (PFD) shows altitude numerals against a dark background. The luminance of the green digits is **240 cd/m²** and the surrounding bezel area is **12 cd/m²**. Compute the **Michelson contrast** and comment on its adequacy for instrument-panel legibility under night-time cockpit conditions. If the design standard requires $C_M \geq 0.85$, what change would you recommend? (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Part (a): 7 Marks**

1. **Snellen Principle (1.5 marks):** Defines the minimum visual angle for reliable character recognition. The *full character* must subtend ≥ **5 minutes of arc**; the *critical detail* (e.g., stroke gap) must subtend ≥ **1 minute of arc**. **Design implication:** Gives the rule $h_{\min} = d/685$, used to size road signs, gauges, displays, and printed labels.

2. **Michelson Contrast Formula (1.5 marks):** $C_M = (L_{\max} - L_{\min})/(L_{\max} + L_{\min})$, ranging from 0 (invisible) to 1 (maximum contrast). **Design implication:** Display designers must ensure $C_M \geq 0.7$ for sustained reading and $C_M \geq 0.85$ for safety-critical displays. Falling below 0.4 renders text marginally legible.

3. **Tinker's Stroke-Width Rule (1.5 marks):** The ratio of stroke width $W$ to character height $H$ should lie in $[1/8,\; 1/6]$, with optimum at **1/7**. **Design implication:** Going thinner than 1/10 fragments strokes; thicker than 1/5 fills counters (closed loops) of letters like "e" and "o." Designers of LCD fonts and engraved panels anchor their typeface geometry around this optimum.

4. **Synthesis (2.5 marks):** Together these three metrics govern the *spatial* (Snellen), *luminance* (Michelson), and *structural* (Tinker) dimensions of legibility. A balanced design ensures that all three criteria are met simultaneously. Failure in any one — too small, too faint, or too bold — destroys legibility regardless of the other two being optimal.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b): 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $L_{\max} = 240 \text{ cd/m}^2$ (digit luminance).
- $L_{\min} = 12 \text{ cd/m}^2$ (bezel/background).
- Design standard: $C_M \geq 0.85$.

**Step 1 — Write Michelson's formula.**

$$C_M = \frac{L_{\max} - L_{\min}}{L_{\max} + L_{\min}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$C_M = \frac{240 - 12}{240 + 12} = \frac{228}{252}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$C_M \approx 0.905$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the design standard.**

$$0.905 \geq 0.85 \quad \checkmark$$

The contrast is **adequate** — comfortably above the 0.85 threshold required for safety-critical night-time instrument-panel displays.

**Step 5 — Recommended adjustments (optional improvements).**

Although 0.905 is acceptable, the display could be further optimized by:

- **Reducing the background luminance** from 12 to ~8 cd/m² → new contrast $C_M = 232/248 \approx 0.935$ (improved).
- **Slightly dimming the digits** to ~200 cd/m² while keeping background at 12 cd/m² → $C_M = 188/212 \approx 0.887$ (just above threshold, lower power, lower glare).
- **Adding a black mask layer** around each digit (a *halation control* technique) to enhance figure-ground separation.

> The first option is recommended because night-time pilots are *dark-adapted* and excess digit luminance causes temporary after-images (the "blooming" effect). Slightly darker backgrounds preserve dark adaptation while keeping $C_M$ very high.

**(7 marks awarded: 1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark answer, 1 mark comparison with threshold, 3 marks recommendation + reasoning.)**

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Legibility Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting units conversion:** Students often write $h_{\min} = d/685$ with $d$ in *meters* and $h$ in *mm*, getting answers off by a factor of 1000. Always verify: the formula $h_{\min} = d/685$ requires **both in the same units** (typically mm).
> 2. **Using the wrong contrast formula:** Mixing up Weber's $C_W = \Delta L / L_{\text{bg}}$ with Michelson's $C_M$ loses a full mark in numericals. Read the question carefully — if both luminances are explicitly given, it is almost always Michelson's.
> 3. **Ignoring the safety factor in design problems:** KTU examiners reward the *applied* legibility design, not just the raw minimum. Always add a 1.5×–2× safety margin and justify it in one sentence.
> 4. **Confusing legibility with readability:** A classic 2-mark trap. Keep the distinction crisp.
> 5. **Skipping the engineering implication:** Numerical answers without a concluding remark (e.g., "this height is suitable for daytime driving at 80 km/h") lose the "application" mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Legibility**

- **Definition:** Legibility = ease of *recognizing individual characters*. Readability = ease of *comprehending meaning*.
- **Three Pillars of Legibility:** Spatial (Snellen), Luminance (Michelson), Structural (Tinker).
- **Snellen's rule:** Character ≥ **5 arc-min**, detail ≥ **1 arc-min**.
- **Engineering formula:** $h_{\min} = d / 685$ (same units on both sides).
- **Conversion factor:** 1 rad ≈ 3438 arc-min.
- **Tinker's optimum stroke ratio:** $W/H = 1/7$ (acceptable range 1/8 to 1/6).
- **Michelson contrast:** $C_M = (L_{\max}-L_{\min})/(L_{\max}+L_{\min})$; aim for $C_M \geq 0.7$ (general) and $\geq 0.85$ (safety-critical).
- **Minimum inter-character spacing** ≈ 1 stroke width; **minimum line spacing** ≈ 1.5 × character height.
- **Always apply a safety factor** of 1.5×–3× on top of Snellen's minimum for real-world engineering displays.
- **Typeface rule:** Sans-serif for screens and signs; serif for sustained paper reading; avoid dot-matrix for HFED.
- **Illumination rule:** 200–500 lux for paper; 100–250 lux for VDTs; instrument panels are designed for dark adaptation (lower lux + higher contrast).
- **Observer variability:** Aging (presbyopia), glare, vibration, and reduced glance time all *degrade* effective legibility — design margins must absorb these.
- **KTU hot topics:** Snellen's principle, Michelson contrast, stroke-width ratio, minimum height from viewing distance, and the distinction between legibility and readability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Coding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Coding (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Human Factors Engineering and Design**, **Coding** is defined as the systematic process of assigning distinguishable sensory symbols, signals, or patterns (visual, auditory, or tactile) to displays, controls, equipment, processes, or information categories so that the user can **rapidly identify, recognize, discriminate, and respond** to them with minimum cognitive load, minimum search time, and minimum error rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** *"Coding is the technique of using physical features (such as shape, color, size, position, or sound) to label or differentiate displays, controls, or warning signals, thereby enabling the operator to interpret information pre-attentively and act on it with reduced reaction time."*

Mathematically, the **effectiveness of a code (E)** can be conceptualized as a function of the number of discriminable categories it can produce:

$$E = f(D_c, R_t, E_r)$$

where:
- $D_c$ = Discriminability (number of clearly distinguishable categories)
- $R_t$ = Reaction time (lower is better)
- $E_r$ = Error rate (lower is better)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Traffic Signal:**
> Imagine you are driving on a busy road. You don't have time to read a sentence. Instead, the system **codes** information using **three colors (Red, Yellow, Green)**, each placed in a **fixed position (Top, Middle, Bottom)**, and each emitting a **distinct sound pattern in pedestrian crossings**. Because the signals are *coded*, you recognize the meaning **within 0.5 to 1.0 seconds**, even in poor visibility, with minimal conscious thought. This is precisely what industrial coding achieves in cockpits, control rooms, hospitals, and power plants.

**Another Analogy — Kitchen Spice Jars:**
Your mother labels spice jars using:
- **Shape** of the jar (round for salt, tall for pepper)
- **Color** of the cap (red for chili, green for coriander)
- **Size** of the label icon

Even in the dark, you identify the right spice. This is **multi-modal coding** — a hallmark of excellent human-factors design.

---

## 1.3 Why Coding is Critical in Engineering Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU examiners love this topic:** Coding is a high-frequency Module 4 question because it links **basic psychology (perception)** directly to **applied engineering design** (displays, HMIs, aviation, medical devices).

| Engineering Domain | Example of Coding |
|---|---|
| **Aviation Cockpit** | Color-coded warning lights (red = critical, amber = caution, green = normal) |
| **Medical Devices** | Color + shape coding of syringes and IV lines to prevent drug mix-ups |
| **Industrial Control Rooms** | Audible alarms with distinct pitch/temporal patterns for different faults |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Tactile dot on the "F" and "J" keys of a keyboard |
| **Automotive Dashboards** | Icon-based warning lamps (engine, oil, battery) using shape coding |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Codes (Overview)

Codes are classified into **three primary sensory modalities**:

1. **Visual Codes** — conveyed through light, color, shape, size, position, motion.
2. **Auditory Codes** — conveyed through pitch, loudness, temporal pattern, timbre.
3. **Tactile / Haptic Codes** — conveyed through texture, shape, vibration.

A detailed breakdown is provided in **Section 2**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Principle Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Major Code Modalities

### A. Visual Coding

Visual coding is the **most widely used** code modality in human-machine systems. It uses the human eye's ability to discriminate multiple physical features simultaneously.

**Sub-categories of Visual Coding:**

| # | Visual Code Type | Physical Variable | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Color Coding** | Wavelength of light | Warning lights, pipelines, wires |
| 2 | **Shape Coding** | Geometric form | Icons, buttons, switches |
| 3 | **Size Coding** | Magnitude of feature | Knobs, gauges, text |
| 4 | **Position Coding** | Spatial location | Toggle switch up/down |
| 5 | **Brightness Coding** | Luminance | Backlit displays, LEDs |
| 6 | **Motion / Flash Coding** | Temporal change | Alarms, emergency beacons |
| 7 | **Alphanumeric / Pictorial Coding** | Symbolic content | Labels, signage |

### B. Auditory Coding

Auditory coding is essential when:
- The operator's **eyes are occupied** (e.g., driving, piloting).
- The signal must be **perceived in 360°** (e.g., fire alarms).
- **Visual coding is overloaded** in the workspace.

**Sub-categories:**

| # | Auditory Code | Variable | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pitch Coding** | Frequency (Hz) | Low-tone for "low fuel", high-tone for "fire" |
| 2 | **Loudness Coding** | Amplitude (dB) | Louder = more urgent |
| 3 | **Temporal Pattern Coding** | Beep-burst sequence | "Beep-Beep-BEEP" = critical alarm |
| 4 | **Timbre Coding** | Spectral content | Voice vs. bell vs. horn |

### C. Tactile / Haptic Coding

| # | Tactile Code | Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Texture** | Differentiating tools by feel |
| 2 | **Vibration Pattern** | Mobile phone silent alerts |
| 3 | **Raised Symbols (Braille)** | Accessibility |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Principle / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **mandatory KTU revision summary** — these principles appear frequently in ESE question papers.

| Principle | Statement | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **P1: Redundancy Principle** | Use **at least 2 codes simultaneously** (e.g., color + shape) | Increases detectability and reduces confusion in degraded conditions |
| **P2: Minimum Number of Codes** | Never use more codes than the operator can **reliably discriminate** (max ≈ 5–7 for color, 5 for shape) | Avoids confusion and misidentification |
| **P3: Compatibility Principle** | Code meaning must be **consistent with user expectations** (red = danger, green = safe) | Leverages learned cultural associations |
| **P4: Standardization Principle** | Use the **same code for the same meaning** across all equipment | Builds operator mental model |
| **P5: Distinctiveness Principle** | Codes must be **clearly separable** in the chosen sensory channel | Avoids "borderline" identifications |
| **P6: Perceptual Limit Principle** | Respect Weber's Law — perceived difference $\Delta I$ is proportional to baseline intensity $I$ | A code differing by 10% may be undetectable |
| **P7: Population-Specific Coding** | Account for **color-blindness** (~8% of males) | Color alone is risky; pair with shape |

---

## 2.3 Color Coding — Deeper Analysis

Color is the **most powerful** visual code because it is processed pre-attentively by the brain. However, it has strict guidelines.

**Standard Color Code Conventions (ANSI Z535 / IEC 60073):**

| Color | Meaning | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Red** | Danger / Emergency / Stop | Fire alarm, emergency stop button, critical fault |
| **Yellow / Amber** | Caution / Warning | Abnormal condition requiring attention |
| **Green** | Normal / Safe / Run | Equipment operating normally, "go" signal |
| **Blue** | Mandatory Action | "Wear ear protection", "Read manual" |
| **White / Grey** | Neutral / Information | Status messages |
| **Orange** | Warning of dangerous parts | Live electrical terminals |

**Weber's Law Applied to Color Coding:**

For two colors to be perceived as **different**, the wavelength difference must satisfy:

$$\frac{\Delta \lambda}{\lambda} \geq k$$

where $k$ is the Weber fraction (≈ 0.01–0.02 for hue discrimination in the normal photopic range).

**Just Noticeable Difference (JND) for Brightness:**

$$\frac{\Delta L}{L} \geq 0.08 \text{ (Weber fraction for luminance)}$$

This means if a control panel uses LED brightness to code urgency, the **brightness ratio** between two adjacent levels must be at least **8% higher** than the previous level to be reliably distinguished.

---

## 2.4 Shape Coding — Engineering Utility

Shape coding is **superior to color** in conditions of:
- Low illumination
- Color-blindness
- Smoke, fog, vibration
- Monochrome displays

**Recommended Shape Set (per Sanders & McCormick, 7th ed.):**

The number of shapes the human can reliably discriminate is **between 5 and 7** for quick identification. Common shape-coding alphabets include:

- Circle, square, triangle, cross, plus, star, hexagon
- For aviation: triangle = caution, circle = mandatory, square = information

---

## 2.5 Auditory Coding — The ICAD Principle

The **International Community for Auditory Display (ICAD)** recommends that auditory codes be designed with:

1. **Distinct frequency** (pitch) for each category.
2. **Distinct temporal pattern** (rhythm/sequence).
3. **Appropriate loudness** (60–80 dB above threshold for alerts).
4. **Compatibility with the urgency** (higher pitch → higher urgency).

> [!NOTE]
> **Hazard Matching Rule:** High-pitched, fast, irregular sounds are perceived as more urgent than low-pitched, slow, regular sounds — independent of the actual hazard level. Designers must use this carefully to avoid **false alarm desensitization**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Coding Application | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Nuclear Control Room** | Color + position + audible | Critical alarms detected in <1 s |
| **Aviation (TCAS)** | Verbal aural ("TRAFFIC, TRAFFIC") + visual | Pilot warned even head-down |
| **ICU Monitors** | Audible + color + icon | Nurse detects desaturation while multi-tasking |
| **CNC Machines** | Tactile + color-coded tool holders | Wrong tool insertion prevented |
| **Consumer UI** | Icon + color + text | International usability |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Selection Logic

## 3.1 The Coding Selection Decision Matrix

A common KTU question type is: *"Given a specific engineering scenario, recommend an appropriate coding scheme and justify it."* The decision logic is derived step-by-step below.

### Step 1: Identify the Operator's Visual/Manual Load

**Question to ask:** *Are the operator's eyes occupied?*

- **YES** → Use **Auditory** or **Tactile** codes.
- **NO** → Visual codes are usually best (highest information density).

### Step 2: Identify the Environmental Conditions

| Condition | Best Code Choice |
|---|---|
| High ambient noise | **Visual** |
| Low light / smoke | **Tactile** or **Shape** (not color) |
| 360° perception required | **Auditory** |
| Vibrating environment | **Auditory** (visual flicker may be missed) |
| High cognitive load | **Pre-attentive** feature (color, motion) |

### Step 3: Determine the Number of Categories

Use **Miller's Law (7 ± 2 chunks)** as the upper limit:

$$N_{codes} \leq 7 \pm 2$$

- If $N_{codes} \leq 3$: Single code (e.g., color only) suffices.
- If $3 < N_{codes} \leq 7$: Use **2 redundant codes**.
- If $N_{codes} > 7$: Use **hierarchical coding** (group codes into families).

### Step 4: Check for User Population Constraints

Account for:
- **Color blindness** (≈ 8% of males, 0.5% of females) → avoid color-alone.
- **Hearing impairment** → add visual backup to auditory codes.
- **Cultural variability** → avoid ambiguous symbols.
- **Language barriers** → prefer **pictograms** over text.

### Step 5: Apply the Redundancy Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** *For any safety-critical system, codes must be redundant (minimum 2 independent channels).*

**Redundancy Equation:**

$$R_{total} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - r_i)$$

where $r_i$ is the reliability of the $i$-th code channel, and $n$ is the number of independent channels.

**Worked Example:**
If a color code has reliability $r_1 = 0.90$ and a shape code has $r_2 = 0.85$, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{total} &= 1 - (1 - 0.90)(1 - 0.85) \\
&= 1 - (0.10)(0.15) \\
&= 1 - 0.015 \\
&= 0.985
\end{aligned}
$$

So the redundant system gives **98.5% reliability** — a huge improvement over the single best code (90%).

### Step 6: Verify Against Standards

Check conformance to:
- **ANSI Z535.1–Z535.6** (USA — Product Safety Signs)
- **ISO 3864** (International — Safety Signs)
- **IEC 60073** (International — Color coding for indicators/actuators)
- **MIL-STD-1472** (USA Military — Human Engineering)

---

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Cockpit Warning Coding

**Scenario:** A fighter jet cockpit must alert the pilot to **5 distinct system failures**: engine fire, hydraulic failure, fuel low, radar lock, and ejection seat armed.

**Step-by-step KTU-style solution:**

**Step A — Number of categories = 5.** This is within Miller's 7 ± 2 limit, so a single redundant scheme is feasible.

**Step B — Visual load:** Pilot's eyes are on the HUD/outside. So **auditory component is mandatory.**

**Step C — Select color sub-codes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Engine fire} &\rightarrow \text{Red} \\
\text{Hydraulic failure} &\rightarrow \text{Amber} \\
\text{Fuel low} &\rightarrow \text{Amber (with different shape)} \\
\text{Radar lock} &\rightarrow \text{Red (flashing)} \\
\text{Ejection armed} &\rightarrow \text{Green}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Apply shape redundancy** (per P1):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Engine fire} &\rightarrow \text{Red + Flame icon} \\
\text{Hydraulic} &\rightarrow \text{Amber + Drop icon} \\
\text{Fuel low} &\rightarrow \text{Amber + Pump icon} \\
\text{Radar lock} &\rightarrow \text{Red + Crosshair icon (flashing)} \\
\text{Ejection} &\rightarrow \text{Green + Seat icon}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step E — Add auditory redundancy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fire} &\rightarrow \text{Loud, high-pitch, continuous tone} \\
\text{Hydraulic} &\rightarrow \text{Lower pitch, intermittent} \\
\text{Fuel} &\rightarrow \text{Two-tone chime} \\
\text{Radar} &\rightarrow \text{Rapid pulse ("bweep-bweep-bweep")} \\
\text{Ejection} &\rightarrow \text{Verbal "EJECTION ARMED"}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step F — Compute reliability:**
Let each channel have $r = 0.85$ reliability. Three independent channels:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{total} &= 1 - (1 - 0.85)^3 \\
&= 1 - (0.15)^3 \\
&= 1 - 0.003375 \\
&= 0.9966 \\
&\approx 99.66\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This satisfies aviation safety standards (typically > 99.9% required for catastrophic hazards).

---

## 3.3 Common Coding Failures — KTU Pitfalls

| Failure Mode | Cause | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Color-only coding** | Ignored color-blindness | Red-green confusion = wrong action |
| **Too many codes** | Exceeded 7 ± 2 | Confusion, slow response |
| **Inconsistent coding** | Different colors for same function in different machines | Operator error |
| **Cultural mismatch** | Symbol not universal | Misreading |
| **Single-channel coding** | No redundancy | Failure in degraded conditions |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of Codes in Human Factors Engineering

The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the complete taxonomy of coding modalities and their sub-categories as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Coding in Human Factors] --> B[Visual Codes]
    A --> C[Auditory Codes]
    A --> D[Tactile and Haptic Codes]

    B --> B1[Color Coding]
    B --> B2[Shape Coding]
    B --> B3[Size Coding]
    B --> B4[Position Coding]
    B --> B5[Brightness Coding]
    B --> B6[Motion and Flash Coding]
    B --> B7[Alphanumeric and Pictorial]

    C --> C1[Pitch Coding]
    C --> C2[Loudness Coding]
    C --> C3[Temporal Pattern Coding]
    C --> C4[Timbre Coding]

    D --> D1[Texture Coding]
    D --> D2[Vibration Pattern Coding]
    D --> D3[Static Shape Coding]

    B1 --> E1[Red, Amber, Green etc.]
    B2 --> E2[Circle, Triangle, Square]
    B6 --> E3[Flashing, Blinking Beacons]
    C3 --> E4[Beep Burst Sequences]
    D2 --> E5[Mobile Phone Silent Alerts]
```

## 4.2 Coding Selection Decision Flow

The next diagram shows the **decision logic** an ergonomist follows when selecting a code for an engineering display.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Start: Define Information Category] --> Q1{Are operators eyes occupied?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Aud1[Use Auditory Code]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Environmental visibility poor?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Shape1[Use Shape or Tactile]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Number of categories}
    Q3 -->|1 to 3| Simple1[Single channel code acceptable]
    Q3 -->|4 to 7| Red1[Apply 2 channel redundancy]
    Q3 -->|Greater than 7| Hier1[Use hierarchical or grouped codes]
    Simple1 --> Std1[Verify against ISO 3864 and IEC 60073]
    Red1 --> Std1
    Hier1 --> Std1
    Aud1 --> Std1
    Shape1 --> Std1
    Std1 --> Final[Validate with User Testing]
```

## 4.3 Redundancy Principle Block Diagram

This block diagram illustrates how redundant coding channels combine to yield higher system reliability.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Input[Information Source] --> C1[Code Channel 1: Color]
    Input --> C2[Code Channel 2: Shape]
    Input --> C3[Code Channel 3: Auditory Tone]
    C1 --> Merge[Operator Perception Layer]
    C2 --> Merge
    C3 --> Merge
    Merge --> Out[Correct Identification]
    Merge -.->|Failure of one channel| Backup[Remaining channels still convey info]
```

## 4.4 Color Code Meaning Map (Standard Convention)

```mermaid
graph LR
    R[Red] --> R1[Danger or Emergency]
    R --> R2[Stop or Critical Fault]
    Y[Yellow or Amber] --> Y1[Caution or Warning]
    Y --> Y2[Abnormal Condition]
    G[Green] --> G1[Normal or Safe State]
    G --> G2[Go Signal]
    B[Blue] --> B1[Mandatory Action Required]
    W[White or Grey] --> W1[Neutral Information]
    O[Orange] --> O1[Warning of Live or Dangerous Parts]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEBMT742]**

**Define coding in the context of human factors engineering. List any four visual coding techniques with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Coding is the assignment of distinguishable physical features (visual, auditory, or tactile) to displays, controls, or signals so that the operator can identify them rapidly with minimum search time and error rate.

**Four Visual Coding Techniques (1.5 marks):**

| Technique | Example |
|---|---|
| **Color Coding** | Red stop button on industrial machines |
| **Shape Coding** | Triangular caution sign on roads |
| **Size Coding** | Large knob for primary, small knob for secondary control |
| **Position Coding** | Toggle switch UP for ON, DOWN for OFF |

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEBMT742]**

**Explain why redundancy is a key principle in coding design. Mention one engineering example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Explanation (2 marks):** Redundancy means using **two or more independent code channels** (e.g., color + shape) to convey the same information. This protects against failure of any single channel due to environmental degradation, sensor limitations, or user disability (such as color blindness). It dramatically increases system reliability per the formula:

$$R_{total} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - r_i)$$

**Engineering Example (1 mark):** In an ICU ventilator alarm, an **audible tone** is paired with a **flashing red light and a textual message** on the display. Even if the nurse's hearing is impaired or the room is dark, the alert is conveyed.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEBMT742]**

#### (a) Classify the different types of visual codes used in human factors engineering. Discuss the advantages and limitations of color coding in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Visual Codes (3 marks):**

The principal visual codes are:

1. **Color coding** — uses wavelength to differentiate categories.
2. **Shape coding** — uses geometric form (square, triangle, circle).
3. **Size coding** — uses magnitude of a feature.
4. **Position coding** — uses spatial location.
5. **Brightness / luminance coding** — uses intensity of light.
6. **Motion / flash coding** — uses temporal change.
7. **Alphanumeric / pictorial coding** — uses symbols, letters, icons.

**Color Coding — Advantages (2 marks):**

- Processed **pre-attentively** (recognized in <250 ms without conscious effort).
- High **information density** — many distinct hues in a small space.
- **Universal** — transcends language barriers.
- **Intuitive associations** — red = danger, green = safe (culturally learned).

**Color Coding — Limitations (2 marks):**

- **Color blindness:** ~8% of males and 0.5% of females have red-green deficiency.
- **Environmental dependency:** Color is unreliable in low light, smoke, fog, or vibration.
- **Display dependency:** Monochrome displays, faded prints, and aging LEDs degrade color fidelity.
- **Context dependence:** The same color may mean different things in different industries.
- **Limited categories:** Only **5–7** colors are reliably distinguishable.

**Conclusion:** Color is a powerful code but must be paired with redundant channels for safety-critical applications.

---

#### (b) Design a coding scheme for a hospital ICU bedside monitor that must display 6 different alarm conditions to a nurse whose eyes are often on the patient, not the screen. Justify each design choice. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify the constraints (1 mark):**
- Operator's eyes are on the patient (not the screen) → auditory code mandatory.
- High-noise environment (other alarms, staff conversations) → auditory code must be distinct.
- Safety-critical → redundant coding required.
- 6 alarm conditions → within Miller's 7 ± 2 limit.

**Step 2 — Auditory coding scheme (2 marks):**

| Alarm | Pitch | Pattern | Loudness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cardiac arrest** | Highest (≈ 3000 Hz) | Continuous warble | Loudest (≈ 75 dB) |
| **Ventilator disconnect** | High (≈ 2500 Hz) | 3 short beeps repeating | 70 dB |
| **Oxygen desaturation** | Mid-high (≈ 2000 Hz) | 2 short beeps repeating | 65 dB |
| **IV line occlusion** | Mid (≈ 1500 Hz) | Single beep every 5 s | 60 dB |
| **Drug infusion end** | Low-mid (≈ 1000 Hz) | Single chime | 55 dB |
| **Battery low (device)** | Low (≈ 500 Hz) | Single soft pulse every 30 s | 50 dB |

**Step 3 — Visual (redundant) coding scheme (2 marks):**

| Alarm | Color | Flash Rate | Icon |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cardiac arrest | **Red** | 4 Hz flash | Heart with cross |
| Ventilator | **Red** | 2 Hz flash | Lung icon |
| O₂ desaturation | **Amber** | 1 Hz flash | O₂ symbol |
| IV occlusion | **Amber** | Steady | Drop icon |
| Drug end | **Blue** | Steady | Syringe icon |
| Battery low | **White** | Slow pulse | Battery icon |

**Step 4 — Justification (2 marks):**

- **Auditory mandatory** because eyes are on the patient.
- **Pitch hierarchy** matches perceived urgency (Psychoacoustic studies show high pitch = high urgency).
- **Temporal patterns** are distinct, so the nurse can identify the alarm by *sound signature* alone.
- **Color and shape redundancy** ensure correct identification even in poor visibility or color-blindness.
- **Total reliability** with 3 independent channels: $R = 1 - (1-0.85)^3 \approx 99.7\%$.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEBMT742]** *(Alternative Choice)*

#### (a) Explain the principles of effective coding design. Discuss the role of auditory coding in industrial alarm systems. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principles of Effective Coding (3.5 marks):**

1. **Discriminability** — Codes must be clearly distinguishable from one another.
2. **Redundancy** — Use at least two independent channels.
3. **Compatibility with user expectations** — Use culturally learned meanings.
4. **Standardization** — Same code = same meaning across equipment.
5. **Perceptual adequacy** — Difference must exceed the **Just Noticeable Difference (JND)** per Weber's Law: $\Delta I / I \geq k$.
6. **Population coverage** — Account for color blindness, hearing impairment, language.
7. **Minimum number of codes** — Limit to **7 ± 2** (Miller).
8. **Conspicuity** — Code must attract attention when needed.

**Auditory Coding in Industrial Alarm Systems (3.5 marks):**

- Auditory codes are essential when **operator's eyes are occupied** or in **360° perception environments**.
- **Pitch coding:** Higher pitch = higher perceived urgency.
- **Temporal patterns:** Distinct rhythms (e.g., 2-beep, 3-beep, continuous) help the operator identify the alarm type.
- **Loudness coding:** Louder = more urgent, but must not exceed **85 dB** (occupational safety).
- **Timbre coding:** Use of distinct voice, bell, horn, or buzzer tones.
- **Standards compliance:** IEC 60601-1-8 (medical), ISO 7731 (industrial auditory danger signals).
- **Risk:** "Alarm fatigue" from too many false or nuisance alarms — codes must be **prioritized** so that the most critical alarms are the most attention-grabbing.

---

#### (b) Compare visual, auditory, and tactile coding. For each modality, list two engineering applications and state one major limitation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Modality | Engineering Applications | Major Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| **Visual** | (1) Color-coded pipeline markers in chemical plants. (2) Icon-based dashboard warnings in cars. | Affected by lighting, smoke, color blindness, and operator's gaze direction. |
| **Auditory** | (1) Fire alarms in buildings. (2) Verbal collision warnings in aviation ("TRAFFIC, TRAFFIC"). | Affected by high ambient noise; can cause "alarm fatigue"; may startle the operator. |
| **Tactile** | (1) Braille on elevator buttons for the visually impaired. (2) Vibration alerts in smartphones for silent mode. | Low information density (only 2–3 distinguishable categories); limited to hands-on use. |

**Comparison summary (2 marks):**

- **Visual codes** have the **highest information density** but require the operator to look.
- **Auditory codes** have **omnidirectional reach** but can be masked by noise.
- **Tactile codes** are **silent and private** but have low bandwidth.

**Conclusion:** Best practice in safety-critical systems is to use **all three modalities in a redundant hierarchy**, with the most critical information being conveyed via the channel least likely to fail in that scenario.

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on "Coding" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "coding" with "labeling"** — Coding is about *perceptual* discrimination (color, shape, sound), NOT textual labels. Marks are lost when students define coding as "writing names on buttons."
> 2. **Ignoring color blindness** — Any coding scheme that relies **only** on red/green will be penalized. Examiners expect explicit mention of **redundancy** for color-blind safety.
> 3. **Violating Miller's 7 ± 2 rule** — Students propose 10+ color codes. Maximum reliable is **5–7**.
> 4. **Forgetting environmental factors** — Vibration, smoke, and ambient noise *change* the optimal code. Examiners reward answers that mention these.
> 5. **No mention of standards** — Top answers cite **ISO 3864, ANSI Z535, IEC 60073, or MIL-STD-1472**. Bonus marks awarded.
> 6. **Missing the redundancy formula** — When asked to design, students often forget to **compute** the reliability of redundant channels. Show the equation!
> 7. **Ignoring population constraints** — Cultural and accessibility factors (Braille, color blindness, hearing impairment) are expected to be addressed.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Coding (Module 4, PEBMT742):**
>
> - **Definition:** Coding = assigning distinguishable sensory features (visual/auditory/tactile) to displays, controls, or signals for fast recognition.
> - **Three modalities:** Visual, Auditory, Tactile/Haptic.
> - **Visual codes:** Color, Shape, Size, Position, Brightness, Motion, Alphanumeric.
> - **Auditory codes:** Pitch, Loudness, Temporal pattern, Timbre.
> - **Tactile codes:** Texture, Vibration, Static shape.
> - **Color standard:** Red = danger, Amber = caution, Green = safe, Blue = mandatory, White = info, Orange = live parts.
> - **Miller's Law:** Use **7 ± 2** codes maximum per display.
> - **Redundancy:** Always use ≥ 2 independent channels for safety-critical.
> - **Reliability formula:** $R_{total} = 1 - \prod (1 - r_i)$.
> - **Weber's Law:** $\Delta I / I \geq k$ — minimum discriminable step.
> - **Account for color blindness** (~8% males) — never rely on color alone.
> - **Standards:** ISO 3864, ANSI Z535, IEC 60073, MIL-STD-1472.
> - **Auditory hazard rule:** Higher pitch + faster tempo = higher perceived urgency.
> - **Avoid alarm fatigue** — prioritize alarms and minimize false triggers.
> - **Always justify** your code choice using the selection decision matrix (Step 1–6 of Section 3).
